# Ars Magica Chronology

[OriginalMadman v2.52b wip, 2026-03-31] \
IMPORTANT! This document version is open for public commentary and correction. You may submit corrections directly in it by just editing - which will be reviewed and taken into account for approval/rejection. Thank you for your help in this community endeavour. Keep any discussion about this document here: [https://forum.atlas-games.com/t/ars-magica-chronology-an-in-progress-updated-really-big-fat-timeline-of-the-order/171381](https://forum.atlas-games.com/t/ars-magica-chronology-an-in-progress-updated-really-big-fat-timeline-of-the-order/171381)

### What is this?

This document contains a vast chronology for the Mythic Europe of the Ars Magica role-playing game, detailing historical events relevant to the setting. It covers a vast span, beginning in prehistory and extending to the 13th century. The timeline highlights the rise of magical traditions, the formation of the Order of Hermes, and significant events impacting the Order and Europe. \
 \
It’s compiled, edited and written by fans from various Ars Magica sources - now open license, as well as real-world history and mythology. It includes fictional annotations with commentary and clarifications, sometimes argumentative and challenging, sometimes sharp-witted or judgmental. Discrepancies and conflicting information exist in the source material, but we have tried to maintain consistency and canon as best as possible.

The goal is to provide a rich and interesting resource for both players and storyguides (gamemasters), as well as encourage support for Ars Magica. It can also be used as-is for playing other roleplaying games - or simply read for enjoyment, as it contains no game-related rules or data. Previous knowledge, or possession of Ars Magica is not strictly required, but helpful. An extensive glossary of terms is provided for reference.

## Introduction

*"The gods sleep on, and all who know the truth hope that they will sleep on until the end of time, for if they stirred from slumber and walked once more upon the earth, they could well destroy all of civilization, and likely the Order along with it. Always remember, despite how the Old Ones might appear, they are not human, but monstrous indeed."*

*"Today, it is said, the Old Ones sleep upon Mount Olympus, in the forest of Tertius Quid, and some say at Stonehenge. The dwellings of the gods do not exist in time as most things do, but rather return to time periodically as they will. Only if you visit those places at the right moment will you be able to enter the gods’ palaces and gardens, and maybe meet with them, if you are lucky or accursed enough."*

– Archmage Infamitus, The True Origins of the Magical Traditions

This is a chronology and history of Magic and the Order of Hermes. 

It is written mainly from the point of view of the order and annotated by various magi, but mainly a specific magus of House Mercere, and events may be colored from that. 

Some actual historical dates may not coincide with other histories, mainly because authorities and historians tend to disagree. Events were mainly entered in the order found, but events inside the Order are usually listed before mundane events. 

Often, a range of mundane and Hermetic dates do not quite agree; this is usually an artifact of approximation and rounding.

Events considered of great import to the Order (at least by the fictional author) are in boldface.

The gloss and the introductions to the centuries contain short comments, exhortations, references to related events, color commentary and unsolicited opinions. The opinions noted in the gloss and the century introductions are strictly opinions of the fictional and somewhat diffident author(s). Readers are encouraged to disagree with them.

While the (fictional) author is assumed to be extremely well informed, he is not to be considered omniscient, nor morally correct.


### Compiler’s Intention

I put this chronology version together because I originally just wanted to find a good one for personal use at my own table (a long running campaign in the Rhine Tribunal, partly chronicled here for those interested: 

[https://docs.google.com/document/d/10td_pRNap4pv72N06DfB-zCaDwbJ9UIayzjkMqxZI0U/edit#heading=h.gjdgxs](https://docs.google.com/document/d/10td_pRNap4pv72N06DfB-zCaDwbJ9UIayzjkMqxZI0U/edit#heading=h.gjdgxs) ).

The jump off starting point was the timeline compiled by Tim S. O’Brien in 2001-2002-ish and shared by Erik Tyrell on the Atlas Games forum in 2006 here: [https://forum.atlas-games.com/t/hermetic-timeline/1003](https://forum.atlas-games.com/t/hermetic-timeline/1003)

(see this post for additional credits, many people contributed)

*Update April 2025 for v2.50: Unfortunately, the majority of these original entries are now gone in the necessary "not-Open License" purge. Still, it is what got me started and this document wouldn't be where it is without that timeline.*

When I got started, this base consisted of about 45 of these pages.

However, it was in need of a lot of updates and fixes. The previous work only covered earlier editions so anything related to the rewritten setting for the current Ars Magica 5th edition (Arm5e) had to be added. After far too many hours of work put in, the document had more than quadrupled in size and I decided to just share it forward as hopefully it can somehow enrich my fellow sodales campaigns as well. I intend to keep it updated with community provided comments and fixes, as well if/when any new Ars Magica releases should happen. See Appendix at the end for notes and versioning information.

This chronology document is mainly a compilation and should be considered as a freely put together fan resource for the Ars Magica game, as released under open license. No infringement on the rights of Atlas Games or anybody else is intended. Please continue to support Ars Magica and Atlas Games by buying all the excellent supplements available!

For updates, discussion and comments; here is my thread on the Atlas Games forum: \
[https://forum.atlas-games.com/t/ars-magica-chronology-an-in-progress-updated-really-big-fat-timeline-of-the-order/171381](https://forum.atlas-games.com/t/ars-magica-chronology-an-in-progress-updated-really-big-fat-timeline-of-the-order/171381)

Enjoy,

OriginalMadman [OM]

## Glossary of Terms


<table>
  <tr>
   <td>Aegis of the Hearth
   </td>
   <td>A special ritual invented by Notatus early in the Order's history and shared at the Grand Tribunal of 799 that protects a covenant from magical and supernatural intrusion. It must be cast annually at a vis cost. Its defensive capability is of critical importance to the Order and is of key importance to its dominance. The Order carefully and violently guards its secret.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Apprentice
   </td>
   <td>A student trained in Hermetic magic by a magus for approximately 15 years. Apprentices are bound by the Oath of Hermes upon completing their Gauntlet and becoming full magi.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Arcane Connection
   </td>
   <td>A magical link between an object or creature and its source, allowing spells to be cast at a distance and/or better penetrate magical defenses. Connections range from weak (lasting days) to permanent, and can be strengthened through Fixing Arcane Connections in a laboratory.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Archmagus \
Archmaga
   </td>
   <td>A prestigious title granted to highly accomplished magi. A Magister may hold a single proxy sigil of a retired magus, while an Archmagus can hold up to two. Archmagi are typically recognized for great magical deeds or contributions to the Order.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Artes Liberales
   </td>
   <td>The seven classical liberal arts, the foundation of medieval academic education. Important for Hermetic magic and academic studies. Divided into Trivium (grammar, logic, and rhetoric) and Quadrivium (arithmetic, geometry, astronomy, and music). Sometimes expanded to include medicine and architecture.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Aura
   </td>
   <td>A supernatural presence influencing an area. Auras come in four types - Magic, Faerie, Divine, and Infernal - and affect magical activities performed within them.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Blatant Gift
   </td>
   <td>A stronger and more disruptive version of The Gift, making social interactions even more difficult. Mundanes react with outright hostility or fear.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Botch
   </td>
   <td>A severe magical failure that causes unpredictable consequences, often negative. Results vary from simple spell failure to Warping, Twilight episodes or magical disasters.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Ceremonial Casting
   </td>
   <td>A method of enhancing Spontaneous Magic by taking extra time to perform ritualistic gestures, chanting, and using symbolic tools.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Certamen
   </td>
   <td>A formal magical duel resolving disputes within the Order. The duel is non-lethal and relies on mental strength, magical ability, and choice of Techniques and Forms. While decisive in political matters, it cannot force a magus to violate the Code or relinquish fundamental rights. Normally it does not cause harm (that's taboo), but the winner may cast a spell upon the loser.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Charged Item
   </td>
   <td>A magic item with a limited number of uses, created without needing permanent vis investment. Useful for one-time powerful effects.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Code of Hermes
   </td>
   <td>The legal framework governing the Order of Hermes, drafted by Guernicus and influenced by Tytalus. It defines acceptable behavior among magi and establishes mutual cooperation, restricts interaction with mundane politics, prohibits scrying on fellow magi and bans forming alliances with demons on pain of death.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Coill Trí
   </td>
   <td>A legal entity in Hibernia that allows the Order of Hermes to negotiate treaties collectively rather than dealing with each Irish magical tradition individually. Its membership rivals that of the Hibernian Tribunal.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Consor
   </td>
   <td>In the Normandy Tribunal, a valued mundane lord may become an acknowledged asset of the covenant and declared consor, thereby allowed to be granted magical support.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Court Wizard
   </td>
   <td>A magus who serves a noble or ruler, often violating the Code of Hermes by interfering with mundane affairs. Many Jerbiton magi navigate this practice carefully.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Covenant
   </td>
   <td>A community or home of magi who live and conduct research together, their laboratories, libraries, and resources. Originally, covenants were organized by House, but over time, they became more diverse communities.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Cursors
   </td>
   <td>Athletic grogs in the Normandy Tribunal who, during disputes, physically prove whether a vis source is within the legal seisin range of a covenant by running a trial distance.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Diedne
   </td>
   <td>The forsaken House, known for its druidic roots and later accused of diabolism. Eradicated in the Schism War. Rumors persist that some Diedne traditions survived in secret.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Dominion
   </td>
   <td>The Divine magical aura, the influence of God. Spreads where the faithful act, stronger near or inside holy sites. Church bells extend Dominion.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Domus Magna
   </td>
   <td>The headquarters of a Hermetic House. Examples include Durenmar (Bonisagus), Harco (Mercere), Coeris (Tremere), Crintera (Bjornaer), and Verdi (Verditius).
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Durenmar
   </td>
   <td>The first covenant founded by Bonisagus, located in the Black Forest and the domus magna of House Bonisagus. It houses the Great Library and is the site of the Grand Tribunal.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Duresca Scrolls
   </td>
   <td>Controversial documents allegedly revealing Founder Guernicus' secret plan to dominate the Order through manipulation of the Peripheral Code. Their authenticity remains disputed.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Enigma
   </td>
   <td>A central philosophy within House Criamon involving the understanding of the universe's mysteries and Twilight. Criamon magi pursue enlightenment through riddles and meditation.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Faerie
   </td>
   <td>Beings and forces from the Realm of Faerie. Often capricious, sometimes malevolent, but bound by strict contracts and bargains, they embody human myths, stories, and emotions. Faeries manipulate mortals for vitality and exist in self-reinforcing narratives.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Familiar
   </td>
   <td>A magical creature bonded to a magus, strengthening their magic and offering companionship. The bond requires Vis and time in a laboratory to establish.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Fast Casting
   </td>
   <td>A technique allowing a magus to cast a spell in reaction to an opponent’s action. Requires quick thinking and magical agility. Used to rapidly cast a spell in reaction to immediate danger.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Filius/Filia/Filii
   </td>
   <td>Latin for son, daughter, and children. Terms for an apprentice who has passed their Gauntlet and their relation to their former master (Parens).
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Forms
   </td>
   <td>The ten aspects of magic representing natural phenomena that Hermetic magic can manipulate: Animal, Aquam, Auram, Corpus, Herbam, Ignem, Imaginem, Mentem, Terram, and Vim. Combined with Techniques to create spells.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Formulaic Magic
   </td>
   <td>Predefined, structured spells that a magus can cast with consistency and precision, as opposed to the more flexible Spontaneous Magic. Formulaic spells require study and are written in Laboratory Texts.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Gauntlet
   </td>
   <td>The final test for an apprentice to prove their worthiness as a magus. Each House has its own Gauntlet tradition.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Gentle Gift
   </td>
   <td>A version of The Gift that does not provoke instinctive mistrust in mundanes. Highly valued by magi who interact frequently with the mundane world.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Grand Tribunal
   </td>
   <td>The Order's most important gathering. Representatives from each Tribunal convene every 33 years at Durenmar, the Order’s founding site. It settles large-scale disputes and sets precedents for Hermetic law.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Grog
   </td>
   <td>A trained mundane soldier or servant who serves a covenant and its magi.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Gruagachan
   </td>
   <td>Powerful hedge wizards from Celtic traditions.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Hedge Wizard
   </td>
   <td>A derogatory term used within the Order for non-Hermetic magicians. Some hedge traditions (e.g., Gruagachan, Folk Witches) possess unique magical abilities but lack Hermetic Theory’s flexibility.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Hermetic Arts
   </td>
   <td>The structured magical system of the Order, invented by Bonisagus, comprising five Techniques and ten Forms. The foundation of all Hermetic spells.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Hermetic Magic
   </td>
   <td>The powerful and versatile magic system developed by Bonisagus, integrating many pre-existing magical traditions into a unified framework based on the works of Hermes Trismegistus.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Hermetic Oath
   </td>
   <td>The solemn vow taken by all members of the Order, binding them to the Code of Hermes. Breaking this Oath can result in Wizard’s March.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Hermetic Theory:
   </td>
   <td>The unified magical system developed by Bonisagus, integrating the wisdom of classic philosophers and diverse magical traditions into a structured framework. It remains the foundation of all Hermetic magic.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Hermetica
   </td>
   <td>A body of alchemical, astrological, philosophical, and magical writings attributed to Hermes Trismegistus. Influential in both mundane and magical traditions.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Historia Magica et Ordo Hermetis
   </td>
   <td>The seminal historical chronicle of magic and the Order of Hermes, written from a Hermetic perspective. It is known for both accuracy and bias.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Hoplite
   </td>
   <td>A magus who enforces Hermetic law through combat, often hunting criminals (Renounced Magi) or dealing with supernatural threats. Many are from House Flambeau.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Houses of Hermes
   </td>
   <td>One of the twelve groups within the Order of Hermes, each with its own philosophy and tradition. The True Lineages trace their heritage directly to the Founders and maintain strong traditions, they are Bonisagus, Guernicus, Mercere and Tremere. The Mystery Cults are Houses that focus on unique magical mysteries, and are Bjornaer, Criamon, Merinita and Verditius. The Societates are Houses defined by shared philosophies or magical approaches and consists of Flambeau, Jerbiton and Tytalus. The outlier is House Ex Miscellanea, which is about half of the Order, but it is more of a disparate collective of various magical traditions. Ex Miscellanea was originally founded as Ordo Miscellanea, a competitor to the order, by Pralix after her war with Damhan-Allaidh. It was subsequently brought into the Order as a new House.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Laboratory
   </td>
   <td>A magus’s private workspace for researching spells, creating magic items, and conducting magical experiments. Laboratories are highly personalized, and require significant resources.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Laboratory Text
   </td>
   <td>A written record of magical research, spells, or enchantments, useful for replication.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Longevity Ritual
   </td>
   <td>A complex magical process that extends a magus’s life, delaying the effects of aging. Requires Vis and laboratory work, but does not prevent eventual death.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Magister
   </td>
   <td>A rank signifying a senior magus within a Tribunal. A Magister holds authority over younger magi and may act as a mentor, judge, or enforcer.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Mundane
   </td>
   <td>Ordinary people without magical powers. Mundanes instinctively distrust those with The Gift, making social interactions more difficult for magi.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Mystery Cults
   </td>
   <td>Secretive factions within the Order that possess specialized magical knowledge.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Nous
   </td>
   <td>The All-Mind or divine intellect, which Hermes Trismegistus meditated upon to understand the universe. A concept in Hermetic philosophy.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Order of Hermes \
<em>Latin: Ordo Hermetis</em>
   </td>
   <td>The dominant magical society to which most powerful European magi belong, uniting them under a common magical system and legal framework.
<p>
Also known as The Hermetic Order, and often referred to as just the Order (capitalized).
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Ordo Miscellanea
   </td>
   <td>A loose coalition of hedge wizards in northern and western Europe, aggressively recruited by Pralix during the Schism War and rivalling the Order afterwards. Later integrated into the Order as House Ex Miscellanea.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Parens
   </td>
   <td>The magus who trained an apprentice. The title is used formally once the apprentice has passed their Gauntlet.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Parma Magica
   </td>
   <td>A special defensive minor ritual known only to the Order, protecting an individual magi against magic, the supernatural and the negative effects of The Gift. Magi need to renew it at every sunup and sundown. Its defensive capability is of critical importance to any individual magi as well as the Order and is of key importance to its dominance. It is taught to all new magi by their parens as the final step once they pass their Gauntlet, and it's one of the Order’s most closely guarded secrets.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Penetration
   </td>
   <td>The ability to bypass a spell target’s Magic defenses. Increased through Arcane Connections and Sympathetic Connections.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Peregrinatores
   </td>
   <td>Wandering magi in the Rhine Tribunal who seek hospitality from covenants without permanent affiliation. Their lifestyle is tolerated but can be politically precarious.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Philosophia Perennis
   </td>
   <td>The "perennial philosophy" - a concept in classical Hermeticism advocating the synthesis of various mystical and intellectual traditions into a single, unified pursuit of wisdom.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Praeco
   </td>
   <td>The presiding officer at a Tribunal, typically the eldest magus present. They generally hold no additional power but serve as a ceremonial and administrative leader.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Primus/Prima
   </td>
   <td>The leader of a Hermetic House, often chosen by political maneuvering or merit.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Quaesitores
   </td>
   <td>Magi charged with interpreting and enforcing the Code of Hermes. Most are from House Guernicus, though some outsiders achieve Quaesitorial rank. They judge disputes beyond simple inter- or intra-covenant resolution and protect the Order. They must maintain accreditation to officiate at Tribunal.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Realm
   </td>
   <td>One of the four supernatural forces: The Divine, Magic, Faerie, and Infernal.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Redcap
   </td>
   <td>A non-Gifted messenger of House Mercere, easily identified by their traditional red cap. Redcaps serve as messengers, traders and diplomats within the Order.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Regiones
   </td>
   <td>Singular: Regio. Magical or supernatural realms that are partially or completely separate from the mundane world, requiring special means to enter. Regiones exist within all four supernatural Realms (Magic, Faerie, Divine, Infernal).
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Renounced
   </td>
   <td>Magi who violate the Code to an irredeemable degree (e.g., attacking fellow magi after a Wizard’s War ends) and are formally expelled from the Order. They are marked for death.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Ritual Magic
   </td>
   <td>Powerful and complex magical ceremonies requiring vis and significant time to perform.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Sahirs
   </td>
   <td>Powerful Islamic sorcerers in Iberia. In 780, Iberian Hermetic magi negotiated a truce with them, avoiding open conflict.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Sanctum
   </td>
   <td>A magus’s private and protected domain, often containing their laboratory. Protected by law and often marked with a Sanctum Marker.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Schism War
   </td>
   <td>The devastating civil war in the early 11th century that eradicated House Diedne and nearly tore the Order apart​. The war’s causes remain a subject of debate, and its wounds linger centuries later.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Scrying
   </td>
   <td>Using magic to observe or spy on someone from a distance. Forbidden and highly taboo against fellow members of the Order.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Seisin
   </td>
   <td>In the Normandy Tribunal, a vis source within a day’s march of a covenant is considered automatically owned by that covenant. A seisin recognized by the Tribunal grants its owners exclusive right of harvest.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Senex
   </td>
   <td>A legal role specific to the Normandy Tribunal. The senex of a covenant nearest to a town has superior rights to operate there, provided its church bells can be heard within the covenant. In particular, this means that if the actions of the senex collaterally damage the interests of another covenant, then 'ruin' has not been caused. The senex is judged to be the covenant in which the town’s church bells sound the loudest, as determined by a Quaesitor. Therefore, if a town’s church bells cannot be heard at all in a covenant that covenant is too distant from the town to claim senex rights, and for many towns therefore there is no extant senex.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Sodalis/Sodales
   </td>
   <td>Singular/Plural. A formal term for a fellow member of the Order.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Spell Mastery
   </td>
   <td>Advanced knowledge and skill in a specific formulaic spell.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Spontaneous Magic
   </td>
   <td>Unstructured magic that is flexible but often weaker than Formulaic spells.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Stringles
   </td>
   <td>A coven of witches based on the island of Polyaigos. Their traditions predate the Order, and they remain semi-independent.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Sympathetic Connection
   </td>
   <td>A magical link that increases the likelihood of penetrating magical resistance.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Talisman
   </td>
   <td>A highly personal magical item, uniquely attuned to its owner.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Techniques
   </td>
   <td>The five fundamental magical manipulations: Creo (create), Intellego (understand), Muto (change), Perdo (destroy), and Rego (control).
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>The Founders
   </td>
   <td>The original 13 magi who established the Order of Hermes.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>The Gift
   </td>
   <td>The rare magical ability that allows a person to work magic. It also causes mundane suspicion and discomfort unless mitigated by Gentle Gift or Parma Magica.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Token
   </td>
   <td>A physical object that grants immunity to the effects of an Aegis of the Hearth when given by its casters.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Tribunal
   </td>
   <td>A regional assembly of magi held every seven years to resolve disputes, vote on legal matters, and administer Hermetic law. Also refers to the geographical divisions of the Order.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Tropaeum
   </td>
   <td>A vis source officially owned and regulated by a Tribunal rather than an individual covenant.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>True Faith
   </td>
   <td>Deep, unwavering religious belief that provides supernatural protection against magic.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>True Lineages
   </td>
   <td>The four Houses that can trace their apprenticeships directly to the Founders (Bonisagus, Guernicus, Mercere, Tremere).
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Twilight
   </td>
   <td>A mystical state in which a magus is overwhelmed by raw magical energy and the magus becomes absorbed into magic, or even removed from the natural world. It can be temporary or permanent (Final Twilight). Over time, Warping and Twilight experiences accumulate and distort a magus’s mind and body. A real problem for elder magi.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Vis
   </td>
   <td>Magical essence, typically crystallized in physical form (such as rare herbs, gemstones, or body parts of magical creatures). It is necessary for rituals, enchantments, and longevity magic. It is the primary currency of the Order.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Voting Sigil
   </td>
   <td>A symbol representing a magus’s vote in Tribunals. It remains valid in perpetuity until evidence of their death is presented.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Warping
   </td>
   <td>Long-term exposure to powerful magic or high auras (6+ level) causes gradual supernatural transformation. Very powerful spells (level 30+) can cause immediate Warping​Praecepta Hermetica - E…
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Wizard’s March
   </td>
   <td>A formal declaration that a magus is an outlaw and can be hunted and killed by other members of the Order.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Wizard's Sigil
   </td>
   <td>All magic used by a magus leaves a personal imprint (like a magical fingerprint). This can be used to identify magic use and track its caster. Quaesitors specialize in analyzing these imprints​.
<p>
The term 'sigil' or 'voting sigil' also refers to the voting symbol of each magus which remains valid in perpetuity until evidence of their death is presented.
   </td>
  </tr>
  <tr>
   <td>Wizard's War
   </td>
   <td>A formal conflict between magi, declared in advance and limited in duration. It is the only legal means for one magus to slay another without being Renounced.
   </td>
  </tr>
</table>





# Historia Magica et Ordo Hermetis

*A History of Magic and the Order of Hermes, by Radulphus Stilum filius Mercator scholae Mercandi Rerum Scriptor ab Harco.*


## Foreword

The History of Magic and our great Order of Hermes is, sadly, incomplete. Accounts of many of the most interesting and crucial events in our history are lacking, related mainly by tradition or recorded only in the off-hand references of elder magi. Some of these events were not properly recorded, magi usually being otherwise occupied with experimentation and enchantment.

 \
Others, particularly Tribunals, were recorded, but often the original records are lost, damaged, or destroyed. Perhaps more detailed records still languish in the libraries of Durenmar, Magvillus, and lost Val-Negra, but recovering them has not proved easy.

The following Chronology is divided up into logical ages of history, where each age chapter begins with an overview of the age and its most important aspects from the Hermetic view.

The timeline lists the mundane date, the Event, and the commentary or margin notations of the author and other "contributors" (in italics). The Tribunal location of the Event is noted first, if known and clear.


## The Order of Hermes

*Wise words, although written by my decaying hand, remain imperishable through time; imbued with the medicine of immortality by the Nous [the All-Mind, or God]. Be unseen and undiscovered by all those who will come and go, wandering the wastelands of life. Be hidden, until an older heaven births human beings who are worthy of your wisdom. *

– The Last Words of Hermes Trismegistus. Having sounded this prayer over the works of his hands, Hermes was received into the sanctuary of eternity. 

*Let us work together as one and grow hale and strong.*

– The Hermetic Oath, Bonisagus the Founder

Nearly all wizards of power in the civilized world are members of the Order of Hermes (also Enigmatic Order of Hermes or, informally, the Order).

The Order of Hermes was founded in 767 A.D. by the witch Trianoma and the wizard Bonisagus. Trianoma had the desire to unite the disparate magi of Europe into a single group, who could learn from one another. She did not know how to accomplish this goal until she met Bonisagus who had developed a breakthrough in communicating and manipulating magic - a consistent theory termed "Hermetic Magic" for its roots in both the Greek deity Hermes upon which the ancient Roman Cult of Mercury was based, and the works of the legendary figure Hermes Trismegistus (Hermes the Thrice Great) in the third century BC [see timeline entry C. 299-200 BC]. 

Hermes was informed by Nous (the All-Mind, or God) to meditate on the nature of the universe being reflected in his own being, to extract the divine powers of nature and unite them with the powers of this soul. The universe is a text to be read, "The Book of Nature" and through our divine intellect, we are able to unite with it and understand it. The universe is met in the mineral salts of a spagyric [herbal] tincture; the psychic centers or ‘stars’ are experienced through the influence of the planets on one's personal horoscope.

The eclectic nature of classical Hermeticism was founded on the fundamental premise that the desire for knowledge can be satisfied by consulting a variety of sources finding their roots in the ‘philosophia perennis’ (Perennial Philosophiae). It is synthetic in that diverse ideas are not only tolerated, but also unified into a seamless whole. Expanding on this, Bonisagus' revolutionary work on Magic Theory would allow magi to share information using a common frame of reference.

While magicians at this time were scattered, rarely social and highly distrustful of each other as a rule, two factors strongly favored mutual cooperation. One was Trianoma's political vision of an organization that would unite the Gifted for their mutual benefit. The other - arguably more important -  was Bonisagus' second breakthrough, the Parma Magica (loosely translated as "magic shield"); a highly efficient and easily taught personal ritual which could allow these disparate individuals and traditions to meet on common ground with some assurance of safety.

Trianoma became Bonisagus' student, learned his theory and the Parma, then traveled throughout Europe recruiting other wizards. Eleven magi joined her. Bonisagus and the eleven magi established twelve Houses in a fellowship called the Hermetic Order of Hermes. The Order quickly recruited like-minded wizards, teaching the protective Parma Magica to magi who would take the Order's Oath of mutual cooperation. The wizards who refused to take the Oath were not taught the Parma Magica.

With very few exceptions, magi who quit or refuse to join the Order are hunted down and destroyed.

While each of the Order's twelve Houses maintains a distinct baseline or tradition in pursuing and transmitting knowledge and power, the Order is also divided into Tribunals, each defined by a geographic region. Each Tribunal holds a gathering of its magi once every seven years; attendance is not mandatory, though it is essential for certain procedures (e.g. those who have completed their apprenticeships are formally presented for official membership; the Quæsitores judge the types of disputes deemed beyond simple inter- or intra-covenant resolution). 

Once every 33 years, each Tribunal sends a representative to the Grand Tribunal at the site of the Order's founding in Schwarzwald (the Black Forest), the original covenant of Durenmar.

 \
Most of the Order speak Latin as a common language, enabling magi from across Europe to share magical books and spells.

For an outsider's perspective, read "Dialect on the Wizards of Hermes" by Father Reginald, a priest who spent time observing the Order as a guest of a covenant.


## The Four Major Crises

There have been four major crises in the Order since its founding in 767 AD.



1. The war against the Spider, and the subsequent forming and later integration of House Ex Miscellanea.
2. The attempt by Tremere the Founder to usurp control of the entire Order, ending in the Sundering.
3. The Demonic Corruption of House Tytalus.
4. The Schism War, a civil war within the Order which led to the destruction of one House.


### The War against the Spider

Dav’nalleus, or Damhan-Allaidh in Gaelic, meaning Spider, was a powerful hedge magician opposed to the Order’s expansion into the British Isles (starting about 798). He constructed an alliance of powerful Anglo-Saxon rune wizards and monstrous shape-changers, the Gruagachan. 

Pralix of House Tytalus led the effort to destroy him from 803. Eventually she succeeded in 810, with the help of many hedge wizards from different traditions, including the Columbae and the Damhad-duidsan. This alliance went on to become the Ordo Miscellanea afterward, which for a time was a direct rival to the Order of Hermes. This tension was resolved in 817 when the Ordo Miscellanea became House Ex Miscellanea. 

*The general consensus is that Damhan-Allaidh was slain in the decisive battle of Pralix's war, but there are some who think he escaped. It is rumored that one day he or his descendants will return to take his vengeance. *


### The Sundering

Tremere was the last of the Founders remaining, and his ultimate goal was world domination. His plan was to finally dominate the Order by declaring himself Overlord of the Order at the Grand Tribunal of 850. However, his schemes had not yet come to full fruition when in 848, a mysterious group of magi opposing Tremere managed to shatter his power structure. While Tremere did negotiate peace terms with his Sundering enemies, with the intervention of the Primus of House Guernicus, his memories of the event were removed with his consent as part of the agreement.

*The exact details of the Sundering, including the magi responsible, are still not completely known.*


### The Corruption of House Tytalus

By mid-10th century AD, the practice of bargaining with, summoning, and attempting to control demons had become widespread in House Tytalus, including at the highest level. A member of the House informed on the Prima, and House Tytalus was purged after the Special Tribunal of 961.

*It is important to note that Tytalus magi have a very different perspective on these events. To House Tytalus, the informer was the real criminal.*

*A possible enabling factor of the Schism War of the early eleventh century?*


### The Schism War

Perhaps the single defining event in the history of the Order of Hermes since its foundation.  The Schism War in the early 11th century (C. 1003-1012) was a civil war within the Order. It had substantial ramifications for many of the Order's institutions and organizations, up to and including the adoption of Certamen for the resolution of disputes of a certain magnitude between members of the Order. Eventually, House Diedne was cast out of the Order and Houses Tremere and Flambeau led the effort to destroy it.

*The Schism War was terribly divisible and remains a haunting memory to this day. Many details of the Schism War are still mysterious, including the degree to which House Diedne was guilty of the crimes of which it was accused. *


# Abridged Timeline 

5201 BC: God creates the World.

3545 BC: The Flood.

C. 3200 BC: The Titans and Olympians clash in the Titanomachy.

3001 BC: The Tower of Babel fails.

C. 2800 BC: King Gilgamesh builds the walls of Uruk and pursues immortality.

C. 2700 BC: The great magus Imhotep counsels Pharaoh Zoser in Egypt.

2166 BC: Birth of Abraham the Patriarch.

2091 BC: Abraham enters the covenant with God and departs for Canaan.

2067 BC: Sulfur and fire rain down on Sodom and Gomorrah.

C. 2000 BC: Egyptian Cult of Thoth at its height.

1685 BC: Prometheus steals the secrets of Ignem from Olympus.

1592 BC: Moses, a child of prophecy, is born under persecution.

1550 BC: Moses has a direct revelation of the True Name of God.

C. 1500 BC: Hephaestus trains Gifted individuals on Lemnos.

1446 BC: Moses leads the Hebrews from Egypt and parts the Red Sea.

1445 BC: God descends upon Mount Sinai with the Ten Commandments

1406 BC: Jericho falls through miraculous means.

C. 1230 BC: Setne finds the Book of Thoth.

C. 1020 BC: Saul, first king of Israel, purges the Canaanite necromancers.

1004 BC: The Ark of the Covenant is installed in Jerusalem.

950–940 BC: King Solomon authors the Key of Solomon.

753 BC: Foundation of Rome by Romulus and Remus.

586 BC: Nebuchadnezzar sacks Jerusalem and destroys Solomon’s Temple.

C. 570–495 BC: Pythagoras teaches that numbers are the essence of all things and establishes harmonic ratios - resonant to Hermetic magic’s structure of numerical magnitudes, celestial alignments, and arcane correspondence.

c. 515–450 BC: Parmenides of Elea teaches that change is illusion and that true reality is singular, eternal, and unchanging. His cosmology directly challenges the senses and lays the groundwork for deep metaphysical thought.

C. 500 BC: Greek Cult of Hermes at its height.

495–435 BC: Empedocles teaches that all matter is composed of four primordial elements - fire, air, water, and earth. He asserts that Love (sympathy) and Strife (antipathy) govern the mingling and separation of these elements, anticipating key principles of Hermetic correspondences and magical resonance. 

C. 490–430 BC: Life of Zeno of Elea, who formulates a series of paradoxes of motion and plurality, intended to defend the teaching that reality is one and unchanging.

469–399 BC: Socrates pioneers the dialectic technique, urging relentless questioning and the pursuit of truth through introspection. He claims Knowledge is intrinsically Good, and Ignorance Evil, a view later echoed by Hermetic scholars.

C. 428–348 BC: Plato founds the Academy, and establishes the Realm of Forms that explain magical archetypes, Essential Traits, and the metaphysical truth behind magical correspondences.

C. 420 BC: The magician Asclepius, helps end a plague in Athens.

390 BC: Rome is sacked by Celts, aided by Druids. In response, Romans turn eastward for magical power and adopt Hermes (as Mercury), seeding the Cult of Mercury.

C. 384–322 BC: Aristotle, student of many disciplines, auctor of logic, founder of the Lyceum school, argues for the existence of a Fifth Element, finer than the four material elements, which make up the heavenly bodies and influence the mundane world.

C. 299–200 BC: Hermes Trismegistus completes multiple alchemical, astrological, philosophical and magical works - the "Hermetica" - establishing the base of Hermeticism.

287–212 BC: Archimedes of Syracuse, mathematician, engineer, and philosopher, astounds the ancient world with his mastery of geometry, hydraulics, and mechanical invention.

C. 200 BC: Plentarch, the Pontifex of the Pompeii Temple, collects 38 powerful rituals of the Cult of Mercury and carve them into 38 golden tablets.

63 BC: The Romans, under Pompey, with the help of powerful rituals, conquer Judea and enter Jerusalem.

1 Levant: Three magi, each a great astrologer and wise man, travel from the ends of the world to witness the birth of the infant Jesus.

C. 33: Jesus of Nazareth, who has become a wonder-worker, is executed by mundane rulers.

61 Stonehenge: Cult of Mercury and Romans destroy the druid sanctuary of Mona Insula to break the rebellion of Queen Boudicca.

64 Rhine: Establishment of a Mercurian temple at the later site of Durenmar in the Black Forest. The priests of Mercury attempt to usurp the primeval power of the Hercynian Forest that covers most of Germany.

395: Roman Empire divided into East and West.

410: The Visigoths under Alaric I sack Rome after a third siege.

412: Cult of Mercury is officially disbanded.

717: Bonisagus discovers Mercurian rituals in an abandoned well.

720: Bonisagus retreats to his cave in the Alps to develop the Parma Magica.

731: Trianoma and Viea meet Bonisagus in his seclusion in the Alps, working on his magnum opus, the Hermetic Theory of Magic.

747: Trianoma meets Mercere. He is the first magus to accept her invitation to form an Order.

754: Bonisagus leaves his alpine cave and travels to Durenmar in the Black Forest and establishes a permanent base at the site of a ruined Mercurian temple, teaching his theories to the wizards that Trianoma sends him.

767: The First Tribunal. The Twelve Founders gather at Durenmar and form the Order of Hermes. Bonisagus is the first to swear the Oath, followed by the Founders: Trianoma, Birna (Bjornaer), Criamon, Diedne, Flambeau, Jerbiton, Mercere, Merinita, Guernicus, Tremere, Tytalus, Verditius. The Code of Hermes and the rules for Wizard's War are established.

767–773: The magi of the nascent Order scatters outward from the central location of Durenmar. While Bonisagus, Merinita, Birna (Bjornaer) remains in or near the Rhine, Tytalus and his followers are found mainly in the northwest of Europe, Tremere removes himself to the far eastern reaches of the Frankish Empire - the Transylvanian Alps, Flambeau to the Pyrenees, Diedne to Gaul, Jerbiton and Criamon into the Alps; Mercere, Guernicus and Verditius to Italy and beyond. Through aggressive recruitment, the Order grows quickly.

771: Birna and her followers travel to the island of Rügen and hold a council. Thereafter House Bjornaer holds a gathering there every twelve years.

772: Founders Tremere and Bonisagus develop Certamen. Both are apparently pleased to have developed a non-fatal method of dueling.

773: Second Tribunal of the Order: The Rights of Founder Bonisagus ruled to extend to all members of his House, including the right to claim another magi's apprentice, and the requirement that magi of that House must share their discoveries with the Order, and that magi must share their knowledge with members of Bonisagus, unless they can convince a Tribunal that the magical knowledge must remain secret.

773: While working with Bonisagus in the lab, Founder Mercere loses his Gift.

778 Iberia: Founder Flambeau repeatedly clashes with Founder Guernicus and other Quaesitores over his interference in the wars between mortal Christians and Muslims. He is prevented from campaigning by a Quaesitorial visit, contributing to the final failure of a new thrust into Spain by King Charlemagne.

788: Death of Founder Flambeau and several of his filii at the Battle of the Tower in Iberia.

798: The first confrontation with the Spider, Damhain-Allaidh, in Brittany.

802: Quendalon retakes control of House Merinita and converts it to his vision. Irencillia becomes the new domus magna of the house, and it becomes dominated by faerie magic.

803–810: The Spider War: Pralix fights Damhan-allaidh (the Spider) in Britain and Caledonia.

807: Founder Tytalus, one of the most feared wizards of his time, enters Maddenhofen Woods to challenge the Queen of Faerie "for his heart’s desire".

811: Ordo Miscellanea formed by Pralix out of the remains of her army, and soon rivals the Order.

817: Fourth Grand Tribunal. Ordo Miscellanea is admitted to the Order as House Ex Miscellanea, and the Tribunals of Hibernia, Loch Leglean and Stonehenge are created as part of the merger. Redcaps are also brought into the Order. Primus Quendalon announces House Merinita's new dedication to Faerie. Certamen is accepted as decisive in all disputes. 

818: Mercere dies.

825: Death of Jerbiton.

831: Founder Birna speaks her final words to the magi of her House and disappears into the forest in bear-form.

832: Fifth Grand Tribunal. The last that Bonisagus and Trianoma attended. The Rules of Certamen are restricted to forbid the use of certamen to force a magus to violate the Code or give up his established rights. Apprentice Law ruling that a magus must provide his apprentice with three month’s training or risk losing that apprentice to another magus, if the apprentice agrees. Troubling reports of dangerous encounters with "The Order of Odin". Uncertain about the rune wizards’ strength, the Order uncharacteristically decides to seek peace. The Tribunal of Iberia is denied recognition as an independent Tribunal.

837: Death of Founder Verditius. This leaves Founder Tremere as the last living original Founder.

848: Sundering of House Tremere.

863: Prima Pralix of House Ex Miscellanea disappears while returning from a visit to her former domus magna Fudarus.

865: Sixth Grand Tribunal. The Tribunals are formally set as the Rhine, Frankish (Normandy), Lotharingian (Provençal), Britannian, Roman, Alpine (Greater Alps), Theban, Oriental (Eastern, or Levant) Tribunals. The Tribunal rules that no magi may serve mundanes - and that Certamen cannot be used to defy a Tribunal judgment, make a magus break the Code of Hermes or ignore a Code violation. 

898: Seventh Grand Tribunal. Quaesitors required to maintain accreditation in order to officiate at Tribunal. The Irish magi declare that they leave the Britannian Tribunal, which splits into the new Tribunals of Stonehenge, Hibernia and Loch Leglean.

931: Eighth Grand Tribunal. It is ratified that no Hermetic covenants may be founded in Africa for 300 years from 925, the year the Sahirs joined the Order.

961: Corruption of House Tytalus Special Tribunal: Prima Tasgillia Ex Tytalus and two other leaders of the House, and several members of Tytalus are tried, convicted, and executed for Diabolism. This marks a turning point in the Order’s relations with Hell and a strict anti-diabolic interpretation of the Code

964: Ninth Grand Tribunal. The Theban Tribunal is divided: the Tremere-controlled north forms the Transylvanian Tribunal, the south remains the Thebes Tribunal.

997: Tenth Grand Tribunal. The Tribunal rules that every sanctum shall be clearly marked.

1003–1012: The Schism War. House Diedne destroyed.

1030: Eleventh Grand Tribunal. Settles most of the remaining matters of the Schism War. The New Slavonic Tribunal is given formal authority over the settlement of new covenants in Slavic lands, despite protests from the Rhine Tribunal.

1063: Twelfth Grand Tribunal. Wishing to rein in the power of the Rhine Tribunal, the Grand Tribunal rejects its claim over the Polish territory left by the fall of the covenant of Grand Silesia. This land (along with all Slavic lands) is granted to the New Slavonic Tribunal. The Tribunal also decrees that "The Servants of Satan, who so often make themselves enemies of our Order of Hermes, may never again be friends of the Order."

1096–1099: The First Crusade.

1096: Thirteenth Grand Tribunal. The boundaries of Iberia are redrawn to limit the political power of House Flambeau there.

1122: Proclamation of the Concordat of Worms, settling the Investiture Contest between papacy and monarchy.

1129: Fourteenth Grand Tribunal. The re-founded Tribunal of the East is ratified by the Order, and named the Levant Tribunal. Border of Provencal and Normandy Tribunals adjusted to the "modern" boundaries.

1147–1149: The Second Crusade.

1162: Fifteenth Grand Tribunal. The Rhine Tribunal once again loses a boundary

dispute over Poland to the Novgorod Tribunal.

1189–1192: The Third Crusade.

1191: Avarret of Bonisagus is elevated to Primus of House Bonisagus, marking

a return to the conservative philosophy of reﬁning the existing Hermetic theory.

1195: Sixteenth Grand Tribunal. Survivors from the Levant blame counter-Crusading magi for the destruction of several covenants, saying that they had assisted the Muslim forces against them. However, no wrongdoing was found by Quaesitors and no definite evidence is presented.

1202–1204: The Fourth Crusade. The sacking of Constantinople leads to excommunication of the entire crusade.

1215: The Fourth Lateran Council is convened by Pope Innocentius III to establish three main objectives: crusading, church reform and combating heresy. 

1217: Start of the Fifth Crusade.

1219: The Pope forbids secular clergy from absenting themselves from their pastoral duties to study medicine or law.

# Chronology

## The Age of Creation and Exile 

*"In the beginning, the world was formed by Divine decree, and all things were in harmony. Yet, from the first days, some among the angels rebelled, some among the mortals defied, and some among the spirits refused to bow. Thus, the Realms of Power were sundered, and the great contest of wills began."*

– unknown scholar of House Criamon

*In the Before, the Eternal God, the One True Being of unchanging perfection, existing in perfect unity as the Holy Trinity, created the Celestial Host, angelic beings of pure spirit who were organized into the Nine Choirs. Among them, Lucifer, the Morning Star, was the most radiant, a Seraph of unmatched beauty, wisdom and might. Lucifer was flawless in all ways, but therein lay his doom. Pride took root in him - he desired not to serve but to rule. When God revealed His plan for humanity, that fleshly creatures, formed from the dust of the earth, would one day rise above even the angels - that through them, God would take on mortal form - Lucifer rebelled.*

*A third of the host followed him in defiance, and thus began the War in Heaven. The loyal angels, led by Michael the Archangel, clashed against the rebels in a battle that shook the pillars of creation. In the end, Lucifer was cast down, his brilliance twisted into ruin, his name forever cursed. His followers fell with him, stripped of their grace, becoming the first demons. Where they landed, Hell was formed - not by divine decree, but by their own corruption. The Infernal Realm, a prison of their own making, became their dominion, yet one from which they can never truly escape. Thus, before the world was shaped, the great division was set - Heaven above, Hell below, and the mortal realm yet to come, caught between them.*

*God forged the cosmos and brought forth Adam, establishing the first sacred Covenant with His creation. Yet, Adam and Eve were cast out of Eden, the flaming sword of the Cherubim sealing their paradise. Exiled to the harsh, barren lands of Nod, they struggled to survive by tilling unforgiving soil and building humble altars in remembrance of a lost perfection. As their descendants formed the first human lineages - marked by Cain’s cursed wanderings and the birth of Seth - the hidden realms of Magic and Faerie stirred in secret, echoing faint remnants of Eden in secluded, mystical places.*

*Meanwhile, the celestial order itself began to unravel. The Watchers, a host of 200 rebellious angels led by Shemihazah and Azazel, seduced by mortal beauty, descended upon Mount Hermon, and took human wives, uniting with mortals to birth the Nephilim. These giants, endowed with unnatural strength and forbidden knowledge, unleashed an age of violence and dark arts. The prophet Enoch arose to challenge their dominion, and the archangels descended to bring judgment. The Nephilim were decimated and doomed to become demons upon death, while the Watchers were condemned to chthonic prisons for 70 generations. Corruption still reached its zenith - and culminated in Methuselah’s passing, so God decreed the Flood to purge the earth of its iniquities. *

*Some Hermetic scholars theorize that this early war of Heavenly retribution and its precursors led up to the eventual Sundering of the Realms: the Divine began to withdraw from direct governance, setting the framework for miracles and faith; the Magic Realm, once in harmony with the Divine, started to emerge as an independent Power; the Faerie Realm flourished amid the burgeoning wealth of human myths and fears; and the Infernal, ever watchful, strengthened by the newly cast down, scheming in the shadows to corrupt early sorcerers and kings.*

5201 BC: God creates the World and Adam. The First Covenant between God and His creation is established. The world is perfect, and Eden stands as the pinnacle of Divine order, untainted by sin or death. \
*The date is a well-known and established fact by Saint Jerome of Constantinople, and we should abide by no other nonsense by Plato and other so-called "scholars" claiming ancient civilizations before this time.*

*The first man, Adam, is given dominion over all creatures, and his first companion is not Eve, but Lilith, formed from the same earth, created alongside Adam as his equal. She refuses to submit to him, and she speaks the Ineffable Name of God and flees the Garden, passing beyond the borders of Eden and entering the unknown.  \
Some say she vanished into the Magic Realm, where she took residence in now forgotten places of power, while others claim she fell into the clutches of dark spirits, becoming the mother of demons. The Talmudic and Akkadian traditions tell of her seduction of unwary men, her theft of infants, and her transformation into a winged night-stalker feared in all lands. Others say she became the first Faerie Queen. Whatever her fate, her story is the first warning of what happens to those who defy Divine order.*

C. 5100 BC: Adam and Eve are cast from the Garden, the gates sealed by the Cherubim with the flaming sword, severing perfect Divine communion. The exiles settle in the land of Nod, a barren and wild region and labor in hardship tilling the ground, yet the earth yields thorns and thistles. They build a simple altar of stone and offer sacrifices, remembering their lost Paradise. Satan and his angels continue to torment them, appearing as false visions to deceive them.

C. 5100–5000 BC: Adam and Eve’s descendants establish the first human lineages. God curses Cain for killing Abel, marking him and sending him to wander before founding the city of Enoch. Seth is born to replace Abel and his righteous line remain in the mountains and continue worshiping God. The Faerie and Magic Realms stir, forming reflections of Eden in hidden places.

*Some claim Cain was cursed not just with wandering, but with an unnatural resilience, creating the first being resistant to death.*

C. 4500 BC: Lusting after the comely daughters of men, The Grigori (the Watchers), a host of 200 angels led by Shemihazah and Azâzêl, descend to Mount Hermon and swear a mutual rebellious pact. They take mortal wives and sire the Nephilim, the children of fallen angels and mortals possess unnatural strength and knowledge of magic but are tyrannical and cannibalistic. They teach mankind strange arts, including sorcery, metallurgy, and astrology as well as cosmetics, jewelry, and seductive charms.

*Sêmîazâz, their leader, Arâkîba, Râmêêl, Kôkabîêl, Tâmîêl, Râmîêl, Dânêl, Êzêqêêl, Barâqîjâl, Asâêl, Armârôs, Batârêl, Anânêl, Zaqîêl, Samsâpêêl, Satarêl, Tûrêl, Jômjâêl, Sariêl. These are their chiefs of tens.*

*Shemihaza taught enchantments, root-cuttings and witchcraft while Azazel taught men to create bracelets and ornaments as well as forge swords, knives, shields, and breastplates - ushering in an age of violence and war.*

*The watchers essentially "spilled the beans" on divine knowledge, which humankind was not yet ready or authorized to wield. *

C. 4300 BC: Enoch rises as a prophet against the Watchers. He receives visions of Heaven and prophecies the coming judgment. Humans rebel against the tyrannical Nephilim fighting against them with limited success. The Nephilim led by Og and Sihon, feast on human flesh and then wage war against one another.

4271 BC: Adam dies at the age of 930 years. In his final centuries, he witnesses the full corruption of Cain’s line and the rise of the Nephilim. His sons and grandsons mourn for him, burying him in the Cave of Treasures. With Adam’s death, the last link to Eden is severed - his children must now navigate a world fully fallen.

4214 BC: The archangels (Michael, Uriel, Raphael, Gabriel) look down and see Earth drowning in savagery. They bring the matter before God: "The earth cries out; the souls of men are perishing!". The Watchers realize their error fathering the Nephelim with human women and plead with Enoch to intercede on their behalf before God. Enoch ascends to Heaven without dying. At God’s throne he receives stern judgements against the Watchers and returns with the bad news.

*Travelling the Divine regiones!*

C. 4213–4000 The archangels descend to battle the fallen. The Nephilim are routed and hunted - but also turn on each other. God decrees that their spirits will become demons after death. The Watchers are forced to witness the destruction of their children, then they are chained in chthonic prisons for 70 generations until the day of final judgement. The remaining humans are still corrupted by knowledge of war, sorcery, and seductive arts. \
*It’s been nearly five and a half-thousand years, those are some very long generations of over 75 years each by now…*

4058 BC: Creation of the World according to Maimonides.

*Obviously not as informed as us, as he incorrectly has the Flood in 2402 BC - so this can probably be ignored. Why he, as a Jew, doesn’t even follow the Jewish calendar escapes me.*

C. 4000 BC: Fall of the civilization of the Old Ones, per Archmage Infamitus. *Some magi equate this culture with Atlantis. Plato claims Atlantis existed much earlier and sank ~9500 BC, but we know this is impossible since that would be before the creation of the world. Infamitus and Plato may have their dates wrong.*

3871 BC: Unhappy with the continuous sin and violence among the mortals, God warns of more coming judgment.

3760 BC: Creation of the World according to the Jewish calendar. 

*Obviously an incorrect assumption by their incompetent scholars.*

3545 BC: Enoch’s son Methuselah, the last man to walk in the ways of the Lord beside his grandson Noah, dies at 969 years of age having lived the longest life. God can now send the Flood and it wipes out all corrupted lineages. Noah survives on his Ark and strikes land on mount Ararat.

*Before the flood, Noah had instructed his granddaughter Cessair to find a land uncontaminated by vice. She led the first group that landed in Hibernia [Ireland], and hoped that island would provide a safe haven from the Great Flood, but they were cursed by God for following the warnings to sail west from a stone pagan idol and her people died when the tide rose above Hibernia’s mountains. The only survivor was her husband, the druid Fionntan (Fintan), who survived by changing into a salmon. He could also assume the shapes of a stag, eagle, or boar, and is rumored to still be alive. \
Others say he was cursed by God to remain alive to tell the tale. \
No man was left alive but Noah, but if he was a fish then that changes things.*


## A New Dawn and The Age of the First Magi (3500–1685 BC)

*The Flood had passed, washing away the old world in divine judgment. The land, once ruled by giants, corrupted kings, and the whispers of the fallen Watchers, now lay silent beneath an unbroken sky. Yet the past was not so easily erased.*

*Noah’s descendants scattered, carving new nations from the mud - Sumer, Akkad, Egypt, and Elam - raising stones against the sky in defiance of time and fate. Misr, son of Ham, became Egypt’s first king, and his sons built cities that still bear their names.*

*But beneath the fields and altars of this new world, the remnants of the old stir. Buried ruins hide pre-Flood wonders - antediluvian mysteries - and the spirits of forgotten ages whisper to those who dare listen. In the distant reaches, are the children of Cain and the last of the ancient bloodlines walking unseen, their knowledge of lost magics undiminished? As the first cities rose along fertile riverbanks, mortal ambition merged with lingering fragments of ancient lore, fueling the earliest glimmers of organized magic.*

*The Tower of Babel collapses in ruin. Whether it was an affront to the Divine, a flawed magical experiment, or a forgotten bargain broken, the result is the same - language itself fractures, and humanity is scattered. The tongues of men are no longer one, and the unity of early sorcerers is lost to confusion and exile.*

*Some Bonisagus theorists claim the Tower was not merely an ambitious work of masonry but a vast Hermetic precursor, an attempt to stabilize the boundaries between Realms or even elevate all of humanity into magical enlightenment. House Criamon interprets the event as a Turning Point in the Enigma, a moment when mortal hubris interrupted the natural flow of destiny. Meanwhile, Mercere scholars whisper that the loss of a universal language was not an act of Divine punishment but rather an act of sabotage, ensuring that magic would remain fragmented and controlled by hidden forces possibly the remnants of a lost faerie power or the enigmatic Titans.*

*In Sumer, priest-kings consulted omens in star-strewn skies, forging the bedrock of astrological practices that would echo down the centuries. Pharaohs in Egypt built monuments that soared toward Heaven and boasted of hidden powers sealed within desert sands - Imhotep’s near-divine mastery of medicine and architecture a legend that still inspires House Bonisagus to this day. Meanwhile, mighty Gilgamesh led Uruk, rumored to possess a spark of divine blood, ever hunting the key to immortality.*

*Though the spark of Hermetic unity was centuries from fruition, this age ignited the first true flame of mage-craft. Word by word and rite by rite, mankind reclaimed forgotten wisdom. The new nations might have labored under the mortal yoke of agriculture and kingship, but their magi-in-the-making pulled threads of ancient power from every corner of the reborn world - laying the foundation for all Hermetic ages to come.*

C. 3500–3000 BC: Noah’s descendants repopulate the earth.

*Scholars have been calculating an extraordinary growth pattern here and that women must have borne 6-10 children on average, and under quite favorable conditions for a growth rate of 2-3% annually. We’ve never seen evidence of more than just below 1% over time in known history afterwards, but of course very little reliable research is available. Perhaps Noah’s immediate descendants retained at least some of God’s grace for longer and healthier lives than we have now in the 13th century.*

C. 3200 BC: The Titans and the faerie gods of Mount Olympus clash in a ten year cosmic battle; the Titanomachy. The titans lose and are imprisoned in Tartarus, their powers tempered by the magic chains that bind them.

*The turning point for the ~~gods~~ faeries, which won them the war, was obtaining three fabulous weapons from the Kyklopes: Hades stole the titans’ weapons using the helmet of invisibility, Poseidon distracted them with his trident, and Zeus struck them down using the thunderbolt. *

3001 BC: The Tower of Babel, a colossal ziggurat built to bridge the divide between Earth and the heavens, fails.

*"Yahweh had not asked men to build the tower or to pierce the vault; the decision to build it belonged to men alone, and they would die in this endeavor just as they did in any of their earthbound tasks. Yet now that he stood at the base of the tower, his senses rebelled, insisting that nothing should stand so high."*

*An arcane mega-project by the ancients to reach the celestial spheres? *

*It is unclear if they managed to breach even the lunar sphere or not.*

*Legends persist that remnants of Babel’s foundation still lie hidden, their stones infused with vast reservoirs of vis. Some say that a regio still echoes with the voices of those lost tongues, and that those who find the heart of Babel may recover its secrets - or unleash its doom once more.*

C. 2800 BC: The mighty King Gilgamesh of Uruk, two-thirds divine and one-third mortal, reigns in Sumer. He battles with gods and monsters, builds the walls of Uruk and pursues immortality. \
*Hermetic scholars argue over whether Gilgamesh was a practitioner of some ancient magic, perhaps an early form of Theurgy or a lost magical tradition now extinct. Some magi speculate that the hero's journey to seek eternal life was inspired by an encounter with ancient magical beings, possibly remnants of a pre-Hermetic magical tradition. Was his close companion Enkidu, a wild man shaped by the gods, a faerie-being or a magical creature brought forth from the Primordial Realm?*

*Some Hermetic historians believe that the tale of the herb of immortality, stolen by a serpent, could be linked to the Divine Realm’s restrictions on mortal access to eternal life.*

*Though most mundane scholars dismiss Gilgamesh as mere legend, a few magi whisper that his spirit or legacy still lingers in the world, waiting to be found in the deep ruins of the East. But if the tales are true, then Gilgamesh’s desperate pursuit of immortality should serve as a lesson to us all. Yet, if he had succeeded, what knowledge might we have lost? What if he still walks the earth, hidden by time and the whims of the gods?*

C. 2700 BC: In Egypt, the great magus Imhotep counsels Pharaoh Zoser, also called Tosorthros, as a vizier, high priest, healer, and architect. Imhotep raises the Step Pyramid at Saqqara, a feat of engineering and mystical significance that elevates the power of the pharaoh, discovers powerful healing rituals, and performs many wonders. His father was the god Ptah and his mother a mortal woman.

*His mastery of medicine and healing rituals was so profound that later generations deify him as a god of healing, akin to Asclepius of the Greeks. Some magi speculate that his arts were a fusion of what would later become Hermetic Magic, blending Rego, Creo, and Corpus in ways that predate Bonisagus by millennia.*

*Some texts suggest that Imhotep founded the Cult of Thoth, the god of wisdom and arcane knowledge, who would later be equated with Hermes Trismegistus. If true, then Imhotep may be the progenitor of Hermetic thought, a bridge between the divine revelations of Egypt and the later teachings of Alexandria.*

*Some Hermetic scholars argue that the pyramids are not just tombs, but vast magical engines - designed not merely for royal burials, but to channel mystical energies, creating a resonance between the heavens and the earth. Some even claim that deep within the hidden chambers of the oldest pyramids, ancient spells of longevity or resurrection still pulse with forgotten power. What secrets did he bury beneath the sands of Saqqara? What lost wisdom still lies waiting in the desert?*

C: 2400 BC: Hayk of Armenia defeats tyrant god-king Bel Marduk of Babylon by shooting him with "an arrow of his own bow".

*Taking advantage of an Arcane connection?*

C. 2300 BC: The Greek prince Partholón and his sons brought art, crafts, and cattle to Hibernia. They cleared four large pastures from the pristine wood and witnessed the birth of four lakes. They also encountered the Fomórach, monstrous Magic giants hailing from unknown, northern islands. Settling in the north, the Fomórach continually raided the Partholónians. Eventually the Partholónians killed the Fomórach in a great battle, but to their own undoing. They left the corpses on the field, and a foul plague emanated from the bodies and killed all the Partholónians.

C. 2250 BC: In the great city of Ur in Sumer; Enheduanna, benevolent high priestess of Inanna (Sumerian goddess of love and war) and daughter of Sargon the Great, brings great prosperity to Sumer over many decades, and have the power to "turn a man into a woman and a woman into a man".

*Some mystagogues believe Enheduanna to have been a proto-theurgist or faerie regent, whose rites invoked spirits beyond the celestial dome. Her famous transformation hymn may reflect contact with boundary-crossing entities from the Magic Realm, or even aspects of the Changing Court of Faerie, now long vanished beneath the sands of Sumer.*

2166 BC: Abram, later known as Abraham the Patriarch, was born in Ur of the Chaldeans to Terah, who was approximately 130 years old at the time. \
*Abraham’s line is of particular interest to Hermetic scholars concerned with the origins of Divine Magic. The early patriarchs - Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob - are each associated with signs, dreams, and invocations that border on the supernatural, yet remain squarely within the authority of the Dominion. Some magi argue that these signs represent an alternate model of miraculous power - one that seems wholly inaccessible to those with The Gift, and yet potent enough to command fire from Heaven.*

2091 BC: At age 75, Abram obeys God’s call and departs from Haran to journey to Canaan. God promises to make him a great nation, bless all peoples through him, and give the land of Canaan to his descendants. This marks the beginning of the covenant between God and Abram - later formalized through ritual and sealed with circumcision - initiating the Divine claim on a territory previously ruled by ancient Magical cults and faerie lords.

*A Divine binding pact that reshaped entire auras and severed mortal lands from older Magical and Faerie ties. Some speak of it as a Turning of the Age, the moment when the Divine Realm established preeminence over a territory that had long been under the influence of chthonic powers and elemental cults.*

*God promised that his descendants would be as numerous as the stars.*

2081 BC: Abram, now dwelling in Canaan for a decade, voices his doubts to God: he remains childless, and his heir is but a servant. In response, God leads him beneath the night sky and promises descendants as countless as the stars. Abram believes, and God credits it to him as righteousness. That night, God formalizes His covenant: Abram sacrifices animals and arranges the carcasses in a ritual path. As the sun sets, a deep darkness falls, and a dreadful vision overtakes him. A smoking firepot and a flaming torch - manifestations of the Divine - pass between the pieces. God declares that Abram's descendants will be strangers in a land not their own, oppressed for four hundred years, but they will emerge with great possessions. The land, from the river of Egypt to the Euphrates, is promised to his offspring.

The night seals a divine promise in blood and fire, binding Heaven and Earth with words and flame - the covenant has taken shape in ritual and prophecy.

2067 BC: At age 99, Abram becomes Abraham as God renames him. God renews His covenant, promises a son through Sarah, and commands circumcision as the sign of the covenant. Abraham circumcises himself and all the males of his household that very day.

2067 BC: Sulfur and fire rain down on Sodom and Gomorrah, destroying the cities and the entire plain. Fleeing the annihilation, Lot and his family are warned not to look back. Yet his wife, overcome by grief or awe, turns her gaze toward the flaming ruin - and is immediately transformed into a pillar of salt. This act, recorded in sacred texts, is viewed by Hermetic scholars as one of the earliest known Divine anchorings of a mortal soul.

*One of the most infamous acts of Divine wrath recounted in sacred tradition. It is said by some magi of the Levant that no vis may be gathered near the site, and that rituals performed in its shadow draw only phantoms and echoes.*

*Criamon initiates believe the valley was once a Magic regio sacred to ancient elemental spirits, possibly destroyed not only by Divine wrath, but as a violent displacement of local supernatural order. Perhaps she did not die, but was frozen mid-transformation - as in Twilight - her spirit caught between Realms at the moment of ultimate judgment. Some suggest that the "pillar of salt" may be a Twilight-frozen Arcane Connection - an anchor of trauma echoing the final moments of annihilation, and that those who meditate beside it experience visions of burning skies and Divine silence.*

2066 BC: Birth of Isaac, the miracle child born to Sarah and Abraham in old age, as promised by God. He represents the anchoring of the covenant into the material world.

*And possibly the divine "fixing" of Arcane Connection to a bloodline, an open secret used by House Merinita. \
Abraham was 100 years old, his wife Sarah, aged 90.*

2054 BC: The Binding of Isaac. God tests Abraham's faith by instructing him to offer Isaac as a sacrifice on Mount Moriah. At the last moment, God provides a ram as a substitute, reaffirming His covenant with Abraham. ​

C. 2045 BC: Cast out with his mother Hagar at Sarah’s demand, Ishmael, the firstborn son of Abraham, is sent into the desert. Nearly perishing of thirst, the pair are saved when God opens Hagar’s eyes to a hidden well - a sign of continued Divine favor despite the rejection.

*In Hermetic circles, the line of Ishmael is of great esoteric interest. While the Hebraic covenant follows Isaac, some mystics of the East, including the sahir of the Cradle and the Crescent, trace their magical ancestry through Ishmael. His descendants are said to have preserved ancient Chaldean astrological rites and desert-born spirits of wind and star, passed down orally in secret lineages.*

*Some Merinita theorists argue that Ishmael’s exile mirrors a faerie origin tale - the cast-out scion who gains sovereignty over wild lands and strange spirits. Others believe his destiny was to form a countercurrent of Magical practice, one which diverged from the Divine path yet retained echoes of covenantal potency.*

2026 BC: Isaac marries Rebekah, a relative from Mesopotamia. Some suggest this alliance reconnects his bloodline to older spiritual currents of the east. *Rebekah is sometimes seen in Merinita interpretations as a liminal figure - a faerie bride accepting the weight of a Divine destiny.*

2006 BC: Rebekah birthes Jacob and Esau. The twins are born in tension - Jacob clutching Esau’s heel. From birth, they represent divergent destinies: Esau aligned with the wilderness and temporal might, Jacob with covenantal inheritance and spiritual subtlety.

C. 2000 BC: Egyptian Cult of Thoth at its height, as indicated by the research of Primus Fenicil of Guernicus in the late 9th century.

1978 BC: Famished from the fields, Esau sells his birthright - the double portion inheritance of the firstborn - to Jacob for a bowl of lentil stew, demonstrating a disregard for his inheritance.

*Kabbalists interpret this moment as a rejection of Divine destiny in favor of temporal desire. Hermeticists also cite it as an early example of Essential Nature distortion, wherein the soul’s higher path is forfeited for ephemeral gain.*

1929 BC: Now elderly and blind, Isaac intends to bless Esau - the firstborn. But Rebekah, favoring Jacob, orchestrates a deception: Jacob dons goatskin and brings venison as Esau would, receiving the patriarchal blessing instead. This act of substitution cannot be undone; blessings are understood as Divine Speech-Acts, irrevocable once pronounced. Esau’s wrath is swift; he vows to kill Jacob once their father dies.

*From a Hermetic perspective, this blessing is a binding supernatural ritual, not just a benediction. Some Bonisagus theorists posit that the words spoken became an Arcane Connection to the Divine plan, binding Jacob to it forever.*

1928 BC: Fleeing the wrath of his brother Esau, Jacob lies down in the wilderness and dreams of a stairway reaching from Earth to Heaven, with angels ascending and descending. He awakens with awe, declaring the place to be the gate of Heaven, reaffirming the covenant promises. Later he arrives in Haran, where he encounters Rachel, daughter of Laban, and falls in love with her. He agrees to serve Laban for seven years as bride-price for her hand.

*This moment is widely accepted among Kabbalists and Hermetic mystagogues as a Tiered Regio Revelation. The vision did not merely take place in Jacob's mind but occurred in a locus where the celestial and terrestrial realms overlapped, opening a regio boundary into the Celestial Spheres. The ascending and descending angels may represent choirs or invocations - or possibly paths of initiatory descent and return, mirroring the journey toward transcendence.*

*The ladder is not built of rungs, but of names and mysteries. It is the tree reversed and reflected in time.*

1921 BC: After serving Laban for seven years, Jacob is deceived on the wedding night. Laban instead gives Jacob Leah, Rachel’s elder sister, veiled and concealed. By the time Jacob discovers the deception, the marriage had been consummated, and the union sealed under the customs of the land.  \
When Jacob confronts Laban, the latter justifies the act by appealing to local custom that: "It is not our custom to give the younger before the firstborn." Laban then offers Rachel as well - but only on the condition that Jacob serve him for another seven years. In an act of love, Jacob agrees.

*A symmetrical act of trickery against the trickster…*

*Some Hermetic Kabbalists argue that Leah and Rachel represent two spiritual paths. Leah, associated with vision and hiddenness, is linked to Binah (Understanding) - the higher, veiled spiritual reality. Rachel, the beloved, represents Malchut (Kingdom) - the tangible, manifest world.*

1916 BC: Joseph is born to Jacob and his favored wife Rachel during Jacob's extended stay in Haran. This event adds complexity to the family dynamics, as Joseph's brothers become envious of him.

1914 BC: Now 14 years in Haran, Jacob’s second term of service ends and he begins building his flocks. He stays 6 more years, innovating breeding methods - possibly through proto-Magic or Animal lore.
