1908 BC: After serving Laban for twenty years - fourteen for his wives and six for his flocks - Jacob secretly departs Haran to return to Canaan with his household. Before leaving, Rachel steals her father Laban’s teraphim - household idols for fortune and spiritual protection. The idols are hidden beneath Rachel’s saddle. Their spiritual resonance is lost - perhaps fractured and released.

*A regio in Haran reportedly flickers into nonexistence not long after.*

*The teraphim are spirit-vessels, likely tied to Faerie or Magic regio guardianship. Their theft likely disrupted a covenant or pact Laban had with local spirits, explaining both his furious pursuit and the subtle decline of his fortunes thereafter.*

*Some suggest that Rachel transferred the protective bond from her father’s household to Jacob’s in a ritual of transference that granted subtle magical inheritance to her descendants. Others speculate this theft introduced spiritual instability into Jacob’s line, opening them to later misfortunes and strange destinies.*

1906 BC: Jacob prepares to reunite with his estranged brother, Esau. Fearing their meeting, Jacob sends his family and possessions across the Jabbok River, remaining alone on the opposite bank. During the night, a mysterious being - possibly a faerie, angel, or the Divine itself - engages Jacob in a prolonged wrestling match until daybreak. Despite sustaining a hip injury during the struggle, Jacob persists, refusing to release the being until he receives a blessing. In response, the figure bestows upon him the name "Israel," meaning "he struggles with God," in honor of Jacob's perseverance and transformation. His transformation marks him as a liminal figure between the mundane and supernatural. Subsequently, he reconciles with Esau, ending years of estrangement.

*Heretic Merinitans have whispered that Jacob’s opponent was not an angel, but a faerie prince of thresholds, a guardian of passage who marked Jacob as "Israel" to bind him into the grand tale of the Chosen.*

1898 BC: At the age of 17, Joseph, the favored son of Jacob and Rachel, is sent to check on his brothers who were tending their father's flocks near Shechem. Upon finding them in Dothan, Joseph's brothers, fueled by jealousy over his favored status and his prophetic dreams suggesting he would one day rule over them, conspire against him. Initially plotting to kill him, they ultimately decide to sell him to a caravan of Ishmaelite traders for twenty shekels of silver. These traders take Joseph to Egypt, where he is sold to Potiphar, an officer of Pharaoh and captain of the guard. 

1886 BC: After interpreting Pharaoh's dreams predicting seven years of abundance followed by seven years of famine, Joseph rises to power as Pharaoh’s right-hand man and helps Egypt through the severe famine. \
*Local folklore holds he was a skilled magician, rumored to possibly be even Imhotep under another name. It might also explain his, or his grandfather Abraham’s longevity if not explained by them being direct descendants of Noah.*

*Would it be heresy to suggest he had something to do with that famine in order to rise to power? What if Joseph’s dreams, accurate though they were, came from a tainted source - a spirit of desolation masquerading as an angel of truth. Regardless, the engineered scarcity allowed for the centralization of power - and the subjugation of surrounding peoples.*

1875 BC: Due to the severe famine, Jacob, at the age of 130, migrate to Egypt with his family, totaling seventy individuals. They settle in the land of Goshen, reuniting with Joseph after many years, leading to generations of intermingling with Egyptian magical traditions and possible exposure to spiritual corruption.

*Or enhancement.*

1859 BC: Jacob dies in Egypt at the age of 147. Before his death, he blesses his twelve sons and requests to be buried in the Cave of Machpelah in Canaan.

1806 BC: Death of Joseph. With his death, political protection wanes. Oral traditions suggest that Pharaohs begin to fear the Hebrews' power, leading to increasing control - possibly influenced by court magicians hostile to the Divine. Before his death, he assures his brothers that God will lead them back to the Promised Land and request that his bones be carried out of Egypt when the time comes.

*He is later embalmed and placed in a coffin in Egypt.*

C. 1800 BC: Arriving from Scythia, Nemed and his tribe were the third group arriving to Hibernia - having fled his homeland to escape punishment for killing his parents. Setting out with a great fleet, his tribe saw an incredibly tall, golden tower rising from the western ocean. Built on an island, this fantastic tower was only visible when the tide ebbed. After landing on the shore, the incoming tide surprised Nemed and he lost most of his fleet. It was a year before the tribe saw land again, accidentally finding Hibernia. Landing with soldiers, families, and druids, the Nemedians promptly settled, bringing lore and craft to the untamed wilderness. They cleared several plains and watched several lakes erupt from the ground. Their prosperity was soon noticed and coveted by the Fomórach. Rushing from the northern oceans, the Fomórach captured Nemed’s people and

taxed them with a heavy tribute. By this time, Nemed had died, leaving his sons and grandsons to suffer the weight of Fomórach oppression. Plotting rebellion, the Nemedians sent to Greece for aid. The Greek king responded with a host of soldiers, druids, druidesses, wolves, and venomous animals. Nemed’s sons led an assault on the Fomórach main fortress on Tory Island and successful destroyed it. Using water magic, the Fomórach druids raised a huge wall of water to drown the Nemedians, but also drowned many Fomórach. Both sides incurred heavy losses, but it was the Fomórach who won the day. The Nemedians were devastated. Three surviving grandsons of Nemed split the survivors into three groups and departed. Hibernia was again uninhabited by humans.


## The Age of Hermetic Predecessors (1685–250 BC)

*This era marks the twilight of myth and the dawn of structured magical thought, as divine wonders, arcane secrets, and philosophical inquiry intertwine. The ancient world is shaped by prophets, sorcerers, and scholars, whose legacies whisper through the corridors of time and echo in the foundations of Hermetic magic.*

*The Egyptian priesthood and Persian magi wield vast mystical influence, blending celestial wisdom with sacred rites. The Hebrew prophet Moses, trained in the magic of Pharaoh’s court, performs wonders that challenge Egypt’s own priestly sorcerers, while in the East, the Zoroastrian magi of Persia refine the art of astrology and cosmic divination.*

*Meanwhile, Greek mystery cults - the Orphics, Pythagoreans, and Eleusinians - seek enlightenment through ritual, purification, and secret knowledge. Their insights shape the philosophical frameworks of Plato and Aristotle, whose theories on the nature of reality, form, and essence will later become essential to Hermetic understanding.*

*The melting pot of Alexandria becomes the crucible of magical synthesis, where the wisdom of Hermes Trismegistus, Thoth, and the Hellenistic Mysteries converge. Scholars and magi exchange knowledge, laying the groundwork for the Cult of Hermes, a philosophical and magical lineage that will one day inspire the Order of Hermes itself!*

*Though the Age of Hermetic Predecessors ends with the rise of Rome, its legacy endures - preserved in hidden texts, lost temples, and the secret traditions that survive to shape the future of magic.*

1685 BC: Prometheus defies Zeus and steals the secrets of Ignem magics from the gods of Olympus.

1592 BC: Moses. Moses, a child of prophecy, is born under persecution - hidden among reeds. Later initiates into the Ars Theologica consider his early life as preparation for Divine theurgy. \
*The birth of Moses, prophet and lawgiver of the Hebrew people, is held by some Hermetic scholars to be a moment of profound resonance across both the Divine and Magic Realms. \
His name has been whispered in esoteric circles in connection with lost Egyptian theurgic rites, secret Names of Power, and the obscure proto-tradition of Hebraic Magic - possibly an antecedent to the more systematized Kabbalistic methods. Criamon even identify him with the archetype of the Wanderer, a soul eternally exiled from the Garden yet bearing the Law back to mankind.*

1559 BC: Kekrops, the first King of Athens. He teaches the Athenians the first elements of civilized life such as marriage, reading and writing, and ceremonial burial. He defies Zeus and forbids sacrifice of any living creature to the gods.

*It is said that Athena became the patron goddess of the city in a racing competition with Poseidon.*

1550 BC: Theophany at the Burning Bush: Moses encounters the Divine in a form of fire that does not consume, and has a direct revelation of the True Name of God (YHWH). \
*No mortal man may gaze upon God and live, save that man be unmade and remade. As Moses was. \
*

C. 1500 BC (allegedly): Hephaestus builds a forge on Lemnos and gathers and trains Gifted individuals to assist him. The Gifted wizards remain after the god’s departure, living apart to mitigate the innate hostility and suspicion they felt for each other, but continuing to work at the forge. Though never prosperous, the wizards of the forge endured, training apprentices and continuing their tradition of magical enchantments.

*This eventually becomes the Verditius covenant Ingasia. With the introduction of the Order of Hermes and Parma Magica, the wizards could truly work together, and membership blossomed from two to three to six and more. Legally, Ingasia was founded after Verdi; but historically, Ingasia is an ancient forge created by Hephaestus himself. This has given Ingasia’s members the idea that the covenant is older and more venerable than Verdi.*

1460–1100 BC: An Age of Heroes in the Mediterranean and Levant region. The great heroes Hercules, Ulysses, Theseus, Achilles, Aeneas, Jason, and Perseus roam the world, performing great deeds and slaying monsters. This is also an age of magic: the witch Medea, the enchanter Daedelus, and the sorceress Circe all belong to this period.

1447 BC: A series of escalating supernatural devastations afflict Egypt.

*There has been debate whether these are acts of pure Divine will or if Moses utilized a form of Divine Magic unknown to the Order.*

*~~Or something else?~~ Heresy!*

1446 BC: Exodus: Moses leads the Hebrews from Egypt after the death of the firstborn and the parting of the Red Sea. \
*Some have interpreted this as an ultimate confrontation between Divine power and a decadent magical empire built on ancient, possibly Infernal pacts.*

1446–1406 BC: 40 Years in the Wilderness: A period of purification and transformation. The Hebrews are reshaped into a holy people, the Law is received, and supernatural sustenance (manna) manifests.

*Some Criamon view this as a passage through mythic time, rather than mundane geography.*

1445 BC: God descends upon Mount Sinai in fire, and the Ten Commandments are given. A region of overpowering Dominion, this site remains inaccessible to Hermetic magi to this day, warded by Divine potency.

*What an experience to witness a moment of perfect Divine manifestation within the world, a Dominion aura of incalculable strength, likely exceeding even the highest ratings of known holy sites throughout Europe. Did it briefly become a terrestrial Celestial Regio in its own right?*

*Some fringe members of House Verditius speculate that the original tablets were not merely symbolic, but vessels of immense Divine vis - so infused with celestial essence that they would render any who touched them dead or sanctified beyond recall. The true tablets are, of course, long lost… or perhaps merely hidden from unworthy eyes?*

C. 1440 BC: The Tabernacle, A portable holy sanctuary is built according to Divine specifications.

*Hermetic theurgists speculate it was a mobile regio anchor, designed to house and filter Divine auras.*

1406 BC: Denied entry into the Promised Land, Moses dies on Mount Nebo.  \
*His burial site is hidden; some magi believe it became a small Celestial Regio, now lost in the wilderness.*

1406 BC: Joshua leads the conquest of Canaan: The Hebrews cross the Jordan and Jericho falls through miraculous means. The conquest marks the expulsion of lingering Magical/Faerie cults that once ruled Canaan, often through Divine annihilation. \
*While the Order of Hermes generally avoids deep inquiry into the Divine Realm, a few bold magi - particularly from House Criamon and the more theurgically inclined members of House Bonisagus - have speculated that the conquest of Canaan marks a profound boundary event: the assertion of Divine order over lands once saturated in potent and ancient Magic and Faerie influences. The destruction of Jericho and Ai, both sites with pre-Abrahamic cults and prehistoric regiones, may have represented more than mundane conquest - it was the literal banishment of older powers.*

C. 1400 BC: The fourth invaders to Hibernia were the Fir Bolg. Legends claim they are the descendents of one of Nemed’s grandsons, who served as slaves in Greece before escaping and returning there. After legendary battles, including confrontations with dead warriors animated by demonic spirits, the Fir Bolg leaders eventually became Magic creatures. The Fir Bolg divided Hibernia into provinces, established kingship and justice, and brought peace.

*Fir Bolg means "the men who swell with battle fury," although some monks incorrectly think it means "Men of the Bag" because their masters made them move earth using leather bags.*

1320 BC: The theft of Europa. On a spring morning in the lands of Phoenicia, the king’s daughter, Europa, wanders along the shore when a white bull of extraordinary beauty approach. Enchanted by its gentleness, she climbs upon its back - only for the creature to charge into the sea and carry her across to Crete. There, the bull reveals itself as Zeus, and from their union descends the line of Minos. \
*The theft of Europa signifies a trespass of the Magic (or Faerie) Realm into mortal sovereignty, and the birth of new lineages touched by magic. The echoes of her cries still ripple through the Magic auras of Crete.*

C. 1300 BC: The ancient Cymric civilization flourishes around Albion (Britannia), guided by magician-priests; they were a peaceful, nature-worshiping people. In the tradition of their ancestors, the Cymry captured the magical essence of nature in their engravings - which could control many natural phenomena.

*In time, the Cymric culture is destroyed by invading Gaels and Brutus, and the Cymric and Gaelic cultures merge into the Celtic people. The heritage of the Cymric priest-magicians lives on in the druids of later years.*

C. 1247 BC: Perseus slays Medusa the mortal Gorgon. The other two Gorgons pursue Perseus, but, wearing his helm of darkness, he escapes. He then visits King Atlas who had previously refused him hospitality; and in revenge Perseus turns him to stone by showing him the severed Gorgon head.

*Somewhat uncertain date. Another source has this at 1347 BC.*

C. 1240 BC: Queen Cassiopeia in the kingdom of Aethiopia claims she and her daughter Andromeda are more beautiful than all of Poseidon’s nereids. The angered god responds by unleashing the dragon Ceto (or Cetus), and directing it to devastate the country’s coasts and ports. The oracle of Ammon announces that no relief would be found until the king exposes his daughter Andromeda to the monster, and so she is fastened naked to a rock on the shore. Perseus slays the monster and, setting her free, claims Andromeda in marriage.

*Even after Perseus defeated it, tales of the Leviathan abound and remain to this day. It is said that Ceto still hunts the waters of the Red Sea and the Eastern Mediterranean, flying between the two as whimsy strikes it, harassing local traffic to sailors’ fear and superstition. *

C. 1230 BC: Theseus slays the Minotaur in Crete. \
*A clear example of Faerie narrative collapse in the region - the substitution of heroic agency for propitiatory sacrifice. The Aegean underwent a slow unraveling of older rites after this date, making way for Classical worship and, later, the erasure of Magic auras by the rise of the Dominion. \
The ruins of Knossos may lie silent, but some magi have claimed that on certain nights, when the moon is full and the air heavy with jasmine, a new Labyrinth forms in shadow, and the Minotaur waits once more - neither alive nor dead, but half-real and wholly hungry.*

C. 1230 BC: Setne, aka Prince Khaemwaset, fourth son of Ramses II finds the Book of Thoth in the tomb of Prince Naneferkaptah. Despite the wishes of Naneferkaptah’s ghost, Khaemwaset takes the book, along with a curse. Setne meets a beautiful woman who seduces him into killing his children and humiliating himself in front of the pharaoh. When he discovers that this episode was a mirage created by Naneferkaptah, Setne returns the book to Naneferkaptah’s tomb. To further appease the dead Prince, Setne also finds the bodies of Naneferkaptah’s wife and son and has them interred, and the tomb resealed.

*Setne and his wife Mehusekhe have been praying for a son. They have one and name him Se-Osiris after being instructed to do so in a dream. Se-Osiris matures rapidly and amazes his tutors with his magical abilities.*

C. 1220 BC: An ancient Nubian wizard comes to Pharaoh Ramses II’s court to challenge and embarrass the Egyptian wizards. The Pharaoh and his courtiers are at a complete loss. Ramses calls his son, the wise Setne, but he too is puzzled and asks for a delay of ten days, but his son, the Pharaoh’s grandson, 12-year old Se-Osiris opposes the Nubian. He uses scrying to read the sealed scroll of the challenge without breaking it. The evil wizard then summons a forceful snake, but Se-Osiris turns it into a worm. Then he asks a dark fog to suffocate Ramses, but Se-Osiris dispels it with light. Eventually, foaming with rage, the Nubian creates a massive wall of fire and throws it at him, but Se-Osiris turns it back toward the Nubian and it destroys him. 

1194–1184 BC: The Trojan War. The city of Troy falls, but its ruins remain tainted by the unfulfilled prophecy of Cassandra, who foresaw the destruction yet could convince no one. \
*To this day, magi passing through the region in Anatolia report hearing whispers of lost truths. Some claim the remnants of her Sight persist as a regio, accessible only to those who accept doomed fates.*

C. 1183 BC: The hero Odysseus encounters Circe, a powerful sorceress who transforms his men into swine. \
*Her island, Aeaea, is rumored to still exist within a regio, hidden beyond the mundane world. A tradition of House Ex Miscellanea claims descent from her lost tradition, and some hedge wizards invoke her name for shapechanging magic.*

1175 BC: Samson, gaining great strength through Nazirite rites and vows, massacres the Philistine army using only the jawbone of a donkey. He’s captured, his hair cut off, and his eyes gouged out after being betrayed by his harlot lover Delilah. When the Philistines take Samson into their temple of Dagon, Samson asks to rest against one of the support pillars. After being granted permission, he prays to God and miraculously recovers his strength, allowing him to bring down the columns, collapsing the temple and killing himself as well as all of the Philistines.

C. 1150 BC: The exiled Brutus of Troy, after having sacked Acquitaine, arrives in Albion. He drives out the giants led by Gog and Magog plaguing the island, conquers the remaining Gaels and Cymri - obviously weakened from infighting, and renames the island "Britain" after himself. He chooses a spot on the River Thames for his capital, and builds the city of New Troy. His lieutenant, the great warrior Corineus became ruler of Cornwall, which got its name after him.

*The magician who predicted great things for the unborn Brutus also foretold he would kill both his parents, which he did,  driving him into exile.*

*When he died, twenty-three years after landing in Britain, his sons divided the kingdom between them. Locrinus, the eldest, took England, which was called Loegria after him. Kamber took Wales, and it was named Kambria after him, while Albanactus took Scotland, which at that time was called Albany. *

*New Troy is in time corrupted to Trinovantum, and the city is later called London.*

C. 1050–1000 BC: The Tuatha Dé Danann were the fifth invaders to Hibernia, the last wave of primeval peoples before the arrival of the Gaels. After the war with the Fomórach, an escaped group of Nemed’s descendents started worshiping  continental Celtic faeries, who took the group to a mystical homeland in the north. In four fabled cities, the Tuatha Dé educated their followers in culture, art, and magic, and interbred. The group that returned to Hibernia contained the pagan faerie gods, their children, their followers, and their druids.

Tuatha Dé first defeated the Fir Bolg, and later the Fomórach driving them from the island. Placed under a powerful Tuatha Dé curse, the Fomórach could not set foot on Hibernian soil without suffering dire consequences.

*The Tuatha Dé brought four great treasures with them, signs of their sovereignty and indicators of their power: The Stone of Lia Fál, that roars beneath the foot of a rightful king; The Spear of Lug which guarantees victory to he who holds it; The Sword of Nuadha that always strikes its target; The Dagda’s Cauldron, that supplies unlimited stew and can feed an entire company of men.*

*The covenant of Circulus Ruber claims to have found the Dagda’s Cauldron.*

C. 1050 BC: The Hebrews request a king. The prophet Samuel anoints Saul and the Kingdom of Israel Established. His early reign is marked by military success but also by increasingly erratic spiritual disobedience.

C. 1020 BC: Saul the Benjaminite, first king of Israel, purges the Israelite priesthood eliminating the Canaanite necromancers and other magical traditions. \
*After David fled from Saul, he sought assistance from Ahimelech, the priest at Nob. Ahimelech provided David with consecrated bread and the sword of Goliath, unaware of the conflict between Saul and David. When Saul learned of this, he accused Ahimelech and his fellow priests of conspiracy. Despite their protests of innocence, Saul ordered their execution. His officials refused to carry out the command, but Doeg the Edomite complied, killing eighty-five priests and putting the entire town of Nob to the sword, including men, women, children, and animals.*

*The massacre is seen by scholars as a tainting of the Divine hierarchy in Israel and marks the fall of Saul’s spiritual legitimacy.*

1011 BC: Saul fears a military disaster against David and so attempts to discover the will of God by consulting the last necromancer, the witch Zephaniah, in the city of En-Dor. She summons the spirit of Samuel, to whom Saul pleads for help. The ghost of Samuel refuses Saul; he says that God had forsaken Saul, and that he and his dynasty will be extinguished the following day. The next day, as the ghost predicted, the Israelites are defeated, Saul’s three sons are killed in battle, and Saul kills himself after being sorely wounded by Philistine archers.

*This act may have opened an Infernal regio at En-Dor, and remains a cautionary tale about the dangers of spiritual desperation.*

1010–970 BC: The Reign of King David, the slayer of Goliath and a man "after God's own heart". David unifies Israel, subdues enemies, and establishes Jerusalem as the political and spiritual capital - the city that becomes the nexus of Divine presence. His lineage is seen in both Jewish and Christian traditions as messianic.

*He danced before the Ark and brought Divine order to a chaotic land. His harp may yet echo in Faerie shadow. His psalms are considered by some Hermetic theorists to be poetic theurgies - spiritual invocations disguised as song.*

1004 BC: The Ark of the Covenant is installed in Jerusalem. David brings the Ark to the city with great ceremony. This event causes a massive increase in Dominion aura, centered on Mount Zion.

*Hermetic attempts to trace the Ark’s movements have yielded only Warping and ominous dreams.*

C. 970–930 BC: The Reign of David's son, King Solomon the Magus, King of Judea. He constructs the First Temple in Jerusalem - placing within it the Ark of the Covenant, said to contain immense Divine power - as well as a vast palace for himself. He is a great magician, able to summon all manner of spirits to work his will. His mastery over spirits, demons, and angels becomes legendary, giving rise to a magical tradition distinct from later Hermeticism. His Great Seal is still a powerful ward and device for spirits and his Ring is said to be able to command them all.  \
*Some see him as a proto-theurgist or even a Divine Hedge Wizard, granted insight into angels, demons, and celestial correspondences. There are Hermetic theorists that believe elements of Hermetic Rego magic originate from Solomonic command over spirits. He could fly on the wind with a magic carpet and spoke the languages of beasts and birds. His wisdom was widely known; he answered the riddles of the Queen of Sheba and wisely judged the most perplexing cases. He ruled over mortals and spirits, brought the wealth of the world to him in his capital of the Levant, and explored the depths of the sea and the heights of the sky. *

966 BC: Construction of the First Temple Begins, a structure to house the presence of God, and perhaps the world's most powerful Divine regio. \
*Some Hermetic scholars speculate that the Holy of Holies functioned as a ritual anchor to Heaven itself, accessible only by the high priest.*

950–940 BC: Legends arise of Solomon controlling spirits, binding djinn, and authoring grimoires such as the Key of Solomon. Hermetic mystagogues still debate whether this reflects true Divine Magic, Faerie glamour, or even a suppressed Infernal pact.

*He knew the Names. That is all we must know.*

931 BC: After Solomon's death, the unified kingdom splits into Israel (north) and Judah (south). The Dominion weakens over the land as idolatry spreads.

*From a Hermetic view, regio boundaries may have fractured, leading to a multiplicity of overlapping Divine and Faerie sites.*

C. 900 BC: The Sons of Mil, the Milesians, led by the poet and sorcerer Amhairghin, arrive in Hibernia and defeat the Tuatha Dé, besting their violent resistance and magical trickery. During their conquest the sons meet three faerie goddesses who claim to be Ireland: Banba, Fódla, and Éire. Each promised victory if a son would marry her, and three sons obliged.

*This tradition, a provincial ruler marrying a land-goddess, is still practiced.*

C. 870 BC: On Mount Carmel, Elijah calls down fire from Heaven to defeat the priests of Baal.

*This may be interpreted as a full realm-overrealm confrontation, with the Dominion forcibly banishing entrenched Faerie and Magical cults.*

863 BC: Bladud becomes king of the Britons and rules for 20 years. Bladud's father sent his son to be educated in the liberal arts in Athens. After his father's death he returned with four philosophers, and founded a university at Stamford, Lincolnshire (which flourished until Saint Augustine of Canterbury suppressed it on account of heresies which were taught there). Bladud encouraged magic throughout the realm, and built Kaerbadum or Caervaddon (Bath), creating the hot springs there by the use of magic. He dedicated the city to the goddess Athena or Minerva, and in honor of her, lit undying fires, whose flames turned to balls of stone as they grew low, with new ones springing up in their stead. He finally died, it is said, when he fell from the sky while flying, and was dashed to pieces on the temple of Apollo in London.

*According to ancient texts, Bladud had gathered a personal library hidden in a secret cave somewhere in his realm.*

814 BC: The Phoenician princess Dido founds the city of Carthage.

*Carthage worshiped and obeyed its terrible gods in return for protection against the Great Hunger of the southern desert. At the height of the city’s power it controlled a mighty trading empire that stretched from the Gulf of Sidra to the Atlantic Sea, and had colonies in Spain, Sicily, Sardinia, and Corsica. It was destroyed by the Romans, and its fields salted, after a three year siege in 146 BC. What allowed this far-flung city-empire to oppose Rome throughout three conflicts over the course of almost 120 years? There may be fragments and relics of these Punic traditions hidden in the ruins and wilds of the Maghreb, perhaps concealed by those who survived the legions’ conquest.*

*It is known that the rest of the Mediterranean reviled the empire’s tradition of sacrificing the "first fruits" to their gods in exchange for favor and assistance, which included burning the firstborn children of prominent citizens in enormous bronze altars.*

 

C. 800–500 BC: The time of The Servants of Aita, god of the underworld, in Etruria (western Italy). They summon the ghosts of the recently dead and force secrets from them. 

*Later they became the Servants of Pluto, priests of Mercury Psychopompos, the Conductors of Souls, when the Cult of Mercury swallowed their tradition during the reign of Augustus (27 BC-14 AD). It is likely this was the ancestral tradition of Guorna the Fetid, and thereby Tytalus and Tremere.*

776 BC: The First Olympiad is held to commemorate Zeus and the other gods of Mount Olympus.

753 BC: Foundation of Rome by the twins Romulus and Remus. The twins, raised by a lupa, form a band of outcasts and found Rome by abducting the women of the Sabine tribe. Romulus kills his brother Remus and reigns as Rome's first king. The Lupercalia, a rite invoking wolf-spirits for strength, continues for centuries.

*"Lupa" can mean either She-Wolf or prostitute. Hermetic scholars have argued over whether this was a mere legend or a sign of a faerie-blooded lineage.*

*Some hedge wizards still claim to descend from the line of Remus, exiled and transformed into the first werewolves of Europe.*

722 BC: Assyrian Conquest of Israel: The northern kingdom falls. Its ten tribes are exiled, possibly lost in mundane and magical senses.

*Some Hermetic explorers claim to have found that the tribes were taken into regiones and may persist there in altered form.*

701 BC: The Assyrian army besieging Jerusalem is mysteriously destroyed overnight, allegedly by an angel of the Lord. 

*Or was it magic? This is one of the rare military-scale manifestations of Divine Power documented outside Sinai.*

683 BC: Beginning of Athenian democracy.

658 BC: Byzas of Megara (a city-state near Athens) founds the city of Byzantium.

C. 640–609 BC: Josiah purges pagan cults and restores the worship of the God of Israel. His prophet, Zephaniah, speaks of coming judgment and the Day of the Lord. Hermetic mystics view Josiah’s reign as a final attempt to restore spiritual alignment before the cataclysm of exile.

*What was lost with Josiah cannot be reclaimed by kings. Only prophecy remains.*

C. 600 BC: Zoroaster (or Zarathushtra) founds the Zoroastrianism faith of the Persian Empire on the principles of monotheism. 

*In his divine hymns, the Gathas, he reveals to humanity there was one supreme and all-knowing God, Ahura Mazda ("Wise Lord"), locked in eternal conflict with His opposite, Angra Mainyu ("Destructive Spirit"). To destroy Angra Mainyu, God created an immaculate material world based on seven pillars, which are personified by the Amesha Spentas ("Beneficent Immortals"). God knew that Angra Mainyu would attack this world, bringing with it disorder, falsehood, sorrow, cruelty, disease, and suffering, so he also created Man, the masterpiece of His creation, to play the final and decisive role in the great cosmic battle between Light and Dark.*

*For over a thousand years, Zoroaster’s wisdom flourished as the state religion of three mighty Persian empires, and there was great hostility and antipathy between the Zoroastrians and the Mercurian priests of Rome. Ultimately, the Romans were diminished by the spread of Christianity, but the Zoroastrians were pushed farther east by the spread of Islam, and were nearly decimated when waves of Turks swept across the Islamic world in the early eleventh century. Zoroastrian priests, called Magoi, are renowned for venerating fire as a symbol of God’s purity. Every Zoroastrian temple has a sacred fire; it is essential to their religion. They also pursue truth as their highest ideal, with justice close behind. Zoroaster taught that since the world is essentially immaculate and good, man should live well and enjoy its gifts, although always in moderation since the states of excess and deficiency are part of Angra Mainyu.*

C. 600 BC: The Persians were the second world empire represented in several dreams God gave to King Nebuchadnezzar of Babylon. These dreams so troubled the king that it kept him awake all night. He sought their interpretation amongst his magicians, astrologers and sorcerers. There was, however, one catch. These 'wise men' had to first state what the king dreamed before they gave its meaning! The king was so serious about the matter that he declared if his counselors could not tell him his dream they, and their entire households, would receive the death penalty.

*Nebuchadnezzar's prophetic dreams came through more than sixty years later.*

597 BC: Nebuchadnezzar besieges Jerusalem which surrenders. Jeconiah, his court and other prominent citizens and craftsmen are deported to Babylon. The start of the Babylonian captivity and the Jewish diaspora. Jeconiah's uncle, Zedekiah, is installed as vassal king of Judah.

589–586 BC: The second siege of Jerusalem. It ends with the Babylonians under Nebuchadnezzar sacking Jerusalem and destroying Solomon’s Temple, bringing an end to the Kingdom of Judah. The Ark vanishes, its fate unknown. The Exile begins.

*This marks the collapse of a great Divine regio. Some have speculated that the Temple may have been absorbed into a regio at the moment of its ruin, preserved like Arcadia holds Faerie echoes of lost glory.*

C. 580 BC: Periander, the tyrant of Corinth, with the help of the Necromanteion (Oracle of the Dead) of Acheron summons his dead wife Melissa to find out where she has buried a treasure, and while she tells him, she also remind him that he'd gotten too frisky and violated her corpse. She demands a sacrifice of clothing, which results in his orders that the women of the city publicly strip and hand over their clothes.

570–540 BC: Visions of the Prophet Ezekiel: His writings describe celestial beings, wheels within wheels, and a chariot-throne of God. Many Criamon and Kabbalistic mystagogues view his visions as encounters with Divine Mystery Cult elements, containing encoded cosmological truths about the structure of the Realms.

*He saw the Merkabah, more real than any Form, more essential than any Arcane Connection.*

C. 570–495 BC: Lifetime of Pythagoras of Samos, philosopher, mystic, and mathematician. He studied in Egypt and Babylon, and taught at his school at Croton. Pythagoras teaches that numbers are the essence of all things and founds a secretive brotherhood combining mathematics, ethics, and ritual purification. He introduces the idea that the cosmos is governed by harmonic ratios - a concept deeply resonant with Hermetic magic’s structure of numerical magnitudes, celestial alignments, and arcane correspondence.

He claims that the soul is immortal, undergoing cycles of reincarnation, and may be purified through philosophy and music - ideas preserved within House Criamon’s doctrines and echoed in the Cult of Orpheus. His discovery of the Music of the Spheres, the inaudible resonance of celestial harmony, later influences Hermetic astrology and the conception of the Great Chain of Sympathy.

*The wisdom of Pythagoras is known throughout the Order, and beyond. His lost works are sought after as potent sources of insight into pre-Hermetic traditions. The Fraternity of Samos preserves what it holds to be his mystical, musical, and mathematical legacy, and, almost uniquely among Mystery Cults, members openly and proudly admit their membership - but never their secrets. *

C. 550–330 BC: The Achaemenid Empire, founded by Cyrus The Great and ended by Alexander the Great; at its height, it encompasses Anatolia, Persia, the Levant, as well as Transoxiana and Egypt, and also engages in significant territorial disputes with the Greek city-states. Zoroastrianism established by Cyrus as the official state religion. Fire temples are built across the empire under Antaxerxes II. Zoroastrian calendar is formalized. Zurvan heresy emerges.

539 BC: The End of Babylon. King Belshazzar of Babylon holds a great feast for a thousand of his lords, and commands that the Temple vessels from Jerusalem be brought in so that they can drink from them, provoking the ire of God. As the Babylonians drink, a hand appears and writes on the wall. Belshazzar calls for his magicians and diviners to interpret the writing, but they are unable even to read it. The queen advises Belshazzar to send for Daniel, renowned for his wisdom. Daniel is brought in, and the king offers to make him third in rank in the kingdom if he can interpret the writing. Daniel declines the honor, but agrees to the request. He reminds Belshazzar that his father Nebuchadnezzar's greatness was the gift of God, and that when he became arrogant God threw him down until he learned humility: "the Most High God has sovereignty over the kingdom of mortals, and sets over it whomever He will." Belshazzar has drunk from the vessels of God's Temple and praised his idols, but he has not given honor to God, and so God sent this hand and wrote the words "MENE, MENE, TEKEL, UPHARSIN" and Daniel interprets them for the king: "MENE, God has numbered the days of your kingdom and brought it to an end; TEKEL, you have been weighed ... and found wanting;" and "UPHARSIN", your kingdom is divided and given to the Medes and Persians.

Three days later, Babylon is destroyed by Persians under Cyrus the Great.

*What became of the sacred vessels from the temple after Belshazzar’s feast?*

*Cyrus restored the Jewish community in the Holy Land, and undoubtedly many of the Temple treasures were also taken back there.*

*The profanation of the vessels could have rendered them unusable in the eyes of the Jewish priests, and they may have been left behind.*

538 BC: Following the decree of Cyrus the Great, king of Persia, the first wave of exiled Jews is permitted to return to Jerusalem. This expedition is led by Zerubbabel, heir to the Davidic line, and Joshua the High Priest, with the express purpose of rebuilding the Temple and restoring sacrificial worship. Though the journey is long and perilous, over 40,000 souls make the return - accompanied by sacred vessels taken by Nebuchadnezzar a generation prior.

The site of the ruined Temple is described in obscure records as being littered with silent echoes, where once Divine presence resided. The laying of new foundations disturbed both the lingering ghosts of the past and subtle spiritual forces - some of which resisted the new order, while others stirred as if remembering their old oaths. Magical traditions speak of this moment as a shifting of regio boundaries: The Dominion reasserting itself, pushing back Infernal corruption and severing latent Magical flows once drawn upon by ancient cults.

*The ashes of exile had cooled, but the fire of holiness flickered anew.*

*Hermetic Kabbalists believe this moment constitutes a restoration of the physical and metaphysical covenant, repairing the Arcane Connection between Heaven and Zion that was ruptured in 586 BC.*

C. 535–475 BC: Heraclitus of Ephesus, called the Obscure, teaches that all is in flux, and that fire is the fundamental substance of the cosmos. He posits a Logos, a rational principle underlying all change, which some Hermetic theorists interpret as an early understanding of Divine order or the hidden laws of the Magic Realm. His reverence for fire as both creative and destructive force finds echo in Hermetic spellcraft, especially in the manipulation of Ignem, which Heraclitus metaphorically saw as the soul of transformation.

Though he leaves no school, his cryptic maxims become seeds for both mystical and rational traditions. His writings are quoted in Criamonic texts as koans to be contemplated during meditation, and Flambeau magi invoke him as a spiritual patron of elemental mastery. A few in House Merinita even speculate that Heraclitus was a faerie-changed philosopher whose every word burns with layered meaning.

*His assertion that “you cannot step into the same river twice” is embraced by certain magi - particularly in House Flambeau and House Criamon - as a reflection of the transient, ever-shifting nature of the world and the illusion of permanence.*

522 BC: Gaumata the Magian seizes the Achaemenid throne by impersonating Smerdis, the rightful heir to Cambyses (son of Cyrus the Great), and reigns for eight months before he’s slain by Darius, who became suspicious that it was not his actual brother. Darius and his assassins run through the castle, slaughtering Magian eunuchs and priests until they finally reach the man who called himself King. They kill him on the spot, chop off the heads of the dead and go out into the streets waving the decapitated heads in front of the people, telling them that their king was an imposter replaced in a Magian plot. They tell the people to grab any weapon they can find, hunt down every Magi, and slaughter them all.

*For years to follow, they made The Slaughter of the Magi an annual holiday.*

516 BC: The Jews complete the Second Temple in Jerusalem. Though far less grand than Solomon’s, it reestablishes a stable Dominion.

*The voice of the Lord was not heard. But the shadow it cast was heavy upon the stones.*

*Some Hermetic architects have noted the geometric precision in its construction, suggesting use of ancient Divine theorems lost to modern magi.*

*In Divine terms, this act restores the axis mundi of the Covenant - the link between Heaven and Earth. While no visible miracles accompany its consecration, some esoteric commentators claim the Shekhinah, the indwelling presence of the Divine, once again hovers in silence over the Holy of Holies - though it is said to never speak again as it once did.*

*Some in House Bonisagus speculate that the Temple's completion marks the fixing of a permanent Divine regio, one layered deep within the mountain of Zion. Attempts to penetrate it magically are said to fail utterly, as if the regio exists in a place beyond Hermetic reach - a sign of Divine supremacy over magical access. Meanwhile, dissenting Merinita grumble that an older power, once buried beneath the site, was finally sealed or exorcised during the rites of completion.*

c. 515–450 BC: Parmenides of Elea teaches that change is illusion and that true reality is singular, eternal, and unchanging. His cosmology directly challenges the senses and lays the groundwork for deep metaphysical thought. 

He is said to have glimpsed the still point at the heart of reality, what Criamon calls the Axis of Time, and his disciple Zeno defends this vision with paradoxes that strain both logic and the boundaries of magical motion.

*Parmenides is revered by House Criamon, who see in his monism a precursor to the Path of the Circle and the Enigma. His writings, preserved in poetic verse, speak of a journey to the goddess of truth, interpreted by some magi as an allegory for contact with a Magic Realm being or even an aspect of the Divine.*

513 BC: Darius of Persia takes Thrace. The first Persian king to cross into Europe.

C. 509 BC: The monarchy of Rome is overthrown after the king of Rome, Tarquin the Proud, rapes his friend’s wife, Lucretia, who commits suicide. A Republic replaces the monarchy, which conquers the known world in the next five hundred years.

*In some ways the Order resembles the Republic of Rome. Both are ruled by the wise men selected by their elders.*

C. 500 BC: Greek Cult of Hermes at its height.

C. 499–449 BC: The Persian Wars.

495–435 BC: Life of Empedocles, Greek philosopher and mystic, who teaches that all matter is composed of four primordial elements - fire, air, water, and earth - a model later mirrored in Hermetic Forms. He asserts that Love (sympathy) and Strife (antipathy) govern the mingling and separation of these elements, anticipating key principles of Hermetic correspondences and magical resonance. A poet, healer, and thaumaturge, Empedocles is rumored to also have possessed miraculous powers, and some Criamon magi revere him as a proto-Enigmatist.

C. 490–430 BC: Lifetime of Zeno of Elea, student of Parmenides, who formulates a series of paradoxes of motion and plurality, intended to defend the teaching that reality is one and unchanging. His paradoxes - such as Achilles and the Tortoise, and the Arrow in Flight - are regarded by House Criamon as glimpses into the illusion of time, and are meditated upon by mystae seeking to unravel the Enigma.

*Some Hermetic magi consider Zeno's thought a bridge between reason and mysticism, positing that his paradoxes reflect flaws in mortal perception rather than contradictions in the cosmos. Others - especially among House Bonisagus - dismiss him as a sophist. Yet his thought endures in magical theory on the limits of Hermetic motion, the discreteness of magical time, and the structure of Momentary versus Diameter durations.*

*In Criamon circles, it is whispered that Zeno's final paradox, never written down, concerns a motionless journey taken inward rather than forward - interpreted by some as a riddle pointing toward Final Twilight or transcendence.*

490 BC: Battle of Marathon. The Persians took Eretria through treachery, and then moved on Attica with 20,000 men to confront Athens. They clashed at Marathon in a momentous victory for the Greeks; despite facing twice their number, the Greeks suffered just 192 casualties compared to the 6400 lost to the Persians.

480 BC: Battle of Thermopylae and Battle of Salamis. Darius’ son Xerxes I launched another attack on Greece taking Thessaly, Delphi, and Argos with ease. This time it was Sparta who raised the defense, convening the first Panhellenic Congress at Corinth, at which 31 city-states granted the Athenian Themistocles supreme command over its forces, although overall inter-allied command remained in Spartan hands. The Spartan 300’s defeat at Thermopylae delayed the Persian forces long enough to save the Greek fleet at Artemisium from annihilation, although Athens and Acropolis were sacked. The Persian fleet was then defeated in the battle of Salamis.

*The most famous last stand, and naval victory, of all time.*

469–399 BC: Life of Socrates, philosopher of Athens. He teaches through dialogue and irony, fostering the flowering of Greek philosophical inquiry. He pioneers the dialectic technique, urging relentless questioning and the pursuit of truth through introspection. He claims Knowledge is intrinsically Good, and Ignorance Evil, a view later echoed by Hermetic scholars. His students, including Plato, carry his method into realms both philosophical and mystical.

*Renowned for his paradoxical wisdom - "knowing that he knew nothing" - he attributes his insight to a daimonion, a personal guiding spirit, leading some magi to speculate he was touched by a Divine or Magic spirit. *

457 BC: Under the reign of Artaxerxes I of Persia, a decree is issued permitting the scribe Ezra - a descendant of Aaron and master of the Law of Moses - to lead a group of Jewish exiles from Babylon back to Jerusalem. Bearing imperial authority and equipped with silver and gold, Ezra's mission is to reestablish Temple worship, restore Divine law, and purify the returned community. This event marks a spiritual renaissance and is viewed by Hermetic historians as the formal end of the Age of Exile and the beginning of the Second Covenant Era - a time when the remnants of Israel, despite lacking kingship or independence, become guardians of sacred texts and rituals of immense symbolic potency.

*From this year forward, the Law walked again among the stones of Zion, not as thunder from the mountain, but as ink upon parchment.*

*In some esoteric traditions, this return is seen as the final sealing of the Covenantal Dominion Aura over Jerusalem - one that thwarts both Magical and Infernal encroachment in subtle but immutable ways. The year 457 BC is also calculated by some Christian mystics as the beginning of the Seventy Weeks Prophecy of Daniel, culminating in messianic expectations centuries later.*

445 BC: Nehemiah rebuilds Jerusalem’s walls.

431–404 BC: Peloponnesian War fought between Sparta and Athens.

428–348 BC: Life of Plato, philosopher and mystic of Athens. A disciple of Socrates and teacher of Aristotle, Plato founds the Academy, the first enduring institution of higher learning. His doctrine of the Realm of Forms - a transcendent world of perfect archetypes - is later adopted by Hermetic theorists to explain magical archetypes, Essential Traits, and the metaphysical truth behind magical correspondences. 

His idea of the Great Chain of Being, an ordered cosmos linking all levels of reality from divine to base matter, becomes foundational in Hermetic cosmology. Some interpret The Republic as a veiled treatise on ruling magi, where philosopher-kings are allegories for gifted sages guided by Reason (Logos) and the Nous.

Plato’s mystical writings, especially the Timaeus, describe a universe shaped by a Demiurge using eternal Forms - echoing the Hermetic view of creation through abstract Arts and Techniques. His thought bridges Magic, Divine philosophy, and early alchemy, and is studied in both Bonisagus' theory and the Mysteries of the Houses alike.

*Plato's work is based on the work of elder philosophers, including Parmenides, Empedocles, Zeno of Elcea, and most importantly Socrates.*

C. 420 BC: The magician Asclepius, a great healer, helps end a plague in Athens. 

C. 400 BC – 668 AD Africa: Time of the Garamantian Kingdom, sprung in the Fezzan and sprawling through the Libyan desert, it is ruled by sorcerer-kings.

*Their nobility hunted nomadic tribes for sport from horse-drawn chariots. *

*Even in death, slaves served their Garamantian masters. The lords of the Fezzan mastered potent necromantic magics allowing them to control hordes of walking dead, which did not thirst and ignored the biting winds of the desert. These dessicated corpses, blackened by the sun and the Garamantian magics, worked fields, dug the mines, and served until their forms no longer remained functional. They managed to repulse the mighty Roman legions (and the Cult of Mercury) until 15BC, at which time they became a nominal client state.*

*The final sorcerer-king king was taken away in chains by Arab warriors in 668. *

* *

390 BC: Rome is sacked by Celtic tribes, aided by powerful Druids. This humiliating defeat prompts Romans to seek arcane strength from the East, mirroring their adoption of Greek culture and philosophy. They embrace the worship of Hermes - Greek god of magic and messengers - under his Roman guise as Mercury, laying the ideological foundation for the Cult of Mercury.

C. 384–322 BC: Life of Aristotle. Student of many disciplines, auctor of logic, founder of the Lyceum school. He argues for the existence of a Fifth Element, finer than the four material elements, which make up the heavenly bodies and influence the mundane world. He argues against predestination, but allows that the heavens influence via the fifth essence.

*The Fifth Element is identified by some magi as Vim.*

382–336 BC: Life of Philip II of Macedon. After defeating the Greek city-states of Athens and Thebes at the Battle of Chaeronea in 338 BC, Philip II led the effort to establish a federation of Greek states known as the League of Corinth, with him as the elected hegemon and commander-in-chief of Greece for a planned invasion of the Achaemenid Empire of Persia. However, his assassination by a royal bodyguard, Pausanias of Orestis, led to the immediate succession of his son Alexander, who would go on to invade the Achaemenid Empire in his father's stead.

356–323 BC: Life of Alexander the Great, student of Aristotle, he succeeded his father Philip II to the throne in 336 BC at the age of 20. In 13 years he conquers the civilized world, from Greece to Egypt to Persia to the frontier of India, before dying of overeating. He was undefeated in battle and is history's greatest and most successful military commander. A new age of civilization briefly flourishes. Some of the fundamental concepts of magic, the ideas of Magoi and Philosophers, met and mingled in this period.

*Unfortunately, this also means the slaughter of the Mazdean priests, destruction of priceless literature and fire temples destroyed.*

332 BC: Alexander the Great enters Jerusalem, he is greeted by the high priest and shown in prophecy. The fusion of Hellenistic and Semitic mysticism begins here.

*Hermetic lore suggests he may have crossed through a regio and been influenced by a vision from the Divine Realm. *

300 BC: Pharmakeutria of the Temple of Asklepios at what is today Epidauros is

driven out by the barbarian Goths.

C. 300 BC: Greek philosopher Democritus of Abdera introduces atomic theory, arguing that the world is composed of ultimately indivisible tiny essences, atoms. Although Democritus was opposed to the supernatural, his "atoms" are thought by some magi as the fundamental components of the elements.

*Atomism is a weird and fundamentally flawed theory, crude and spiritually hollow, and devoid of reverence for the essential nature of Form and Essence. His dismissal of sympathy, spiritual cause, and celestial influence is outright ludicrous. How could it ever be incorporated into Hermetica Magica? Of much more use, he also introduced the "democratic system", a political method where participants rule by vote, a version of which is used by the Order.*

C. 299–200 BC: Sometime during this century, the legendary but elusive Hermes Trismegistus completed multiple alchemical, astrological, philosophical and magical works - the "Hermetica" - establishing the base of Hermeticism. Many are unfortunately lost, and of others only fragments and unreliable translations from Greek and Arabic remain. Surviving works include: The Salmeschoiniaka (the "Wandering of the Influences"), perhaps composed in Alexandria, which deals with the configurations of the stars; The Art of Eudoxus, a treatise on astronomy; the Liber Hermetis ("The Book of Hermes") an important work on astrology laying out the names of the decans (a distinctly Egyptian system that divided the zodiac into 36 parts); The Brontologion, a treatise on the various effects of thunder in different months; The Peri Seismōn ("On Earthquakes"), a treatise on the relation between earthquakes and astrological signs; The Book of Asclepius Called Myriogenesis, a treatise on astrological medicine; The Holy Book of Hermes to Asclepius, a treatise on astrological botany describing the relationships between various plants and the decans; The Fifteen Stars, Stones, Plants and Images: a treatise on astrological mineralogy and botany dealing with the effect of the stars on the pharmaceutical powers of minerals and plants; and Cyranides, a sublime work on the magical powers and healing properties of minerals, plants and animals.

*Most of the texts are presented in the form of a dialogue, a favorite form for didactic material in classical antiquity. The most well known treatise in the Corpus Hermeticum is its opening treatise, which is called the Poimandres - who can be considered to be a sort of deity, or attribute of God as nous or "mind". "Who are you?" said I.*

*"I am Poimandres" he said, "the Nous of Sovereignity. I know what thou desirest, and I am with thee everywhere."*

*There is speculation that ancient Hermetic works lost to the Order still may remain in Arab lands, as a number of iterative treatises in Arabic has been found in the Levant. Clement of Alexandria was under the impression that the Egyptians had forty-two sacred writings by Hermes, writings that detailed the training of Egyptian priests.*

*Some claim that no mere mortal could have come up with such a body of work, and that he was the Greek god Hermes or the Egyptian god Thoth. Nevertheless, the legacy that remains speaks for itself and we stand on the shoulders of giants. Founder Bonisagus was right to name the Order after him.*

287–212 BC: Archimedes of Syracuse, mathematician, engineer, and philosopher, astounds the ancient world with his mastery of geometry, hydraulics, and mechanical invention. He built burning mirrors that set Roman ships aflame and machines capable of lifting cities - feats interpreted by Verditius magi as evidence of True Automata, lost to time. Bonisagus himself is said to have studied copies of Archimedes’ diagrams when formulating Hermetic theory of Corpus and Terram interactions. His principle of leverage - "Give me a place to stand, and I will move the Earth" - is cited metaphorically as a parable of Arcane Connection.

His death at the hands of a Roman soldier, despite orders to spare him, is mourned by scholars as the loss of one who might have become a Foundational Figure in Hermetic history, had he lived in a later age. Fragments of his lost works, such as the Mechanical Sphere and writings on infinity, are hunted in the libraries of the Theban and Roman Tribunals.

*Another giant, but there is disagreement over whether he was a magician.*

*His grasp of mathematical abstraction is so profound that some Hermetic magi speculate he had access to a form of non-Hermetic magical Insight, perhaps through communion with the Magic Realm or tutelage by an Airy Spirit of Genius.*

C. 250–100 BC: Composition of the Septuagint & Rise of Apocalyptic Literature. Hebrew scriptures are translated into Greek in Alexandria. Books like Enoch and Daniel emerge, full of visionary journeys, angelic hierarchies, and heavenly courts - fertile ground for theurgists and the later Solomonic traditions.

*The heavens open in symbols, and the stars speak. This is not madness - it is codified mystery.*


## Rome and the Cult of Mercury (250 BC – AD 476)

*As the Roman Empire spreads across the known world, so too does its magic, order, and ambition. The scattered mystery cults and arcane traditions of Greece, Egypt, and Persia are absorbed into a grand synthesis of magical practice, forming the Cult of Mercury - a vast, disciplined order of spellcasters who harness ritual magic to bolster Rome’s dominion.*

*The Mercurian magi, named for the swift-footed messenger god, master the art of large-scale magical ceremonies, empowering armies, foretelling omens, and maintaining the spiritual harmony of the Empire. Their magic is deeply entwined with civic and military life, granting them immense influence. Some Hermetic scholars claim that the Parma Magica itself has roots in their shielding rites, while others believe their magic was lost to the slow decay of Rome.*

*However, even as the Cult of Mercury thrives, other forces rise. The Mysteries of Mithras, Isis, and Dionysus challenge its dominance, offering secret initiations and personal enlightenment. The early Christian Church, growing from an underground sect into a force of divine authority, begins to denounce pagan magic as heretical, setting the stage for future conflict between the Dominion and the arcane.*

*As the Empire collapses under the twin pressures of barbarian invasion and civil war, so does the Cult. The sacking of cities, the corruption of rituals, and the crumbling infrastructure of the Empire shatter its cohesion.  The Priests of Mercury were hard pressed to survive, much less record histories. The magic of the Cult of Mercury begins to change in this period, shifting from a communal activity to an individual craft. Each line develops an idiosyncratic understanding of magic, leading to vulgarization.*

*By the fall of the Western Empire in 476, the Cult of Mercury has all but vanished, leaving behind only fragments - a handful of secretive magi, lost texts, and rituals that will one day be rediscovered by the founders of the Order of Hermes. Though Rome has fallen, its magic is not truly dead - merely waiting for a new age to rise.*

C. 200 BC: The Pontifex of the Pompeii Temple, Plentarch of the Cult of Mercury calls together 38 Mercurians to meet with him in Pompeii, each of whom possess a powerful ritual associated with their temple. He collects these rituals and carve them into 38 golden tablets. Plentarch teaches each priest a special ritual he had discovered that would allow them to commune with their gods in concert, which makes it possible for the priests to cast rituals associated with every temple as long as they work together. 

*Through vast mystical networks, the Priests of Mercury gathered in distant temples connected together by Roman roads, and channeled powerful magical forces to aid the Roman military in remote parts of the Empire. Due to the extensive resources and centralized power required to construct a Roman road and then enchant the milliaria, the magic was quickly lost in the fragmented years following the fall of Rome, as the Cult rapidly splintered into mutually hostile traditions.*

*The original 38 golden tablets are gone. However, two of these rituals have been adapted for use by Hermetic magi, becoming the spells Vision of the Scrying Pool and Aegis of the Hearth. The rest remain lost, although House Guernicus claims Fenicil rediscovered some of these rituals, which they retain to assist in the dispensation of Hermetic justice. Some Hermetic magi continue to scour Europe for traces of the Order of Mercury, certain that the other rituals would as easily convert to Hermetic practice.*

C. 190–120 BC: Hipparchus, master astronomer and mathematician, creates the first star catalogue and discovers the precession of the equinoxes, proving that the heavens themselves shift over time. For Hermetic magi - especially astrologers and members of House Bonisagus and House Criamon - this revelation defines the nature of Astrological Modifiers and the dynamic motion of celestial influences. His work lays the foundation for accurate planetary ephemerides, without which no horoscope, or astrological spell, could be cast with precision. 

*Some magi claim Hipparchus’ insight came not from mere observation but from a Magic Realm Insight - a momentary glimpse into the harmonics of the celestial spheres. House Merinita whispers that he may have spoken to a Faerie aether-spirit; Jerbiton, that he worked under Divine guidance.*

*Fragments of his Commentary on Aratus are prized as sources of insight in astrological mysteries, and it is said that a perfect astrolabe of his design still functions at The Cave of Twisting Shadows, domus magna of Criamon, aligning itself not to the stars - but to the deeper truths behind them.*

167–160 BC: Maccabean Revolt and Cleansing of the Temple. Jewish rebels resist Hellenistic idolatry and restore the Temple. This sparks a Divine resurgence.

*Merinita claims that Antiochus’ abominations stirred ancient faeries of desecration, who were driven out not by force, but by ritual purity and prayer.*

146 BC: End of 3rd Punic War; destruction of the trading city Carthage by the Romans. The Carthaginians are massacred and enslaved, although some escaped to the corners of the Empire, others beyond the Ring of Fire, and others by sailing west into the Atlantic, to the island of Antilia. The magi of Carthage left behind fragmentary texts, and some artifacts, now being investigated by the Sansaron covenant established upon the ruins (Roman Tribunal).

141 BC–224 AD: Empire of the Parthians in Persia. Revival of Mazdean faith, rediscovery of many lost traditions and scriptures.

116–27 BC: Lifetime of Marcus Terentius Varro, polymath author of 620 books, and ancient Rome’s greatest scholar; "the third great light of Rome" (after Virgil and Cicero).

*Varro decided to focus on identifying nine of these arts: grammar, rhetoric, logic, arithmetic, geometry, astronomy, musical theory, medicine, and architecture. Using Varro's list, subsequent scholars defined the seven classical Artes Liberales. His De lingua latina libri XXV (On the Latin Language in 25 Books) defined many of the principles of which we use Latin today.*

106–43 BC: Lifetime of Marcus Tullius Cicero, author of the treatise "De Divinatione" (On Divination). 

*His influence on the Latin language was immense. He wrote more than three-quarters of extant Latin literature that is known to have existed in his lifetime. He introduced into Latin the arguments of the chief schools of Hellenistic philosophy and created a Latin philosophical vocabulary with neologisms such as evidentia, humanitas, qualitas, quantitas, and essentia, distinguishing himself as a translator and philosopher. It is unfortunate that he got beheaded by order of Mark Antony.*

100 BC–100 AD: Little is known about the history of the Cult of Mercury in this era. Certainly the Cult began this period at a zenith, active and powerful, the magic lifeblood of the Roman Empire. 

70–19 BC: Lifetime of Publius Vergilius Maro, the grandmaster poet-mage Virgil, in the city of Naples. He learned divination magic, possibly from the tomes of Solomon. He prophesied the coming of a golden age heralded by the birth of a wondrous child and he owned a bronze head and animated a statue that would bite off the hands of unfaithful wives. He fell in love with Pheobilla, who betrayed him and arranged for the whole city to mock him. In vengeance, he deprived the whole city of fire for three days, except for fire gained from between Pheobilla’s legs.

*As you for sure already know, the works of Virgil almost from the moment of their publication revolutionized Latin poetry. The Eclogues, Georgics, and above all the Aeneid became the standard texts in school curricula. Who cannot but weep reading about the tragic death of Queen Dido?*

63 BC: The Romans, under Pompey, with the help of powerful rituals, conquer Judea and enter Jerusalem. From this point, many Hermetic scholars believe the veil between Realms begins to thin, as diverse spiritual traditions - Roman, Persian, Hebrew, and Egyptian - collide.

*Jerusalem becomes a liminal city in every sense.*

58–51 BC: The Romans expand into Celtic Gaul, led by the general Julius Caesar. Two powerful traditions of magic clashed along with the foot soldiers of their civilizations. The highly organized Cult had developed elaborate rituals which gave them great advantages over the disparate groups of druids, and not ending until the Roman legions had marched to the Atlantic Ocean.

From their stronghold in Britain, the druids rallied their brethren in Transalpine Gaul (France) in a massive rebellion. Although this too was crushed by Caesar.

*The Gallic Wars were a key factor in Caesar's ability to win the Civil War and declare himself dictator, which culminated in the end of the Roman Republic and the establishment of the Roman Empire.*

55–54 BC: Julius Caesar invades Britain twice. Cassivelaunus was king of Britain, and with the help of the Druids he eventually got the better of the Romans. Caesar sent him a letter, demanding that he submit to Roman rule and pay him tribute. Cassivelaunus indignantly refused, reminding Caesar that the British and the Romans were sprung from a common stock, and should maintain friendly relations. In response, Caesar invaded Britain, but the British repelled him. Seething with anger, Caesar prepared his fleets again, and sailed up the Thames. But sharpened stakes underwater ripped the bottoms out of his ships, so that many of his troops drowned. Many of those who made it to shore were slaughtered by the Britons, so that Caesar once again was forced to retreat. Cassivelaunus held great celebrations for the victory, at which a quarrel arose between him and Androgeus, the duke of London, which grew into a war. Androgeus was hardpressed by Cassivelaunus, and wrote to Caesar, asking for his help. Caesar came, and together they defeated Cassivelaunus, who was forced to pay tribute to Rome.

*The Druids continued to harass the Romans from the safety of their enchanted island Mona Insula whenever they could. They probably had a hand in the sinking of the ships.*

50 BC: Immediately after Julius Caesar's victory on the continent, the Cult of Mercury begins to prepare a complex ritual to destroy the druid's sanctuary on Mona Insula.

*It takes them more than a century, but it finally bears fruit in 61 AD.*

38 BC: The Roman general Marcus Vipsanius Agrippa establishes the new settlement Colonia Agrippensis, in the lands in the Cologne Lowland vacated by the Eburones. 

*In 50, the colony is upgraded to a city and in 89 AD it becomes the administrative capital of the new province Germania Inferior. The city finally falls to the Ripuarian Franks in AD 459.*

37–4 BC: Reign of Herod the Great, the Roman Jewish client king of the Herodian kingdom of Judea. Herod rebuilds and expands the Temple magnificently. While a political figure, his reign is wrapped in legend and paranoia.

*Flambeau claims to have found a grimoire that suggest that Herod attempted to harness Divine power through architectural ley alignments.*

27 BC–14 AD: Reign of Caesar Augustus, also known as Octavian, the first Roman Emperor and the greatest leader in history. The reign of Augustus initiated the imperial cult as well as an era associated with imperial peace, the Pax Romana. The Cult of Mercury worked elaborate ritual magics, requiring large groups of priests, to expand and protect the glory of eternal Rome.

*Intertwined with the Empire, the Cult of Mercury grew and flourished. You could even say it was the Empire.*

16 BC: Roman founding of Augusta Treverorum (Trier) as a regional capital.

*This is the oldest city in Germany and predates the Romans.*

1 Levant: Three magi, each a great astrologer and wise man, one from Asia, one from Europe, one from Africa, travel from the ends of the world to witness the birth of the infant Jesus and bring him Gifts of frankincense, gold, myrrh. Each mage is a king, Gaspar the Young of Persia, Melchior of Saba, and Balthazar the Old of Sheba. The account relates that the Magi had met King Herod, who sought the child Jesus to kill him, and after finding the child had a vision to evade the King, and returned to the countries by a different route.

*Some claim that they abandoned their crowns and went to India to die, others that they went to the city of Sewa, where they died after living 100 years. One author calls them Hormizdah, Yazdegerd and Perozdh, another calls them Hor, Basanater and Karsudan.*

*The most profound intervention of the Divine Realm into human history.*

9 Rhine: Three Roman legions under the leadership of Publius Quinctilius Varus march to northern Germany. Varus was instructed to conquer the Germanic tribes and to demand payment. While many locals opposed the new commander, he also had some allies among the Germanics. However, Arminius the Cheruski was not one of them. Himself trained to war in Roman service, he rallies the tribes around him and lures the Roman legions into an ambush. The numerically far inferior Germanic tribes used their knowledge of the area, built ramparts and used the element of surprise for their advantage. The Romans were led into the deep, dense Teutoburger forest and moorlands and could not keep their usual formations. Thus, they stood no chance and were almost entirely destroyed. Varus took his life to escape captivity.

The Romans made no further concerted efforts to conquer and permanently hold Germania beyond the Rhine and the Agri Decumates.

*Perhaps Rome's greatest defeat and one of the most decisive battles in history. *

*Hermann, like the rest of the Cheruski, was a devotee of Tius (Tīwaz / Tyr), the one-handed battle god of the Germans, and the magic of his priests, together with their mastery of the forest, gave the assembled tribesmen strength, speed, and stamina greater than that of their opponents.*

C. 30: In the reign of Emperor Tiberius, Roman law punishing poisoners is extended to those who use magic to harm others, raise storms, blast crops, raise the spirits of the dead, or incite love. The penalty is death.

*Note that all of these acts may be considered baneful.*

C. 33: The grown Jesus of Nazareth, who has become a wonder-worker, is executed by mundane rulers. His followers spread word of his wonders and found Christianity. \
*Upon the death of Jesus Christ, the veil of the Temple is torn, and supernatural events ripple through the land. Some magi believe this moment marks a shift in Divine power, altering the way miracles manifest. Some cryptic Quaesitorial records suggest that ancient magics were permanently weakened by this event.* 

C. 35–100: The Apostles spread Christianity and the New Testament is written.

43: Emperor Claudius restarts the conquest of Britain after the British king,

Guiderius, tries to throw off the Roman yoke. Claudius invades to bring him to heel. Winning the first battle, he then kills Guiderius by treachery.

*This may have been following some very bad advice by the druids.*

C. 45: A Samaritan named Simonus Magus works many wonders in Rome. He cures diseases, raises the dead, makes himself invisible, animates statues, summons corpses from thin air, changes shape into animals, and flies through the air. Church tradition claims that he is exposed as a tool of the Devil by Saint Peter.

*He is said to have offered Peter silver for the secret of Peter’s magic. Refused, Simonus is then said to have asserted his godhood, and announced that he would fly to Heaven to prove it. St. Peter prayed to God and Simonus Magus crashed to the earth and died.*

C. 50 Hibernia: Era of Cuchullain, a great hero.

*They say he is an incarnation of the Irish god Lugh, who is also his father.*

61 Stonehenge: Destruction of the druid sanctuary on the island Mona Insula by Cult of Mercury and Romans, led by Governor Suetonius Paulinus. This was to break the rebellion of Queen Boudicca, the Iceni warrior queen. Most druids are put to the sword, and the remaining scatter.

*The Cult prepared this for over a century. Mona Insula, later called Anglesey, was later the site of the Diedne covenant now called Nameless Covenant.*

64 Rhine: Establishment of a Mercurian temple at the later site of Durenmar in the Black Forest. The priests of Mercury attempt to usurp the primeval power of the Hercynian Forest that covers most of Germany.

74 Rhine: The Black Forest Road is completed with the aid of Mercurian priests.

C. 85: The high priest of Apollo, Plutarch, predicts a coming cessation of oracles and the decline of the old gods.

C. 87: The Roman Empire completes the Stanegate (a Roman road) in Northumbria and thereby secures its conquest of Britain.

98: Christian missionaries vanish on mission through Ring of Fire. The Ring of Fire lies in the far south of Africa, separating the World from whatever lies south of it.

C. 100: Maria the Jewess, an alchemist and prophetess in Alexandria invents several kinds of alchemical apparatus, such as the tribikos, the kerotakis, and the bain-marie (named after her).

*The tribikos is a kind of alembic with three arms that is used to obtain substances purified by distillation. In her writings (quoted by Zosimos), Maria recommends that the copper or bronze used to make the tubes should be the thickness of a frying pan and that the joints between the tubes and the still-head should be sealed with flour paste.*

*The kerotakis is a device used to heat substances and to collect vapors. It is an airtight container with a sheet of copper upon its upper side. When working properly, all its joints form a tight vacuum. The use of such sealed containers in the Hermetic arts led to the term "hermetically sealed". The kerotakis is said to be a replication of the process of the formation of gold that is occurring in the bowels of the earth.*

*The bain-marie (Maria's bath), is a device which limits the maximum temperature of a container and its contents to the boiling point of a separate liquid: essentially a double boiler. It is extensively used in alchemical processes for which a gentle heat is necessary.*

C. 100–170: Lifetime of Claudius Ptolemaeus (Ptolemy), philosopher and astronomer of Alexandria. He builds upon the legacy of Hipparchus to compose the Almagest, a comprehensive model of the heavens rooted in geocentric harmony. His system of epicycles and deferents, while later challenged, becomes the paradigm of celestial motion throughout Europe. He invented the gnomon and the globe. 

For Hermetic magi, Ptolemy’s influence is vast. His Tetrabiblos codifies astrological correspondences, defining the movements of stars and planets as arcane connections to fate, temperament, and magic. The model he constructs becomes the basis for all Hermetic calculation of astrological modifiers - without it, no horoscope, electional casting, or Hermetic ritual would be possible.

Ptolemy published his techniques and around 8,000 coordinates in a work of seven volumes, called "The Geography".

*The last known complete version of this work was lost with the Great Library of Alexandria and House Mercere has offered 100 pawns of vis for a complete copy of The Geography. Many magi, suspecting that it contains the exact locations of hundreds of ancient magical sites, would refuse to sell, even at that price.* 

*Within House Bonisagus, Ptolemy is venerated as the mortal who best charted the interplay of the Divine and Magic Realms in the firmament. Astrological mystagogues claim his Tetrabiblos is not merely a book but a coded initiation text, and a rare complete copy inscribed with stellar inks is said to reside in the Libri Aetheris collection at Durenmar.*

*Some Criamon claim that Ptolemy grasped the secret of the perfect circle, and that his true gift was seeing time as a wheel, not a river. Thus, Ptolemy stands not only as a sage of stars, but a hidden geomancer of the heavens, his writings shaping the very casting parameters of Hermetic magic.*

108: The dragon Pan Caudarax departs Flanders for the "Ice Land." It leaves behind a fabled hoard, including the Rosewood Crown, an oracular device. It vows to return on the day of the Crescent Sun to defend its hoard against the Warlock of De Panne.

*The dragon’s name may be a scribal error.*

129–216: Lifetime of Galen of Pergamon, the legendary physician and magician. 

*The grandmaster of anatomy, medicine and Corpus. As vivisections on humans were strictly prohibited at the time, Galen would encourage his students to go look at dead gladiators or bodies that washed up in order to get better acquainted with the human body.*

154–222: Lifetime of Bar Daysan, a Syrian magician possibly ancestral to Founder Criamon.

*Bar Daysan's heretical cosmology and commentary on it only survives in later sources (at least outside the House of Criamon), but can be outlined as follows:*

*The world began with the four pure and uncreated elements of light, wind, fire, water, respectively located in East, West, South, North (and are each able to move throughout their own, individual regions).
