Above the plane on which these four pure elements rest is the Lord, and below is the darkness. At one time and by chance, the four pure elements exceeded their boundaries and began to mix. Taking up the opportune chance, darkness also mixed with them. Distressed, the elements appeal to God to separate the darkness from them, but God is only partially successful in this procedure. The Lord uses the mix to create the world, but the remaining darkness in the mix acts as the cause of evil in the world since then and until today. The world is allotted a period of 6,000 years to exist. Purifications through conception and birth take place, but at the end of the allotted period for the Earth, a definitive purification will take place that will expunge darkness from the world.*

165–180: After the Roman army violates a temple in the city of Seleucia on the Tigris (Mesopotamia), the Antonine Plague they bring back literally decimates the Roman Empire.

*A powerful curse indeed. Between 5-10 million died of the Roman Empire’s c. 75 million population. Thereafter, population declined.*

C. 175 Stonehenge: Lucius, king of the Britons has heard of the Christian faith and asks the Pope Eleutherius for missionaries to be sent. The pope happily agrees and sends Faganus and Duvianus, who convert the whole country within a few years, establishing archbishops at London, York and Caerleon-on-Usk.

*To the definite ire of the remaining druids. Another long-term plot by the Cult?*

C. 204–270: Plotinus, founder of Neoplatonism, teaches that all existence flows from The One, an ineffable source beyond Being itself. From this emanates the Nous (Divine Mind) and the World Soul, which in turn shapes the material world. His vision of reality becomes foundational to mystical theology and magical metaphysics. Plotinus’ idea of emanation explains the relationship between the Magic Realm, Forms, and the physical world. His notion that the soul can ascend toward unity with The One through contemplation and purification resonates deeply with later seekers of Twilight transcendence and Mystery Initiations.

*Some Criamon magi believe Plotinus glimpsed the Enigma, while Jerbiton magi admire his effort to bridge philosophy, aesthetics, and metaphysical yearning. His disciple Porphyry recorded his teachings, which may remain potent sources of insight for magical theorists seeking to integrate Divine and Magical understanding.*

C. 220 Hibernia: Reign of Cormac mac Airt. Protected since birth by five magic wards, Cormac could not be hurt by wounds, drowning, fire, druid magic, or wolves. He became the high-king at the age of 30, and during his reign Ireland’s rivers were full of fish, the fields full of bees making honey, and the forests ripe with fruit.

249–262: Another great plague, even worse than the last, severely reduces the Roman Empire, and the structural integrity of the imperial machine begins to burst apart. 

*The plague was rumored to be a curse from Aethiopia. At its height, 5,000 people a day were dying in Rome. The population of Alexandria dropped from 500,000 to less than 200,000.*

C. 250: Alexandrine philosopher Plotinus founds Neoplatonism, argues that magic is the basis of all action and change, states certain basic rules of sympathy and essential nature, and further develops the concept of the Chain of Being. His followers, particularly Porphyry and Iamblichus, develop these rules into powerful rituals and divide magic into Theurgy, higher magic based on invocation of divine power, and Goetia, baser magic using compelled spirits. 

C. 260 Hibernia: Era of Finn Mac Cool and his Fenian band, who patrolled their lord's lands against robbers and invaders. They eventually become overbearing and are suppressed.

270 Rome: The son of a priestess of Deus Sol Invictus (the unconquered sun), becomes the new Emperor Aurelian. He wants to unify the Empire and to that end he again proclaims Deus Sol Invictus as the official deity of the Roman State. He declares that Sol was the sum of the attributes of all other gods, and thus is to be worshiped alone. The emperor institutes new rites, designed to unite all the disparate practices of the different priesthoods into one common prayer ceremony, builds them one of the most splendid temples in Rome,

and establishes a dedicated college to ensure its continuation.

*This must have been hard to swallow for the Cult of Mercury.*

C. 270–304: Cyprian of Antioch received a liberal education in his youth, and particularly applied himself to astrology; after which he traveled for improvement through Greece, Egypt, India, etc. He was renowned as a powerful thaumaturgist, whose magical wonders brought him a great reputation. In the same city lived a beautiful young lady named Justina, who had converted from paganism to worship Christ. A rich young man named Aglaias wooed her, but she rebuffed him. After a forceful abduction failed, he went to Cyprian for help. Cyprian consented for a price, and employed all his magical arts against the girl, who in chastity and purity spent her time in prayer and fasting, but his demons fled from her in terror and his spells failed. Cyprian again employed sorcery, sending "the chief of demons" to Justina, who likewise was conquered by a certain "Sign of Christ". Brought to despair, Cyprian made the Sign himself and in this way was freed from the toils of Satan. Thus he realized his sin. He went to the bishop, repented, burned his books on pagan magic, and was made a priest of God. Justina became a deaconess, and they had many adventures before suffering for their faith as holy martyrs in 304.

*During the persecutions of Diocletian, Cyprian and Justina were seized upon as Christians, the former was torn with pincers, and the latter chastised; and, after suffering other torments, both were beheaded.*

*St. Cyprian and St. Justina are the patron saints of magicians who repent, and who turn their knowledge to the support of the Divine. The Holy Society has a small but pious following who use prayer and miracles to fight the enemies of God.*

C. 270 Normandy: Three Christian missionaries are beheaded on the road to the Temple of Mercury, in the city of Lutetia, now called Paris. The decapitated Bishop Denis picked up his head and walked several miles while preaching a sermon on repentance.

*The site where he stopped preaching and actually died was marked by a small shrine that is now the Basilica of Saint-Denis, the burial place for the kings of France.*

C. 280: Saint George of Cappadocia slays a dragon with his lance Ascalon in Silene, to destroy the local custom of feeding it maidens. The king was so grateful that he offered him treasures as a reward for saving his daughter's life, but George refused it and instead he gave these to the poor. The people of the city were so amazed at what they had witnessed that they became Christians and were all baptized.

284: Administration of Roman Empire temporarily split between East and West.

287: The provost Dacian first tortures, then beheads Saint George. As the provost returns homeward, fire falls from heaven, consuming him and all his servants.

*Near a town called Ramys, is a chapel now desolate and in ruins where the body of Saint George, but not the head, lies. His tomb is a hole, allowing a man to put his hand inside it. When a mad person is brought there, and if he puts his head into the hole, he shall immediately be made perfectly whole, and again have his understanding.*

C. 300: The Greek-Egyptian Gnostic Mystic Zosimos of Panopolis, and his sister Eusebeia, both magicians, write a twenty-eight volume encyclopedia of magic and alchemy - the "Cheirokmeta" (Greek: ‘Things made by hand’). He defined alchemy as the study of ‘the composition of waters, movement, growth, embodying and disembodying, drawing the spirits from bodies and bonding the spirits within bodies.’ Unfortunately this oldest known work of alchemy, said to contain secrets of true transmutation, is now largely lost. \
*The great work may yet be recovered from some dusty old vault? \
Zosimos wrote that there are two sciences and two wisdoms, that of the Egyptians and that of the Hebrews, which latter is confirmed by divine justice. The science and wisdom of the most excellent dominate the one and the other. Both originate in olden times. Their origin is without a king, autonomous and immaterial; it is not concerned with material and corruptible bodies, it operates, without submitting to strange influences, supported by prayer and divine grace.*

*The symbol of alchemy is drawn from the creation by its adepts, who cleanse and save the divine soul bound in the elements, and who free the divine spirit from its mixture with the flesh.*

*As the sun is, so to speak, a flower of the fire and simultaneously the heavenly sun, the right eye of the world, so copper when it blooms - that is when it takes the color of gold, through purification - becomes a terrestrial sun, which is king of the Earth, as the sun is king of heaven.*

303: Emperor Diocletian begins suppressing Christians and reviving the old religion. This continues until the conversion of Constantine in 312.

305 Rome: Milan becomes capital of Western Roman Empire.

306 Iberia: Synod of Elvira forbids communion be given to sorcerers who have killed others via magic, which it claims can only be performed through use of evil spirits.

*This century sees wide condemnation of magic by the Church, most of it aimed at those who use magic for immoral purposes.*

309: Another great plague reduces the Roman Empire over the next five years. 

313: Christianity recognized as the official religion of the Roman Empire by Emperor Constantine.

*This was an easy transition, since preparations for monotheism had already been laid in place by the sun worshippers, and over time the cult began to more closely resemble Christianity, while Christianity changed in many ways to comfort its newly converted Romans. Churches were built facing east, the Lord’s day became Sunday, God was often referred to as Sol Iustitiae (the Sun of Justice), and the celebration of the birth of Christ was changed from Epiphany (January 6) to the Natalis Invicti, the day honoring the sun god’s birth (December 25).*

314: Synod of Ancyra orders five years of penance for those who use magic for divination or healing.

*The objection to healing is surprising.*

C. 325 Levant: The bones of the Three Magi are recovered from the Levant, and brought to Milan as holy relics of the Church.

330 Thebes: Capital of the Roman Empire moved to Constantinople by Emperor Constantine.

356: Emperor Constantine II closes all non-Christian temples.

356 Normandy: Barbarian Alamanni invades Gaul.

357: Emperor Constantine II forbids magic of all kinds, under penalty of beheading.

360: The Huns invade Europe.

360: Picts cross Hadrian’s Wall and invade Britannia.

C. 370 Normandy: King Gradlon of Brittany builds the city of Ys in the Bay of Douarnenez for his daughter, Dahut. Ys is built below sea level, behind a great dike, with sea locks to allow ships access to the port. Ys becomes a powerful capital in a short time. Princess Dahut, a dissolute woman, takes a new lover each night and strangles him each morning. One day, a Red Knight comes to Ys, and Dahut falls in love with him. At his instigation, she steals the king's key to the sea locks. The sea locks were opened, and the city submerged, and only King Gradlon escaped.

*Dahut may have been a druidess or sorceress. She seems to have owned a magical black mask her lovers were required to wear. It strangled them when she was done with their services. The Red Knight sounds like a devil, or possibly a faerie. The bells of Ys are still said to ring on stormy nights.*

391: Much of the Library of Alexandria burned by order of Emperor Theodosius, who wishes non-Christian works destroyed. Many of the great Alexandrine philosophers are persecuted, some killed.

*Also see 642.*

395: Final split of Roman Empire between East and West. Emperor Arcadius rules in the East from Constantinople, while Emperor Honorius rules the West from Rome.

C. 400–500 Transylvania: Sometime in this period, a Balkan pagan named Muj undergoes a series of adventures and becomes a sorcerer. His line is eventually adopted by Founder Tremere.

C. 400: St. Augustine of Hippo introduces many higher philosophies to the Church, including the idea that the Church exists on a privileged level, removed from the cares of the world. Augustine also argues that the old gods are utterly false, and that all spirits are evil demons, and that magic is based wholly on demonic aid. His opinions on magic influence the Church still.

Augustine’s principle of elevation is also current among some magi of the Order, who argue that the Order is above petty mundane concerns. This theory was influenced by the immunity of the Church in Rome to sacking by the invading barbarians. Rome is later viciously and repeatedly sacked, and the churches not spared.

400–410: Visigoths invade Italy and sack Rome. Afterward they remove to Gaul, and eventually come to rule Hispania. They bring with them their magicians, members of the tribe and advisors to their kings. These magicians are said to be goetic summoners and enchanters similar to the Odin-followers of Ultima Thule. Their tradition dwindles in the coming centuries. 

It is not certain if the magicians of the Visigoths survived the conquest by the Moors. They may have contributed to the magical line of Flambeau, through Delendar.

C. 400: Some Seekers claim that the Cult of Mercury fights a great battle against a dangerous and destructive old god, losing the best magi of the Cult, leading to its dissolution.

*A Sardinian mage of House Merinita claims that the Cult of Mercury could see the coming future and many fled into Faerie to await a new age.*

*Terith of Jerbiton claims that the Cult of Mercury was corrupted by demons and the Cult had become diabolists, forgetting their old ways and ultimately disbanding.*

410: The Visigoths under Alaric I sack Rome after a third siege. Slaves open the Salarian Gate and Goths loot the city for three days. Comparatively few Roman men are killed and women raped. Only two churches are burned, and people who took refuge in churches are usually spared. Many Romans who survive the Sack flee to Africa, or to the Eastern Empire. It is the first time since 390 BC that Rome has fallen to an enemy.

*The city which had taken the whole world was itself taken.*

412: Cult of Mercury is officially disbanded, over one hundred years after it became ineffective. The remaining Roman magi move out of the dangerous cities and gradually disperse into the countryside.

*The Cult’s reliance on rituals requiring many priests to perform may have been their undoing as it requires large amounts of vis, as well as coherence and organization, things the Empire can no longer provide.*

C. 415 Rhine: Siegfried, a knight from the city of Xanten on the lower Rhine, hears of the great beauty of Kriemhild - the most beautiful woman in the world, sister of the Burgundian king, Gunther, and decides to woo her.

Siegfried firstly won a great treasure from the Nibelungs (two princes and brothers named Schilbung and Nibelung), by slaying them. After taking the Tarnkappe (a cloak of darkness) from Alberich, the dwarven treasurer of the Nibelungs, he rose to become ruler of Nibelungland. 

Siegfried killed the dragon Fafnir in Westerwald and bathed in its blood, after which his body became invulnerable. (He had only one vulnerable spot, between his shoulder blades, where a large leaf had rested on his skin as he was soaked in the dragon’s blood.)

King Gunther allows Siegfried to marry Kriemhild, on the condition that he helps him to gain the hand of Brünhild, the legendarily strong queen of Iceland. Siegfried does this by cheating and deceiving Brünhild, and also stealing her ring and girdle - gifting them to Kriemhild.

*Brünhild’s humiliation lingers and many years later it all ends in tragedy with the death of all the leading participants. The treasure of the Nibelungs remains lost, the hoard stolen and supposedly sunk in the Rhine by the traitor Hagen.*

429: Vandals invade Africa.

C. 430–450 Hibernia: Saint Patrick christens Ireland. He performs many miracles, banishes all snakes from the island, bests and exiles the druids after winning a magic contest, and uses a three-leaf clover to explain the Holy Trinity.

*St. Patrick achieved overwhelming success and forever cemented the Christian faith in people’s hearts. His mortal remains are interred in Down Cathedral.*

430–453 Rhine: The great knight Siegfried arrives in Worms, weds Kriemhild, and is later slain.

445: Attila murders his brother and becomes undisputed leader of the Huns.

450–458 Normandy: Reign of the sorcerer-king Merovech of the Franks, founder of the Merovingian dynasty. The early Merovingian kings were sorcerers whose magical powers were vested in their long hair, which they never cut. Merovech remains shrouded in mystery, but among his many feats, he helped to repel Attila the Hun at the Battle of Chalons. Ending Attila's westward conquests.

*Tracing his heritage to Duke Francio who fought alongside King Priam at Troy - Merovech was supposedly the offspring of the wife of King Clodio - descended from the Jewish House of David - and was already pregnant when she went swimming in the ocean and was seduced and impregnated a second time by the Quinotaur, the five-horned bull-beast of Neptune. Thus Merovech had two fathers, and his royal bloodline was mixed with magic.*

*All of the Merovingians bear a curious birthmark in the form of a red cross. Philip II, like many of the former kings of the Franks, has the Royal Touch, which cures the King’s Evil (scrofula). Many believe this is a Purifying Touch inherited from Merovech despite the fact that Philip’s blood links to Merovech are tenuous. Another popular theory is that the Royal Touch was a gift from Saint Rémi to Clovis and his descendents. It is a gift from God bestowed on all Frankish kings by the anointing with the holy oil.*

450: St. Geneviève prays for the Huns to avoid Paris, and they ignore the city.

451 Rhine: Attila conquers the Burgundians at Worms and weds the widowed

Kriemhild.

451: The Battle of Châlons: An alliance of Romans, Franks, and Visigoths led by the legendary Merovech defeat the Huns and Ostrogoths in Gaul, ending Attila’s westward conquests.

452: Huns invade Italy. Pope Leo miraculously saves the city of Rome from the army of the Huns.

452: Venice founded.

453 The Burgundians war on the Huns and are destroyed; Attila the Hun is

killed.

455: The Vandals sack Rome.

459: Cologne falls to the Ripuarian Franks, ending its time as a Roman city.

C. 465–525?: Career of Merlin, greatest magician of Britannia. Son of a faerie and a mortal woman, born able to speak, he first appears as a boy to King Vortigern of the Saxons, issues prophecies, and resolves a problem vexing the king. Later Merlin makes great wonders, transports the Stonehenge to England, and arranges the getting of Arthur by Uther Pendragon upon Igraine of Cornwall. He could change shape and made prophecies still studied today. Toward the end of his life he advises Arthur in the early part of his reign.

*The final fate of Merlin is disputed. Some say he was betrayed and imprisoned by his apprentice, Nimue, Others claim he retired to the lands of Faerie or into a regio. Some in the Church claim he was the son of the Devil.*

*Talk about intervening in mundane affairs!*

470–485 Successful campaigns of the Visigoth king Euric across western Europe.

476: Fall of the Roman Empire in the West. The Gothic conquest of Italy is finalized by the deposition of Emperor Romulus Augustus by the Goth king Odovacar. The Imperial robes and regalia are transferred to Constantinople.

*The beginning of the Dark Age.*


## The Dark Age of Magic (AD 476–731)

*With the fall of Rome, the once-great magical traditions of the Cult of Mercury collapse, leaving Europe in chaos and darkness. The structured rites and grand rituals of old are forgotten, their secrets buried beneath the ruins of the Empire. In their place, magic becomes scattered, localized, and feared, as hedge wizards, cunning folk, and lone practitioners struggle to survive in a world increasingly hostile to the arcane.*

*The rising power of the Christian Church brands many forms of magic as heresy or diabolism, forcing magi into secrecy. Druids, Norse seidhr-workers, and Romano-Celtic sorcerers cling to their traditions in isolated pockets, but their numbers dwindle. Meanwhile, the Eastern Roman (Byzantine) Empire preserves fragments of Hermetic wisdom, though its scholars focus more on Divine and alchemical studies than practical spellcraft.*

*The once-mighty magical centers of Alexandria, Athens, and Ravenna fade, their libraries lost to war, fire, and ignorance. The few remaining magi rely on whispered lore, stolen texts, and personal experimentation, but without a unified magical theory, they struggle to advance. Rivalries and suspicion deepen as the effects of The Gift drive practitioners apart, ensuring that magic remains fragmented.*

*Yet in the shadows, seeds of rebirth take root. Mystical lineages pass down ancient knowledge, safeguarding spells and techniques for a time when magic might rise again. In the east, the Arabs and Persians refine astrology and alchemy, keeping old wisdom alive. In the deep forests, mountain caves, and forgotten ruins of Europe, strange magics still stir waiting for a new age of enlightenment.*

*The 6th century see a brief flowering of magic and heroic effort on the fringe of Europe. The Enchantment of Britain and the Court of King Arthur brings a temporary thaw in the Winter of magic.*

*In the early 8th century, a scholar named Bonisagus discovers Mercurian rituals in an abandoned well and begins work on a new magical theory, one that will unite the scattered magi of Mythic Europe and bring an end to the Dark Age of Magic.*

481–511 Normandy: Reign of Clovis, a descendant of Merovech. He fought and defeated the Roman Governor Syagrius in battle in 486 and, having made a peace treaty with the Ostrogoths, came to rule a great empire, freed from Roman rule. Clovis extended his kingdom by war, treachery, and marriage. His wife, Clotilde of Burgundy, was a Christian and changed the course of Clovis’s life. His first son died and the second fell gravely ill as a baby, but Clovis saw his wife’s prayers to her God cure the boy. He was further swayed when, at the tomb of Saint Martin of Tours, he was witness to a miracle when the bishop of Arras, Vedast, gave a blind man back his sight. In 496, when facing the Alemanni in battle, Clovis vowed that he would convert from his worship of Mercury and Jupiter if the victory was his. He defeated them soundly and was baptized by Saint Rémi, archbishop of Reims.

*The Merovingian sorcerer-kings, with the blood of David and of the old gods, were blessed with kingly grace. Provided that they did not cut their hair, they were invincible in battle; they could heal by laying on of hands; they could make crops grow by walking across fields; they had divinatory powers and could interpret the calls of beasts. It is told that Clovis revealed the Holy Grail, which had been brought to Gaul by Mary Magdalene in the 1st century, to the Frankish kingdom. *

*Following the death of Clovis, the Frankish lands were divided among his four sons according to the "Salic law" Clovis had established. This inaugurated a tradition that would lead to disunity and frequent civil wars lasting until the end of the Merovingian dynasty.*

496 Normandy: Clovis is baptized and becomes the first Christian Frankish king. His people follow suit.

*Although paganism takes centuries to fade among the Franks. The Merovingian kings still kept their magical hair long, but now claiming their powers were descended from Christ.*

507 Normandy: Clovis has by now defeated the Romans, the Burgundians, and the Visigoths, united the Franks, and established a capital in Paris.

C. 510–575 Stonehenge: Magical reign of King Arthur of the Britons under the Enchantment of Britain. This period sees a flowering of magical achievement, the crowning glory of Merlin, the wide influence of the magical Ladies of the Lakes, and many magical quests.

*The Reign of Arthur is a shining example of how mortals and magi can coexist.*

C. 520–575 Ultima Thule: Era of Beowulf, who - enchanted with thirty men’s strength, in King Hrothgar’s hall of Heorot - slays Grendel by ripping off its arm. Then he fights the ogre's mother who dwells at the bottom of a lake. He first uses Hrunting, his magical sword but Grendel's mother is immune to its power, so Beowulf kills her with a sword from her armory. Rewarded with gifts from the Danish King, Beowulf becomes King of the Geats.

Fifty years into his reign, a peasant carrying a golden object from a cave accidentally awakens a dragon, which begins to destroy everything in its path. Accompanied by his young relative, Wiglaf, the only one of his warriors brave enough to stand by his side, Beowulf decides to take up his sword and shield for the last time and defeat the Dragon. Unfortunately, the aged King is injured and dies due to wounds received in the fight. After his death, he is ritually burned on a great pyre while his people wail and mourn him. Afterward, a barrow, visible from the sea, is built in his memory.

*The question still remains: what is its location? There are only a few possible locations in Scandinavia for this barrow.*

*Beowulf is said to have gottens his arms and armor from the master blacksmith Wayland, apprentice to Mimir.*

535–540 Rome: Byzantine conquest of Italy from the Ostrogoths. Byzantine emperor Justinian I’s ambition to restore the Roman Empire to its former glory goes well (for the first part). General Belisarius sacks Naples, recaptures Rome in 536, defends a long siege and then captures the Ostrogothic capital Ravenna in 540 thereby reclaiming the Empire.

541–549: The terrible Plague of Justinian kills half the world and leaves desolation in its wake.

*Another powerful curse from the outside, or as part of the fighting? According to Procopius it came to Constantinople by ship from Egypt and started killing 10,000 people in the city daily.*

*However, this point may have been a cover-up as in his secret Anecdota - uncovered much later - he describes acts of potential diabolism by Emperor Justinian, his wife Theodora, the general Belisarius, and his wife Antonina:*

*"And some of those who have been with Justinian at the palace late at night, men who were pure of spirit, have thought they saw a strange demoniac form taking his place. One man said that the Emperor suddenly rose from his throne and walked about, and indeed he was never wont to remain sitting for long, and immediately Justinian's head vanished, while the rest of his body seemed to ebb and flow; whereat the beholder stood aghast and fearful, wondering if his eyes were deceiving him. But presently he perceived the vanished head filling out and joining the body again as strangely as it had left it."*

*The Empress Theodora - as well as Antonina - of the secret Anecdotes are garish portraits of vulgarity and insatiable lust juxtaposed with cold-blooded self-interest, shrewishness, and envious and fearful mean-spiritedness.*

541–554 Rome: While general Belisarius is recalled to the East in the face of renewed hostilities by the Persians, the situation in Italy takes a turn for the worse. The city of Rome changes hands three more times; first taken and depopulated by the Ostrogoths in December 546, then reconquered by the Byzantines in 547, and then again by the Goths in January 550. Finally the resistance of the Ostrogoths is broken in 554 and a Frankish invasion is defeated.

*Justinian managed to recover Italy at an exorbitant cost, estimated at 300,000 pounds of gold. Procopius estimated that 15,000,000 Goths died. How many of those actually died in fighting, as opposed to by plague or pogrom is not told.*

C. 555 Stonehenge: Arthur’s kingdom is blighted, a terrible curse eventually lifted by the successful quest for the Holy Grail.

C. 560 Stonehenge: The Kingdom of Lyonesse, formerly a land or island of Britain off Cornwall, sinks into the sea after the death of its last heir, Tristram, after his treacherous murder at the hands of King Mark. Some claim the Scilly Isles are the mountaintops of this drowned land. 

*This whole quarter of the world seems to be sinking into the sea, if one includes Atlantis, Cimbrinsula, and the many Western Isles said to lie under the waves!*

568: Lombard conquest of Italy.

C. 570 Levant: Birth of Mohammed.

590: Pope Gregorius I establishes an imperial rule in Rome, repairs public works, and feeds the masses with doles of grain. His authority is strengthened after a plague ends after he has had a vision of the Destroying Angel sheathing its sword.

594: St. Gregory of Tours writes the Historia Francorum (History of the Franks).

610: Laylat al-Qadr. An Arab merchant, Mohammed, receives his first revelation and begins to preach a new religion, Islam, in the city of Mecca.

615 Thebes: Earliest written record of magicians at the Mount Minthe site of what will become the covenant of Hedyosmos.

*In antiquity, the site was a temple of Hades, the god of the Underworld. Indeed, it was one of the few cultic centers of this god, since the Eleans were the only men who were known to worship Hades.*

617 Thebes: The wizard Prokopios has a dream vision of Socrates, possibly while at the site of what will later become the covenant of Hedyosmos. He begins his plan to form the Theban League, an assembly of magicians.

622 Levant: Hijra. The prophet Mohammed is driven out of Mecca for promoting an alien god. He settles in Medina.

*Start of the Muslim calendar.*

624 Thebes: First meeting of the Theban League on Delos brings twenty-two magi together. Prokopios’ typikon is debated, but few are convinced.

629: Mohammed and his followers, the Muslims, begin a religious war that will eventually conquer the whole of the Near East and North Africa, and will reach deeply into Iberia.

*Before Islam most middle-eastern wizards either lived in isolated places as hermits, or occupied positions as advisors to tribal rulers. With the rise of Islam, many of these wizards converted. Most continue to serve their chiefs or the new Muslim governors of their home territories, but many join the Muslim armies as warrior mages. The precise size and quality of the magical contingents of Muslim armies vary, depending on the whims of the wizards who joined them, but it became normal for the caliph to be served by a small force of mages.*

630 Levant: Mohammed takes Mecca.

631 Thebes: Second meeting of the Theban League on Delos. Fifty five magi gather, and most agree to join the league and sign typikon.

632 Levant: Mohammed makes the hajj (greater pilgrimage) to Mecca, where his behavior defines the correct procedure for this ritual - that every Muslim must perform once during their life. Shortly after his return he falls ill and dies in June at the house of A’isha, his favorite wife. Abu Bakr is chosen to lead the Umma and takes the title of caliph (successor to the prophet).

634 Levant: Abu Bakr dies. ‘Umar becomes caliph.

636 Levant: Muslim conquest of Syria.

637–651 Levant: The Islamic Conquest of Persia,leading to the end of both Sasanid rule

and Zoroastrian dominance. Much slaughter of Mazdean priests and forced conversions.

638 Levant: Muslim conquest of Jerusalem.

638 Thebes: Third meeting of the Theban League, some of whose members demand protection from the Witches of Thessaly. 

C. 640 Thebes: The pre-Hermetic wizards of the Theban League resurrect the sanctuary of Epidauros.

*It has had a continuous presence of magi ever since. *

641–642 Levant: The Muslims complete their conquest of Egypt. In Alexandria they destroy its famous Library, burning 300,000 volumes to heat baths. This is the end of the Alexandrine center of learning. 

644 Levant: Death of ‘Umar. Uthman becomes caliph.

645 Thebes: Fourth meeting at Delos of the Theban League reports that the logothete was killed by the Witches of Thessaly. 

650 Levant: Six good years into his reign, Caliph Uthman loses the Prophet’s seal ring.

*This marks the turning point into six bad years of rebellion until he is finally assassinated in 656.*

652 Thebes: Fifth meeting Theban League. A group of Dacian necromancers raid deep into the Greek mainland, killing many magicians. Arguments over appropriate response lead to fighting between delegates, many killed. League in disarray.

654 Stonehenge: The last major heathen Anglo-Saxon ruler in England, Penda of Mercia, is killed by Oswiu of Northumbria.

656 Levant: Death of Uthman. ‘Ali becomes caliph.

659 Thebes: Prokopios slain by a trusted friend and archon at the sixth League gathering on Delos.

661 Levant: Death of ‘Ali. Mu’awiya becomes first caliph of Umayyad dynasty.

C. 668–709: Life of the great arabian alchemist Khalid ibn Yazid, second son of Umayyad caliph Yazid I. Among his great works are the Kitāb al-Usṭuqus (The Book of the Element), "al-Qawl al-mufīd fī al-ṣanʿa al-ilāhiyya" (The Instructive Word on the Divine Art), and the translated "Liber secretorum alchemiae" (The Book of the Secrets of Alchemy) and "Liber trium verborum" (The Book of the Three Words).

668 Africa: The last sorcerer-king of the Garamantian Kingdom is taken away in chains by Arab warriors.

*The last remnants of their culture dies out with the coming of Islam, as the Divine auras of worshiping slaves unravels their rituals and the sharp blades of pressing armies cause most of their corpse-warriors to disintegrate on the wind. Without the unliving legions to stop them, the slaves rose up against the Garamantian nobles, led by the soldier-slave caste. In acts of desperation and spite, the surviving lords commanded their few remaining corpse-miners to collapse the tunnels of their foggaras and deny their rebelling slaves the life-giving water that had been pulled from the earth for a thousand years. The remaining slaves and citizenry melted into the local nomadic tribes or emigrated, unable to sustain their kingdom. Without water, without leaders, without slaves or magics, the Garamantians faded away, their cities blanketed by dust and their monuments consumed by the desert. *

	

671 Thebes: The combustible called Greek Fire is used to defend Constantinople from Muslim conquest.

690: Bonisagus is born in Florence.

698 Iberia: Birth of Reculed Annaeus Seneca, to be Founder Flambeau, in Lusitania. This per Flambeau tradition. No documentary evidence.

705: Trianoma is born in Thessaly.

707: Muslims take North Africa.

711 Iberia: Moors invade Hispania. In time they bring the remaining learning of Rome to the cities of Hispania. 

712 Iberia: Reculed Annaeus Seneca apprenticed to magus Delendar. Delendar, a powerful mage skilled in destructive magic, intensely trains the boy.

C. 714 Iberia: An archbishop and six bishops flee the Moorish invasion by sailing west into the Atlantic Ocean. They land on the island of Antilia, and each founds a golden city, the seven cities of Cibola.

714: Guernicus is born.

C. 715: Flambeau is born to a family of petty nobles in Gascony (southwestern France)

*His family estate was caught in the path of the invading Moorish army of Emir abd Al Raman, which crossed the Pyrenees and advanced through France in 732.*

717: The Moors cross the Pyrenees into France.

717: Bonisagus discovers Mercurian rituals in an abandoned well.

717–718 Thebes: Constantinople is besieged by Arabs. During the siege the Emperor Theodosius is deposed and replaced by Leo III. Leo breaks the siege, destroys the Arab fleet, and ends further Muslim expansion into eastern Europe.

718: The English Benedictiner monk St. Boniface begins his missionary work throughout Germany.

719 Provencal: The Moors attack the Visigothic kingdom of Septimania and take the capital Narbonne before proceeding north to Toulouse.

*The Franks send no assistance.*

720: Bonisagus retreats to his cave in the Alps to develop the Parma Magica.

C. 720: Umayyad summoners and jinn at war.

*The downfall of the wazirs came from their callous dealings with the beings they*

*summoned. This may have prevented them from lending their support to the invading armies, and could be a factor that slowed and eventually halted their  expansion.*

721 Provencal: Due Odo, ruler of Gascony and Toulouse managed to defeat the Moor’s advance even without help from the Franks.

*This bought time for Charles Martel and the Franks to prepare, setting the scene for the great victory at Tours 732 and ending the Moors’ advance into Europe.*

721: Guernicus is adopted and apprenticed as a Mercurian earth wizard.

725 Rhine: St. Boniface miraculously fells the Donar Oak, a great pagan relic, in

Thuringia. The potency of pagan magics and relics begins to wane throughout the land, as does belief in the Old Ways. The power of the great Hercynian Forest is broken and divided among the numerous lesser and separate forests it has become.

*Or was it possibly by Charlemagne?*

729: Jerbiton the Founder was born in a secluded Alpine valley in 729, and was named Flavius. He was a cheerful, pleasant child who particularly enjoyed horse riding. Nearby objects changed color to match his mood.

*Jerbiton was the son of the ruler of his valley, so he was raised as a minor nobleman.*

729 Thebes: League of Iconophiles founded by Bernice. It is a loose alliance of magicians coming together to sabotage the iconoclastic imperial armies and rescue icons from the persecution of Emperor Leo III.

730 Iberia: Delendar and Reculed battle Moors and Moorish sorcerers in a battle in the Pyrenees foothills. Delendar is killed in a massive magical attack after being separated from Reculed. Reculed destroys the survivors in a rage, and crosses the mountains into the south of France. Here Reculed takes the name Flambeau.


## The Hermetic Ascendancy (AD 731–832)

*This century is the seed of the Order and the rebirth of magic. The majority of this century belonged to the pre-Founders: Guorna the Fetid, the Pater of two founders Tytalus and Tremere, as well as Pralix; Delendar the fire-magician, pater of Flambeau; the unknown forebears of Bjornaer and Diedne, the two barbarian magi who yet were inheritors of organized magical traditions. The final third of this century belongs to their heirs, the disparate schools brought together by the genius of Bonisagus, who likely only developed his Parma Magica to live in peace.*

*The Order might never have happened if not for the pivotal moment when two witches of Thessaly, the ambitious and politically astute Trianoma and the reclusive Viea, seek out the enigmatic scholar Bonisagus, who lives in seclusion deep in the Alps, working on his revolutionary magical theory. More than just a scholar, Bonisagus has also developed a new defense against hostile magic - the Parma Magica - a shield that not only protects against spells but also suppresses the instinctive distrust caused by the Gift, allowing Gifted individuals to cooperate without fear or paranoia. Viea soon departs, but Trianoma, recognizing the Parma Magica’s significance, becomes Bonisagus’ first apprentice. She learns the Parma, and with this powerful tool in hand, begins recruiting other magi, laying the groundwork for what will become the Order of Hermes.*

*For over three decades (732–767), Bonisagus’ alpine cave - and later, Durenmar, the Order’s first covenant - becomes the meeting place for Europe's greatest magical minds. Here, magi share their wisdom, contributing their own traditions to Bonisagus’ growing Hermetic Theory. Many disputes and rivalries are reconciled, as Trianoma persuades wizards of all traditions to embrace a common magical framework, mediated by the developing Code of Hermes.*

*By 767, the Order of Hermes is officially founded, with Twelve Houses, a set of laws, and Grand Tribunals held every 33 years. Bonisagus continues refining Hermetic Theory, though he is increasingly withdrawn from politics, focusing instead on pure magical research.*

*The founding magi brought their own strengths: *



* *Bonisagus obviously the Hermetic Theory and his greatest gift - the Parma Magica, making the Order possible.*
* *Trianoma, a divinatory and oracular witch of Thessaly, trained in prophecy and foresight, was the political architect of the Order.*
* *Guernicus codified the Peripheral Code, ensuring the Order governs itself through law, not war.*
* *Mercere established the Redcap network, ensuring reliable communication and coordination between covenants. And he created Hermetic travel magic, refining spells and enchanted items to aid long-distance movement.*
* *Tremere advocated for centralized control and internal discipline, which helped prevent early schisms in the Order. Though he did not create the Code, he worked tirelessly to shape the Order’s political and martial structure, ensuring it would function as a disciplined society rather than a chaotic cabal of wizards.*
* *Flambeau created Hermetic Battle Magic, developed Creo Ignem to its peak of destructive power, making fire a feared weapon and ensuring that the Order had a martial tradition capable of defending its members. *
* *Tytalus also saw the need for conflict and pushed for a militant stance, but unlike Flambeau he emphasized psychological and social manipulation, shaping House Tytalus into rivals, provocateurs, and master manipulators. During the founding of the Order, he disrupted debates, questioned every authority, and pushed others to prove themselves through struggle. His rivalry with his disciple-sibling Tremere was legendary - where Tremere sought command, Tytalus mocked the very idea of rulership, believing that leaders should be challenged constantly.*
* *Birna (Bjornaer) truly resisted integration, but agreed after Trianoma’s persuasion, she represented the wild, untamed magic of nature, proving that the Order could accommodate diverse non-Roman traditions.*
* *Merinita advocated for the protection of magical places, ensuring the Order preserved not just vis sources, but entire magical landscapes. She developed the first structured Hermetic techniques for understanding and manipulating regiones, which becomes crucial for covenants built in magical and faerie sites. *
* *Verditius provided the greatest system of enchanted item creation, shaping the Art of Magic Item Crafting into a precise science. *
* *Criamon introduced esoteric philosophy into the Order, ensuring that Hermetic magic could be more than just power, it could be enlightenment.*

*The Grand Tribunal of 832 marks the end of the Founders’ era. Bonisagus and Trianoma attend for the last time, their vision realized. The Order they built is no longer a fragile alliance of scholars but a powerful magical society. Yet, already, the seeds of ambition, intrigue, and conflict are taking root. The age of pioneers is over. The age of Hermetic politics and power struggles begins.*

**731: Trianoma and Viea meet Bonisagus, who at the time lived in seclusion in the Alps, working on his magnum opus, now known as the Hermetic Theory of Magic. He has also developed a new method of defending against magical attack, the Parma Magica. Viea soon departs. Trianoma becomes Bonisagus’ apprentice, learns the Parma Magica and begins approaching magi to come to his cave in the Alps to share magical secrets.**

*The history of House Bonisagus records that Trianoma found Bonisagus*

*by following the advice of the dragon Ladon of the Garden of the Hesperides. Others say she used magical coordinates from surviving fractions of Ptolemy’s lost great work "The Geography" to find him.*

**732–767: Many magi councils Bonisagus in his Alpine cave, and later at Durenmar. Many issues are reconciled, and there is further development of Hermetic theory and the Code of Hermes. Bonisagus bases his Hermetic Theory on the ancient wisdom of the classic philosophers, moderated by the practical advice of experienced magi. Many of the Roman magi continue their old Mercurial magic traditions, which gradually dwindle as the Order grows and Hermetic theory takes deeper root.**

*It seems likely that Hermetic theory continued to be developed for the remainder of Bonisagus’ life. Only Bonisagus is thought to have attended the whole of this period; the other Founders are believed to have come and gone.*

732: Bonisagus accepts Notatus as his second apprentice.

*There are some references that Notatus may have had an earlier parens, but all records of that seem to have been lost.*

732 (October 10) Provence: Flambeau fights in the Battle of Tours with the Frankish hero, mayor of the palace and power behind the throne, Charles Martel to repulse a Moorish army led by Abd er Rahman near Poitiers. This halts the expansion of the Moors into western Europe. Flambeau is shot with an arrow, his wound festers and he languishes with fever but he is saved by Laberius, a descendant of the Roman cult of Mithras, who recognized Flambeau’s magical potential and made him his apprentice.

*Before that battle the invaders cut a swath of destruction along their northward march. Moorish outriders attacked and burned the young Flambeau’s family estate, slaying his kinsmen. *

C. 733–?: Flambeau and his master Laberius support their mundane lord and enjoy great success raiding and skirmishing against the moors in the early battles of the Reconquista. Eventually, the moors assemble a group of five or six sahirs (jinii-summoning wizards) and lure them into an ambush. The young man who would become Flambeau managed to escape with his life, but his master, his mundane lord, and most of their knights and men-at-arms were massacred by the sahirs’ jinn. He swore to avenge his master, but could find no friendly magi to complete his apprenticeship. After five years in seclusion in a cave in the Pyrenees, he had perfected a single, powerful attack similar to Pilum of Fire. It was then that he adopted the nom de guerre Flambeau and returned to Iberia to avenge his master’s death. 

Flambeau managed to hunt down three of the sahirs who had slain Laberius,

but the others eluded him. After fighting a number of Infernal jinn, Flambeau became convinced that all sahirs were demon-summoners, and he attacked them whenever he found them. He had slain as many as a dozen Moorish sorcerers and become one of the most feared wizards in Europe by the time Trianoma approached him with an offer to join the Order. At some point in this period, Flambeau takes his first apprentice, Apromor.

*Flambeau was apparently impressed by the Parma Magica blocking his attack on Trianoma!*

736 Thebes: League of Iconophiles hold a meeting on Mount Athos. It is not a success, and from this point on they return to communicating by messenger.

737 Normandy: Charles Martel’s son, Pépin the Short (father of Charlemagne), becomes the first Carolingian king by completing the seizure of the throne from weak Merovingian heir Childeric III, whose hair was shaved off to strip him of his magical powers before he was forced into a monastery.

*Legends persist, however, of other Merovingian heirs who carry the royal blood and escaped Pépin’s usurpation of the throne.*

*Pépin made the Carolingians the ruling dynasty of the Franks and the foremost power of Europe. Known as a great conqueror, he was undefeated during his lifetime.*

*It is likely he had magical help to the throne, possibly from Flambeau, but there are no established records of this.*

C. 740: The League of Iconophiles founder Bernice travels to the Alps to explore Rhaeto-Roman culture and takes Jerbiton to Thessalonica where his apprenticeship begins.

*Bernice named her apprentice Flavius Ierbitonis, "the fair haired one of the town of yesterday."*

746: Guernicus’s master is murdered. He begins his quest for justice, which establishes his reputation.

746 Levant: Hundreds of Jinn lay siege to Damascus, demanding that the city turn over two wazirs who they knew hid within.

*After many such retributive acts, the population of summoners in the Mythic Middle East was dramatically reduced, and the term wazir ceased to be associated with wizards. Their ruthless tradition of summoning mostly died out everywhere but on the fringes of the empire.*

747: Trianoma meets Mercere. He is the first magus to accept her invitation to form an Order.

C. 747–767: The mysterious maga Merinita comes out of the wilderness, and convinces the sorceress Birna (Bjornaer) to abandon her tradition and join the new Order. Birna is condemned by her former fellows, including the sorceress Gyongy.

*The tradition that Bjornaer left still holds her House a grudge.*

C. 750 Iberia: The high point of Muslim learning in Iberia.

C. 750 Iberia: Founder Flambeau invents the predecessor to the spell "Pilum of Fire" in order to hunt down the Sahirs who murdered his master. He is visited by Trianoma, who convinces him to participate in the meetings at Durenmar.

753 Thebes: Imperial armies systematically sack iconophilic monasteries. The icons and relics it seized were burned or thrown into the sea. The League of Iconophiles could not ensure any site within the Empire would remain unmolested, so a coterie of young Iconophiles was dispatched to Jerbiton’s valley - eventually founding in 758 what is today the covenant of Valnastium in the Alps.

753: Guernicus entombs the wizard responsible for his master’s murder.

754 Rome: Frankish conquest of Italy.

**754: Bonisagus leaves his alpine cave and travels to Durenmar in the Black Forest and establishes a permanent base at the site of a ruined Mercurian temple, teaching his theories to the wizards that Trianoma sends him.**

754 St. Boniface is martyred by suddenly appearing Frisian bandits, while persuading his armed comrades to lay down their arms; he is buried in Fulda.

*Is this weird happening not too strange a coincidence?*

756 Iberia. After the Umayyad prince ʿAbd al-Raḥmān fled Baghdad, and with the help of members of his family based in the North of Africa, he gains enough support to take control of Córdoba. After several strategic maneuvers, both political and warlike, Abderraman declares independence from the ʿAbbāsid Empire.

757: Trianoma meets Guorna the Fetid who led a cult of necromancers around Naples. With great trepidation, she invited the hideous necromancer to Durenmar to study under Bonisagus, in order that her powerful magic could be integrated into Hermetic Theory. Unknown to Trianoma, Guorna’s three pupils - Tytalus, Tremere, and an as-yet untrained girl called Pralix - had recently fled across the Adriatic Sea to Dacia after discovering her horrific plot to transfer her

soul from her pus-filled, leprosy-riddled body into one of theirs.

*Perhaps the greatest necromancer who ever lived, and heir to the secrets of death and the dead! Some necromancers search for the skull of Guorna the*

*Fetid, hoping to learn the techniques of the archnecromantrix. Others fear she would find a way to claim the body of her pupil.*

C. 758–762: While Guorna studied with Bonisagus, Tytalus took charge; he was the eldest of the three, and had studied under the witch for nearly three decades. He ordered Tremere to find wizards to aid them, while he himself sought out magical assistance to oppose Guorna’s own magic. Her power derived from the gods of the dead (despite the secularization of their religion), so Tytalus sought out those spirits imprisoned in the Underworld by those gods, spirits filled with resentment, rebellion, hatred, and vast power - the titans. He forged numerous pacts with these fearful spirits, and, bolstered by the necromantic wizards recruited by Tremere, the two destroyed the remnants of Guorna’s tradition in Naples, and then waited for the witch herself to return from the Black Forest. The trap set by the two brothers was too well-planned for Guorna to survive, despite even her new Hermetic teaching. After the defeat of Guorna, Tytalus and Tremere (and Pralix) marched on Durenmar, intent on eliminating the wizards they considered her allies and co-conspirators. However, the diplomacy of Trianoma forestalled their wrath, and the brothers were convinced of their innocence; indeed, they were persuaded to accept tutelage under Bonisagus instead. Tytalus found the adoption of Hermetic magic very difficult because of his extensive training and substantial power in his native tradition, although he was able to learn the Parma Magica. Pralix became the joint apprentice of Tytalus and Bonisagus, and she received a full understanding of both sorcery and Hermetic magic from her two masters.

*Tytalus claimed to have inflicted the killing blow against his mater, ripping her heart from her pus-filled chest. Guorna, however, had her revenge: with her dying breath she cursed her matricidal pupil with her own affliction of leprosy.*

759 Thebes: Trianoma seeks out Bernice in Thessalonica, but is at first unable to convince the Iconophiles to join her new league: they believe it is too geographically diffuse to be effective. A lengthy period of negotiations ensue

*She is particularly fascinated by the young Jerbiton for reasons never made clear. *

760 Thebes: After lengthy discussions with Trianoma, Bernice of Thessalonica agrees to aid in Bonisagus’ plans, realizing that the Parma Magica may be the only way to overcome the problem of The Gift preventing magicians gathering together. She instructs her protégé, by then usually called Jerbiton, to deliver some of her books to Bonisagus and answer his questions.

*Jerbiton assisted Bonisagus in refining the Arts of Imaginem and Mentem, and helped perfect the system of vocal and gestured guides to spellcasting. He also convinced Bonisagus to name his fields of study the "Arts," and the capacity to use magic "The Gift." *

*Bernice of Thessalonica eventually accepts membership in the Order. *

762: Trianoma meets Guernicus.

763 Thebes: Bernice of Thessalonica dies before the first Tribunal can be held. Jerbiton takes her place. 

*The Iconophiles however, were still too busy countering imperial and Infernal forces to pay attention to the musings of a dozen hedge wizards in the Black Forest. Jerbiton, and the other magi who were members of his household, participated in the Order only as a secondary concern. The Order was an interesting idea, and the Parma Magica was useful, but the future of civilization was being fought for in Constantinople, and none of Jerbiton’s new "sodales" seemed interested.*

**767 February 21, III Pisces: After years of negotiations, threats, and recriminations, Trianoma finally managed to get twelve of the most powerful wizards to agree on a set of nine precepts, which she called the Oath of Hermes. The Twelve Founders gather at Durenmar and form the Order of Hermes during the meeting of the First Tribunal. Bonisagus is the first to swear the Oath, followed by the Founders: Trianoma, Birna (Bjornaer), Criamon, Diedne, Flambeau, Jerbiton, Mercere, Merinita, Guernicus, Tremere, Tytalus, Verditius.**

*The mundane and Hermetic date disagree in the histories, for unknown reasons. Tytalus became a proponent of a democratic structure for the Order, and much of the early Code of Hermes was framed as a result of public debates between Guernicus and Tytalus.*

*Many assumed that Tremere, the youngest and possibly weakest Founder, would join House Tytalus. However, he found allies among the other Founders and he brought another powerful necromantic remnant group from Dacia to the Tribunal. Tytalus was unwilling to battle Tremere and his followers.*

*The 3rd day of Pisces (February 21, Julian Calendar) falls under the dominion of Jupiter, the great benefic, yet also carries the currents of intuition, dreams, and secret wisdom, favoring those who seek truth beyond the material world.*

*"As the Fishes swim between worlds, the veil is thin, and those who walk between the seen and unseen may claim their birthright."*

*What this day signifies:*



* *A Day of Mysticism & Secrets – The veil between worlds thins, and those who seek prophecy, divination, or hidden knowledge may find answers beyond reason. *
* *Fortune in Matters of the Soul – Acts of charity, compassion, and spiritual devotion bear fruit, as Jupiter’s influence magnifies the rewards of kindness.*
* *Sages, mystics, and those who walk between worlds are favored on this day. As are poets, musicians, and visionaries whose art flows like water; seafarers and wanderers, for fortune favors the drifting soul; healers, alchemists, and those who work with hidden forces.*
* *The Tides of Fate Move in Silence – It is an opportune time for whispered alliances, secret negotiations, and long-laid plans to take shape.*
* *But beware of Deception – As the waters of Pisces blur the edges of reality, those who deceive, or are deceived, will feel the weight of unseen consequences.*

**767 First Tribunal: Although magi counseling Founder Bonisagus had been present for decades, this is considered the first meeting of the Order. Naturally, there are more than the Twelve Founders attending the Tribunal, including some or all of the magi who had been adopted by the Founders. Among the first decisions in the Tribunal are the rules for Wizard’s War: The War must be declared on to the defending mage on the full moon prior to its beginning. The War begins on the night of a full moon and continues until the following full moon. Those who continue after this time are rogue and shall be Renounced.**

*Trianoma may have performed the practical duties of the Praeco. Several magi depart after the Founding and establish other covenants, based on the Durenmar model.*

*According to the original rules for Wizard’s War; those who continue in the conflict after the month shall be considered rogue, and "shall have a Wizard’s March declared against them". Better late than never?*

767–773: The magi of the nascent Order scatters outward from the central location of Durenmar. While Bonisagus, Merinita, Birna (Bjornaer) remains in or near the Rhine, Tytalus and his followers are found mainly in the northwest of Europe, Tremere removes himself to the far eastern reaches of the Frankish Empire - the Transylvanian Alps, Flambeau to the Pyrenees, Diedne to Gaul, Jerbiton and Criamon into the Alps; Mercere, Guernicus and Verditius to Italy and beyond. 

*Through aggressive recruitment, the Order grows quickly. Some of the Founders were intent on eliminating all magicians (competition) except for members of the Order. Facing such hostility, many druids joined House Diedne simply to avoid lethal hostilities.*

After 767: Founder Flambeau collects a school of apprentices in preparation for his long delayed campaign against the Moors, including Delendos and probably Abydaros. Founder Flambeau and his apprentices scour Europe for hedge magicians to battle. He also attracts some new followers, including maga Kypris of an Egyptian tradition, whose Lineage seeks the Phoenix, as she did.

*By this time Founder Flambeau’s first apprentice, Apromor, had matured into a full magus. He may have been a magus at the time of the Founding. Some accounts claim that Founder Flambeau took his first apprentices after the Foundation of the Order, others that he had already trained Apromor.*

*Traditionally, Apromor is an Archmage by the time the rules for Wizard’s War are developed. The order of events is variously reported.*

C. 767–770: Founder Flambeau, hunting hedge wizards, destroys a band of three such hiding in the catacombs under Rome. These wizards drew power from an artifact called Vardian’s Skull. Flambeau shatters the skull and slays the wizards. Later, these catacombs become the site of Vardian's Tomb covenant.

768–771 Normandy: After the death of King Pepin the Short, the Frankish realm was divided according to the Salic law between his two sons: Charlemagne and Carloman I. Carloman I suddenly and conveniently dies in 771 from a "severe nosebleed".

*At the time of his death, he and his brother Charlemagne were close to outright war, which Charlemagne's biographer Einhard attributes to the miscounsel of Carloman's advisors.*

768–814: Reign of Charlemagne (Latin: Carolus Magnus, German: Karl der Grosse). This great mortal king wisely ruled Normandy, Provence, northern Italy, the Rhine, the Alps, and northern Hispania from his palace at Aachen. He expanded the realm of civilization, improved education, dealt justly with mortals and magi, and was considered a friend of the Order. His court did not fear magi or magic, and did not disdain the use of enchanted devices. His twelve great paladins sought adventure across Europe and Africa. He was in time crowned Emperor of Rome, but all his works and wonders passed in two generations after his death. *Charlemagne’s court also clashed with many hedge magicians of the era, including the wizard Atlante.*

770 Rome: Harco, domus magna of House Mercere, is founded by Mercere in Piedmont, northern Italy - the center of Europe.

*He envisioned this covenant as a great crossroads, a trading center where magical and non-magical commerce and information could flourish under the influence of the new Order. *

C. 770–774: Founder Flambeau visits Durenmar with his train of apprentices. Inspired by the value of a stable base, he founds Val-Negra covenant in the mountains of the Pyrenees - officially in 774. Here he intensely trains his apprentices, pushing many into Gauntlets after only a few years.

*One of Founder Flambeau’s apprentices may have been Ignis Festi, who fought in the war against Damhan-Allaidh, but it is not certain.*

771 Rome: Charlemagne becomes King of the Lombards. He confirms Papal lands acquired in earlier decades, but asserts his sovereignty even in Papal lands.

771: Birna and her followers travel to the island of Rügen and hold a council. Thereafter House Bjornaer holds a gathering there every twelve years.

**772: Founders Tremere and Bonisagus develop Certamen. Both are apparently pleased to have developed a non-fatal method of dueling. The reactions of the other founders are unrecorded, but it was propagated with the help of Trianoma and Jerbiton. **

*This marks the start of Founder Tremere’s machinations to control the Order?*

772 Iberia: According to legend, two of Criamon's disciples settle in the cave that would end up being the covenant of Barzaj, firstly named as Indal Eccius.

**773 Second Tribunal of the Order of Hermes: The Rights of Founder Bonisagus ruled to extend to all members of his House, including the right to claim another mage’s apprentice, and the requirement that magi of that House must share their discoveries with the Order, and that magi must share their knowledge with members of Bonisagus, unless they can convince a Tribunal that the magical knowledge must remain secret.**

**Procedures for lesser Tribunals set: a Tribunal shall consist of at least twelve magi from at least four covenants.**

**Hereafter the term Tribunal comes to be applied to territories ruled by lesser Tribunals and the Tribunal of the Founders and Primi is called the Grand Tribunal. This early use of the term Covenant indicates its antiquity. The term is also used at the early point to refer to the home of Founder Bonisagus, Durenmar. There must have been at least three others already, only five years after the Founding. These early covenants were mostly of a single House.**

**Founding of the covenants of Crintera, domus magna of House Bjornaer, and Fenistal the temple of Diana, domus magna of House Merinita.**

**Tremere, the only Founder that is known to have been prosecuted for a violation of his Hermetic Oath, is declared innocent.**

*To the chagrin of french magi, it was also clarified that Brittany belongs to the Britannian Tribunal.*

*The provisions in the Code of Hermes referring to the Tribunal apply equally to any other Tribunal that has power over the magus and that is presided over by a Quaesitor. A Tribunal must include twelve or more magi from no less than four covenants. As the Order grew, a single Tribunal was soon no longer enough to effectively govern all magi.*

773 (winter): While working with Bonisagus in the lab, Founder Mercere loses his Gift.  Some say this was an accident, brought about by a flaw in the Parma Magica or his weak Gift, while others say Mercere gave it up voluntarily in pursuit of greater magical mysteries. He devotes himself to delivering messages in the Order. Unfortunately, Founder Mercere has trained only two apprentices and his magical secrets of transformation eventually fade from the traditions of his house. Founder Bonisagus spent many seasons attempting to recover Founder Mercere’s Gift, but no progress was recorded.

C. 774: Founder Tytalus binds his spirit familiar in the form of a huge black dog, who he claimed was son of Hekate and brother to Kerberos. He names his dog "Tremere".

*Out of humor or spite, or both? He devoted considerable effort to thwarting the plans of his "little brother". In his writings, Tytalus often refers to Tremere and his followers as "wolves," due to their self-adopted symbol, but always in a negative way, emphasizing their savage nature, unpleasant disposition, habit for feasting on the corpses of the slain, and inability to be domesticated.*

774 Charlemagne defeats Desiderius and becomes King of Lombardy. He confirms the "donation" of these lands to the pope, and becomes protector of the Church.

774 Rome: Pavia conquered by Charlemagne.

775 Rome: Verdi covenant founded by Founder Verditius in Sardinia.

775 Thebes: House Tremere invades the area that today comprises the Thebes Tribunal, slaying many wizards who had not joined the Order of Hermes and taking vis and magical items as spoils. The Byzantine magi in the northern part of the region form the Theban League to resist the Tremere, and join the Order of Hermes under Jerbiton’s leadership.

*Trained in the Parma Magica, these magi were able to reclaim territory from House Tremere.*

776 Thebes: The Founders Tytalus and Jerbiton work together against the Tremere influenced rise of Bulgaria.

776 Rome: Harco covenant founded by Founder Mercere in the foothills of the southern Alps. This likely marked a reduction in Founder Bonisagus’ attempts to recover Mercere’s Gift.

778 Iberia: Battle of Rencesvals. King Charlemagne has conquered most of northern Hispania in a long campaign, except for Saragossa. He is approached by the Moorish king of Saragossa, Marsile, and convinced of Marsile’s good intention to surrender his kingdom and become Christian agrees to return with Marsile to France for the Moor king’s baptism. The Moors plot with a French traitor, Ganelon, to fall on Charlemagne’s rearguard, destroy it, and kill the commander, Roland, one of the French paladins. The rearguard is destroyed after a bloody battle in which Roland refuses to sound his horn to recall the main army until it is too late. Charlemagne returns on hearing Roland’s horn and slaughters the Moors. Afterward he founds the kingdom of Aquitaine.

778 Iberia: Founder Flambeau repeatedly clashes with Founder Guernicus and other Quaesitores over his interference in the wars between mortal Christians and Muslims. He is prevented from campaigning by a Quaesitorial visit, contributing to the final failure of a new thrust into Spain by King Charlemagne.

778 Hibernia: As the first from the Order, two Tytalus and two Merinita magi visit the island, looking for the Tuatha Dé’s four legendary treasures. Assisted by a local ward-maker, the four discover the cauldron of the Dagda buried in one of the mounds at the Brú na Bóinne. Their grave robbing is noticed by the high-king, who sends his druid and a retaliatory warband to confront the thieves. The magi find a defensible barrow and the ward-maker protects it with spells. They repel the first attack, but the druid screams that the king’s men will attack again in the morning. The leading Tytalus answers: "We will defend this mound for a year if need be!" The five defend against the morning’s attack, and three days and two more failed attempts later the king’s men retreat.

*Adhering to the original boast, the magi stayed on the mound for a year.*

Deciding to remain in Ireland, the five relocate to Munster’s western shore, again saying that they will defend their location for a year to prove their worth.

*Named after the protecting wards drawn by the local assistant, the covenant of Circulus Ruber is ratified two years later, at the Tribunal of 780.*

C. 780 Normandy: Carolinus, vulgar name Charles de Foix, the mage-smith of Charlemagne, trains apprentices who are adopted by Founder Jerbiton. Carolinus worked many fine magical works, possibly including the sword of Roland, Durendal, and the magic mirror of Galienne.

*The mage-smiths continue to flourish in House Jerbiton still today?*

*Other sources attribute the mighty sword Durendal to the legendary smith Wayland.*

780: First regional Tribunal meetings. Unfortunately, records are sparse.

780 Iberia: The Iberian magus Ranemiro ex Guernicus and Tancoystl ex Diedne convince the Iberian magi to initiate a truce with the peninsular magical traditions, with the notable exception of the Sahirs, until 792. The covenants of Lucis, Duresca, Brigantia and Salionca are founded.

C. 780 Iberia. Grimoald Niger, well known among Flambeau's followers, disappears. His exploits battling peninsular sorcerers are recorded in *Comentarii de bello Iberico*.

780 Rhine Tribunal (1st): Covenant Arae Flaviae founded in Swabia. Covenant Rethra founded in Pomerania.

782: King Charlemagne orders the organization of schools in his kingdom, and generally promotes literacy and education.

*Sadly, the quality of mortal education has been very inconsistent over the years.*

782 Iberia: The truce terms between the Order, Galician Tempestiarii and Asturian Vates are defined.

784 Iberia: The Asturian Vates join the Order, under the protection of House Diedne.

788 Iberia: Death of Founder Flambeau and several of his filii at the Battle of the Tower in Iberia; magus Delendos is the sole survivor. He reports that Founder Flambeau struck down many spirits fighting for the Moorish sorcerers and finally struck down the whole of the enemy with a final mighty spell powered by a massive amount of vis, the first fons et origo.

Magus Abydaros filius Flambeau was not at this battle. He went on to study the less destructive aspects of Ignem. Abydaros vanished into far Ethiopia some time after his pater’s passing.

*Of course, we have only one witness to this battle, and he is unlikely to be all-informed…*

789: The Founder Jerbiton meets with Charlemagne, after having adopted a

number of apprentices of the emperor’s mage-smith, Carolinus. Charlemagne issues a decree outlawing sorcery and divination, punishable by slavery; and paganism and diabolism, punishable by death. "Benevolent magic" such as that practiced by Jerbiton, is permitted.

*Sorcery in this case probably means maleficia, harmful magic.*

790 Iceland: Irish priests (called "papar" by the Norse) reach Iceland and use it as a retreat in the summer.

C. 790 Stonehenge: Vikings begin raiding Britain, often aided by powerful rune wizards.

791 Normandy: Branugurix, domus magna of House Diedne, founded in a regio close to Mount Dol in Brittany. 

*Its name was actually Bren Gwrac’h but the order adopted a Gallic version of the name for ease of use in Latin. Disappeared in the Schism War 1012.*

792 Iberia: After numerous unsuccessful attempts to make the Tempestiarii join the Order, Tancoystl ex Diedne refuses the Tempestirarii’s ultimatum. Taking advantage of the situation, Dehewint ex Flambeau resumes the campaign against this tradition.

792 Rhine: The last recorded sighting of the Founder Merinita - she departs to places unknown to seek the spirit of the wilderness. The new Primus Quendalon

founds Irencillia covenant.

*She is said to still appear to magi occasionally, which is nonsense of course.*

793 Hibernia: Founder Diedne arrives, hoping to recruit Ireland’s druids, but her arrogant "Join or Die" is rejected by the chief druid Sechnassach, who treats the obnoxious foreigner’s threat as little more than a child’s tantrum and gave her no further thought as she departs. 

793 Loch Leglean: Vikings sack Lindisfarne, the sacred heart of the Northumbrian kingdom, and among the bloodshed, strange omens arise - a black sun, spirits rising from battlefields, and accounts of warriors touched by death who cannot be slain. Skaldic traditions claim that Hel, the Norse goddess of the underworld, momentarily opened her gates. The event causes significant consternation throughout Christian lands. Hermetic scholars suspect a powerful Infernal regio was at work, feeding off the carnage and desecrating the very place where the Christian religion began in the nation.

*In this year fierce, foreboding omens came over the land of the Northumbrians, and the wretched people shook; there were excessive whirlwinds, lightning, and fiery dragons were seen flying in the sky. These signs were followed by great famine, and a little after those, that same year on 6th ides of January, the ravaging of wretched heathen men destroyed God's church at Lindisfarne.*

*The relentless campaigns of Charlemagne against the pagan North had led to the destruction of their sacred sites and holy places. The great king had felled Jôrmunr, the revered tree of the Norse. What greater vengeance than to strike at the very foundations of the Christian God? After all, it was from Lindisfarne that the missionaries who sought to convert Norway had first set forth.*

793 Rome: Auctus covenant founded in Sardinia.

794 Iberia: Tancoystl ex Diedne attempts to stop the attacks on the Tempestiarii, but is accused of collaborationism and forced to reveal the location of the shrines of this tradition. The few Tempestiarii who survive the ensuing attack join House Diedne to survive, but most of their Mysteries are lost.

C. 797: Founder Tytalus bests the hedge wizard Evander of Ravenna in a game, and sponsors Evander to House Verditius, where he teaches the construction of astrological instruments.

797–817 Hibernia: Diedne returns with twenty-seven members of her House. Divided into three squads of nine, they stalk through Ireland in search of druids. Each is made the same offer, to join or die. Most chose to die. Diedne’s ruthless pogrom lasts over 17 years. 

The Ulster-born Cuin-dallán, Latinized as "Quendalon," returns from the Rhine to save what he can of the older magic traditions. Aided by other members of House Merinita, he accepts some into his House and hides others in Connacht. Meanwhile, the Diedne magi forms covenants and sets down firm roots.

*The age of the Irish druids was over.*

798: Pralix, now a powerful maga in her own right, introduces her master to the young maiden Hariste, whom she had intended to take as her apprentice. Hariste was enchantingly beautiful, and overcome with unfamiliar emotion Tytalus immediately became besotted and stole her away to his home, where he founds the covenant Fudarus to provide a defensible place to train her. The furious Pralix was left to take his place as acting leader of the House, though she never claimed the title of Prima.

*His leprosy made her forever unattainable, and he refused to sully her perfection. In frustration, she became determined to punish her master for his reticence, thus giving rise to a tradition of rivalry between master and pupil that persists to this day in House Tytalus.*

798 Normandy: The first confrontation with the Spider Damhain-Allaidh in Brittany. A magus of House Tytalus aggravated Damhan-Allaidh by kidnapping one of his trusted aldormen. The sorcerer didn’t even waste time negotiating; he simply caused the stones of Carnac to crush his impudent foe and his three companions. Damhan-Allaidh made it clear to the sole survivor that the Order of Hermes would not have a foothold in Britain.

*If he’d known more about the Order of Hermes, Damhan-Allaidh would never have uttered such words within earshot of Fudarus, and Pralix won the right, as eldest filia of the Founder, to accept the challenge he had unwittingly set. *

C. 799 Levant: Charlemagne, a man of wisdom and culture, had more allies in the Order of Hermes than the Quaesitores would like to admit. One result of the embassies he exchanged with Harun was that he managed to secure the caliph’s permission for the Order to found two covenants in the Levant. 

*Harun’s reign was an age of wonders, when interaction between the mundane and magical Realms was at its height. It’s even said that in some areas jinn and humans interacted freely, sharing communities and trading with one another. Harun himself took an interest in Hermetic magic, and it is rumored that he was taught its use by members of the Order. *

**799 Third Tribunal of the Order of Hermes: Rules for Certamen set; it is to be respected as a method of resolving dispute; the guidelines for choosing Technique and Form are set; winning magi may not challenge again over the same issue until they have been challenged in turn; vis may be used in formal certamen. Any magus refusing to engage in certamen, or refusing to abide by the results thereof is betraying the spirit of the Code and is to be punished. \
The Spider Damhan-Allaidh is declared an enemy of the Order following the slaughter of four magi at Carnac.**

**A "Tribunal of the East" is to be established in the Levant, albeit only two covenants will be allowed by the caliph and the Order has not made further progress in this direction. \
Ranemiro ex Guernicus is expelled from the Order for having broken the Iberian truce. He is granted one year to put his affairs in order. The rest of the Iberian magi must pay a large sum of Vis within seven years.**

***Notatus presents his major breakthrough "Aegis of the Hearth" to much acclaim. \
***Technically the First Grand Tribunal. The Grand Tribunals following this one are to occur on a cycle of thirty three years, barring emergency Tribunals.*

*Those who refuse to accept certamen challenges, therefore, or refuse to abide by the decision of a certamen may suffer more violent coercion by the offended magus and will be punished by their Tribunal. *

799 Rhine: Quendalon, First Primus of Merinita, enters faerie woodlands in Bohemia, leaving Myanar in charge of the House.

*Whether he entered Maddenhofen Wood is not noted, although it seems likely; the forest is near Irencillia.*

800 Iberia: Égica ex Flambeau arrives to Lucis to execute Ranemiro ex Guernicus, but finds only Ranemiro's apprentice, Tello, and an intense Divine Aura. Tello swears that his parens has ascended to the Heavens along with the covenant. 

800 Rome: King Charlemagne is crowned Emperor of Rome by the pope, a re-establishment of the Holy Roman Empire. This closes a period of open friendship between the king and the Order, as the pope is jealous of wise men outside the Church. However, Emperor Charlemagne does issue laws forbidding vigilante attacks against suspected witches.

*Flavia filia Jerbiton reported in attendance at the coronation. Charlemagne was both surprised and displeased at this papal act, but he was clearly fitted for the role.*

801 Iberia: The Iberian magi agree to pay a local tax to cover the costs of the fine imposed by the 799 Tribunal.
