1054 Novgorod: Iaroslav the Wise’s death brings on a new civil war. An emergency Novgorod conclave agrees not to oppose Vseslav, a prince and Volkhv, who was one of the participants. The Volkhvy hedge wizards were considered hostile to the Order, but a policy of non-aggression is adhered to.

*This was potentially deadly. Vseslav’s actions raised the general consciousness, both Church and State, of the practice of illicit magic within Russia. Should the Tribunal step in to counter Vseslav? Should they regard him as a rogue hedge wizard and kill him? The fact was that he was a prince of the House of Rurik, and to act against him would be a violation of the Hermetic Code and would draw attention to their presence. But plainly Vseslav was making the war worse than it would otherwise be. Plainly he was a direct threat to the Tribunal, but equally plainly to act against him would be contrary to Hermetic principles and a provocative, dangerous action. In the end, the Tribunal meeting in emergency conclave, elected to do nothing.*

*Temporary stability descended on Russia when three of Iaroslav’s sons formed a triumvirate. One of their first actions was to lure Vseslav to a peace conference, seize him treacherously, and hurl him into prison. This action backfired badly, for within a year the Cumans had begun pressing hard on Pereyaslavl and the common people were muttering about broken oaths and God’s vengeance on traitors.*

1054: A new star appears, beginning about the twelfth night of the month of Cancer, and lasting for twenty-two months.

*Another omen, but for what?*

1054: The Eastern patriarch of the Church publishes accusations against the

West, citing four theological errors that include using unleavened bread in the

Eucharist and fasting on Saturdays, the enforcement of celibacy on the priesthood, and their practice of adding "and the Son" ("filioque") to the Nicene Creed, suggesting that the Holy Spirit proceeds from Christ in addition to the Father. The Byzantine emperor Constantine IX Monomachos closes the Western churches in Constantinople and orders the Latin monasteries to conform to the Eastern practices. The pope protests that the Roman Church has allowed the Greeks their strange ways only by sufferance, and sends three legates to argue the matter, who are insolently received. They excommunicate the emperor, and in turn the patriarch excommunicates the legates.

*After this final volley, the theological dust settles and the separation of the two Churches seems complete.*

1055 Levant: Seljuks take control of Baghdad and the caliph.

1055 Rome: The Fifteenth Verdi Competition.

1055 Rome: Fortunata of Jerbiton, covenant Cupressa Nigra is acquitted of charges of spying using spirit magic.

c. 1057 Loch Leglean: The mortal Caledonian King, MacBeth, is overthrown by Malcom Canmore, Duncan’s heir, with the aid of two unidentified magi.

1058 Stonehenge: Gruffyd ap Llewelyn, a mortal prince of Wales, lays siege to Blackthorn as part of a campaign against England.

1060 Stonehenge: Gruffydd ap Llewelyn sacks Cad Gadu, burning it to the ground.

1060 Thebes: 28th Theban Tribunal hears of the destruction of the stringles witches, and recognizes the covenant founded on Polyaigos by those who destroyed the threat, led by Hoplite Quaesitor Laskaris. Resolving that the Tribunal must respond more forcefully and swiftly against such enemies in the future, Laskaris pledges his covenant to this purpose.

1061-1068 Rome: Quaesitor Iernilus tours the Roman Tribunal to investigate covenant interaction with mortals.

*He discovers that sale of enchanted items to mortals is epidemic in the Tribunal!*

1061 Rome: A Wizard’s War is fought in the streets of Venice between a Tytalus and a Verditius magus.

1061-1123 Rome: Fortunata of Jerbiton, senior maga at Cupressa Nigra, abandons her covenant and moves to a remote island, but opens her house in Venice to the Redcaps.

*She acquired a reputation as a powerful eccentric over the next several decades as she continued translating ancient texts, writing Hermetic texts on spirit lore, and circulating a few texts on Principles Lore starting the Mystery Cult Lore. *

1061 Stonehenge: Siege of Blackthorn relieved by King Harold Godwinson.

1062 Stonehenge: Blackthorn drives the magi of Tagelyn out of their caves, moving their own covenant to the safer location. 

1062 Stonehenge: Blackthorn seizes the caves of Tagelyn covenant as their own, a more defensible site than their old fortress.

*The surviving magi of Tagelyn later found the covenant of Ashenrise in the Hibernian Tribunal. *

*Among the classic texts lost in the struggle is a copy of the rare Physiologus, a classic bestiary. It is generally assumed that Blackthorn had always intended this betrayal.*

1062 Transylvania: Tremere conference at Coeris.

**1063 Twelfth Grand Tribunal: The Rhine Tribunal seeks to extend over Polish lands at Grand Tribunal, claiming that the territory is unclaimed after the fall of Grand Silesia; the New Slavonic Tribunal pushes through a measure granting final authority over settlements of all covenants in Slavic lands.**

**The Primus of Jerbiton proposes that demons be declared Enemies of the Order in perpetuity; the Tribunal determined that this resolution might start a war between the Order and the Infernal and instead passed an amended resolution that demons cannot be Friends of the Order.**

**A truce is negotiated between House Flambeau and House Tytalus, as their squabbling over House Diedne’s particularly plentiful covenants in Brittany had gotten out of hand and spilled outside the Normandy Tribunal. Special Tribunals are held to divide the disputed territory.**

**The Flambeau Primus Entisimon, who had been encouraging the conflict, is forced to resign in disgrace.**

**The formation of the covenant of Lapis Crudus is sponsored in an attempt to heal the rift which had formed between the houses, to include two members of each house, plus a follower of Jerbiton and a Bonisagus of Trianoma’s line.**

*In a somewhat uncharacteristic show of solidarity, the Flambeau ended the conflict by refusing to fight the Tytali any more. For the Tytali involved, the conflict had become less about issues and more about the struggle itself. By cleverly removing themselves as opponents, the Flambeau effected a truce.*

*Wishing to rein in the power of the Rhine Tribunal, the Grand Tribunal rejects its claim over the Polish territory. This may be the Tribunal at which the "Larta mage" precedent was established. Durenmar has proposed adjusting the boundaries of the Rhine and Novgorod Tribunals at several Grand Tribunals since. *

*The key passage decrees that "The Servants of Satan, who so often make themselves enemies of our Order of Hermes, may never again be friends of the Order."*

1064 Normandy: Lapis Crudus covenant is established in the forest of

Fontainebleau in Ile de France. Considered to be part of the line of Sinapis.

*Lapis Crudus was founded on the spoils of the Schism War, and was an important player in the politics and culture of the Normandy Tribunal for the century that followed.*

1065 Stonehenge: Cad Gadu refounds and rebuilds on its old site, but in the higher levels of its regio. 

*However, the Tribunal is weak, with only four active covenants, two of which - Cad Gadu and Lux Draconis - are barely clinging to survival.*

C. 1065 Iberia: Influx of Moorish sorcerers into northern Iberia, fleeing Almoravid persecution. The Almoravids destroy some southern covenants. After this period more non-Flambeau magi oppose the Moors in Iberia.

*The prior Moorish invaders had been reasonably tolerant of sorcery. The hard line of the Almoravids must have been quite a shock to the "Roman" covenants.*

1066: A great comet is sighted in the heavens.

*This is taken as a sign of victory by Duke William of Normandy, who conquers the kingdom of England under it.*

1066–1068 Stonehenge: Harold’s Vengeance covenant founded in the Anglian fens to oppose the Norman invasion. It repeatedly and blatantly violates the Code and is Marched within two years, all its magi suffering death. 

1067 Iberia (Al-Andalus): Yulyan ex Bonisagus, of Fafhs al-Luyy, is revealed as an informer of the northern magi. He earns the enmity of his Andalusian sodales, and commits suicide soon after. 

1067 Provencal: Maga Consuelia founds Mistridge covenant, in the Pyrenees.

1067 Rhine Tribunal: Dankmar ﬁnally achieves recognition as a Rhine covenant. Covenant Roznov settled by three magi of House Ex Miscellanea. *Due to its location in the Carpathian Mountains it could have easily claimed membership of either the Novgorod Tribunal or the Transylvanian Tribunal instead of the Rhine. However, seeing an opportunity, Durenmar and Crintera between them offered more resources than the other Tribunals. Roznov was inducted into the Rhine Tribunal in 1074.*

1067 Rome: Cupressa Nigra covenant is abandoned when the remaining junior

magi join other covenants.

1067 Thebes: 29th Theban Tribunal ostracizes Cosmas, follower of Verditius, of the covenant Diadelphia. Surprisingly, Cosmas’ rival brother follows him to the Roman Tribunal and Diadelphia is officially disbanded.

1068 Iberian Tribunal of Al-Andalus: The regulations of the Great Tribunal on the sale of magical objects to mundanes are clarified as 'one magical object per year, per mundane prince', as a concession to the Verditius of the Tribunal.

**1068 Special Roman Tribunal: Treaty of the Roman Tribunal negotiated by Quaesitor Iernilus finalized and ratified after great debate. It limits conditions of sale of enchanted items to mortals, limits such items to limited uses, limits mercenary services, forbids revealing magi to mortals, and mandates a review at every Tribunal. Since this time, all of the official covenants of the Tribunal are required to maintain lodgings in Venice, where this Treaty was finalized. **

*The treaty was not binding outside of the Roman Tribunal but became a seminal precedent on sale of enchanted items in Peripheral Code. Unfortunately it is riddled with flaws. Most notably, the treaty governs only cash sales, ignoring barter and gift.*

*House Verditius decreased the number of items a member could sell to mundanes to one magical item per year, and the selling maga must use her network of intermediaries during the exchange. This was later revised to one magical item per mundane race (Jew, Muslim, Genoese, Milanese etc). This limit only applies to mundane buyers; any number of magic items can be sold to members of the Order of Hermes.*

1069 Novgorod: The Volkhv Vseslav is released from prison by rioting Kievans

and takes control of the city. Again, the Tribunal elects to do nothing. Vseslav’s reign in Kiev is brief, as his brother Iziaslav returns and drives him out. 

*Patrick of Dunderry was an old man by now, but still passionate in his advocacy of non-aggression and he managed to keep the Tribunal distant from the continuing conflicts. Vselav retired to Polotsk, dabbling in unpleasant magics and scribing his dangerous knowledge in six massive tomes of black serpent skin.*

1069 Thebes: Bjornaer covenant Estropi destroyed by the invasion of the Seljuk Turks.

C. 1070–1073 Loch Leglean: Horsingas covenant founded in Loch Leglean Tribunal by magi fleeing the Norman Conquest. 

Sources conflict on the founding date. This is not surprising, given the covenant’s generally poor condition.

1071 Thebes: Battle of Manzikert, between a Byzantine Christian army of 60,000 commanded by Emperor Romanos IV and an Arab (Seljuk Turk) army of 100,000. The Byzantine forces are routed and the battle ends Byzantine power in Asia Minor. Central Anatolia is lost, forever changing the Byzantine Empire. In the aftermath, some local hedge magi join the Order for protection, including the original pater of Prima Ex Miscellanea Immanola.

1071: Tremere magi begin to join multi-House covenants again.

1072 Special Iberian Tribunal: The Iberian Tribunal debates the presence of Barcelona covenant in the city of Barcelona. After many hours an agreement was reached for the magi of Barcelona covenant to have Hermetic jurisdiction over the city and serve the interests of the Order in it, and for external magi to obey conventions of subtlety while visiting.

*Barcelona covenant had been situated for 58 years by the time this issue came up. The issue was probably spurred by inter-covenant rivalry.*

*In response to accusations of mundane interference, the magi of Barcelona called for a reading of the Code, from the presiding Quaesitor. At the quotation of, "Nor will I interfere with the affairs of mundunes and thereby bring ruin upon my sodalis", the magi pointed out that the abjuration was to not bring ruin upon their sodalis. Indeed, Flambeau himself had used this interpretation to justify actions that were damaging to the Order, more damaging than mere attendance at parties and discussion of poetry with counts. The magi of the Tribunal eventually agreed with the Barcelonan position and granted the Covenant jurisdiction over the city, and over all magi that would enter it. In return the covenant promised to keep peace between magi and mundanes, and to serve the Order's interests in the city. The covenant has continued without formal opposition ever since.*

1072 Normandy: Nauche-Fleur covenant, vassal of Montverte, founded.

*This covenant operated a school near to Niort in Aquitaine, where instruction in both martial and academic subjects funded the opulent lifestyle of the magi. The covenant’s role in mundane politics was ever a source of controversy in the Tribunal; consequently none of the apprentices it trained remained to continue its work. The covenant died in 1166 when the last of its founders entered Final Twilight.*

1072 Transylvania: Tremere conference at Coeris.

*We really need to find some inside information..*

1073 Levant: Covenant Al-Arama founded on a mountain crag in the Taurus Mountains of Lesser Armenia. Primarily concerned with trade with a number of covenants in the Roman Tribunal.

1073 Rome: The Sixteenth Verdi Competition.

1074 Rhine Tribunal: Roznov covenant inducted in the Tribunal.

*To the ire of Novgorod and Transylvania, or was this just another machination by House Tremere? *

1074 Thebes: 30th Theban Tribunal recognizes new covenant of Xylinites in Constantinople.

*The monastery was originally founded as a lavra (a community of monks living in cells) in 823. In the late 10th century Empress Zoe’s first husband Emperor Romanus III Argyrus was particularly lavish in endowing the monastery. He made two particularly important donations - in 1030 he founded an orphanage and school supported by the state treasury and run by the monks, and in 1031 he donated a tiny but beautifully crafted icon of a little-known saint, St. Loukia. In 1071 Tikh of Jerbiton, a scholar paid to teach at the orphanage school, noticed the icon and that it had unusual attributes. He realized that St. Loukia was Loukia of Berytus (Beirut), a Montanist heretic who claimed to physically enter paradise by the power of the Holy Spirit, and who was executed in the fourth century for blasphemy. After seeking the abbot’s permission, Tikh removed the icon from the church and placed it in the school room of the orphanage. He was amazed when a Magic aura slowly developed around the heretical magical icon, and swiftly moved it to the orphanage schoolmaster’s house where he lived. Needing space for his laboratory, he uncovered and partially drained a huge vaulted water cistern under the orphanage and east grounds, founding one of the most enduring Constantinople covenants.*

*The demand from the palace for eunuchs had long exceeded supply, and with*

*the relaxation of the laws against castration many families saw having a eunuch son as a potential route to wealth and influence. The school attached to the orphanage had long accepted paying scholars from the city, and now a large number of youngsters elected or were volunteered by families to be educated there in the art of music, and subsequently castrated if their voices were suitable for the eunuchs of the choirs, the castrati. In addition, politically inconvenient children were often sent to the monastery, and castrated to render them ineligible for the throne or prevent inheritance disputes. The monastery thus became an important source of eunuchs destined for the imperial palace. Former students at the palace promoted the orphanage’s interests, while the Jerbiton magi enjoyed a lavish lifestyle, ample opportunities for study, and indirect influence over the imperial court.*

*However, the only remaining member from the sacking of Constantinople in 1204, Artoud of Criamon, established a Clutch (a Criamon covenant) dedicated to mystical research here instead.*

1075 Iberia (Al-Andalus): The couple Rebeca Ex Miscellanea and Al-Layt ex Criamon found Jujasjun as their home. 

1075 Iberia (North): Langobriga is established by a couple of magi expelled from Duresca. 

1075 Rhine: A Cologne woman is accused of sorcery for "bewitching" men with her art, and hurled from the town wall.

*Almost certainly Plebian hysteria.*

C. 1075: A Muslim sect of Arabian wizards found the Brethren of Purity, a mysterious group of religious magi.

1076 Thebes: The magi of Kyddopetre disbanded. With the loss of Cilicia to the Seljuk Turks, their site no longer fell within the Byzantine Empire. While not a legal problem, the magi felt too isolated from their fellow Greeks to remain and

joined other covenants.

1079: The great church reformer, Pope Gregorius VII, decrees that all cathedrals must make schooling compulsory for priests.

1081 Iberia: The Andalusian Praeco, Al-Asmarun, is sent as a negotiator with the northern Tribunals to formalize the separation between the two Tribunals. The northern covenants, instead, impose an ultimatum: Al-Andalus must reunify with Iberia within seven years, or the irregularities of the Andalusians will be denounced to the Great Tribunal.

1081 Normandy Tribunal: Perpuaca’s books are declared the first tomes of the Library of the Normandy Tribunal. It is ruled that the books owned in common by the Tribunal are only available as prizes at the Tourney. Winning a book in the tournament grants exclusive access to that tome for a total of seven years; at which point it is returned to the Tribunal’s store. The book store is maintained at Confluensis, and a list of the books and their authors is a matter of public record. These books cannot be consulted by any member of the Tribunal unless they have been won at the Tourney. 

Florum Covenant is ordered by the Tribunal to dismantle its linen factory, which is equipped with blatantly magical devices for the production of cloth. It is ruled that the excessive amount of fine quality cloth produced is being used to unduly influence the mundane population of Ghent. 

1081 Rhine Tribunal: Dankmar covenant is accused of being in league with the faeries of the Black Forest.

1082 Thebes: Venice establishes a naval base on Rodos.

1082 Transylvania: Tremere conference at Coeris.

1083 Stonehenge: Voluntas covenant founded by Norman magi in Yorkshire.

1085 Iberia: Conquest of Toledo by Christian forces led by the great mortal hero called El Cid. Toledo was an important center of classical and magical learning in Moorish Iberia and home to several libraries and numerous works of classical antiquity, until then unknown in Europe. Many important texts from fallen Rome are recovered from the libraries of Toledo. The fame of Toledo as a center of magical learning extends even to mortals. Many famous non-Hermetic magicians learned their craft here.

1085 Rhine: A major raid against Durenmar by forces of the King of the Black

Mountain, a powerful faerie lord of the Black Forest, results in the death or capture of a number of Bonisagus magi and many covenfolk. \
*None of those captured are ever returned and their fate is unknown.*

1086 Iberia: The Almohads begin the conquest of the taifas.

1087 Normandy: William the Conqueror dies at the age of 60 - at the priory of St Gervais in Rouen - from injuries he sustained by falling from his horse.

*William’s body stank and rapidly decayed, it was defiled by servants, and abandoned by the sycophantic nobles who had gathered to his deathbed. This was a vile augury of Normandy’s fate as the duchy fell into anarchy and revolt. Eventually, William was taken from the priory to Caen, and as his body arrived a fire broke out that threatened the town. Against a backdrop of acrid black smoke and fire, beneath the bleak solidity of Caen Castle, William’s funeral was conducted at the Men’s Abbey. His bloated corpse burst free of the coffin during the ceremony, and the stench was so foul that the presiding bishops scrambled to complete the rite.*

1088 Normandy Tribunal: Florum covenant appeals against the former ruling, citing that they were now so poor that their access to laboratory supplies was virtually non-existent. They claim that their magical power is therefore being limited by the previous ruling of the Tribunal. The appeal is dismissed as spurious by Quaesitor Celestin, who remarks that "the mundane resources

of a covenant cannot be considered to contribute towards the magical power of its members."

*This ill-considered comment was enshrined in the Peripheral Code of the Normandy Tribunal, although the legal loophole that it created would not be exploited for several decades. *

1088 Rhine Tribunal: Magi Xantippes and Julia, both Jerbiton, and maga Selena Tibicena, House Bonisagus, announce their intention to establish a covenant in the city of Trier dedicated to recovering the lost secrets of the Romans. No sponsors of the proposed covenant are forthcoming from the covenants of Irencillia, Crintera, or Dankmar, and therefore the First Tribunal does not recognize this proposed covenant.

1089 Iberia: The Tribunal of Al-Andalus accepts the Iberian ultimatum and the process of integration begins. 

1089 Normandy: Another case of ergotism. This time only a small area is sent mad.

*Who was sending these curses? A faerie lord, or one of our magi?*

1090 Iberia: Yusuf ibn Tasufin unifies the taifas in the Almoravid Caliphate.

1090: In the town of Votting, three women are accused of blasting crops. They pass a trial by ordeal, but the townspeople are unconvinced. They are whipped to make them confess, but maintain their innocence. They are finally burned alive. 

*Mortal justice in action.*

1091 Iberia: The Sahirs of Jibal al-Rahma abandon the Order. 

1091 Rome: The Seventeenth Verdi Competition.

1091 Stonehenge: On Friday 17th October, London is hit by a great Tornado that strikes the heart of the city, causing a great deal of damage. The London bridge built by William the Conqueror is destroyed and the church of St Mary-le-Bow completely levelled, to the extent that four huge 26 foot rafters were driven so far into the earth that only four feet remained visible above ground. Other nearby churches are demolished by the tornado, as are over 600 buildings. For all the damage, there are just two known fatalities in a population of 18,000.

*The tornado was likely the work of a Saxon magus from House Ex Miscellanea, striking back against Norman rule. In the years after 1066, Saxon resistance had been crushed, their traditions suppressed, and Norman churches and castles rose over sacred sites. This magus, likely trained in the old storm-calling traditions and perhaps working with Tempestaria weather witches, saw London as the heart of Norman power and the Church as a force erasing Saxon identity. Using a mix of Hermetic and hedge magic, he summoned a storm fueled by faerie might - having made a pact with the Wild Hunt. The tornado struck London hard, tore through the city and destroyed St. Mary-le-Bow, a clear symbol of Norman authority. The very institution that had legitimized the conquest, crowned William’s father, and sought to erase Saxon identity.*

*Whether the magus was taken by the Wild Hunt, quietly found and burned as a heretic, or simply disappeared into the mists of history, his name was lost. But the devastation he caused sent a clear message: A warning as much as an attack, a reminder that no matter how strong the Normans believed themselves to be, the land itself had not accepted them.*

*Nonsense! The culprit was a Flambeau apprentice, who had been tasked with destroying the London bridge for his gauntlet, set by an old nostalgic pater who had destroyed it as part of his own gauntlet in 1014, while in the retinue of Olaf II of Norway. In 1091 the bridge had been warded strongly against Ignem so another method was needed.*

1092 Thebes: The Grammaton covenant is disbanded as it becomes apparent that their research is not going to achieve its goal, despite several interesting innovations and discoveries. The remaining magi joins other covenants.

1092 Transylvania: Tremere conference at Coeris.

*Still nothing?*

1093 Rhine Tribunal: Magus Borov of Jerbiton, who had trained his apprentice in the court of a mortal lord, is charged to bring his apprentice fully into the Order or risk Renunciation.

*The magus became embroiled in the court’s politics, and aided the Margrave in his mundane wars and the wrath of the Margrave’s enemies was thereby brought upon the Order. Furthermore, the apprentice chose to side with the mundane factions, rather than serve the interests of the Order. The case upheld the precedent of the Grand Tribunal of 865 that no magus may become a servant to any mundane. *

*A special Tribunal, as it is out of cycle.*

1095 Iberia: Magi of Normandy establish Andelos. Victrix is founded on the initiative of House Tremere.

1095 Normandy Tribunal (33rd): Covenant of Nauche-Fleur is accused of entering into a feudal obligation with their local baron. The covenant protests that the oath was taken by an unGifted companion who acted the role of Lord of the Manor on behalf of the magi. The Tribunal rules that the magi are innocent.

1095 Provencal: Gaston of Valloire, a nobleman of the Dauphiné founds the Order of the Hospitallers of St. Anthony, named after the fourth century Egyptian hermit. The Order is confirmed by Pope Urbanus II in the same year, at thanksgiving, for the son's miraculous cure from Saint Anthony's fire thanks to the relics of Saint Anthony the Great.

1095 Rhine Tribunal: Magi Xantippes and Julia, both Jerbiton, and maga Selena Tibicena, House Bonisagus, together with Arliandus of Tremere, again announce their intention to establish a covenant in the city of Trier. The First Tribunal approves the founding of Treverorum covenant. 

1095 Rome: In this year, a census indicates that independent magi outnumber members of official covenants in the Roman Tribunal.

*This is completely unacceptable!*

1095 (November): At the Council of Clermont, Pope Urbanus II denounces corruption among the clergy and the breakdown of peace in the Frankish lands. He calls for the restoration of the "Truce of God" and urges that noble violence be turned outward, against the infidel foes of Christendom in the East. Framing the campaign as both penitential pilgrimage and holy war, he declares that Christ commands it. The response is overwhelming. Many noble and common folk alike swear oaths to take up arms - setting in motion the crusades.

*The Order records surging Dominion auras in France and the Rhine, and a worrisome zeal among young magi who view the call to purify the world as extending also to magical affairs.*

1096: The Peasants' Crusade (Peregrinatio Pauperum). Peter the Hermit, a charismatic preacher of dubious orthodoxy out of Cologne, rallies a vast multitude of poor pilgrims, paupers, and displaced knights under the banner of the Cross. Inspired by fiery preaching and visions of reclaiming the Holy Land, thousands of common folk set off for Jerusalem in a great tide of hope and desperation. Lacking papal sanction and any formal martial structure, the self-declared crusaders travel eastward through the Holy Roman Empire and the Byzantine lands toward Constantinople. Their passage is marked by rioting, destruction, and pogroms against Jewish communities, particularly in the Rhineland. Quaesitors later record testimony of Divine miracles intermingled with signs of Infernal temptation along their path.

The Order largely watches in alarm and silence. Covenants along the route, particularly in the Rhine and Greater Alps Tribunals, note disturbances in the local Auras and an upsurge in mundane suspicion of magical practitioners. Several young magi are chastised for involvement - either through attempts to protect local Jewish enclaves or for secret efforts to influence or hinder the mob. 

Peter is joined by Walter Sans Avoir, a Frankish lord, and his small group of knights at Constantinople. Emperor Alexius I Comnenus, eager to usher such volatile pilgrims beyond his walls, grant them passage across the Bosporus. Though Peter pleads for caution, urging delay and discipline, the ill-led multitude can not be stayed. Impatient and intoxicated with zeal, they plunge into northwestern Anatolia in October, and swiftly meet their doom at the battle of Civetot, near Nicaea, against the Seljuk Turks - the host was surrounded and slaughtered.

Peter returned to Constantinople, whether to gather reinforcements or to preserve his own skin is uncertain. Walter remained with the vanguard and was struck down by seven arrows as the Seljuk emir Kilij Arslan swept down upon them like a scythe through unripened wheat. 

A few survivors are absorbed into later waves of the First Crusade.

*It is certainly whispered that some Tytali encouraged the chaos for their own obscure ends.*

*There are massacres of Jews in Worms, Speyer, Cologne, Trier (where many choose suicide by drowning instead of forced baptism), Mainz (where many commit ritual suicide with knives in the episcopal palace), and Bohemia. Henry IV attempts to punish those responsible but is not supported by the Church.*

*The turmoil left scars in both mundane and magical Europe: strained Christian-Jewish relations, a rise in Dominion auras where martyrdom occurred, and a new precedent for mass pilgrimage as a tool of holy war.*

*The Peasants' Crusade is remembered as a cautionary tale among both mundanes and magi - of fervor without discipline, and of how swiftly the Dominion can sweep across the land in the wake of suffering and zeal.*

**1096 Thirteenth Grand Tribunal: House Jerbiton complains of Flambeau support of mundanes in Iberia; the boundaries of Iberia are redrawn to limit the political power of House Flambeau there.**

While giving up land area, this move effectively limited House Flambeau's power within the Iberian Tribunal and curtailed House Flambeau's interference in mundane affairs.

*Though House Flambeau could not alter the decision of the Grand Tribunal, they did not fully respect it. Many members of Flambeau left Provencal to join covenants in Iberia. Without the prestige and power of lost Val-Negra, however, they did not wield the political power they had in the past. Many Flambeau magi settled for venting their anger on a personal level. In particular, Muslim sorcerers who had  joined the Order after fleeing the Almoravids were subjected to frequent attacks by members of House Flambeau. The violence of House Flambeau galvanized their opponents, in much the same way the initial intolerance of the Almoravids drew the Christian states out of their lone slumber.*

1096: The First Crusade begins proper, as the better organized and funded "Princes' Crusade" armies leave for the East a few months after Peter the Hermit's ill-fated Peasant's Crusade. This is the first time the Church resorts to direct, aggressive warfare, although by proxy. The crusade aims to gain the Levant from the Muslims, for religious reasons.

With banners raised and crosses sewn upon their cloaks, the great lords of Europe lead their armies eastward: Raymond of Toulouse, Bohemond of Taranto, Godfrey of Bouillon, and others destined to become legends - or calamities.

*Some within the Order suspect that Divine intervention played an active role; others whisper that Infernal pacts may have guided the course of certain warlords. Nevertheless, the First Crusade saw an explosion of Hermetic activity in the Levant. Many Hermetic magi took part in the crusade. Members of Houses Flambeau, Tremere and Tytalus in particular joined the crusading armies. Some were genuine crusaders, fired by zeal for their faith. Others recognized the opportunity to plunder the resources of the East, while some Flambeau magi also sought revenge for what they saw as the murder of their founder by Muslim wizards. Some Muslim magi attempted to oppose the Hermetic onslaught. Latin chroniclers noticed in particular the activities of the sorcerers of Jerusalem, who used their magics to curse Latin war machines and build up the city’s defenses. However, these Muslim wizards were few and far between, and not being centralized in a single organization, they were unable to stand for long against their Hermetic opponents.*

*Later the crusading Hermetic magi were joined by others with purposes of their own. Members of House Verditius were keen to partake of the spoils, while magi of House Criamon wished to consult with the mystics of the area. Many Jerbiton magi also traveled to the Levant, keen to take advantage of both its riches and the culture. Against the protestations of the older covenants, the new arrivals set up new covenants and marked out territories. *

1097: The First Crusade gains momentum as the crusader princes unite under the uneasy guidance of Emperor Alexius I Comnenus in Constantinopel. In the spring of 1097, they cross the Bosphorus and besiege the city of Nicaea, long held by the Seljuk Turks. After weeks of blockade and combat, the city surrenders - not to the crusaders, but to Byzantine agents in a secret accord. Tensions rise between East and West.

Soon after, the crusaders begin their perilous march into the heart of Anatolia, crossing burning plains under relentless sun. At Dorylaeum, they are ambushed by the forces of Kilij Arslan, but through desperate courage and timely reinforcement, the Latins prevail. The battle becomes legend among pilgrims and troubadours, and some speak of ghostly riders aiding the crusaders - whether angelic or otherwise is debated. The road ahead remains uncertain, but the armies press on toward Antioch, driven by faith, glory, or madness. 

They lay siege to Antioch in October. After stripping the surrounding area of food, the crusaders are forced to look farther afield for supplies, opening themselves to ambush. On 31 December, a force of 20,000 crusaders encountered a relief army led by Duqaq, ruler of Damascus, heading to Antioch and defeat them.

1098: As the siege of Antioch continues, supplies dwindle and one in seven of the crusaders are dying from starvation. People begin deserting. 

Still, a second relief force under the command of Duqaq's brother Ridwan, emir of Aleppo, is defeated in February - and the crusaders manage to take Antioch in June, although the citadel remains in the hands of the Turkish defenders. But the crusaders are immediately besieged in turn by a massive Muslim relief army under Kerbogha, atabeg of Mosul. In a time of direst need, the leaders of the crusade are approached by a southern French servant named Peter Bartholomew, who has received several visions of St. Andrew, revealing to him the location of the Holy Lance that pierced the side of Christ. Following his instructions, the crusaders find the Lance under the cathedral Church of St. Peter - sparking fervent revival among the starving host. Against all expectation, carrying the Holy Lance in a sortie from the city - they successfully shatter the siege, driven by zeal and perhaps something more. 

*The crusaders continued to carry the Lance into battle until 1101, when it was lost in Asia Minor. No one knows what became of it after then.*

1098 Transylvania: The entire wing of Coeris that contains the legendary Schism War veteran Hermanus of Tremere’s laboratory disappears in what is assumed to be a magical accident and Hermanus’ Final Twilight. Hermanus was the magus most responsible for the defenses of Coeris. Of great concern to the House Tremere, the Unbarred Gateway Vexillation is formed by the Tremere to search for Hermanus and his laboratory. After five years this vexillation concludes that neither could be located by any method known to the Tremere and dissolves.

*There are rumors he discovered evidence of a group of Diedne sympathizers within House Tremere, and they are somehow responsible. Others claim he enchanted his laboratory with the ability to move and left Coeris behind forever to explore the world without having to leave the safety of his heavily warded laboratory.*

1099 (July): The First Crusade concludes with some considerable success as Jerusalem falls to the crusaders. The blood of thousands flows through its streets in Divine justice, and a Christian kingdom is declared in the Holy Land - placing Godfrey of Bouillon as Defender of the Holy Sepulchre. The conquest sends reverberations through all four Realms. Pilgrimage becomes fashion, and the Church’s temporal power swells. 

*The First Crusade establishes a Latin presence in the East that shall endure for generations, and also opens new roads - both mundane and mystical - between Europe and the lands of the sunrise. Eastern knowledge, exotic vis sources, and forgotten magics begin to trickle westward, though often tainted by strife or warped by conflicting paradigms. Relics - true and false - proliferate, and many are imbued with strange magical or miraculous properties. Some Hermetic magi seek to study them; others recoil from the potent Divine aura that now cloaks the Levant.*

*Back in Europe, the Church is emboldened. The call of crusade is proven potent, and murmurs arise of future expeditions. In the Levant, the magical landscape is forever altered: ancient regiones collapse or are swallowed by the rising tide of sanctity; Infernal forces, once rooted in ancient corruption, retreat or transform.*

1099 Loch Leglean: German and Spanish Flambeau magi hunt non-Hermetic magi in Scotland; they are stalked and defeated one by one by a Damhadh-Duidas, a mighty gruagach, who strips them of their Gift. Threats to retaliate are met with a unified Tribunal resolve to defend Loch Leglean hedge wizards against foreign magi.

*This served only to enflame tensions between Flambeau and Ex Miscellanea. No action was taken against Damhadh-Duidas for these acts, a violation of his Oath. Of course, there is no record of Damhadh-Duidas having taken the Oath, or if he’s kin or even the actual Damhadh-Duidas from the Spider War.*

1099 Normandy: Infelicitas covenant, vassal of Fudarus, is founded after Rancrax of Tytalus lost a wager with his rival, and was forced to leave Fudarus as per the terms of the bet.

*The covenant foundered in 1158 when Rancrax was slain in Wizard’s War by a member of Moles Magna, and the remaining two magi were not interested in continuing at the site.*

1100 Normandy: Nidi covenant founded in Aquitaine, vassal of Oleron.

1100 Stonehenge: Lux Draconis enters Final Winter when its last member dies. Blackthorn laid claim to the property of the defunct covenant. None of the other covenants had enough strength to challenge them, although Voluntas tried, and the infusion of money, books, and vis put Blackthorn firmly on the road to recovery.

*This property conflict is the foundation of the enmity between Blackthorn and Voluntas.*

1101 Levant: Covenant Venti Rosa founded, a Mercere caravan traveling through the Tribunal from covenant to covenant.

1101 Thebes: Court of Fools founded, an itinerant Ex Miscellanea covenant full of mystics and soothsayers, touring throughout mainland Greece. 

1102 Iberia: Barda is founded.

1102 Normandy Tribunal (34th): In response to a complaint from three Flambeau magi of Provençal, the Tribunal rules that lacking a clear geographical

boundary between Provençal and Normandy, the border shall be determined on linguistic grounds. Territory disputed by the two Tribunals shall henceforth be decided by the language of the closest settlement; if it is a langue d’oeil then it is Normandy, else it is Provençal.

*Despite protests from Provençal at the Grand Tribunal in 1129, this decision is upheld.*

1102 Normandy: Tyrmander of Flambeau, impressed by the recent crusade, urges his sodales to march against the Saracens, both to crush them, who he sees as the enemies of the Order, and to take from the Church the mantle of protector of civilization, before the Church declares a crusade against the Order. *Tyrmander’s logic is serpentine.*

1102 Transylvania: Tremere conference at Coeris.

*Around this period, some followers of Tremere are now known to have experimented with vampirism, and possibly other forms of unliving existence.*

1103 Iberia: Last of the Andalusian Tribunals. The Great Hall is soon abandoned and the illegal Andalusian Tribunal is dissolved.

A party of Flambeau magi murder every Sahir in the former covenant of Jibal al-Rahma.

1103 Provence: Magus Pierre de Galve of Jerbiton is accused of endangering his sodales by copying books out of his covenant’s library, and trading these copies to mortals. It was feared that texts of the Order may reveal the Order and cause bad feelings against magi. Magus Pierre was found to have only copied comparatively mundane books, not concerned with the Arts or magic theory, and was acquitted.

1104 Iceland: Mount Hekla erupts, spewing molten rock, smoke, ash, and poisonous vapors. Tales of this wonder filter back to Europe.

*What foul Terram magics are available to the heathens there?*

1104–1107 Stonehenge: Blackthorn sponsors the covenant of Blackrose on the north York moors, near Voluntas. It suffers multiple disasters, including crop failures and vanishing staff, and fails after only three years, the magi recalled to Blackthorn.

*Voluntas is widely considered responsible, but Blackthorn presents no evidence.*

1108 Tribunal of Iberia: Fawd ex Criamon is elected Praeco.

1108 Thebes: 35th Theban Tribunal. Petronas becomes Logothete, and is to serve for almost a century.

1109 Normandy Tribunal (35th): Quaesitor Muirgheal had encountered a group of hedge wizards who were fighting a plague of restless dead, and inducts many of them into the Order as the Ex Miscellanea tradition of the Donatores Requietis Aeternae - despite the misgivings of many magi in the Tribunal.

*A rise in sinister encounters with the restless dead was observed in many parts of France during the 11th century, with frequent hauntings and visitations, and hordes of the walking dead blighting some areas. These occurrences were blamed on a cult of the dead, which was believed to be especially active in  Picardy. Early in the 12th century, a group of hedge wizards, clergy, and others combined forces to combat this threat. They prevailed in a great battle, banishing the restless dead, although many of the group lost their lives and the town was ruined. Thus were the Donatores formed, and several of the hedge wizards in the group decided to remain at the ruins of Nesle, in case the undead should return. *

The Tribunal rules that if a man can get from the covenant’s council chamber to a vis source and back again, on his own two feet and unaided by magic, between sunrise and sunset on a day which is within a month of an equinox, then the vis source is a seisin of that covenant. This ruling superseded previous entries in the Peripheral Code (see 1025) which were unclear as to the definition of "a day’s return march". 

*Nearly every covenant in the Tribunal has a specialist called a cursor, or "runner," usually a highly athletic grog, whose sole purpose is to prove to the Quaesitors in a trial run that their vis source is within this range. Cursors are prized members of any covenant, and some trade them like cattle. *

1109 Rhine Tribunal: Treverorum covenant brings charges against Valdus of House Tytalus for theft of vis, claiming that he has twice harvested from a site belonging to them. Valdus accepts that he harvested this vis, but argues that the site, lying outside the city walls of Trier, does not belong to Treverorum. He also claims that magi of Treverorum had neglected to harvest this vis for themselves. It is ruled that the site, being within a day’s march from the covenant, belongs to Treverorum. However, it is noted that Treverorum has suffered no harm or loss of vis as a result of Valdus’ actions. Valdus is therefore fined one pawn of vis and ordered to seek the permission of Treverorum before harvesting from the site again.

1109 Rome: The Eighteenth Verdi Competition.

1111 Stonehenge: Blackthorn sponsors Magnantum covenant in Salisbury.

*This is the second known attempt by Blackthorn to establish a filius covenant.*

1112 Levant: Aedes Veritatis covenant is established by a young maga from Damascus, Maryam al-Dimashqiyya, as a stronghold for magi of the Order who chooses to take up arms against their Hermetic brethren and fight for the Muslims of the area. The name translated into Arabic (Bayt al-Haqq) carries a

wider meaning of "the House of What is Right".

1112 Transylvania: Tremere conference at Coeris.

1115 Thebes: 36th Theban Tribunal: Thermakopolis founded, a House Jerbiton covenant dedicated to the defense of the city of Constantinople.

*The pride and shame of House Jerbiton, pledged to the protection of Constantinople, a task in which it ultimately failed.*

1116 Normandy Tribunal (36th): Lapis Crudus covenant complain that the magus Koilios had staged a raid on their supply convoy, stealing a significant amount of food and luxuries (but oddly, left vis and glassware alone), thereby causing them significant hardship. However, when challenged, Lapis Crudus are unable to explain what part of the Code has been violated by Koilios. Furthermore, Koilios being a skilled politician from House Tytalus, cites the ruling of 1088, indicating that what had been taken from Lapis Crudus did not infringe the rights accorded them by the Code of Hermes.

*Koilios had picked his target well; Lapis Crudus was not well liked, and Koilios was able to defeat Lapis Crudus and win exoneration for his theft.*

The Tribunal confirms the earlier ruling of 1081 that the mundane resources

of a covenant do not necessarily contribute to the magical power of its members. Specifically, food, consumables, building supplies, arms and armor, and luxury goods are not part of a magus’s magical power.

Magus Praecepsus of Flambeau is accused of using a sword enchanted with Perdo spells to cut down the enemies of the king during the raid on Paris by the count of Meulan in 1111. The Tribunal rules that, since there was no evidence to mundane eyes that magic was involved, there is no charge to answer.

1117 Normandy: Spider’s Palace covenant founded, vassal of Florum, in the former site of Florum.

1117 Rome: The independent covenant of Feritel founded in Genoa. 

1118 Iberia: Magi of Flambeau join an attack by Aragon forces against the Moorish city of Saragossa. A few Moorish magi of the Order defend the city to preserve the city library. In the battle, the Moorish magi are killed, though no formal Wizard's War had been declared.

*In the aftermath, many magi of the Order expressed their outrage at the actions of Flambeau. A special Tribunal was called to deliver the Order's justice upon the magi.*

1118 Normandy: An unfortunate incident reveals the location of the covenant Lapis Crudus and the nature of its inhabitants to several prominent noble families, who all seek to use the magi for their own ends. Lapis Crudus is forced to retaliate with a combination of subtle threats and blackmail, but neither are sustainable in the long term. Seeking a way to free the covenant from this political mire, Aries of House Flambeau tricks a powerful faerie called Candidus into providing protection and concealment for the covenant, keeping it safe from harm. Candidus fulfills the pact by moving the covenant into a faerie regio; but,

resenting the manner in which he had been dealt with, he also seals the borders of the regio, trapping everyone within. The covenant is considered lost.

*Fortunately, some of the covenant’s sources of vis were located within the regio, but the magi needed to consume the output of these seisins to provide themselves with food and tools. That is, until one of their number devised a way to transfer non-living goods into the regio, and the magi got their first taste of meat and wine for nearly seven decades. The trapped magi were forced to rely on their fortunately large vis stocks for their study, and between them exhausted the library’s capacity to teach them. There were some attempts to free the trapped magi, but Lapis Crudus’s vassals, seeing a chance for independence, were not sufficiently motivated to achieve this goal, and the covenant was quickly forgotten - until 1215…*

1118 Thebes: Bogomil leader Basil burnt for heresy in Hippodrome.

**1119 Iberia: The Special Tribunal rules that the magi of Flambeau involved in the attack on Saragossa, in which their Muslim sodales fell, must pay the loot gained to the covenants of the victims. Afterward the Quaesitors decree that magi may not aid mortal powers "overtly or with any sort of magic that can be detected by mortals," leading to a covert state of the war.**

Though the Code of Hermes specifically prohibits the killing of another member of the Order, the evidence against the Flambeau magi is scant. No hard evidence is produced that indicates Flambeau magi are directly responsible for the death of Muslim Hermetic magi at Saragossa. Advocates of the deceased magi argue that the Code also forbids interference in mundane affairs, such that it brings ruin upon others of the Order. The Flambeau responds by saying the deceased magi were the ones interfering in mundane affairs, bringing ruin upon themselves.

In the end, the Tribunal refuses to order the execution of the attacking Flambeau, but orders them to return all loot they had gained to covenants of the fallen magi. After the Tribunal the Iberian Quaesitors issue a blanket decree ordering that no magi of the Order would provide aid to a mundane power "overtly or with any sort of magic that can be detected mortals." This ruling limits the activities of magi supporting Christian armies, as well as limiting those opposing the Christians. And yet, the ruling does not altogether stop impassioned magi. 

*This seems like a softer ruling on the issue than ones in the past.*

*In the wake of the Quaesitors's ruling, the polarity of the opposing sides increase. The southern Iberian Covenant of Estáncia-es-Karida was the first to define itself as a "Roman" covenant. Its members believed that Muslims who ruled the southern portion of the peninsula were the true inheritors of Rome's glory. They said the shaky Germanic alliance that called itself the Holy Roman Empire could not compare to the achievements of Mohammed's followers. In spirit and culture, the Muslims were considered to be leading the way toward a *

*rebirth of the Roman empire. Covenants that supported the Muslims called themselves "Roman" Covenants.*

C. 1120: Adelard of Bath translates Euclid’s Elements from Arabic into Latin. 

*This work is getting popular at universities beginning around 1200, and is getting installed as the mundane "ultimate textbook" in Europe.*

1120 Iberia: Praesidium Abditius is founded, although its statutes are not approved until 1123.

1120: The order of the Knights of the Temple is founded in the Levant to escort pilgrims. The Templars come to be a powerful crusading army and to hold vast lands throughout Europe.

*It is now also one of the great forces backing the authority of the Church.*

1120: Bernard of Clairvaux writes De Laude Novae Militiae, arguing that the Church is justified in using military force to achieve its ends, including conversion of pagans, opposition of the minions of Satan, and defense of the Church and Christendom. Bernard asserts that such defenders should be military orders of monks who have renounced the world and taken vows of poverty, humility, chastity, and obedience as well as knightly vows.

1122: At the city of Worms, Henry V reaches an agreement (the Concordat of Worms) with Pope Calixtus II, which technically brings the Investiture Contest to an end. The emperor concedes that the Church has the right to elect persons to ecclesiastical office, and he promises to support canonical election to bishoprics and abbacies. In return, the pope concedes that the emperor has the right to be present at elections and to perform a lay investiture of German prelates before their consecration to spiritual office. This ensures imperial control of the dues and duties owed to him by bishops and abbots, who are also powerful feudal magnates. In practice, while a victory for the Church, the emperor still retains great influence over the appointment of prelates.

*It seemed the Church won the battle, but in truth the emperor still holds considerable power over the Church. The plots of the Devil (for the reformists’ fears were just) were merely altered a little. Many within the clergy were His minions and many more would be. The Devil learned patience in such matters long ago.*

1122 Transylvania: Tremere conference at Coeris.

*A Wizard's March is declared against the vampiric Tremere, but the events around the March are unclear. The Quaesitors had been investigating these magic-vampire hybrids for some years.*

*It's rumored that Murat of Terezin, single-handedly dispatched over 200 vampires.*

1123 Tribunal of Iberia: Aedes Iris is destroyed by unknown forces. The Iberian Tribunal formalizes the prohibition of founding new covenants in Africa until 1225. The Tribunal is marked by the infamous criticism of the 'Perfect Magi' of 'Alif to the Criamon of Barzaj.

1123 Normandy: Requies Aeterna covenant, vassal of Florum, is founded at the ruins of Nesle in Picardy by Ex Miscellanea magi from the tradition known as the Donatores Requietis Aeternae, "Givers of Eternal Rest," who are dedicated to assisting the dead in leaving the world of the living.

*Several of the hedge wizards (now Ex Miscellanea) in the group had decided to remain at the ruins of Nesle, in case the undead should return.*

*Since its founding, Requies Aeterna has been closely monitored by the Tribunal’s Quaesitors, who remain suspicious of their mundane dealings, especially their contacts with the Church - as a number of the Donatores were trained clergymen.*

 

1123 Normandy Tribunal (37th): Pandion of Flambeau is accused of depriving Antonin of Dragon’s Rest of his magical power by stealing twelve valuable horses from the Redcap, one of which was a magical creature. Pandion is convicted, and ordered to return the magical horse, along with a fine of 10 pawns of vis, but is permitted to retain the other eleven horses as spoils from a lawful raid. The Tribunal notes that while livestock does not in general contribute to a magus’s magical power, enchanted creatures - even those that are not familiars - certainly do. After a request for clarification, Quaesitor Lothaire extends this ruling to exclude covenfolk from a magus’s magical power.

1123 Rhine Tribunal: Magi Schwall of Bjornaer and Fulmonious of Tytalus found the covenant of Rheinstein in the Rhine Gorge with the sponsorship of the First Tribunal. Rheinstein simultaneously announces it will pay a tithe of three pawns of vis each Tribunal to every other covenant, for the next five Tribunals.

1123 Rome: Last encounter recorded with Fortunata of Jerbiton, founder of the Mystery Cult "Principles Lore". It’s suspected she died or entered Final Twilight not long after. She was a famous writer, historian and translator of ancient texts from lost languages as well as a spirit master. Jealous and powerful Hermetic enemies forced her into exile.

*Rumor has it she commanded ancient ghosts to read the texts aloud while she used Mentem magics to understand their words, exceeding Hermetic limits on translation. There were also unproven rumors of Infernalism and magical spying.*

1123 Levant: After the defeat of a Muslim fleet off Ascalon, the sea fills with corpses. They drift, in an ever-expanding ring, until they pile upon distant beaches like walls of driftwood. No fish can be caught on the site of the battle, and voyages that cross the battle’s location are cursed to fail.

*Skulls of the dead of Ascalon are said to be found on ships on ill-fated journeys.*

1123 Thebes: Lamites covenant founded. A short-lived Anatolian covenant devoted to prophecy, and claiming the Phrygian Sibyl as their patron. It is

believed that Lamia killed them all for their presumption in 1127. 

*Although it may have been the faerie called Lamia who objected to the competition.*

1124 Iberia: Jujasjun discovers the addictive properties of the white poppies of the Balion River. The Iberian magi discover the Sahir fortress of al-Minzar, which had been isolated until then. 

1124 Normandy: Lixivia covenant, vassal of Florum, disbands after having been driven into poverty. Alcuin’s Auberge covenant, a small Mercere House in the city of Tours and vassal of Florum, is founded by Redcaps from Lixivia.

*It fell to unknown raiders in 1197.*

1127 Thebes: Lamites destroyed.

1127 Rome: The Nineteenth Verdi Competition.

1127: The Order hosts the Third Secular (every 120 years).

1128 Normandy: A "sorceress" of Ghent is eviscerated and her innards paraded around town. 

*More mob justice of the commoners.*

**1129 Fourteenth Grand Tribunal: **The re-founded Tribunal of the East is ratified by the Order, and named the Levant Tribunal. 

Border of Provencal and Normandy Tribunals adjusted to the "modern" boundaries.

Mendalus of House Merinita proclaims that Quendalon had been wrong, and the Order had a duty to follow the true path of Merinita. 

*While several Merinita took up this pursuit of wilderness magic, starting a sub-tradition in the House, not much else seems to have changed.*

*Rumors have it that Mendalus was actually a hierophant of the Huntress who was left crazed after a protracted Twilight. He believed that he was the messiah they had been searching for, and the other hierophants of the Huntress were forced to silence him by declaring Wizard’s War against him and slaying him.*

The re-founded Tribunal of the East is ratified by the Order, and named the Levant Tribunal. Despite official recognition, the Tribunal differs considerably from the Hermetic ideal. Interference in mundane affairs is commonplace, as is frequent conflict with Muslim mages.

*Some magi, disenchanted with the ravages of their sodales, began to side with the Muslims against the crusaders, provoking further conflict. Wizards’ Wars also*

*became common, as magi disputed over the rich resources of the Levant. The Quaesitors found it difficult to impose their authority on the area because some of their number were as involved in the chaos as any other magi. The Levantine crusader magi usually responded to accusations of breaches of the Code by protestations that their actions were necessary to ensure continued Hermetic presence in the war-torn lands, while the counter-crusaders claimed that their actions were taken to defend the Muslims from the consequences of these breaches. The Tribunal descended into a chaos from which it seemed that it would never recover.*

1130 Iberia: The communal covenant of 'Alif is founded as a demonstration of the ideals of the ‘Perfect Magi’. This group advocates a peaceful and contemplative life for magi, without the coercive influences of an Order. Barda begins its current policy of stealing vis belonging to Caium.

Yezirat Tennyn established.

1130 Normandy Tribunal (38th): Jacinth of Oleron is accused of deprivation in that, conducting a raid against the covenant of Infelicitas, he caused the collapse of the upper story of the covenant. Further, he is accused of attempting to slay a magus through the physical destruction of the covenant. The second charge cannot not be proved, but Jacinth is convicted of the first since two laboratories were destroyed. Quaesitor Lothaire rules that raiding magi are forbidden from damaging the physical structure of a covenant, and reminds the Tribunal that they cannot contravene the Code in an otherwise legal raid. Jacinth escapes a Wizard’s March, but suffers the loss of his familiar.

1130 Levant: The order of the Knights of St. John, the Knights Hospitaller, established sixty years prior to defend pilgrims, is militarized to defend the kingdom of Jerusalem.

C. 1130 Stonehenge: Geoffry of Monmouth writes several books regarding the magus Merlin. The popularity of tales of Merlin, his student King Arthur, and the realm of Enchanted Britain continues.

1132 Novgorod: After the death of Mstislav the Great in 1132 a ruling is made that all members of the Tribunal are strictly enjoined not to reveal themselves to any member of the mortal House of Rurik (the Royal Russian house).

*This pact was to head off accusations of interfering in mundane affairs. Later this rule was extended to cover the Polish Royals as well.*

1132 Transylvania: Tremere conference at Coeris.

*Last report of a Tremere Vampire.*

1133 Iberia: Leading an influx of Arabic learning into European astrology, Abu Ma’shar’s Greater Introduction to Astrology written in 848 is translated into Latin by John of Seville.

*While churchmen from time to time denounce it (usually some nonsense about the "free will" clause), astrology is a reputable science and entering the mainstream. As astronomy, it’s taught in the universities as a liberal art.*

1133 Stonehenge: Natura Antiqua founded to study nature in the Anglian fens.

1134 Normandy: A mysterious storm alters the coastline at Brugge and carves a deep channel, the Zwin, bringing the town to within a mile from the sea. What was once little more than a castle that collected tolls from merchants crossing the Reie river, has its fortunes forever changed.

1136 Normandy: Moles Magna covenant founded, vassal of Oleron. Located on the edge of the Morvan, this covenant is built within a dam of a minor

tributary of the River Yonne. The river forms a small lake at the covenant’s

back, and cascades over the dam hiding them from view.

*It was eventually destroyed in 1182 when the river burst the dam after heavy rain; since the covenant had magically reinforced the dam against such a fate, it is assumed that they had angered a supernatural creature by restraining the river. *

1137 Iberia: The Hermetic side of the Kharijite movement is reborn with the establishment of Chaza'ir. 

1137 Rhine Tribunal: A handful of covenants in the west of the Tribunal form the Apple Gild, with the intent that magi should seek peaceful and friendly relations with mundanes, and that magi should work towards acceptance in the mundane world, so that they no longer need to hide. It also believes that magi should work to improve the lot of mundanes through teaching and trade as well as magic, and that magi should be humble before the Lord. 

*I.e. seeking to push the limits of the Peripheral Code with the extent of their mundane dealings. In almost all cases, while leading to temporary enrichment or significant influence among the nobility, these efforts have resulted in disaster.*

1139 Iberia: Both members of Langobriga perish during the Battle of Ourique; the covenant is dissolved.

Matlus of Jerbiton arrives in Barcelona and takes on the role of a cloth merchant.

*He comes to be Praeco of Iberia.*

1141: Insatella becomes Prima of House Mercere.

1141 Stonehenge: Founding of Burnham covenant in Northumberland.

1142 Transylvania: Tremere conference at Coeris.

1143 Iberia: Death of the Almoravid Caliph Ali ibn Yusuf. A war of succession soon breaks out.

Victrix reorganizes in collaboration with the Knights Templar.

1143 Stonehenge: Natura Antiqua covenant destroyed by a mortal, Geoffry de Mandeville, during a mundane war.

1144: Morienus Romanus’ "Liber de Compositione Alchemiae" becomes the first Arabic alchemical work translated into Latin, by Robert of Chester. 

This is soon followed by Gerard of Cremona’s translation of De Aluminibus et Salibus, attributed to Rhases, and the Aristoteles de Perfecto Magisterio.

*This created a great sensation and a wide spread of interest among mundane, unGifted scholars, and alchemists became common in most large cities as the new science developed. Hermetic Alchemy becomes more readily available.*

1144 Normandy Tribunal (40th): Quaesitor Lothaire is publicly accused of partisanship by Jacinth of Oleron, who cites specific instances where the Quaesitor was a beneficiary of numerous raids conducted by the covenants of Montverte, Fudarus, and their allies. A subsequent Quaesitorial investigation exonerates Lothaire from any wrongdoing owing to lack of substance to Jacinth’s accusations.

*Nevertheless, Lothaire did not regain his status as chief Quaesitor of Normandy, and was invited to join Magvillus in recognition of his long service to the Order.*

1144 Rhine Tribunal: At the urging of the Ash Gild, it is ruled that more information should be gathered about the Norse wizards in lands to the north of the Tribunal - the purported Order of Odin. To this end, Oculus Septentrionalis covenant was formed and dedicated to investigating this potential threat. Uniquely, at the time of its founding, this covenant had no magi and no site, and its charter was written by senior magi of the Tribunal. 

*There are nasty rumors that the Order’s failure to deal with or even grasp the Order of Odin (as well as the difficulties earlier in the War against the Spider) may be due Parma Magica being unusually vulnerable to their rune magic.*

1145 The Almoravid Empire in Iberia fragments into taifas.

*The second ones, in fact.* 

1145 Rome: The Twentieth Verdi Competition.

1145 Partitio Monaviae: Repeated conflicts over vis sources on the Isle of Man lead to a conclave between Stonehenge, Hibernia, and Loch Leglean. They agree to stop harvesting vis on Man and that the island and its vis would go to whatever Tribunal could first found a covenant there.

*There have been at least three attempts to found a covenant on Man, all disasters. This Pact instigated by Crun Clach?*

C. 1145 Stonehenge: Burnham covenant given a grant of immunity by a mortal king, Stephen, later confirmed by the king Henry II.

1147–1149 Levant: The Second Crusade assembles half a million soldiers, marches them on the Levant under the flags of France’s Louis VII and Germany’s Conrad III, and loses them to plague, starvation, and battle wounds.

1147 Iberia: Almohad invasion of Spain. The Almohads capture the Almoravid capital of Seville. This new wave of fundamentalist Muslims spread support for Reconquista covenants. During the rise of the Almohads, the Covenant of Jaferiya rose to power under the leadership of Rasus, a follower of Flambeau

1147: Slaughter of Jews in France, Germany, and Bohemia in religious hysteria.

*Does such a fate await magi if exposed?*

1147 Novgorod: Roslav Lavickovich summoned from Pripet Maior to Crintera, to assume Primacy of Bjornaer.

1148: The well-respected Quaesitor Simprim of Guernicus founds the Transitionalist faction of House Quaesitor. The Transitionalists argue that the Code should be more flexible, and take into account circumstances that more traditional Quaesitors would ignore. In particular, transitionalists believe the law-enforcement powers of the Quaesitores need to be expanded in order to enforce the Code in the face of corruption and political deadlock.

*To Simprim and his followers a law incapable of adaptation doomed the Order rather than helped preserve it. In a number of Tribunals the loss of vis sources and magic auras due to mundane encroachment was becoming an acute issue. In these Tribunals many magi felt the strict prohibition against mundane interference should be relaxed to allow the defense of magic resources. Simprim argued that if the Code demanded that magi sit idle as their magical resources were destroyed, they would be forced to defy it; lawlessness would follow.*

*In addition, he claimed that the Order had outgrown Guernicus’s vision of a loose society of freemen. Simprim maintained that corruption had beset many local Tribunals and action against them was too difficult under the existing system. The traditional rights of magi hindered investigations and trials too much. Simprim suggested more powers be given to the Quaesitores and Tribunals. He called for the Grand Tribunal to have the authority to amend the First Tribunal rulings as it desired and that local Tribunals be given reasonable scope to set policies on mundane interference.*

*Some Quaesitors, mostly younger ones, came to agree with Simprim, at least partially. With this split of opinion, the Traditionalists and the Transitionalists were formed.*

1148 Iberia: The mostly Jewish city of Lucena is destroyed by the future Almohads, and with it the covenant of 'Olam wa-'ed.

1148 Levant: The Pyramids (I) covenant is founded at the Great Pyramid, by magi obsessed with the pyramids of the ancients.

*Nobody knows what horrors they might unleash if they succeed in opening any of the pyramids.*

1149: Conrad III, king of Germany and Holy Roman Emperor, issues a call for each scholarly institution to send a representative to his court.

C. 1150s Novgorod: Expeditions as far as the Urals brings back word of seemingly limitless forests, stretching in unbroken grandeur into the uttermost East. Three Lakes magi visit the area where Thousand Caves covenant is later founded.

*The vis wealth reported sounds staggering.*

1150 Iberia: The Iberian Chamber convinces Salaman ex Guernicus, one of the founders of 'Alif, to renounce his beliefs and write a manifesto exposing the failings of the 'Perfect' ideology.

1151 Iberia: The covenants of Resurgens and Pallantia are formed.

Beit Midrash, a covenant of Jewish magi mostly from ‘Olam, is established in its present location.

Fafhs al-Luyy is dissolved, its resources divided among neighboring covenants.

1151 or 1158? Roman Tribunal: The Tribunal requires that all foreign magi, other than Quaesitors, passing through the Roman Tribunal must announce themselves to each of the Covenants at their embassies in Venice. *This requirement is widely ignored, particularly by magi passing through by ship.*

1151 Normandy Tribunal: A particularly competitive team from the Iberian Tribunal use a variety of then-legal tactics in the Tourney, which are widely felt to have brought the Tourney into disrepute and immediately outlawed by Peripheral Code. They earn 13th position, and are surprised when the Quaesitors declare that place the "Siege of Shame", stating that henceforth no prize will be awarded to the team that holds that position. 

*A tradition upheld by the Tribunal to remind everyone of the shameful episode.*

1151 Rhine Tribunal: Rudophus Bonisagus of Durenmar, through his actions, had attracted the attention of a major demon. Although he attempted for over a decade to kill this demon, he was unsuccessful. The demon began systematic attacks against the Redcaps of the Tribunal. The senior Mercere brings a case against Rudophus for endangering the Order. The Tribunal convicts Rudophus and he is commanded to provide House Mercere with some magical means of protecting themselves from the depredations of demons.

The murder of the Redcap Ioanna in the city of Trier is reported. The Tribunal expresses sincere regret. Magi of Treverorum covenant disavows any knowledge of this incident. A Quaesitorial investigation is ordered.

As accused by Dankmar, and found guilty, Durenmar is fined ten pawns of vis to every other covenant in the Tribunal for plundering faerie sites of the Black Forest for vis, thereby making large tracts of the Black Forest a hazard for magi, and restricted from taking further vis from faerie sites for fourteen years unless a peace was made earlier with the fae. 

Magi from Ombres L’assomption present their notable discovery "The Sphere".

Magi Anaxagoras of Tremere, Ulisterius of Jerbiton, and Forba of Bonisagus are granted a charter to form a covenant, Triamore, attached to the court of Emperor Conrad III.

The choice of site in Lübeck for Oculus Septentrionalis is seriously questioned, however, it is reasoned that it does have one major advantage - it will be very hard for the Scandinavian wizards to attack, unless they sack the entire city.

*However, it seems like Oculus Septentrionalis soon became subverted by the Apple Gild, much to the anger of leading Ash magi. Nevertheless, more moderate Oak magi secretly concede that Oculus Septentrionalis still performs a useful function, by indirectly hindering the pagan power base of the wizards.  Normally, the Order would be rather fearful of mundane and Christian expansion. However, in the sparsely populated Baltic region, such expansion serves it rather well. There is enough space that this should not hamper potential covenants; indeed, covenants require a certain level of mundane trade and civilization in order to prosper. Ideally, the growth of Christianity and trade in the region, as seemingly supported by Oculus Septentrionalis, would hurt the Norse wizards enough so that a direct war would be unnecessary. However, the Hawthorn Gild vigorously opposes this unspoken agenda.*

1151 Stonehenge Tribunal: The Covenant of Semitae, led by magus Gerfallon of Criamon, appears at the Tribunal and ask the Quaesitors to note their attendance. They refuse to answer questions about their background. This may or may not be the same group as appeared at Cad Gadu over the centuries.

*This is one of the few quorate Stonehenge Tribunals of the century.*

1151 Thebes: 41st Theban Tribunal recognized the covenant of Thumachia near Durazzo, founded to protect the western border of the Tribunal in response to the ravages of the Second Crusade.

*The primarily Jerbiton magi trod a fine line between intervention in mortal*

*affairs and protecting their sodales.*

1151 Transylvanian Tribunal: Maga Dorisa of Ex Miscellanea charges Constantine of Mercere, a Redcap, with sneaking into her sanctum and taking twelve pawns of Rego vis, thus depriving her of her magical power. Constantine argues that by contract, Dorisa owed that amount of vis to his sodales, as interest on a loan she had negotiated. He had asked her to settle the debt, and she refused, claiming that she could not pay it. Suspecting that she was lying, he entered her sanctum, avoided her defenses, and retrieved the amount. This he delivered to

his sodales "in her better interests." Dorisa counters that there was also more than twelve pawns of Animal vis in her sanctum, and that the Redcap had taken advantage of the situation by seizing the more valuable stash. After deliberation, the presiding Quaesitor determines that Constantine was due twelve pawns and twelve pawns had been paid, regardless of the customary value of the two kinds of vis. The Redcap had risked his life by entering her sanctum, but since that was not in itself illegal and no law had been broken, the charge was dismissed.

1152 Rhine: Emperor Conrad III dies, upsetting the plan for Triamore.

1152 Transylvania: Tremere conference at Coeris.

1154 Levant: Nur al-Din takes control of Damascus, making himself the supreme Muslim ruler in Syria. Both Nur al-Din and the crusaders turn their attention to Egypt, where the Fatimid caliphate is weak and ailing.

*It is said by some that Nur al-Din had magical powers, taught to him by the mysterious prophet Khidr, that enabled him to obtain whatever he wanted with foresight and to travel immense distances in the blink of an eye.*

*Regardless of the truth in that, the unification of the Muslims under Zangi, Nur al-Din and Salah al-Din was certainly accompanied by a flocking of Muslim magi to their banners.*

1154 Iceland: Mount Hekla erupts for the second time in fifty years, again spewing molten rock, smoke, ash, and poisonous vapors. Rumor claims that the pore is an entrance to Hell.

C. 1154 Rhine: Covenant Ombres L’assomption (1101–1154?), founded by migrating french magi and situated in the Taunus forest in the Bishopric of Panderborn, is abandoned or disappears. 

*It was a poor covenant that struggled to make it from one year to the next.*

1154 Stonehenge: Magus Flumen of Tremere, of Blackthorn covenant, traps the Wild Hunt in an iron mine fitted with iron-bound doors, near Wales. The hills echo with their howls for weeks.

1158 Iberia: Merinitae belonging to the Keepers of the Thousand Tales constitute Al-Qussas.

Ezra ex Bonisagus founds 'Arabot as part of his self-imposed 'exile'.

1158 Hibernia Tribunal: Magus Caesus of Tytalus ordered to disband eight of his nine sancti spread around the Tribunal, and reminded by the presiding Quaesitor that he is supposed to have only one.

1158 Rhine Tribunal: Magus Xantippes of House Jerbiton at Treverorum covenant is found guilty of significant meddling in the affairs of mundanes, having repeatedly used Mentem magics of his own devising on the inhabitants of the city of Trier for personal enrichment, setting in place a chain of events which caused damage to relations between the Order of Hermes and the mundane world, and that may have contributed to the death of the Redcap Ioanna. He is fined ten pawns of vis, ordered to distribute his accumulated fortune to the covenants of the First Tribunal equally, and charged to spend three seasons scribing tomes for the Great Library.

1158 Thebes Tribunal: The apprentice of a dead maga of Merinita is awarded to another maga of Merinita, Mondrasine, instead of the amicus of the dead maga, Nykolis of Jerbiton. Neither wished to challenge the other to certamen, as is usual.

1158 Rhine: Founder Merinita is rumored to be sighted alive in the wilderness. A young apprentice named Agnes announces she had met a lady in green in the forests of Bohemia, and learned a special sort of nature magic from

her. She finishes her training at Irencillia, but is soon hailed by Mendalus’ supporters as a "True Merinitae," believed to have learned Nature Lore directly from the Founder.

*Rumored, but not supported by witnesses. Distrustful magi have suggested that Agnes could have learned her magic from disgraced Diedne wizards, not Merinitae at all.*

1158 Rhine: Friedrich Barbarossa grants the incipient covenant of Triamore a manor in Namur county.

C. 1160: Tractatus de Purgatorio Sancti Patricii by H. of Sawtrey is written by a

monk who may have been Henricus of Basingwerk Abbey. It is a third hand Latin account of the Vision of Hell and Heaven, experienced by a monk called Owain as told to Gilbert, Abbot of Basingwerk.

1160: The unremarkable magus Zurenzialle of Merinita disappears from his covenant in the Greater Alps suddenly and without warning. When his sodales decide that he's dead and enter his laboratory, they discover evidence that he was never human at all, but a faerie being who had been pretending to be a magus. His objective, if any, is a mystery.

*Suspicions that the post-faerie Quendalon was not who he claimed to be seem more likely when considering the evidence of other instances when faeries have taken human shapes and tried to pass themselves off as magi. Zurenzialle seem to have been more effective than most though… Or?*
