1160 Iberia: Abd al-Mu'min wins the war of succession, declaring the Almohad Caliphate. Many of the southern magi flee Almohad fundamentalism.

1160 Stonehenge: Founding of Servus Maris on the Isle of Man. Destroyed within six months by a dragon. One magus is allowed to live to deliver the message to not return. 

*If the Order of Odin can control dragons, there is no assailing them. It seems unlikely to have been Diedne; there would be no warning.*

1160 Provencal: Passage of magus Belraphazor of Jerbiton into Final Twilight at his covenant, Windgraven.

*The Order is well rid of him.*


## The Age of Dominion and Discovery (AD 1160–  )

*The first half of this century sees the continuance of relative calm, although tension mounts within the Order, and friction between Order and Church grows. This period also marks a disturbing trend in the Church of Crusading: the Third Crusade to the Levant, the Albigensian Crusade, the continuing crusade in Iberia, and the crusade in the Baltic point to increasing Papal involvement in temporal matters and willingness to use force. Furthermore, the Church spends much of its energy in conflict with the Empire and other mortal kings. Some magi become concerned that this violent bent will inevitably turn against the Order.	*

*By the 1160s, Europe is firmly in the grip of strong monarchies and reform-minded Church authorities. Vast cathedrals rise above bustling market towns, while new monastic orders and crusading fraternities expand their influence from the Holy Land to the fringes of Christendom. As the Dominion’s aura spreads deeper into once-rural territories, covenants find their magical activities increasingly challenged by interference with spells or the draining presence of constant piety. Even so, these shifting mundane dynamics also open new opportunities for politically savvy magi to broker alliances with powerful nobles - and risk accusations of meddling under the Code of Hermes. *

*At the same time, learning flourishes in newly established universities (like Bologna and Paris), blending classical philosophy, theology, and even a faint whiff of the arcane. Young Jerbiton and Bonisagus magi, curious about the "outside" world of human learning, venture into these centers of study, forging strange partnerships with theologians, mathematicians, and proto-scientists. While some conservative Hermetic elders grumble at such openness, others see it as a wellspring of new insight - translating ancient Arabic or Greek texts on geometry, alchemy, and astronomy that could enrich Hermetic theory.*

*As the frontiers of Mythic Europe shift - be it the Reconquista in Iberia, the crusades in the Holy Land, or voyages probing the Baltic and North Atlantic- entire covenants undertake bold forays into terra incognita. They seek unclaimed vis sites and magical beasts, returning laden with obscure knowledge. House Ex Miscellanea, in particular, expands as newly discovered hedge traditions join the Order - somewhat grudgingly. Yet these adventures carry risk: wizards’ skirmishes with distant sahirs, African magicians, or even rumored enclaves of lost Diedne rituals spark fear that a new crisis could erupt if caution is thrown to the wind.*

*Despite overall peace, not all is harmonious behind covenant walls. House Tremere fortifies its dominance through disciplined cooperative magic, while House Tytalus tries to purge lingering shadows of diabolic scandal from its ranks, pushing its young magi into ever more harrowing "trials" of cunning. House Guernicus grapples with how to enforce the Code in a world where cross-Tribunal covenants, Mystery Cult manipulations, and complex mundane entanglements grow more common. Publicly, the Quaesitores remain the guardians of Hermetic law. Privately, they fret that one wrong move could ignite a new conflagration.*

C. 1160–1180: Maga Susan of Wicklow, House Mercere, of the Line of Patrick of Dunderry, accompanies Sadko the merchant and Georgi Yurivich on travels across eastern Europe. She journeys from Sweden to Constantinople on her travels.

*Susan’s forbear, Quaesitor Patrick, is likely to have been of Mercere as well, partially explaining his aversion to the Schism War.*

**1162 Fifteenth Grand Tribunal: Further arguments between Rhine and Novgorod regarding the border and Poland. Frederick Barbarossa’s march into Poland to place his candidate on the Polish throne had been the perfect cover for magi of Fengheld to accompany him and lay claim to Polish supplies of vis. Fengheld argues for a changing of the border to reflect their interests in Poland. Despite the support of the Rhine’s three Primi, the Grand Tribunal concedes only 10 miles of Polish land to the Rhine, barely sufficient to accommodate Fengheld’s newest chapter**

**House.**

**The Iberian covenant of 'Alif is accused of a Major Crime for writing a Hermetic Code opposed to that of the Order.**

A large game of Bellum with apprentices and young magi turns into a minor riot.

1162 Iberia: Zaloquia is dissolved, its location occupied by King Alfonso II of Aragon.

1162 Transylvania: Tremere conference at Coeris.

1163: As a ecclesiastical response to commercial medical practice and non-monastic education at universities, the Council of Tours decrees that participation of monks in such matters is forbidden.

*This is followed by even harsher rules at the Fourth Lateran Council in 1215.*

1163 Normandy: Construction on the Cathedral of Notre-Dame in Paris begins.

1163 Rome: The twenty-first Verdi Competition.

1163 Stonehenge: Stellasper covenant, in the Scilly Islands, destroyed by a cataclysmic storm of apparently magical origin. No bodies found. Whether the covenant was attacked, attacked something, or an experiment went wrong is undetermined. As the covenant had no allies and no friends, no further investigation was done.

*Cultivate allies, magus. They may aid you in life, and even avenge you in death.*

1163 Thebes: Moero’s Garden is founded in Constantinople, with the purpose: "To assemble the splendid artifacts and literature of the past to highlight the harmony and peaceful cerebral delight of beauty, which would temper hot emotions and promote a sense of well-being and serenity throughout the Order." 

1164: The Holy Roman Emperor, Friedrich Barbarossa, takes the Relics of the Three magi from Milan. They are later installed in a golden shrine in the Cathedral of Cologne.

1165 Iberia: Occelum Durii is re-established, this time as a Hermetic inn, managed from Porta Pandere.

Andelos dissolves; its properties are acquired by Duresca.

1165 Rhine Tribunal: Terschelling covenant is founded by Axior of House Flambeau, a member of Oculus Septentrionalis who disagreed about the eventual site for that covenant.

*Later to become Waddenzee at the Tribunal of 1207.*

A Quaesitorial investigation into reported unrest in the city of Trier has revealed that magi of Treverorum covenant are guilty of interfering in the affairs of mundanes and the Church, to the extent of causing possible harm to their sodales. Treverorum is fined fifty pawns of vis and warned in the strongest possible terms to repair relations with the inhabitants of Trier without delay. Treverorum is ordered to pay this fine by the next Tribunal.

1165 Greater Alps Tribunal: Maga Snadstrich Ex Miscellanea, member of no covenant, ordered to establish no more than one sanctum per season, after complaints that she had moved her sanctum seven times within one year and thereby established seven sancti in series. The Tribunal ruled that a magus cannot establish a new sanctum more than once per season.

1165 Hibernia Tribunal: Magi of covenant Vigil claim the apprentice of one of their Passed sodales; the apprentice is also claimed by magus Caerfloron, who had recovered the body and retained the apprentice after the master’s Passage into Final Twilight. The Tribunal rules neither had clear claim to the boy and that a mage of Vigil therefore must duel Caerfloron for the apprentice.

1165 Normandy Tribunal: Magus Stephios of Tytalus, of Burnham covenant in the Stonehenge Tribunal, asks the Normandy Tribunal to rule on an issue with another covenant, as his Tribunal is routinely inquorate or declared illegal by the Quaesitors. The Normandy Tribunal declines to intervene, declaring that it is unable to make decisions for another Tribunal - whatever its shortcomings.

*The failure of Stonehenge to reach a quorum seems to plague it through the first half of this century, but the records are spotty.*

1165 Provencal Tribunal: Maga Calonogi Ex Miscellanea tried for endangering the Order after preaching the doctrine that magi are Divine, as indicated by their Gift being a manifestation of the Holy Spirit. Her accusers also claim she has thereby blasphemed against God. On the latter count, no ruling as the Tribunal notes that it is not an ecclesiastical court; on the former charge she is found guilty. Ordered to cease this preaching, she refuses and is exiled for seven years.

*The maga is most fortunate; this could have resulted in a death sentence.*

1165 Thebes: 43rd Theban Tribunal recognizes the covenant of Moero’s Garden.

1167 Rhine: A Great Beast is seen at the Bjornaer Gathering of Twelve Years at Crintera. This is seen as a powerful omen.

*And only a year later they were invaded, where were their preparations?*

1168 Rhine: Danish king Valdemar I (along with Bishop Absolon of Roskilde) invades the island of Rügen, a stronghold of paganism and home to Crintera - the domus magna of Bjornaer. While Crintera is safe within its regio, the heathen stronghold of Arkona is destroyed, and the nervous Christians established several churches and a Cistercian monastery to spread the Dominion. 

*Crintera’s decision to wait out the occupation was strongly opposed by the Wilderists, but the opinion of the Harmonists within the House prevailed. Appeals to both the Rhine Tribunal and the Grand Tribunal fell on deaf ears; the House was merely reminded in the strongest terms of its duty to uphold the Oath of Hermes, and many Bjornaer magi are still resentful that the Order failed them in their time of greatest need. *

1169 Levant: Aurora Borealis of House Jerbiton arrives in Levant and founds Domus Pacis.

*Shirkuh becomes vizier of the Fatimid caliphate in Egypt, but dies. Succeeded by nephew Salah al-Din (Saladin).*

C. 1170 Hibernia: As mundane England invades Ireland, the rest of the Order has renewed interest in the Emerald Isle. Until now, most magi consider Hibernia backwards and strange. Most have heard the old stories of Hibernia as a land of faerie magic, but too dominated by the fae for it to be of true magical value.

House Tremere, having received new reports through Stonehenge, were the first

to take notice of Hibernia again. Magi of Stonehenge who went there described it as dangerous, archaic, and at odds with good Hermetic governance. This is naturally something that House Tremere cannot allow to continue.

*Why this keenness for reform when previous challenges to Hibernia’s Peripheral Code have failed? Firstly, while legal, the protections given to hedge wizards,*

*and especially the status of Connacht, seem improper, setting these hedge wizards on a par with the Order. Secondly, magi of the continent are now settling in Hibernia and cling to their own view of the Peripheral Code. In their own way, they are every bit as conservative and resistant to change as their Hibernian cousins.*

*They should follow continental practice; vis is harvested when it is available, the minor traditions of magic are intimidated or ignored depending on their utility, and Connacht should be ripe for the taking.*

C. 1170 Provencal: Covenant Calebais falls. Calebais was supposedly protected by a breakthrough magical veil by Mormulus of Merinita, a "magister of illusion". Although the covenant fell, the veil remains, and prevents arcane investigation of the covenant. The magi also had a device called The Bell of Ibyn, a powerful artifact of mysterious origins, and claimed that because of it, no attack or intrusion could catch the magi unawares. Since that time, no one has ever seen inside the covenant, both because the spells that they would use to examine a potentially dangerous place from a distance do not function, and because they fear traffic with demons. To this day, many magi in the Order are intensely concerned over what happened at Calebais, a covenant that should have been too powerful to destroy, but none have been able to penetrate Mormulus’s Veil of secrecy. The ruins have thus been left entirely undisturbed.

*Its library is said to have included a faithful copy of the Physiologus, thereafter lost. *

1170 Rhine: Roslav Lackovich, Primus of Bjornaer, dies. Urgen is elected as the new Primus.

C. 1170 Stonehenge: The Primus Ex Miscellanea dies. Maga Immanola Ex Miscellanea arrives shortly after, claims the Robes of Dusty Dawn, and is given them, making her the Prima. 

*There is no internal mechanism for selecting the Primus Ex Miscellanea. The first apprentice of the previous Primus might have pressed a claim, but apparently chose not to.*

1171 Thebes: Genoan colony at Galata, Constantinople burned to the ground. Then thousands of Venetians in Constantinople are imprisoned, their ships and property confiscated.

The Venetians raise a great fleet and sail to attack the Empire, but at Chios a terrible plague destroys the Venetian force.

1172 Rhine: The lands of Triamore covenant raided by mortal lords fighting over mortal issues.

1172 Rhine: The covenant Treverorum (1095–1172) in the City of Trier is destroyed. They were consistently in trouble for meddling with the affairs of mundanes, and were eventually destroyed, at the order of the Church.

*Others say it was actually destroyed by someone incredibly skilled in destructive Hermetic magic.*

1172 Normandy Tribunal (44th): The Duke of Brittany is declared an Enemy of the Order, after extensively persecuting magi. A Tribunal of Quaesitors immediately after declares that any mage who acts against the Duke can be held accountable for interfering with mundanes nevertheless.

Magus Anton Roettar of Tytalus asks to be relieved of his unending labor at Fudarus, 12 seasons of work assigned by his sodales without respite or recompense. Roettar felt this to be unfair, and Fudarus argued that their charter stated that the council could decide how much work a member must do for the covenant, without restriction. The Tribunal finds that the covenant has not violated neither its own charter nor the Code.

Guardinia of Merinita accuses Berenguer of Tytalus of molesting the fey. Berenguer claims in his defense that the faeries had stolen his apprentice, and therefore he had every right to take steps to return the child to his care. The Tribunal upholds Berenguer’s defense, but the Praeco admonishes him for having been heavy-handed in his rescue.

Atlax of Tytalus is accused of stealing and reading the messages carried by the Redcap Camille while the latter was hosted at Fudarus. However, Quaesitor Whent cannot ascertain which magi of Fudarus were responsible and holds the covenant responsible for their magus’s crime, and strips Fudarus of the lease of a legacy, and grants the magical site to House Mercere instead.

1172 Rhine Tribunal: Magus Scholastus of House Jerbiton of Treverorum covenant announces that he is the sole surviving member of his covenant, which is duly abolished. He announces that, due to the activities of local churchmen, in his opinion the city of Trier is currently not safe for magi to visit. He refuses to be drawn on further details and departs immediately for the Tribunal of the Greater Alps.

*It is later noted that the fine imposed on the former covenant of Treverorum remains in part unpaid. It is ruled that Scholastus is liable for the remainder of this fine and a request is dispatched to the Greater Alps by Redcap.*

Maga Tandaline of Durenmar complains that her fellow magus Quintus Vergilius has denied her access to her own sanctum by erecting magical walls to obstruct the entrance. The Tribunal rules that Quintus Vergilius has deprived her of magic power by keeping her from her sanctum "clearly hampered her capacity for study and creation", and is punished through the loss of familiar and fined two pawns of vis for each season obstructed.

*It may be of note that no representatives from Crintera attended.*

1172 Roman Tribunal: Luctatio covenant founded, sponsored by Verdi.

Magus Kaldorias brought to trial for banishing a demon which had been terrorizing a certain village, his accusers claiming that other demons may be moved to act against the Order; Kaldorias argues that the Order should be the enemy of demons (dismissed), that demons already work against the Order (granted), and that he had demonstrated to mundanes that magi do not serve the Devil; Kaldorias is convicted for breaking the Code, but not punished.

In the seven years since the last Tribunal, Helvennia of Bonisagus had exercised her right to claim another’s apprentice no less than eight times. All had died within a year. She claimed that the nature of her work was inherently dangerous, and the loss of those apprentices, while unfortunate, was beyond her control. Although the Tribunal was minded to rule in favor of the seven magi who leveled charges against her, the Presiding Quaesitor insisted on the legality of Helvennia’s actions and she was acquitted. However, the Praeco wrote to the Primus of House Bonisagus with the Tribunal’s petition for Helvennia to be censured, but was refused. Helvennia claimed another apprentice in the following year. On the night of the following new moon seven declarations of Wizard War were delivered to her. Despite attempts at arbitration, none of the seven agreed to withdraw their declaration. Helvennia fled into hiding but was found and slain with the aid of her new apprentice.

1172 Thebes: 44th Theban Tribunal. Covenant of Favonius recognized, though at this stage still in construction stages.

*The covenant is definitely unconventional - a flotilla of four main ships accompanied by a host of others, in constant movement around the Aegean Sea. It is the creation of Memnos, one of the most famous Verditii alive, and is driven to constant movement by its thrill-seeking patron spirit.*

1172 Transylvania: Tremere conference at Coeris.

1174–1187 Levant: Salah al-Din promotes himself as the rightful successor to Nur al-Din and begins creating a united state stretching from Egypt to northern Syria. Nur-al-Din is entombed in Damascus.

1175 Thebes: The Philomela launched.

1176 Thebes: Hoping to win back territory lost at Manzikert, Manuel Komnenos led a large army against Iconium, the capital of the Seljuk Turks. With poor planning and a lack of foresight, Manuel decided to attack while in the mountain passes near the fortress of Myriokephalon, and was utterly defeated. He was forced to accept a truce with the Seljuk sultan, which resulted in the loss of more of Anatolia.

1177 Iberia: Azm Alfil is abandoned as a consequence of the conquest of Cuenca by Alfonso VIII of Castile.

1177 Levant: Magister Heliobus of Bonisagus, a respected Hermetic researcher, finds a cabal of priests and priestesses of the Egyptian gods Thoth and Anubis, in the area of the pyramids and the sphinx, as they begin to suffer from attacks by Hermetic magi keen to plunder their magical resources. He joins the priesthood and quickly secures their entry into House Ex Miscellanea to bring them under the protection of the Hermetic Code to stop the attacks.

1178: Something that looks like a blaze is visible on the moon for several days.

*This causes some consternation within the Order, but whatever the event might have been, it is soon forgotten as Hermetic magic cannot affect or reach the moon sphere.*

1178 Levant: The Pyramids (II) covenant is founded by Heliobus of Bonisagus.

1179 (March): Pope Alexander III holds the Third Council of the Lateran, where 302 bishops attend and 27 canons are promulgated: Canon 7 forbids the charging of money to conduct burials, bless a marriage or indeed the celebration of any of the sacraments; Canon 11 forbids clerics to have women in their houses and sodomy; Canon 19 declares excommunication for trying to tax churches and clergy without the consent of the bishop; Canon 23 covers the concern and expulsion of lepers; Canon 25 excommunicates those who engage in usury; Canon 26 forbade Jews and Muslims from having Christian servants and that the evidence of Christians is always to be accepted against Jews.

1179 Rhine: Hildegard of Bingen dies at 81 years of age. The Benedictine abbess was a renowned polymath; active as a writer, composer, philosopher, mystic, visionary, and as a medical writer and practitioner - known and honored throughout Europe for her visions and scholarship. Hildegard's most significant works were her three volumes of visionary theology.

*Hildegard first saw "The Shade of the Living Light" at the age of three, and by the age of five, she began to understand that she was experiencing visio (Latin for "vision") and she recognized that it was a gift that she could not explain to others. *

1179 Rhine Tribunal: Urgen, Primus of Bjornaer accuses the magi of Oculus Septentrionalis of assisting the Danish invasion of Rügen, and thereby damaging Crintera’s magical resources. He ultimately slays their leader in a Wizard’s War.

1179 Levant Tribunal: Magus Henry Le Barre of Jerbiton summoned to the Levant Tribunal charged with interfering with mortals by participating in a crusade, and drawing the wrath of the Moors by using magic in the campaign; Henry argues that he served in the crusade as a noble and fought only to save his soul as any crusader does; the Tribunal allows that he may fight, but without magic and in disguise. For already having aided the crusade magically, Le Barre is fined 20 pawns of vis on penalty of expulsion.

1179 Normandy Tribunal (45th): Magus Damon Le Mont accused of scrying on his sodales at Ad Vis Per Veritas covenant - magically listening to their conversations with servants and grogs. Damon admits to spying on the covenant’s mortal servants, but that his spells only allowed him to hear what the mundanes were saying; the Tribunal rules that indirect spying constitutes actual scrying and fines him three pawns to each mage thereby offended against.

A maga is accused of a low crime by the magi of Fudarus. She is convicted in absentia, having been delayed to Tribunal by mortals.

Magus Pernix of Merinita is accused by magus Visurgis of Tytalus of interfering with mundanes when he used magic to heal a young nobleman who fell from his horse. The accused argues that the only people who knew that magic was used were the injured man himself and the abbot, prior, and infirmarian of the monastery where he was taken to recover. All are friends of his covenant and so there is no scandal or "ruin." Magus Pernix is acquitted.

Camille of Mercere is accused by Buliste of Tytalus of interfering with mundanes, in that she used a local knight to secure the vis from one of her legacies. The knight later admitted to his confessor that he had "done the work of devils," and it was only the swift action of Atlax of Fudarus that prevented ruin from being brought on the Order. The Tribunal agrees; Camille is ordered to pay fifteen pawns of vis to Fudarus in damages, and the vis source in question is declared off limits for at least three decades.

1179 Thebes: 45th Theban Tribunal. The covenant of Favonius docks in Philomela at Delos.

Bartholomew of Mercere is charged with two counts of depriving the magus Errantus of Criamon of his magical power. Errantus and other witnesses describe a confrontation with the Redcap, where Bartholomew had entered the covenant’s council room and wrenched a magical staff from Errantus’s hands, ruining a magical ring the magus had been drawing. During the struggle, Bartholomew kicked Errantus hard enough to knock him down, breaking two of the magus’s ribs and causing him to be unable to perform magic for the rest of the season. The Redcap then took the staff and fled the scene.

Bartholomew reminds the assembly that the last Tribunal found that Errantus had stolen the staff from Justina of Criamon, his filia, and had ordered him to return it to her. Though Errantus had many opportunities to discharge this duty, he had not yet done so, and in fact had continued to use the item. Bartholomew maintains that Errantus planned to use it as much as possible before returning it, and that by taking it from him and delivering it to Justina, Bartholomew was merely helping her reclaim her property. The Tribunal acquitts Bartholomew of stealing the device, and Errantus is ordered to pay Justina twelve pawns of vis in damages for his failure to comply with justice. Because Bartholomew had

attacked and injured Errantus, he is found guilty of the second charge, and ordered to pay the magus three pawns of vis as recompense for his lost season.

1180 Rome: Literatus covenant moves from Bologna to lands outside the city, but maintains facilities in the city.

*Literatus is said to have the finest mundane library in the Order.*

1180 Novgorod: Pripet Maior, long the home of the Praeco, cedes the right to host Tribunal meetings to Three Lakes.

1181 Rome: The twenty-second Verdi Competition.

1182 Iberia: Praeco Matlus of Jerbiton retires from Barcelona after 43 years playing "Louis the cloth merchant."

*He remains Praeco, of course.*

1182 Normandy: Moles Magna covenant, built within a dam of a minor

tributary of the River Yonne, is destroyed when the river burst the dam after heavy rain. Since the covenant had magically reinforced the dam against such a fate, it is assumed that they had angered a supernatural creature by restraining the river. 

1182 Provencal: Andru becomes Primus of Jerbiton, at the young age of 43.

*Many primi of this house are surprisingly young. Do the seniors of the House not strive for this position?*

1182 Stonehenge: Covenant Schola Pythangoranis founded in Norfolk by a group of Jerbiton, in the university town of Cambridge. Curiously, they devote themselves as much to mundane study as to magical work.

1182 Transylvania: Tremere conference at Coeris.

1183 Levant: At covenant Domus Pacis, Aurora Borealis of House Jerbiton has been following the career of Salah al-Din in unifying the Muslim world. Recognizing the danger to the Order, she contacts the caliph and his magical allies and travels to Baghdad to negotiate a possible treaty. Then she contacts the Quaesitors at Magvillus, offering a possible end to the hostilities, and they jump at the chance to impose some order on the chaotic Tribunal.

1185 Novgorod Tribunal: Charter for a new covenant in the Urals is granted, spurred on by the arguments of a newcomer to the Tribunal, the young Hungarian maga Malincka Capcek of House Bonisagus. In a narrow gorge above the headwaters of the Kama river, whose walls were dotted with literally hundreds of dry, spacious caves, connected by ledges, the Thousand Caves Covenant is swiftly settled.

*This covenant later fields controversial theories that rock Hermetic theory.*

*Malincka Capek eventually achieves a key insight into Volkhv magic; that it relies on faerie and mortal interaction, particularly worship. She brings these up at the 1214 Tribunal.*

1185 Thebes: Sicilians attack the Empire, taking Durazzo, and advancing down Via Egnatia. The covenant Thumachia was besieged and destroyed by Norman Sicilians led by William II, who terrorized the lands around Durazzo during their campaign. The surviving magi decided to join other covenants rather than rebuild Thumachia. In August Thessalonica sacked, in an atrocity noted for sacrilege and horror with over 5000 citizens slaughtered, and desecration of holy places. The covenant Oikos tou Eleous is founded by two holy magi in response to that outrage.

In retribution for 1014, House Tremere openly supports a Bulgarian revolt - leading to Bulgarian independence, and in response a few Greek magi announce their support for the emperor and form the League of Advisors, who took up residence in Constantinople to openly participate in the emperor’s court.

*They did not benefit monetarily from this association and claimed they only offered advice, mindfully treading the line between the permitted role of advisor and forbidden post of court wizard.*

1186 Normandy Tribunal: A maga convicted of a low crime in 1179 appeals, bringing evidence that she was innocent; the ruling is reversed.

1186 Hibernia Tribunal: Helles and Pollices, refugees from destroyed Moles Magna in Normandy, travel to Ireland after hearing it’s being settled by the Angevin Empire. Upon arrival, they discovered it is barely settled and that many of the barbaric native customs linger. Needing a home, they decide to civilize the Tribunal’s archaic legal traditions, considering it a worthy challenge to update Hibernia to 13th century standards. In a shocking demonstration of the Tribunal’s barbarity, Helles and Pollices chooses the disinterred corpse of Rhiannon, former Prima of House Merinita, as their cathach and announce their covenant of Ashenrise at the Tribunal meeting. Amidst uproar and declarations of Wizard War, the praeco Conán Derg approves the cathach. Some claim it isn’t a magical treasure, but Conán Derg says that the raw vis that has formed in the skeleton qualifies it as an appropriate cathach.

A maga Ex Miscellanea submits a written request that she have a mortal translator present for the Tribunal, as she spoke no Latin; the Tribunal refuses, notes that she should learn, and rules that no mortals should be present at Tribunal.

*Though it was difficult, Helles and Pollices defended the cathach for a year. Since then Ashenrise has pushed to reform the Tribunal. Helles leads the debate, although as yet he has had little impact.*

*See the Grand Tribunal of 1195 for an additional ruling on languages.*

1186 Iberia Tribunal: Magus Trentus of Flambeau is charged with killing at least one hundred mortals in several battles and raids with magic blatant and spectacular, thereby endangering the Order. Magus Trentus admits to slaying them, but that he had left no witnesses to identify him. He is acquitted.

1186 Levant Tribunal: Crusading magi accuse counter-Crusading magi of breaking the Code assisting the Muslims against them. Several covenants have been destroyed in the war. The Quaesitors find no wrong-doing, and no clear evidence is presented.

1186 Provencal Tribunal: The magi of Windgraven and Mistridge accuse each other of scrying. Mistridge had captured an invisible spy of Windgraven; Windgraven had captured two scouting grogs of Mistridge. Mistridge is acquitted, as it had used no magic to aid the grogs. Windgraven convicted for making their spy invisible and fined 20 pawns.

Magus Hammas convicted in absentia for kidnapping pilgrims, thereby interfering with mortals; punished with the loss of his familiar.

*The Tribunal notes that Windgraven might simply have slain the Mistridge spies without breaking the Code.*

1186 Rhine Tribunal: The Hawthorn Gild argued that Crintera’s wilderness had been invaded and was under extreme threat, and sought approval for a retaliatory strike, trying to invoke the "Guardians of the Forests" ruling. Despite support from the Ash Gild and (surprisingly) the Elder Gild, the Tribunal was split across factional lines, and no decision was made. However, it promised to investigate the affair further. This was insufficient for the more aggressive Wilderists, and a number of prosecutions against Bjornaer magi attacking Danish settlers followed, which most of the House considered to be just rubbing salt into their wounds.

Magus Antonio Manecis is accused of summoning a demon and negotiating with it; the magus claims he had done so to protect his covenant, and that the agreement was to assure the safety of the covenant so long as they did not interfere in its plots against mortals; convicted and Marched.

*The Tribunal notes that the magus’ intentions were worthy.*

1186 Thebes: 46th Theban Tribunal recognizes the covenant of Oikos tou Eleous. The office of the Logothete is altered to be a permanent position at this Tribunal, after Petronas elected for his eleventh term.

*The logothete is the Chief Redcap of the Theban Tribunal. The role of the logothete is to record all business of the Council of Magistrates, and to oversee all bureaucratic procedures. The logothete used to be an elected position chosen every seven years from among the Tribunal’s Redcaps.*

Charges are brought against Fulmen of Guernicus by a consortium of magi led by Archmagus Stregos. During the course of an investigation, Fulmen had discovered certain magical secrets of his target’s mystae. The magus was subsequently cast out for unrelated reasons. Fulmen needlessly disseminated this knowledge to his Hoplites. Archmagus Stregos claims that these secrets

were obtained through breaching the original target’s right to privacy, even though he was subsequently found guilty of a high crime. Fulmen does not contest the charge and apologizes to Stregos for his lapse in judgment. The

Presiding Quaesitor sets the punishment as the loss of his familiar. Heartbroken and disgraced Fulmen renounces his membership in House Guernicus, saving his Primus from expelling him.

*Fulmen joined House Jerbiton and established a good reputation as an advocate and legal advisor*

1186 Iberia: Farusca of Jerbiton becomes head of the council of Barcelona covenant.

1187 Levant: Salah al-Din (Saladin) decidedly defeats the crusader army at the Horns of Hattin near Tiberias, in July, taking many nobles prisoner and the kingdom’s holiest relic, a fragment of the True Cross, being seized. Jerusalem falls in October, and by the end of the year, only a few parts of the Latin kingdom remain in crusader hands. The Second Crusade is over.

1187 Normandy: Atsingani covenant founded as vassal of Montverte, by Aureliana of Ex Miscellanea, of a Byzantine tradition of wandering sorcerers and itinerant seers named Atsingani that once served the Latin Emperor Constantine IX but fell out of favor.

*This is a mobile covenant, consisting largely of soldiers of questionable repute, with wagons and tents. They make a living of sorts either by brigandage or by serving as mercenaries. Once they have stirred up enough trouble and resentment in their current resting place, they flee, only to set themselves up somewhere else where they will attract less attention.*

1187 Stonehenge: Magnantum covenant finally disintegrates under internal tensions, and the magi remove to new covenants or Tribunals.

1187 Stonehenge: Master Flumen of Tremere, Blackthorn covenant, passes into Final Twilight.

*Master Flumen is famous for capturing the Wild Hunt in 1154.*

1188 Levant: The covenant of Foothold is established by two french magi on a hill ten miles east of the coast of the Kingdom of Jerusalem, some ten miles north-east of Acre.

1188 Rhine: The town of Lübeck receives its charter in 1188 from Friedrich Barborossa, Emperor and King of Germany, which superseded a charter previously granted by Henry the Lion. The Emperor’s comprehensive charter details the town’s constitution and administration and includes laws on inheritance, fortifications, coinage, and taxes.

*Also see 1208.*

1189 Richard, later known as "the Lionheart," with the connivance of Philip II, defeats his father Henry II of England at Ballans and succeeds him as King of England, Duke of Normandy, and Count of Anjou.

1189 Iberia: Baruch becomes head Quaesitor of Iberia Tribunal.

1189 Hibernia: The Paruchia of Nerius covenant is founded on Little Saltee island as the tiny monastic tradition with the same name joins the Order of Hermes as part of House Ex Miscellanea.

*The Paruchia of Nerius was inspired by a vision that a Hermetic magus, Fedelmid of Limerick, had of the mysterious magus-saint Nerius while he was traveling on pilgrimage to Rome. In the Alps he stumbled upon a mysterious valley, where he had a series of bizarre visions that were to shape his life thereafter. Seeking out the secrets of Holy Magic he trained an apprentice, Indrechtach, who in turn taught a manaim of his monastery.*

1189: The Third Crusade begins. Many of the great mortal kings, princes, and nobles of the age join. It is generally successful, but ends after its forces dwindle away in a desert.

1189 Loch Leglean: Pact of Crun Clach forbids magi from dabbling in politics, aiding kings or invaders, and sets rules on raiding.

*Naturally, this Pact only reinforces the Code as it stands.*

C. 1190 Novgorod: Stolitzya "The Swimmer," a famous and respected maga of Bjornaer, loses herself in Final Twilight.

*Her giant turtle Heart-Beast is said to have roamed the marshes for decades.*

C. 1190 Normandy: Marie of France writes L’espurgatoire Seint Patriz in Norman French, recounting an otherworldly journey to Purgatory.

1190: Emperor Friedrich I Barbarossa grants Bolognese scholars freedom to move throughout the Holy Roman Empire and safe residences once they arrive at their destination.

*This initial royal grant of protection has been copied throughout western Europe, with many kings granting resident scholars royal protection, legal immunities, and travel privileges, regardless of their nationality.*

1190: Rabbi Moses ben Maimon (Maimonides) writes "The Guide for the Perplexed", a summa on Jewish philosophy and theology, heavily infused with Aristotelian ideas. It has led to controversy among Jewish scholars, and it has caused French and German Jews to vehemently argue with their Spanish colleagues. 

*Apparently they have yet to read the New Aristotle themselves. They have branded Maimonides a heretic and cast suspicions on all of his scholastic works. Anyway, it’s probably best if this work never gets translated into Latin.*

1190 Iberia: Avenzoar, on behalf of the Almohad caliph Abū Yūsuf Yaʿqūb al-Manṣūr, publish an edict in the main cities of al-Ándalus (Seville, Granada, Jaén, Málaga, Almería, Alcácer do Sal, etc.) where he not only order to deliver all the books of ancient sciences to the zalmedina of the place, but also condemn the use of magic and ask the citizens to participate in denouncing any magician before the alfaquíes. As a consequence, many magi from the south begin to leave al-Andalus.

1190 Hibernia: Many magi in Stonehenge feared what the hedge magicians of the Coill Tri might really represent, and that dark magic might underlie Ruaidrí’s reign. Why was Connacht preserved from the conquest? With the new mundane English presence in Ireland, an increasing number of English magi traveled through the Hibernian Tribunal, and many felt their suspicions were justified, especially after a party was attacked by what appeared to be young Irish magi of the Order. Knowing little or nothing of the Irish custom of the macgnímartha, this attack on Damisona of Jerbiton and her party, with the loss of three grogs, led to an increasing outcry through Stonehenge for something to be done about Hibernia.

*Young magi in Hibernia undertake the macgnímartha or youthful exploits: a*

*short period between apprenticeship and becoming a magus when the apprentice leaves the protection of his covenant and spends a year or more without the support of his parens. He has not yet sworn the Oath, does not know Parma Magica, and is considered outside Hermetic law. Most test their power against faeries, some engage mundanes, and others try their luck against Hermetic targets. In this last case, items successfully stolen from covenants are usually ransomed back. Remembering their own youthful exploits, magi rarely treat those in the macgnímartha harshly. Importantly, no mention is made of the macgnímartha in the treaty of Cnoc Maol Réidh and these youngsters often venture into Connacht for sport or fortune*

1190 Levant: The Order of Teutonic Knights is founded in Acre, in Palestine. Emperor Friedrich I Barbarossa dies on crusade, drowned while crossing the river Seleph on horseback.

*The river Calycadmus?*

C. 1190 Novgorod: Leczyca covenant fined heavily for providing Silesian nobles with intelligence about German raids.

*This was presumably at either the Tribunal of 1186 or 1194.*

1190 Rhine: At Triamore covenant, the apprentice Pietre is found to have scried upon maga Daria of Tremere. The boy flees before being punished. Pietre is expelled from the tutelage of his master, Aline of Bonisagus.

1190 Rhine: A fire at Triamore kills Forba of Bonisagus. Not long after, the covenant is besieged by the army of Henry of Brabant, a mortal lord who wishes for the covenant’s land and castle. The siege is broken through intervention of the new Emperor, Henry IV.

1190 Stonehenge: King Arthur’s tomb is found at Glastonbury Abbey, and is seen by Gerald of Wales, among others. The great king is reburied in a fine marble sepulcher behind the high altar.

*This year also saw a spate of attacks and massacres against Jews across England. All but a lucky few of the Jews of Norwich were killed in their homes, while the Jews of York fled to the apparent safety of a local castle. The castle was besieged by a mob demanding that they convert and be baptized, and the surrounded Jews decided instead to take their own lives. Those who could not bring themselves to suicide left the next morning and were murdered by the baying crowd. *

1191 Thebes: Cyprus captured by Richard the Lionheart, who sells it on to the master of Knights Templar, Robert de Sablé.

1191: Magus Avarret of Bonisagus elevated to Primus of his House, marking

a return to the conservative philosophy of reﬁning the existing Hermetic theory.

1191 Stonehenge: Monks at the Abbey of Glastonbury claim to have discovered the Tomb of King Arthur on Glastonbury Tor.

*The claim is disputed.*

1192 Levant: Peace is signed between Richard Coeur de Lion and Salah al-Din, and Aurora Borealis of House Jerbiton architect negotiations between the Order and Muslim leadership in Baghdad.

1192 Normandy: Cunfin covenant founded as a vassal of Confluensis, by Eloi of Jerbiton, in the Champagnois village which takes its name. Cunfin is home to both magi and monks in a Cistercian monastery.

*Magi have occupied the site for much longer than thirty years. This is an unusual arrangement, but it has proven to be a useful one as it allows the Order of Hermes to stay informed about matters within the Church that might concern them. Rumor persists that the monastery with which it shares its name was founded by Valoran, a student of St. Nerius, in 1136. *

1192 Provencal: Lariander covenant founded in a faerie forest, five Tremere and one Merinita.

1192 Transylvania: Tremere conference at Coeris.

1193: Most regional Tribunals are delayed one year to allow preparation for the Grand Tribunal.

1193 Iberia: At the conclusion of the ordinary Tribunal, a small number of magi stay in Harra to discuss how to stop the Almohads: the magi present agree to go to fight the Almohad armies in the next mundane war.

1193 Levant Tribunal: Quaesitors from Magvillus force the Levantine magi to accept the Treaty of Baghdad and hostilities with the Muslims are brought to an end.

*Crusading magi were now less able to justify breaches of the Code as responses to the threat from the Muslims, so their activities became more controlled. As conflict was now at an end, the counter-crusading magi were also forced to stop their interference. Magi who were later discovered intervening in mundane affairs were severely punished. For her part in the brokering of peace, Aurora was invited to become a Quaesitor, a duty that she accepted. To help maintain the treaty, she moved to Baghdad, where she founded a new covenant, the Hermetic Embassy. Soon after, she became an Archmaga.*

*According to the Muslim laws of jihad, truces should have a duration of no longer than ten years. As a result, the treaty has to be renewed every ten years.*

1193: The Third Crusade ends after being reduced by an ill-fated journey in the desert.

1194 Iberia Tribunal: A magus formerly of Barcelona covenant accused of theft of fifty marks during his departure; the covenant argues that such theft diminishes the covenant members ability to study; the Tribunal orders the magus to repay twice the amount taken.

Magus Trentus is charged with slaying a magus near but outside Trentus’ sanctum; Trentus claims the other was intending to steal from his sanctum and that he was defending himself; the Tribunal convicts, but instead of slaying him only takes his familiar.

The issue of support for combatants in the Reconquest of Hispania is debated, ending in dissension between Reconquista and Roman covenants.

*Magus Trentus may have been better served to wait until the mage had entered his sanctum, where he could have slain his enemy without fear.*

1194 Stonehenge Tribunal at Blackthorn inquorate and no representatives sent to the Grand Tribunal.

*Blackthorn was the host, so Voluntas sent no representatives. Schola Pythagoranis had little immediate interest in Hermetic politics, and no Redcap*

*had been able to find Semitae to announce the meeting. Burnham was deeply embroiled in local unrest, and sent no one, so that the Tribunal was declared void, and Stonehenge could not be represented at the Grand Tribunal. Very much to the embarrassment of the Tribunal!*

1194 Normandy Tribunal: Quaesitors bring proof that the maga convicted in 1179, acquitted in 1186, was kidnapped by mortals under the influence of Fudarus. Fudarus covenant is fined 24 pawns for misleading the Tribunal and wasting its time.

Magus Damon Le Mont complains that his former apprentice, now magus Aramin, had wasted six pawns of vis and destroyed mundane artifacts in anger, and demanded compensation from the new mage. The Tribunal rules that a mage is responsible for his apprentice's actions and that he has only himself to blame.

Magus William Fireheart of Flambeau, of Ungulus covenant in Stonehenge, accused of killing three knights by use of magic, enraging the nobility of Normandy and endangering the magi of the Tribunal. Magus William is fined sixty pawns against his Renunciation.

Magus William Fireheart is also accused of killing an apprentice of another magus and destroying valuable laboratory equipment in her care. William claims he was ignorant that she had been an apprentice and that she was killed and the equipment destroyed accidentally while she stood near a mortal he did seek to kill. The Tribunal rules that, intent aside, he had reduced the mage’s magical ability and fines him three pawns.

There is tense discussion at the Tribunal over Thomae of Tytalus, a renowned Hermetic Architect, slayer of beasts, magical mercenary and master of flight, who recently returned from a prolonged absence with the most audacious enchantment the Order has yet seen - a flying castle. This vast structure has caused great alarm in the Order as many elder magi feel that the nobility would never permit such an obvious threat to their rule - thereby being an obvious breach of the Code (not provoking the mundane). To avoid Wizard’s War, Thomae agrees to hide his castle in a distant range of mountains, away from the prying eyes of mundanes.

*The Quaesitors examined Thomae’s castle, but because it was considered his sanctum, even his fellow Architects did not visit it. Also see 1195 Grand Tribunal, and 1198.*

1194 Loch Leglean Tribunal: The Tribunal declares the Knights Hospitaller to be Enemies of the Order following a feud between those knights of the Priory of Torphichen and certain magi. 

*See the Grand Tribunal of 1195.*

1194 Greater Alps Tribunal: After slaying many faeries while seeking the release of valued grogs and covenfolk from a faerie lord, Valnastium covenant accused of molesting Fae. Valnastium argues that they have the right to protect their servants. They are narrowly acquitted.

Magus Syrestus of Bjornaer, of the Rhine Tribunal, accused of endangering his sodales while a guest of other covenants by killing the livestock of nearby mortals while in the shapes of several animals. Fined ten pawns. 

*See the Rhine Tribunal of 1194 and the appeal of 1195 for additional events on Syrestus. The Syrestus case leads to an important precedent in inter-Tribunal rulings.*

1194 Provencal Tribunal: Bentalone covenant accused of aiding a mortal lord against another; Bentalone claims that one mortal served their interests while the other opposed them, and they should be permitted to back one against the other. The Tribunal rules that so long as the magics used were not detected the Code was not violated.

The magi of Bellaquin are accused of preventing a Redcap from taking an announcement of the Tribunal to the magi of Lariander. The Tribunal refuses to condemn or punish Bellaquin.

*See the Grand Tribunal of 1195 for an additional ruling on the Redcap complaint. *

1194 Rhine Tribunal: A young Bjornaer maga from Fengheld called Falke made a passionate plea to permit Crintera to actively resist the occupiers of Rügen. The Quaesitor Caecilius was quite firm that no wilderness had been invaded or destroyed - Crintera and its forest remained intact, a serious setback for the landmark "Guardians of the Forests" ruling.

Maga Marguerite of Flambeau charged with endangering the Order for attacking a mortal baron and his household. Marguerite claims she had done so to punish the mortal for the murder of a Redcap. The Tribunal rules that Redcaps must be protected, and acquits.

Magus Taedetus of Bonisagus accused of stealing vis; he sends word that he cannot appear because of a delicate experiment requiring his attention, and asks for a postponement. The Tribunal rules to allow a delay to the next Tribunal, but threatens to fine him ten pawns if he does not bring proof of the need for a delay.

Magus Syrestus of Bjornaer, of the Rhine Tribunal, accused of endangering his sodales of the Greater Alps while a guest of other covenants by killing the livestock of nearby mortals while in the shapes of several animals. Fined three pawns. The Tribunal also notes that starting a feud with the baron would not improve relations.

*Stung by this rebuke, Crintera descends into a brief period of factional infighting. *

1194 Quaesitorial Ruling: Magus Syrestus appeals his convictions to the Quaesitors, saying that he should only be subject to the fine of his home Tribunal, where he was fined three pawns. The Quaesitors rule that he should pay the fine of the Greater Alps, where the crimes were committed, not the Rhine.

1195 Iberia: The Battle of Alarcos is fought between the Almohads led by Abu Yusuf Ya'qub al-Mansur and King Alfonso VIII of Castile. It results in the defeat of the Castilian forces and their subsequent retreat to Toledo, whereas the Almohads reconquer Trujillo, Montánchez, and Talavera.

*The magi who agreed to stop the Almohads in 1193 are killed.*

**1195 Sixteenth Grand Tribunal: **Regarding the declaration of Loch Leglean that the Knights Hospitaller are Enemies of the Order, the Grand Tribunal rules the declaration too broad, and that only the knights of Torphichen Priory be considered enemies.

The Grand Tribunal orders Bellaquin covenant to pay seventy pawns to House Mercere for interfering with a Redcap.

Avarret, Primus Bonisagus, proposes that Tribunals conduct business only in Latin; the Tribunal criticizes Loch Leglean for often conducting business in Gaelic, but refuses to compel Tribunals to conduct themselves in Latin. Regarding language, the Quaesitors note that they must use Latin to officiate.

Crintera’s appeal to deal with the mundane incursion on Rügen falls on deaf ears, the House is merely reminded in the strongest terms of its duty to uphold the Oath of Hermes.

It has become apparent that Thomae of Tytalus, against the agreements made at the last Normandy Tribunal, has been sneaking his flying castle out to sea in the dead of night, and traveling to desolate places in other Tribunals. However, none of his accusers can demonstrate that he had harmed the Order.

Survivors from the Levant blame counter-Crusading magi for the destruction of several covenants, saying that they had assisted the Muslim forces against them. However, no wrongdoing was found by Quaesitors and no definite evidence is presented.

There is a long debate between magi denouncing Hibernia as in need of reform, preferably the introduction of a Peripheral Code based upon that of Stonehenge, and many magi who favor the right of autonomy of a Tribunal and the right of its magi to set their own Peripheral Code. The parens of the apprentices who in 1190 in their macgnímartha had attacked Damisona were convicted of only a Low Crime, to the outrage of the Stonehenge magi.

*The Grand Tribunal events led to more traffic between Hibernia and the continent, and an increasing number of appeals to Magvillus from magi who felt they had been unfairly treated by local Quaesitors. *

*This was the smallest Tribunal in centuries, as Stonehenge was not properly represented, other than by Prima Ex Miscellanea.*

Eule of Fengheld presents evidence of the hand of four Roman magi of covenant Luctatio, all of House Tytalus, in assisting the Milanese in defeating Barbarossa at the Battle of Legnano in 1176. At their behest, a necromancer from the covenant of Vardian’s Tomb created the plague that halted the emperor’s army at the gates of Rome. Five magi of the Roman Tribunal are Marched.

1195 Iberia: Harra is destroyed in unknown circumstances.

1195 Hibernia: In the immediate aftermath of the Grand Tribunal, the Covenant of Elk’s Run is founded, close to the border with Connacht, by Oswald of Bonisagus and Lugardis of Guernicus. Oswald used wit and trickery to successfully challenge the local magi for the right to settle, and a vast tract of land for vis sources.

*Oswald came from the Stonehenge Tribunal, and his friend Lugardis was clearly a Quaesitor seeking to impose English rule over the Order.*

1195 Normandy: Prima Buliste of House Tytalus enters a protracted Twilight and is declared dead by Harpax, her Hermetic brother, who wins the privilege of serving as the tenth Primus.

*Harpax had been previously defeated by her for the leadership of the House. *

1195 Stonehenge: Quaesitor Trutina joins Burnham covenant.

1196 Rome: Quaesitor Tamgor tours the Roman Tribunal.

C. 1196 Provencal: Maga Consuelia, founder of Mistridge covenant, vanishes on the slopes of Mount Tierne. The maga apparently went there willingly, and no evidence of violence is noted.

1197 Normandy: Alcuin’s Auberge covenant, vassal of Florum, a small Mercere House in the city of Tours, draws unwanted attention and falls to unknown raiders.

*Some believe Fudarus sent the ruffians, but it has not been proven.*

1197 Provencal: The covenant of Windgraven (1060–1197), famed for its cruelty and the necromantic prowess of its magi, is destroyed by Mistridge after having declined far into Winter.

*Few mourned the passing of its reclusive magi, who were widely suspected of diabolism, though this action may have inspired Tres’ destruction of Mistridge shortly thereafter.*

1198: Pope Celestine III dies in Rome. Innocentius III is named as the 176th pope.

1198: Last known sighting of Thomae of Tytalus, the flamboyant creator of the most audacious enchantment the Order has ever seen - a flying castle. It caused quite a stir in the last Grand Tribunal.

*There are many stories of his fate. Some say that he left for Transylvania to work secretly on weapons platforms for the Tremere. Some claim he has gone exploring. Some say that the Devil claimed him for his hubris and that his castle fell away as dust and ash, leaving Thomae to plummet to his death. Many factions are interested in finding Thomae’s castle and its secrets.*

1198 Normandy: Buliste recovers from her three year Twilight (as some suspected she would) and tries to resume her former position as Prima of House Tytalus. Characteristically, Harpax refuses to relinquish control to his rival sibling and a protracted battle ensues.

*No victor has yet emerged decades later. All other full magi have left the covenant of Fudarus (domus magna of the House), which is now completely divided between them.*

*House members supporting the tenth Primus Harpax are "Decimi" and as staff wear revolutionary green, and loyalists of the ninth Prima Buliste are "Fideli" and as staff wear royal purple. Refusing to choose a side invites scorn.*

1198 Normandy: John of Matha and Felix of Valois founds the mendicant Order of the Most Holy Trinity and of the Captives (or Trinitarians) outside Paris. The founding-intention for the order was the ransom of Christians held captive by Muslims, a consequence of crusading and of pirating along the Mediterranean coast of Europe.

1198 Levant: A new Crusading order is founded by merchants from Bremen and Lübeck to defend a hospital in Acre. The Order of Brothers of the German House of Saint Mary in Jerusalem, the Teutonic Knights, is open only to Germans.

1198 Rome: Quaesitor Jarane travels to Rome to investigate the activities of the Tribunal covenants. His investigations and intervention amount to little.

C. 1199 Stonehenge: The monastery of Saint Sylvester in West Brockshire is burned by the vile outlaw captain Owen the Beast.

*Rumor has it is now haunted by Infernal spirits. No one goes there, not even magicians.*

1199 Normandy: Celeres of Bonisagus, an ancient and eccentric magus, arrives at Cunfin covenant and announce that he’s seeking the Holy Grail, and that Cunfin will be the castle from which he and his knights will search for it.

*This proclamation is surely the final proof of Celeres’ madness!*

1199 Rome: The twenty-third Verdi Competition.

C. 1200 Iberia: The number of magi migrating from the Tribunal is increasing. A concern that is addressed in the ordinary Tribunal of the same year.

1200–1220: Annoyed that the Biblical Commandment to keep Sabbath day holy is so widely and blatantly ignored in England, the pope sends Eustace, Abbot of Flay to lead a mission. So eloquent was his preaching, and so marvelous were

the miracles that he performed, that many places closed down their Sunday markets entirely or moved them to another day of the week.

*Another lesson for my sodales to not wake the ire of the church.*

1200: A new crusade is announced against the pagans of the Baltic coast. A new order, the Knights of the Sword, is organized to prosecute the crusade.

1200 Normandy: After a terrible outbreak of "town versus gown" rivalry resulted in the death of several students and citizens, the University of Paris receives its charter, from Philip II. He issues a diploma "for the security of the scholars of Paris", which affirms that students are subject only to ecclesiastical jurisdiction.

*This surely leads to lingering resentment among the burghers of Paris. The chancellor of Notre Dame, Phillipus de Grevia, retains the power to confer degrees and excommunicate members of the university, and he uses these rights to maintain his influence over the university masters.*

1200–1201 Normandy: Atramentum covenant, vassal of Fudarus, is founded by Helios of House Tytalus and Gulo Midusulfis of House Bjornaer and ousted by Fudarus magi the following year.

*Its founding was sponsored just as the battle between the two Tytalan Primi began. Cynics would say that the young magi were duped into preparing the ground for the magi of Exspectatio, who now occupy the spot. The founders went on to form Atramentum Renatus.*

1200 Iberian Tribunal: The subject of support for Christian and Moorish mortals again ends the Tribunal in dissension between Reconquista and Roman covenants.

1200 Stonehenge Tribunal at Blackthorn inquorate.

*Blackthorn called the scheduled Tribunal of Stonehenge, but again no one attends.*

1200 Levant Tribunal: Renewal of the Treaty of Baghdad due in 1203 is fervently discussed, but agreed.

1200 Normandy Tribunal: Maga Pugnatis found guilty of interfering with mortals, in that she had used magic to control the mayor of Laval to obtain access to a vis source. She was ordered to enchant items to allow magi to hide from them.

*The finer details of her crime are curiously absent.*

The magi of Fudarus are claimed to have brought ruin upon the magi of Atramentum by sending an agent to form an artificer guild in the town of Le Conquet. This, it was claimed, ruined the magi of Atramentum - as guild politics interrupted the work of artisans in the town whom they had commissioned to manufacture quality items for enchantment, which in turn delayed Atramentum’s ability to collect vis from a newly discovered source. The Tribunal rules that there is no case to answer. The basis being that Le Conquet is closer to Fudarus than Atramentum, and therefore Fudarus’s activities in the town has precedence. *Effectively, the Tribunal ruled, the magi of Atramentum had "ruined" themselves by relying on the work of mundane artificers, and not long afterwards the magi of Atramentum were evicted by senior magi leaving Fudarus.*

Tribunal determines that the agents of the covenant closest to a town, referred to as the senex, have superior rights to those of the agents of other covenants operating in that town. In particular, this means that if the actions of the senex collaterally damage the interests of another covenant, then ruin has not been caused. The senex is judged to be the covenant in which the town’s church bells sound the loudest, as determined by a Quaesitor. Therefore, if a town’s church bells cannot be heard at all in a covenant that covenant is too distant from the town to claim senex rights, and for many towns therefore there is no extant senex.

1200 Thebes Tribunal: Magus Aescliops of House Jerbiton, itinerant, is noted to have marked any room he resided in as his sanctum; the Tribunal rules that although no mage may mark a covenant room as a sanctum without permission, any mage may mark a sanctum for any period, so long as it marks his true residence. 

*Compare to the ruling of the Greater Alps Tribunal, 1165.*

1200 Rhine: Covenant Rheinstein (1123–1200), founded on an island in the Rhine Gorge close to the border between Bohemia and Meissen is destroyed by an irate mob of mundanes rumored to be led by the archbishop of Trier. 

*Or by influential merchants. What the magi did to upset the locals remains a mystery to this day.*

*The demises of Rudiaria, Oakdell, Treverorum and Rheinstein are believed by some to be linked to a curse on the region of the Rhine Gorge, an area that is rich in magical sites, but where no covenant has endured.*

1200 Rhine Tribunal: Covenant Fengheld complains that Maga Odorpes of Bjornaer had failed to collect and provide six pawns of vis per season as she had agreed; Odorpes argues that she had provided six pawns each season except for Winter, when she hibernated in the shape of her heart-beast, a bear; Tribunal rules that she is still responsible for her obligations, since she chose to spend her winters as a bear, and must make up the difference within seven years.

Horst of House Mercere reports that Rheinstein covenant was attacked and destroyed by an army of irate mundanes, led by the archbishop of Trier. The fate of the magi of Rheinstein is unknown. Horst notes that their actions have in large part contributed to their demise, and may have caused undue attention to the Order of Hermes in the region. 

After the destruction of Rheinstein, yet another covenant lost in the Rhine Gorge, the Tribunal rules that magi are forbidden from practicing any form of magical activity within the area of the Rhine Gorge for the next twenty years.

1200 Rome: Rebelling Italian cities ask for Hermetic aid, offering silver and patronage if they aid in their battles.

1201: The Fourth Crusade to the Levant begins, poorly. The crusade gathers at Venice, but lacks the money for passage. The doge agrees to transport them if the crusaders will help him return the city of Zara to his control and give him half of any booty.

1201 Normandy: Exspectatio covenant, vassal of Fudarus, is founded in place of the failed covenant Atramentum.

Atramentum Renatus covenant is founded, vassal of Exspectatio.

1201 Stonehenge: Maximianus founds covenant Nigrasaxa in Suffolk, and together with Voluntas, Schola Pythagoranis and Ungulus, calls for a second Tribunal.  This gathering is quorate, and Blackthorn, worried that it would lose

control, agrees to host it. Blackthorn thus maintains its dominance, but politics are active in the Tribunal once again.

1201 Stonehenge Tribunal: Covenant Voluntas contests covenant Blackthorn over 12 pawns of vis, as they had a bargain with Robert Wood of Yorkshire, an exceptional archer and woodsman that they had registered in 1192 as a source of Animal and Herbam vis.

The local Redcap network is reorganized to prevent further inquorate meetings.

*Each Stonehenge Tribunal thereafter has been a year late. *

1202 King John of England fails to respond to a summons to attend a French court; Philip II of France claims Anjou and invades Normandy, and supports the claims of Arthur of Brittany against John.

*This marks a turning point in the struggles between the Angevins and Capetians; thereafter, Philip II holds the upper hand in most of France.*

1202: To the chagrin of House Tremere, at the Venetians' request (as payment for shipping) crusaders sack Zadar (Zara), the most prosperous seaport on the Croatian coastline. Prince Alexius, Byzantine exile, offers crusaders aid and wealth in exchange for his restoration to the throne of the Empire. The whole of the Fourth Crusade is excommunicated by the pope for the sack of Zadar.

1202 New Slavonic Tribunal: Covenant Rethra in Pomerania, Oracle of the god Radegast, is destroyed by the Knights of the Sword.

*This covenant fell out of favor in Rhine by allying itself with the New Slavonic Tribunal in the 11th century. It entered a deep Winter for many decades, with no contact with other magi.*

1202 Rhine: The Prima of Merinita, Vinaria of Irencillia, disappears in the woods, much the same way as Merinita and Quendalon had done. Handri of Irencillia resumes the Primacy.

*She returned in 1209.*

1202 Rome: Leonardo Bigollo Pisano (Fibonacci) writes Liber Abaci, about the modus Indorum, the Hindu-Arabic numeral system, including the use of zero; it is the first major work in Europe to move away from the use of Roman numerals.

1202 Thebes: Catella of Bonisagus joins the Covenant of Alexandria

1203: Crusaders besiege Constantinople, Emperor Alexios III Angelos flees into exile. Alexios IV Angelos and Isaac II Angelos proclaimed joint emperors, but fail to meet promises made to the crusaders camped outside the city. By August. tensions between Byzantines and crusaders have mounted to sporadic fighting, and a skirmish leads to the Great Fire of Constantinople.

*Ignem magics at work?*

1203 Normandy: A mortal monk named Eustace puts aside his vows to inherit his father’s lands. It is said that in earlier years Eustace the Monk had learned magic in the city of Toledo.

*See 1205.*

1203 Rhine: Urgen of Crintera resigns as Primus of Bjornaer, but retains a seat on the Bjornaer council; Falke is elected Prima in his place.

1203 Transylvania: House Tremere build a zenodochium in a regio in a

magical lacuna found in the city of Zadar, that after its sacking suffered from violence and lawlessness. Anticipating more fighting, the building is staffed by a competent surgeon and a few assistants. The House loses contact with the men several months later and the regio itself seems to have disappeared.

*Did all the money, murder, and malcontents draw demonic attention?*

1204: Constantinople falls to the Fourth Crusade, and is sacked. Alexios and David Komnenos, long time exiles in Georgia, seize Trebizond, creating the Kingdom of Trebizond. Constantinople is taken again, and the Latin Empire created. William de Champlitte founds Principality of Achaea following the great Battle of the Olive Grove. The Latin Empire attacks exiles who have created a new capital at Nicea, smashing the Nicene forces at the Battle of Adramyttion. Venice claims the most strategically important ports: those of Candia, Rodos, Karpathos, and the Cyclades

*Once boasting three covenants, the fall of the mighty City of the East sees the end of them all. Thermakopolis is destroyed utterly, and its only survivor, Mathieus, is in self-imposed exile. Moero’s Garden flees to Nicaea. The Ex Miscellanea traveling covenant the Court of Fools is unfortunately caught in the city and destroyed in the fires. *

The remaining crusaders of the Fourth Crusade disband after being decimated by plague and desertions. The crusade had been foiled by morale problems, lack of funding, and poor leadership. It has sacked two Christian cities, been excommunicated, and failed to impact the Saracens at all.

*Thus demonstrating that crusades can be fragile.*

*In the aftermath, accusations flew as rapidly as arrows. House Tremere was blamed, as was House Jerbiton. The League of Advisors, which had lost many of its members with the fall of Thermakopolis, couldn’t weather allegations of interference with mundanes, and disbanded. *

1204 Normandy: Eboris covenant founded in Paris by Archmagus Mathieus of Constantinople, follower of Jerbiton and "most prominent witness to the death of history", as a commemoration of the glory of the Constantinople that was; the new Rome, the center of civilization and the tool of God - meant to usher in an age of peace and stability in preparation for the return of Christ.

1205: Pope Innocentius III lays down the principle that Jews are doomed to perpetual servitude, because they had crucified Jesus. Therefore, it is desirable that the Jews remain in Christian society. As long as the Jews maintain their submission, it reminds the world of their crime in rejecting Jesus and the validity of Jesus' teachings.

1205: The Sultanate besieges Trebizond but is forced to withdraw. Greek nobles of Thrace rise in revolt, and Bulgarians under Tsar Kaloyan invade the Latin Empire. Bulgarians capture and kill Emperor Baldwin I. Most members of the covenant of Xylinites flee into exile in Western Europe from Constantinople.

Following the death of Doge Dandolo, Doge Pietro Ziani becomes leader of Venetian Republic.

Battle of the Olive Grove, and subsequent creation of the state of Epiros by defeated rebel Michael Komnenos Doukas.

1205–1207 Thebes: Construction of wall around Venetian Quarter of Constantinople, under Podesta Marino Zeno.

1205–1206 Levant: With the Byzantine refugees comes several Theban magi, also seeking to establish themselves further east. The Tribunal of Thebes supports its magi, and so the border between Thebes and the Levant becomes disputed territory. War threatens to break out between the two Tribunals. 

C. 1205: Ruprecht de Solange of House Tremere, Leczya covenant, claims his sigil. Frania Popovich of Tremere, also of Leczya, is also a sigil-holder, making Leczya a notable center of power in House Tremere.

1205: Amaury de Bène (Amalric of Bena), magister at University of Paris and who taught theological philosophy, dies of grief by humiliation after his doctrines were condemned by the university and, on a personal appeal to Pope Innocentius III, the sentence was ratified, and being ordered to return to Paris and recant his errors.

*See 1209.*

1205 Novgorod: Most recent claimed sighting of Stolitzya "The Swimmer," by Susan of Wicklow.

1205 Normandy: Eustace the Monk quarrels with the Count of Boulogne and enters the service of John of England as a sea captain. He spends several years plaguing the Count with his ship, cunning, and magic.

*See 1212.*

1206 Stonehenge: A peasant named Thurkhill in England is taken by Saint Julian on a tour of Purgatory. Thurkhill confides colorful descriptions of Purgatory's torture chambers.

*Remarkable!*

1206 Novgorod: Yannes Kolski suffers a prolonged Twilight and issues an extended series of prophecies, the Yannes Discourse.

*This may have been his Final Twilight, but the issue is not settled. Magus Yannes disappeared in 1216, while returning from Thousand Caves. His fate remains a mystery.*

1207 Iberia Tribunal: Laertia of Jerbiton reports to the Tribunal on shamanism and the spirit world.

Just before the close of the Tribunal, Terrace of Jerbiton brings illegally obtained evidence of diabolism. Baruch of Guernicus uses Summoning the Distant Image to examine the grounds of Jaferiya. Magi of Jaferiya covenant cast out for diabolism. The magi of Jaferiya escape the Tribunal through magic and diabolic aid.

*No known penalty assigned to Terrace or Baruch.*

1207 Levant Tribunal: Renewal of the Treaty of Baghdad due in 1213 is barely agreed upon.  The war between Thebes and Levant Tribunals is averted through the efforts of Aurora Borealis and other Quaesitors from both Tribunals. Now the Empire of Nicaea is considered part of the Theban Tribunal, while the Empire of Trebizond falls under the sway of the Levant.

*Not all magi are happy with this outcome, however.*

1207 Normandy Tribunal (49th): Maga Pugnatis, having failed to produce the items she was ordered to by the Tribunal of 1200, and failing to present herself to the Tribunal, is expelled from the Order.

The Tribunal determines that a covenant may be sited in a town, and the normal interaction of covenant members with the townsfolk does not thereby cause ruin to other members of the Order of Hermes. Further, the Tribunal determines that a covenant may be granted a town charter by a mundane lord. However, the magi themselves cannot take advantage of any privileges under the charter, as to do so would be effectively the same as swearing an oath of fealty. It is acceptable, however, for a covenant to collectively pay a tax, either in coin or mundane produce.

The Tribunal also deems it acceptable for eligible members of a covenant to vote or hold office in a town.

*Whatever the town charter might state, a magus, however, is not eligible as under the Code of Hermes he cannot swear an oath of fealty to mundane authorities!*

1207 Provencal Tribunal: Held at Aedes Mercurii, the Tribunal spends a whole day discussing the implications of the Church’s attempts to suppress Catharism. Ultimately no firm decision is made, although Praeco Oxioun of Tytalus, leader of Aedes Mercurii, speaks at length about the need to avoid interfering in mundane affairs. Oxioun, Chief Quaesitor Protanus, and the large bloc of Mercurian magi seem content to wait out developments from their mountain sanctuaries despite the misgivings of the lowland covenants of the Toulousain, Narbonnais, and the Rhone Valley. 

*A few months after the Tribunal, the papal legate is murdered near St Gilles and Pope Innocent III declares a crusade against the heretics of southern France. Shortly thereafter Protantus, the Traditionalist Chief Quaesitor, dies and is replaced by the more flexible Tibaut, a Transitionalist without Mercurian affiliations. The coincidence passes unnoticed to most magi, although the deaths mark the beginning of great changes in the mundane and Hermetic politics of Provençal.*

1207 Thebes: 49th Theban Tribunal. To the disappointment of House Tremere, the Tribunal fails to secure a conviction against members of the former League of Advisors for interference with the mundane.  \
Redcap Cosmas ostracized, possibly for dynastic connections.

Petronas abdicates as logothete, after almost a hundred years on the post, he is replaced by Leontius.

Boniface of Montferrat, ruler of Thessalonica, killed in battle by Bulgarians. Tsar Kaloyan is killed by St. Demetrius outside the walls of Thessalonica. Tsar Boril succeeds him to the Bulgarian throne. Podesta Zeno is recalled to Venice appointment by Venetian Council of new Podesta, Ottaviano Quirino. Marco Sanudo captures the island of Naxos.

Covenant of Novocastrum founded on the western border of Thessalonica.

Covenant of Michael’s Tabula in Constantinople formally recognized, it was founded by surviving Flambeau and Jerbiton magi in the catacombs beneath the city, forever pledging their lives to its defense.

*Also see Levant Tribunal this year.*

1207 Rhine Tribunal: Hygwald Veritus of Tremere and Eric Ribecus of Tytalus declare themselves to be the only remaining magi of Terschelling as the others had elected to leave. The covenant was to be renamed Waddenzee (indicative of a wider and more ambitious claim of notional Hermetic territory), and its charter and symbol had changed. This caused uproar in the Tribunal, with the two magi being accused of all sorts of malfeasance, but the Quaesitors who investigated could not uncover any evidence of wrongdoing.

*An uncommon alliance of Houses indeed.*

1207–1214 Iberia: The Shadow Wars. Diabolic Flambeau, "The Flaming Shadow," in Iberia are hunted and gradually eliminated. The Flaming Shadow destroys two covenants and slay most of their inhabitants by the Tribunal of 1214. Fifty magi are slain by the Shadow in seven years, and only three of the renegades are destroyed.

1207 Provencal: The summer covenant of Bentalone (1106–1207) is destroyed by the armies of Simon de Montfort when the castle they inhabited was mistaken for a Cathar stronghold and overrun in a surprise attack.

1208 Thebes: Ruler of Nicea crowned as Emperor Theodore II Lascaris. Leo Sgouros, Greek rebel leader, commits suicide as defeated at the fortress of Acrocorinth.

1208 Rhine: Lübeck’s town charter law was granted to Rostock, by Prince Borwin of Mecklenburg, and their Baltic towns also use Lübeck’s law. The Teutonic Knights, a landowning monastic military order, are suspicious of Lübeck’s charter and promote their own, less autonomous, charter to Baltic towns.

1208 Stonehenge Tribunal: Glenius of Bonisagus reports on encounters with shamans in Novgorod.

The Tribunal rules that no covenant may put more than two pounds of magically created silver per resident, magus or mortal, into circulation.

*The ruling did not cover gold, since gold is not used as currency in England. However, this has brought the issue to the attention of magi throughout Europe, and so most members of the Order practice moderation when using magic to create wealth, to avoid creating a precedent for a Grand Tribunal ruling.*

1208 Provencal: The Albigensian Crusade declared against the Cathars in Languedoc. These Cathars are heretics in the eyes of the Church, the dispute concerning whether the world and its creator are intrinsically evil.

*How different from the Hermetic view.*

1208 Iberia: Archmagus Karendos forms a group of Flambeau to hunt his filius, Rasus, a Renounced magus of Jaferiya. He confronts Rasus in this year, but the renegade calls on demons to oppose his Pater and escapes. Karendos suffers Twilight and must retire from the hunt.

1208 Stonehenge: The mortal kingdom of England is placed under an interdiction by Pope Innocentius III, forbidding the administration of the sacraments, for the interference of King John in Church elections.

1209 (February): The Franciscan Order is founded by the Italian priest Francis of Assisi. He and 11 of his followers journey to Rome where he receives approval of his rule from Pope Innocentius III. Franciscan friars can not own any possessions of any kind. Instead, they wander and preach among the people, helping the poor and the sick. They support themselves by working and by begging for food, but they are forbidden to accept money either for work or as alms. The Franciscans work at first in Umbria and then in the rest of Italy.
