*The impact of these street preachers and especially of their founder is immense, within the next ten years they number some 5,000 followers.*

1209 Hibernia: The maga Swan of Ghent is killed during the Black Monday massacre when Ua Broin raiders killed a number of English settlers outside the walls of Dublin.

1209 Normandy: Four years dead, ten of Amaury of Béne’s followers are burnt before the gates of Paris and Amaury's own body is exhumed and burnt and the ashes given to the winds.

*The doctrines of his followers, known as the Amalricians, were formally condemned by the fourth Lateran Council in 1215.*

1209 (October 21): Pope Innocentius III crowns Otto IV as emperor of the Holy Roman Empire at St. Peter's Basilica in Rome.

1209 Provencal: The city of Bezier is destroyed in the Albigensian Crusade.

The covenant of Mistridge (1050-1209), occupying a magical tower situated in the Val du Bosque, is destroyed in a Wizard’s War led by the magus Tres with his magical siege engines, spurring a major Tribunal case. 

1209 Rhine: Mystifyingly, the former Prima of Merinita, Vinaria of Irencillia, returns after 7 years disappeared.

*She makes no attempt to resume the primacy.*

1209 Stonehenge: King John is excommunicated for interfering in Church elections.

1209 Thebes: Sultan Kaikosru I begins war on the Empire of Nicaea, who ally with the Bulgarians. William de Champlitte of Principality of Achaea dies traveling back to

France, leaving Geoffrey de Villehardouin as regent, and then ruler.

1210: A special synod at Sens decreed that the philosophy of the New Aristotle and all commentaries on those works were not to be read at Paris in public or in secret, under threat of excommunication.

*This ban was repeated in 1215 specifically for the University of Paris, and there are rumors that the pope is planning to establish a commission to expurgate doctrinal errors from Aristotle.*

1210 Iberia: Matlus of Jerbiton, Praeco of the Iberian Tribunal, is found dead with ash on his hands and a circle of ash on his right temple. Cause of death unknown. Terrace of Jerbiton, now eldest, elevated to Praeco.

1210 Stonehenge: Foundation of Libellus, in Suffolk.

1210 Rome: The Holy Roman Emperor is excommunicated by the pope for his military adventures in southern Italy, but the Emperor finishes his conquest of the territory.

1210: Two Seekers, Alphonsus and Tabitha Ex Miscellanea, tour Ultima Thule.

1211: The Teutonic Order of Knights moves to Transylvania to help defend the south-eastern borders of the Kingdom of Hungary against the Cumans. 

1211 Provencal: Lariander (1192–1211), a Tremere spring covenant in a faerie forest at Montgey, Toulouse, is destroyed during the crusade. The magi were mistaken for a community of Cathars after a party of three hundred German crusaders were murdered by rebels as they passed through the forest while in the vicinity of the covenant.

*A terrible example that even the Order can fall victim to the crusaders violence even with non-involvement.*

1211 Thebes: Theodore Lascaris of Nicea defeats the Sultanate in battle, killing Sultan Kaikosru I and capturing the exile Alexios III. Latin Patriarch Tomas Morasini dies; the post remains vacant till 1215.

1212 Iberia: The tide of Reconquista crests in fire and steel: the Battle of Las Navas de Tolosa, followed by the Siege of Badajoz. The Christian kingdoms of Iberia: Castile, Aragon, Navarre, and Portugal - form an uneasy but effective alliance under the blessing of Pope Innocentius III. United by threat more than faith, they march south against the Almohad Caliphate, whose power has grown intolerably bold under the self-styled “Commander of the Faithful,” Caliph Muhammad al-Nasir. On July 16th, beneath the scorched Andalusian sky at Las Navas de Tolosa, the armies of Christendom break the back of Almohad strength in Iberia. Despite near-impossible terrain, political bickering, and treacherous supply lines, the Christian host - led by King Alfonso VIII of Castile - pierces the Caliph’s personal guard. 

Later this year, seizing the opportunity, Christian forces besiege Badajoz, a key Almohad stronghold guarding the borderlands. The city holds out through autumn, but by winter’s edge it yields. The fall of Badajoz opens the western flank of al-Andalus to Christian advance.

The Tribunal of Iberia, ever sensitive to political storms, watch the battle lines carefully. Redcaps report heavy magical interference - mirages disorienting scouts, cursed weather over the mountain passes, and an inexplicable silence in some areas. 

*Legend insists that Navarrese knights, including King Sancho VII "the Strong", shattered the iron circle of slave-warriors with the sheer brutality of their charge. The defeat irreparably fractures Almohad authority. Though al-Nasir escapes, his army does not. Within a generation, the Caliphate collapses into internal strife.*

*Certain Flambeau (Magi of the Flaming Shadow) and Tytalus magi are rumored to have participated - discreetly - as "consultants" to various noble houses, their spells masked behind papal indulgence. A lost correspondence from an unnamed magus in the Order hints that a powerful spirit or jinn bound to the Caliph’s army was unbound at Las Navas, and its vengeance remains unfinished.*

1212 Provencal: Children's Crusade: Thousands of children from all over the Rhineland march on the Holy Land, eventually sold into slavery in the city of Marseilles.

*They claimed the sea would part, allowing them to cross and convert the Muslims of North Africa before marching on to Jerusalem. When they reached Marseilles, the sea did not part, but a large number boarded ships to sail to Africa, and were never seen again.*

1212 Thebes: Marco Sanudo completes conquest of Cyclades. Venetians defeat pirates on Candia, establishing the Kingdom of Candia.

The newly established covenant of Novocastrum is destroyed as Epiros attacks the Kingdom of Thesssaloniki.

*It had little time to prepare for the Greek attack, and was destroyed when its*

*magi unsuccessfully resisted; few in the Tribunal mourned their loss.*

1212 Transylvania: Tremere conference at Coeris.

1212 Novgorod: Three Lakes Covenant celebrates its bicentenary, and decides to take on apprentices. They are attentively trained, and flourish.

*This dedicated training is rare and laudable.*

1212 Rhine: Magus Carolus of Jerbiton from Oculus Septentrionalis covenant in Lübeck visits Crintera, the domus magna of Bjornaer, in an attempt to repair relations with the Rügen covenant. The visit turns sour when his raven familiar, believed to be spying, is blinded by Caprea of Bjornaer - a guest maga there, when it enters the covenant library.

*As a result, relations between the two covenants have further worsened.*

1212 Stonehenge: King John of England and the Count of Boulogne make an alliance. The sea-captain Eustace the Monk, the Count’s enemy and John’s former man, departs John’s service, enters the service of Prince Louis of France, and bedevils both the king and count with his wits and magic.

*See 1215.*

1213: Before slipping into Final Twilight, Primus Avarret of House Bonisagus selects maga Murion as his successor.

*This is a deliberate snub to the arguably more qualified Occultes (who he seemed to have deemed too dangerous), and Murion’s selection signifies the continued dominance of the conservative philosophy in the House.*

1213–1214 Hibernia: The Siege of Praesis: Holzner of Tytalus from the Rhine Tribunal joined the Covenant of Praesis in 1213. A while later, he attempted to steal the cathach of Praesis and flee, but was slain by his sodales. The maga Meadhbh, leader of Praesis, hung his head by its hair from the covenant’s palisade. Word of Holzner’s humiliation reached his parens, Ballack, who then left the Rhine to seek justice in Hibernia. He convinced others that if the Tribunal would not sanction Praesis then they should take the covenant by force. Declaring Wizard War, Ballack struck quickly, but the attack was resisted, and he laid siege to the covenant preventing Meadhbh from attending the Tribunal meeting in 1214, where they might no doubt have appealed for and gained allies. While the covenants of Hibernia debated, the war continued. The sides seemed evenly matched, man-for-man and magic-for-magic, but Ballack’s nerve held out longer. As the last of the Praesis’ allies fled, Báetáin Ua Traimair of Praesis negotiated a peace with Ballack; the cathach in return for safe passage for  Meadhbh. The terms were accepted and Ballack took control of Praesis. Meadhbh claimed sanctuary at the covenant of Qui Sonant Pro Quieto.

*Hibernia’s Peripheral Code does not recognize any need for a break between months of Wizard War. Indeed it assumes that it has been extended unless there is positive evidence that it has not, meaning that in practice it continues until the death of an opponent, surrender, or a treaty.*

*Many Hibernian magi saw this war as a clear cut case of foreign aggression, but by the conventions of the Tribunal, few intervened. Relations between Hibernian magi and those from outside the Tribunal are increasingly strained. Praesis has fallen, and peace may be restored, but it is an uneasy peace.*

*Once the shining example of friendship between the druids of Ireland and the Order, to some the covenant now represents the dangers posed by outsiders, while to others it demonstrates that Hibernia itself needs to change. *

1213 Stonehenge: King John is absolved by the archbishop he had opposed. The king becomes a Papal vassal and all his lands become Papal fiefs.

1214: The Archmagus Hadrianus of Flambeau and his two filii depart Bergen for the remote Norse colony of Iceland, to investigate recent rumors of the "Order of Odin". He’s not heard from again.

*For over a century rumors of a powerful rival order of Norse wizards have circulated within the Order of Hermes. These rumors were given credence by the mysterious destruction of a number of spring covenants founded in Scandinavian lands. A number of Hermetic investigations have headed to the northlands, only to find the occasional weak hedge wizard who knew nothing of any "Order of Odin." This lack of success only heightened the paranoia among many Hermetics. It seems as if this Order of Odin - if they even exist - possess potent powers of disguise and concealment, perhaps even Infernally granted.*

1214 Provence: A mysterious manuscript circulates among Jewish mystics, containing advanced theories of numerology, divine letters, and creation magic. It is said to reveal the lost Shem ha-Mephorash, the True Name of God. Magi of House Criamon whisper that those who read it correctly gain enlightenment, while those who fail are unmade. \
*There are no confirmed reports from Hermetic readers.*

1214 Thebes: Nicaea arrange a truce with the Latin Emperor Henry at the Peace of Nymphaeum. David Komnenos killed fighting the Sultanate. Nicea takes Amatris, but is now isolated from Trebizond by Sultanate territory. Corfu is captured from the Venetians by Epiros.

1214 Iberia Tribunal: Iberian Covenants form a pact of mutual defense in response to the Flaming Shadow.

1214 Hibernia Tribunal: The covenants of the Tribunal debate issues of foreign aggression and the Siege of Praesis, but a Wizard’s War was declared and the covenant itself is not attending. By local convention few come to their aid, and no action is taken by the Tribunal.

1214 Novgorod Tribunal: A delegation of shamans & shapechangers arrives at the Novgorod Tribunal seeking alliance with the Order. The issue is discussed at length, but the matter of bringing it up to the Grand Tribunal is tabled. In the meanwhile, the visitors are told to join House Ex Miscellanea.

The Quaesitors of Three Lakes are asked to consider if Yannes Kolski has entered Final Twilight.

Thousand Caves accuses Pripet Maior of brutally suppressing mortals in the Pripet Marshes. Pripet Maior accuses Thousand Caves of despoiling wilderness.

Finally, the Tribunal is stunned by the revelations of Thousand Caves covenant, which shares with the Tribunal its investigations and success in affecting Auras, involving faeries, mortal worship, and magi-priests.

The Tribunal is closed after the Quaesitors announce that they will investigate both Thousand Caves and Pripet Maior.

*The reason for the shamans and shapeshifters to seek an alliance was not made clear. Possibly they recognized the influence of the Order, or sought protection from another influence.*

1214 Provencal Tribunal: A rustic magus named Tres Ex Miscellanea is brought to trial for interference with mundanes. He had declared Wizard’s War against the magi of another covenant in the region, and with more than a hundred armed men he had marched against their tower, completely destroying it and killing most of the inhabitants. The prosecutors argue that Tres had broken the Code when he hired his army, since he had essentially bought their service with enchanted devices, and that he had thereby become significantly and criminally invested in mundane politics of the region. Tres argues that all of the men in his army belonged to his covenant, which had been duly registered at the previous Tribunal, and that he had the right to support his covenfolk with his magic however he wished. The Tribunal eventually decides in his favor, though the presiding Quaesitor also notes disapprovingly that all of these covenfolk had since left his service.

1214 Rhine Tribunal: Maga Caprea of Bjornaer is found guilty of harming magus Carolus’ familiar and is sentenced to produce a bestiary using her own resources.

After the Tribunal, three magi, Signum Irruptus of Criamon, Theoderich of Jerbiton, and Pancrestis of Bonisagus, decide to establish a covenant in Denmark, on the island of Zealand, in the ruins of Lethra town, which they name Heorot covenant, after the Hall of King Beowulf. 

*The magi of Heorot did not seek any permission to colonize, which may yet cause them difficulty, as Denmark lies within Ultima Thule, which is ceded to Novgorod Tribunal. That Tribunal is ever fearful of Rhine magi expanding into their territories.*

Fengheld magi again bring up the unfairness that the Praeco of the Rhine Tribunal always is the Primus of Bonisagus and suggest that the Rhine Tribunal should reform to match the system used by the rest of the Order. The more cynical magi suggest the reason for this is that Eule of Fengheld is an excellent candidate for the Praeco, as she is likely to be the oldest maga in the Rhine. However, there are at least two other candidates - Caecilius of Durenmar and Schadrith of Dankmar - who may well be older, although the records are in doubt.

*Fengheld is likely to propose, at the next Tribunal, an investigation into the Rhine’s oldest magus, as a first move to break Durenmar’s stranglehold.*

1214 Theban Tribunal: The new logothete Leontius from Alexandria has been unsatisfactory and narrowly escapes being ostracized.

*Records have been lost, lists have gone missing, and the all-important book registering tokens and shards has been found to be faulty. *

1215: The Fourth Lateran Council or "The Great Council of the Lateran" in Rome is convened by Pope Innocentius III to establish three main objectives: crusading, church reform and combating heresy. It was the largest, most representative, and most influential council ever assembled with over 400 bishops - including 71 cardinals and archbishops, over 800 abbots and priors representing some eighty ecclesiastical provinces, 23 Latin-speaking prelates from the Eastern Orthodox Church, and representatives of nearly all Christian monarchs in attendance. 

The seventy-one Lateran canons, which were not debated, were only formally adopted on the last day of the council. They define the teaching of the Catholic Church on transubstantiation, the sacrament; procedure and penalties against heretics and their protectors; prescribed dressing for clergy members; prohibited gambling and spectating theatrical performances by clergy. As well as proclamations that the papal primacy is to be recognized by all antiquity; prohibition against new religious orders; that clerics may neither pronounce nor execute a sentence of death, nor may they act as judges in extreme criminal cases, or take part in matters connected with judicial tests and trials by ordeal;  physicians bound under pain of exclusion from the church, to exhort their patients to call in a priest before prescribing medicine, and thus provide for their spiritual welfare; religious law should not interfere with secular law, and vice versa; tithe payments to have priority over all other taxes and dues; a special dress code for Jews and Saracens to distinguish them from Christians so that "no Christian shall come to marry them ignorant of who they are"; freeing Christian debtors from "extortionate and excessive" interest on loans from Jews; excluding Jews from positions of authority over Christians. The last Canon calls for a new crusade to the Levant to recover the Holy Land, designating June 1st 1217 as the start of the Fifth Crusade.

*The effect of the Great Council will certainly be felt for centuries. The 71 canons were probably drafted by Innocentius III himself. Interesting though, is canon 32 "Ut patroni compententem portionem dimittant clericis", which ruled that priests are entitled to a "fitting portion from the revenues of the church". How clever indeed! *

*As the Council now forbids clergy from cautery or chirurgical incision, this now falls to laymen. The clergy is not allowed to consult these physicians.*

1215 Normandy: David of Dinant, magister in artibus at the University of Paris, is discovered a heretic by Cardinal Robert Robert Caurçon, after a reading of his book, Quaternuli ("little note-books"). His book confiscated, David refused to recant his beliefs and fled the city during the night.

*Rumor has it that David is writing a second book.*

1215 Normandy: The covenant of Lapis Crudus in the forest of Fontainebleau, lost since 1118, reestablishes contact with the Order after their leader Aries of Flambeau succumbs to Final Twilight and the faerie Candidus agrees to renegotiate their pact.

*Since this time, members of the covenant have been able to leave the regio, but only if they are simultaneously replaced by someone of the same social standing from the outside entering the covenant.*

1215 Provencal: The covenant of Filii Sinapis (1063–1215), established by Flambeau of the abandoned Normandy Liege of Sinapis, is abandoned in the wake of the crusade.

Simon de Montfort triumphantly enters Toulouse and installs himself in the Château Narbonnais. A popular uprising occurs, and fighting throughout the city is put down only after great efforts.

1215 Rhine: Magister Signum Irruptus of Criamon founds the covenant of Heorot. Not as a member of the Rhine Tribunal, but it hopes to have its membership approved at the next Rhine Tribunal meeting in 1221. This plan is not without controversy as Heorot is sited on the island of Zealand, which is part of the kingdom of Denmark and therefore clearly within the territory that traditionally forms part of the Novgorod Tribunal. However, because of the perceived threat of the "Order of Odin", the Order has not yet established a covenant in Norse lands that has lasted long enough to attend a Tribunal meeting; thus the territory remains a gray area. 

*The covenant has occupied the hall of Hrothgar, former king of the Danes, who was terrorized by a troll called Grendel before the hero Beowulf slew him. *

*The magi of Oculus Septentrionalis and Crintera must have been fuming as this is clearly stepping on their toes in the north, but many in the Rhine Tribunal greet Heorot’s move with glee. The Rhine Tribunal has long disputed the eastern border with the Novgorod Tribunal; and to "steal territory" from them is the ultimate in one-upmanship. \
*

1215 Thebes: Gervase appointed Latin Patriarch of Constantinople

1215 Stonehenge: England’s rebellious barons force King John to sign the Magna Carta, a proto-constitution that cracks open centuries-old royal absolutism. \
*A raucous middle finger to unchecked power and a bold step toward a modern governance like the Order? A Tremere experiment in Mundane Governance or a Mercere/Jerbiton initiative for stability with a more predictable legal framework for travel and commerce?  \
Was something more sinister in the works? The Church's fierce opposition to certain clauses might stem from recognizing an unnatural, supernatural force at work. \
*

1215 Stonehenge Tribunal: One year late, but quorate. \
*Some magi argue that the principles of the Magna Carta could be adapted into the Peripheral Code to restrain the power of Tribunal Primi or even the Grand Tribunal.*

1215 Rhine: The Bjornaer Gathering of Twelve Years at Crintera.

1215-1216 Novgorod: Quaesitor delegation visits Pripet Maior and Thousand Caves.

*They intend to issue findings at the Tribunal of 1221.*

1215 Stonehenge: Prince Louis of France invades England in May, as part of the First Barons' War (1215–1217), which was triggered by John's refusal to abide by Magna Carta and the barons' increasing frustration with his rule. The sea-captain Eustace the Monk lands knights, soldiers, and siege engines for his lord. He is said to know how to make his ships invisible to mortal eyes.

*See 1217.*

1216: The papacy is at the height of its power and the Fifth Crusade is being prepared. 400 bishops, 800 abbots and representatives of the secular powers congregate. However, Pope Innocentius III dies during the preparations and is succeeded by Honorius III who immediately orders Andreas II of Hungary and Friedrich II (Frederick), King of Germany and Sicily to lead it. Friedrich II is holding back however, as his crown is still contested at home.

1216 Loch Leglean: Thomas Verus of Mercere writes about the Loch Leglean Tribunal.

1216 Novgorod: Magus Yannes Kolski, possibly in Final Twilight, disappears after visiting Thousand Caves Covenant.

*His fate remains mysterious.*

1216 (October) Stonehenge: On his deathbed, king John of England appoints a council of thirteen executors to help his nine year old son Henry reclaim the kingdom and requests that he be placed into the guardianship of William Marshal, one of the most famous knights in England. The loyalist leaders decides to crown Henry immediately to reinforce his claim to the throne. William knights the boy, and Cardinal Guala Bicchieri, the papal legate to England, then oversees his coronation at Gloucester Cathedral.

*The royal crown had been either lost or sold during the civil war or possibly lost in The Wash, so instead the ceremony used a simple gold corolla belonging to Queen Isabella.*

1217: The Fifth Crusade begins.

1217 Novgorod: Pripet Maior raids Thousand Caves, in direct violation of Tribunal traditions.

1217 Provencal: The League of Gastronomers announce that the magus who will most impress the other Gastronomers during the annual feasts between 1218 and 1228, as decided by the Grand Tribunal feast at Durenmar that year, will be selected to arrange the grandest celebration in the Order’s history in 1248, the 2,000th anniversary of the founding of Rome. During the 20 years in-between, all Gastronomers will be expected to aid in the preparations.

1217 Provencal: In the autumn of, while Simon de Montfort campaigns in the north of the region, Count Raimond suddenly returns and the jubilant citizens admit him. On his return, de Montfort finds the city gates locked against him, and Raimond restored as count, so once again besiege the city. After many months, a stone from a siege machine upon the walls strikes de Montfort directly upon the head, killing him. Amaury de Montfort lays claim to Simon’s titles.

*Some suspect this may have been one of the magical siege engines devised by *

*Tres Ex Miscellanea and employed in the destruction of Mistridge. *

1217 Rome: The twenty-fourth Verdi Competition.

1217 Stonehenge: A great sea battle at Sandwich, on the second day of Virgo, in which Eustace the Monk is killed. His invisible ships are revealed to the English sea captains by a mariner, Stephen Crabbe of Winchelsea, formerly under Eustace’s command. Stephen battles his old captain and kills him by beheading. The French fleet is then destroyed by a tempest, apparently at the instigation of St. Bartholomew.

*Perhaps a mage calling himself Bartholomew, who appears in the air above the English and tells them to fear not.*

1218: In a move to facilitate the Reconquista, Pope Honorius III reverses a prior judgment and declares King Ferdinand III the legitimate heir to the Kingdom of León. This papal endorsement strengthens Ferdinand's claim and bolsters efforts to reclaim territories from Muslim control in the Iberian Peninsula.

1218 Iberia: The Royal, Celestial and Military Order of Our Lady of Mercy and the Redemption of the Captives (the Mercedarians), a new mendicant order is established by Peter Nolasco in the city of Barcelona, for the redemption of Christian captives. Its members are required to take a fourth vow: to die, if necessary, for another who is in danger of losing their faith. 

1218 Iberia: Alfonso IX of León grants the studium generale title to the cathedral school of Salamanca, thus creating the Studii Salmantini.

1218 Iberia: Duresca, Barcelona, Estancia-es-Karida linked by Hermes Portals under Iberian Defense Pact.

1218 Hibernia: The ard-ollamh Érenn (the Chief Bard of Ireland) Giolla Ernain Ua Martain dies. A successor is not yet chosen.

*Not only the personal bard of the high-king, he is also the king of the bards. Bards have inherited the traditional respect for druids given by the native Irish. While the concept of bardic immunity - once guaranteed by the high-king - has lapsed, the reputation of a bard holds a great deal of weight. *

1218 (March) Stonehenge: King Henry III of England extends an offer of safe conduct to Llywelyn the Great, leading to a meeting in Worcester. The resulting treaty acknowledges Llywelyn's dominion over Wales, with the stipulation that he pays homage to Henry and returns certain castles acquired during recent conflicts. This accord solidifies Llywelyn's authority and brings a measure of stability to the region. 

1218 (May–June) Levant: A substantial crusader expeditionary force, numbering approximately 30,000 and led by King John I of Jerusalem, embarks from the port city of Acre. Supported by a fleet of Frisian ships, they set sail with the strategic objective of capturing the Egyptian city of Damietta, aiming to weaken the Ayyubid hold on the Holy Land. The crusader fleet reaches the fortified harbor of Damietta, situated on the right bank of the Nile River. Sultan Al-Adil, caught off guard by the sudden invasion, mobilizes an army from Syria, while his son, Al-Kamil, advances northward from Cairo to establish a defensive position south of Damietta.

The crusader army launches a series of attacks against the formidable defenses of Damietta but faces staunch resistance and fails to breach the city's walls. In response, they devise innovative siege weaponry, including a novel design attributed to the German chronicler and preacher Oliver of Paderborn: two ships bound together, supporting a siege tower and ladder to facilitate assaults on the city's fortifications. \
*Oliver later reported in his chronicle, Historia Damiatina.*

1218 (June) Provencal: While leading an assault during the siege of Toulouse, Simon de Montfort is mysteriously struck and killed by a projectile from the city's defenders. His death marks a significant turning point in the Albigensian Crusade, leading to a shift in leadership and momentum.

1218 (August) Levant: After intense combat, the crusaders succeed in capturing the strategically vital Chain Tower, a key defensive position controlling access to the city via the Nile. This victory allows them to cut the heavy chain that spans the river, effectively opening a route for their fleet and tightening the siege on Damietta.

Meanwhile, Sultan Al-Adil I falls ill and dies in Damascus after an 18-year reign over the Ayyubid dynasty. His death prompts a division of power: his eldest son, Al-Mu'azzam, assumes control over Syria, while his younger son, Al-Kamil, takes command in Egypt. This transition occurs amidst the ongoing siege of Damietta, adding complexity to the Ayyubid defense efforts.

1218 (September) Levant: Cardinal Pelagius arrives at the crusader encampment with additional reinforcements and asserts the primacy of ecclesiastical authority over secular leaders, challenging King John I's command. \
Sultan Al-Kamil orchestrates a sudden assault on the crusader encampment. Forewarned of the maneuver, King John I leads a counterattack that decimates the Egyptian advance guard, reinforcing the crusaders' position and morale during the protracted siege.

1218 (November) Levant: A severe three-day storm inundates the crusader camp, causing significant damage to supplies and infrastructure. In the aftermath, an epidemic sweeps through the debilitated forces, claiming the lives of approximately one-sixth of the soldiers.

*Were the sahirs involved?*

1218 Thebes: Ivan Asen’s son returns from exile, and leads a rebellion in Bulgaria. After a seven month siege Tsar Boril was captured, blinded and consigned to a monastery, and Tsar Ivan Asen II crowned. Geoffrey de Villehardouin II becomes Prince of Achaea following his father’s death. Genoese traders readmitted to Constantinople. 

*Ivan Asen II's reign is noted for territorial expansion, including the annexation of regions such as Epirus, Albania, and parts of Macedonia, significantly enhancing the influence and reach of the Bulgarian state.*

1218 Transylvania: Tremere Decennial conference at Coeris. Primus Umno retires

to lead the Burning Acorns Vexillation. Poena becomes Prima. Begins a series of research covenants.

1218: Bilera elected as Guernicus Prima.

1218 Iceland: Icelandic lawspeaker Snorri Sturluson writes the Gylfaginning ("The Deluding of Gylfi") while visiting Norway.

1219 (January) Rhine: A catastrophic storm surge breaches the dikes of the Northern Netherlands, inundating vast regions and resulting in the deaths of thousands. The deluge reshapes the coastline, creating the Zuiderzee and submerging entire villages.

*Some magi speculate that the flood was not solely a natural disaster.* 

1219 (January) Levant: Through diplomatic negotiations during the Fifth Crusade, the city of Jerusalem is handed back to the Christian Kingdom under a peace treaty. 

*This transfer is short-lived, as control shifts again in subsequent years. The event sparks discussions among Hermetic scholars about the fluctuating Dominion auras in the Holy City and the potential for ancient relics to resurface during these transitions.*

1219 (July) Levant: The Knights Hospitaller launch a raid on the town of Burlus in Egypt's Nile Delta. While initially successful in pillaging, the knights are ambushed on their return, leading to the capture of several members, including Grand Master Guérin de Montaigu.

1219 Levant: The True Cross covenant is founded in the wake of the crusader conquest of Damietta, seeking to establish a Hermetic presence in the city.

1219 (August) Novgorod: An Estonian force encircles the Lihula stronghold, occupied by an invading Swedish crusader army led by Karl the Deaf. The Estonians besiege the fortress and, after intense combat, the Swedish troops attempt to break out but are ultimately defeated and killed.

*This battle highlights the fierce resistance of the Estonian tribes against northern crusaders and the volatile nature of power in the Baltic region.*

1219 Provencal: A second crusade begins, directed by the French royal court. This is supported by the nobility of Northern France in order to incorporate the South within greater France. The crusade takes Marmande and conducts a massacre there, before being repulsed at the walls of Toulouse by Count Raimond.

1219 (September) Rome: Prima Imanitosi of House Verditius succumbs to Final Twilight while experimenting in her laboratory. Next in line for Primus is Stouritus of covenant Ingasia on Lemnos, but he refuses to relocate to Verdi as tradition demands and declares Ingasia the new domus magna. 

*This is what you get for having no House means to override a Primus decision.*

1219 Thebes: Gervase, the Latin Patriarch of Constantinople dies.

*The post is still to be filled. *

1219: A papal decree (expanding prohibitions set at the 1215 Fourth Lateran Council) forbids secular clergy from absenting themselves from their pastoral duties to study medicine or law.

*The Church is obviously suspicious that the medici will put the patient's physical health before their Spiritual well being, and their fees before either…*

*Upset by its growing popularity, the masters at Paris University are explicitly forbidden by the pope to teach civil law, and also banned from teaching the New Aristotle.*

[---]

1220: Papal decree demands that degrees at the University of Paris are bestowed on the master’s recommendation alone, and states that the chancellor of Notre Dame, Phillipus de Grevia, is compelled to follow their recommendations.

*Also see 1200. This surely exacerbates the tension.*

1220 Iberia: Alfonso IX of Leon founds a new university in Salamanca with ambitions to rival the best on the continent. However, in popular opinion, the university is associated with sorcery.

*A cave on the site was previously believed to be a school for black magic. In Spanish, Salamanca can mean "cave", "an evil iguana" and "sleight of hand". The Salamanquesa is a reptile with magical attributes in the Spanish tradition.*

1220 Levant: Far northwest of the Tribunal, the seer of Bayalaqan - the most important city in Arran, having ravingly predicted a "storm gathering in the east" is proven right as the Mongols descend on the city, slaughter the inhabitants and burn it down.

1220 (May) Stonehenge: At the age of twelve, Henry III's second coronation at Westminster Abbey. During the ceremony, he is reminded of his duties to maintain peace and defend the rights of the English crown, while the barons swear fealty to him. \
*This coronation marks a pivotal moment in English history, with potential implications for the stability of the region and the interests of magi operating within the British Isles.*

1220 Thebes: War breaks out between the Latin Empire and Nicea. In the summer Sultan Kaikawus I dies, succeeded by his son Kayqubad I who begins to fortify the Anatolian highlands

1220 (Summer Solstice) Provencal: Flambeau tournament held at Castra Solis (domus magna), at summer solstice.

1220 Normandy: Magus William Fireheart of Ungulus, last of that covenant's line of Flambeau, killed in Wizard’s War with a mage of Fudarus.

*The volatile William Fireheart had dodged Renunciation for years, and was no doubt pleased to die in battle.*

1220 Novgorod: An Estonian crusader force, supported by Revalians, Harrians, and Vironians, attempts to conquer the Danish stronghold of Tallinn. The siege lasts for 14 days but ultimately ends with the Estonian forces retreating. 

*This event underscores the ongoing struggle for control in the Baltic region and the resilience of the Danish occupiers. The failed siege also leads to shifts in local power dynamics, with potential ramifications for the magical landscape of the area. ​*

1220 Rhine: Following a ritual murder libel, a violent mob attacks the Jewish community in Erfurt, Duchy of Thuringia. The synagogue is stormed, and many Jews are tortured and killed, while the Jewish quarter is razed. 

*The tragic event reflects the pervasive tensions and dangers faced by minority communities in medieval Europe. There is some speculation that the unrest may have been exacerbated by Infernal influences seeking to sow discord and suffering.*

1220 Rome: Mathematician Lionardo Bonacci of Pisa (Fibonacci) publishes Practica Geometriae, a treatise on geometry and a sequel to his 1202 work Liber Abaci (on arithmetic and the use of Hindu-Arabic numerals).

1220 (November) Rome: In a grand ceremony in Rome, The German king Friedrich II (Frederick II) is crowned Holy Roman Emperor by Pope Honorius III - after promising to undertake a new crusade. Following his coronation, Frederick implements significant reforms in Sicily, establishing it as an absolute monarchy with Palermo as its principal seat.

The hedge wizard, famous philosopher and astrologer Michael Scotus returns to Palermo and becomes court magician for the Emperor.

*Hermetic scholars observe Frederick's court with interest, particularly his patronage of scholars and rumored engagement with esoteric knowledge.*

*Scotus, among many other things, has written "Every astrologer is worthy of praise and honor, since by such a doctrine as astrology he probably knows many secrets of God, and things known to few." It is said that the legendary Michael Scotus feasts his friends with dishes brought by blessed spirits from the royal kitchens of France, Spain, and other countries. He is also rumored to have petrified a coven of witches, the "Tall Meg and Her Daughters" stone circle in Cumbria.*

1221: Regional Tribunal Meetings.

1221 Novgorod Tribunal: Debate about the new terror out of the East, a barbarian people called the Mongols that have appeared to the east and south-east of the Tribunal.

*Reportedly, the Mongols are also supported by Gifted individuals of an unknown tradition.*

1221 Levant Tribunal: Renewal of the Treaty of Baghdad due in 1223 is heatedly discussed. Crusading magi have called vehemently for the rejection of the treaty this time, so that they can aid their mundane allies in Egypt. A scramble has begun to gather votes before the next meeting.

*The fate of the Tribunal rests on the outcome of this meeting. The treaty may be kept, in which case little will change, or it may be rejected, in which case the Tribunal may slide into chaos again.*

1221 Levant: After a protracted campaign, the crusader forces occupying the Egyptian city of Damietta attempt to advance towards Cairo but are encircled by Sultan Al-Kamil's army. Cut off from supplies and weakened by disease, the crusaders are compelled to surrender. In August 1221, they evacuate Damietta and agree to an eight-year truce, marking the unsuccessful conclusion of the Fifth Crusade.

1221 Rhine Tribunal: Prima Murion of Bonisagus is Praeco and Caecillius of Bjornaer from Durenmar is presiding Quaesitor. 

Fengheld proposes that an investigation into the Rhine’s oldest magus should be conducted, and presented at the following Tribunal.

*Surely as another move to break Durenmar’s stranglehold on the Tribunal.*

New covenant Heorot on Zealand is voted into the Tribunal after a great speech by their leader Magister Signum Irruptus of House Criamon. There is debate however, as the area traditionally belongs to Novgorod - but they haven’t done anything there for a very long time so the concern is dismissed.

Irencillia and Crintera again lament on mundane encroachment on the forests and want the Order to take firm action. Nothing concrete happens and the issue will be brought up again next Tribunal.

Another ridiculous suggestion from Irencillia to move the entire Order into faerie regions is laughed off and not even voted on.

Daria La Gris of Tremere, leader of Triamore, accuses Tabania for interference with the mundane. When Tabania was a peregrinator in Triamore, she found out that a local noble had a permanent magic item - a coat that keeps the rain out. She had protested this, but Daria had respectfully told her not to interfere with the covenant’s business. Tabana then stole the coat, as she did not agree that mundane can have magical items, but was found out by the noble who complained to Triamore. After some debate, the presiding Quaesitor orders Tabania to return the cape and pay three vis to Triamore. 

*There is now a fresh legal precedent for mundanes to own magic items. Perhaps this was just a cleverly engineered ruse to get this exact result?*

1221 Stonehenge: Archbishop Stephen Langton convenes a church council at Osney Abbey in Oxford. This synod enacts several canonical statutes, notably imposing stringent measures against the Jewish population. These include prohibitions on social interactions between Jews and Christians, the imposition of distinctive identifying badges for Jews, and restrictions on the construction of new synagogues. \
*These decrees exacerbate existing tensions and lay the groundwork for future expulsions of Jewish communities in England. ​*

1222 (May) Levant: A devastating earthquake strikes the island of Cyprus, causing significant destruction and loss of life. The tremors are felt across the Eastern Mediterranean region.

*A destructive event of significant "magnitude"?*

1222 Novgorod: As part of the ongoing Livonian Crusade, Danish forces attempt to conquer the island of Saaremaa. Despite their efforts, the campaign fails to secure the island from the heathens.

1222 Rome: The University of Padua is founded, with coastal access through its neighbor-city Venice.

*Promptly emerging as a prominent center for learning and scholarship in Europe.*

1222 Stonehenge Tribunal: Late but quorate.

1222 Transylvania: Tremere conference at Coeris.

1222: The six-year-old Erik Eriksson is elected as King Erik XI of Sweden. His minority reign necessitates the establishment of a regency council.

1222 (October): Total lunar eclipse.

1222 (December) Rome: A powerful earthquake strikes the city of Brescia on Christmas Day, leading residents to evacuate and seek refuge outside the city walls to avoid collapsing structures.

*Again?*

1223: The Emperor’s court magician, the hedge wizard Michael Scotus is offered the role of Archbishop of Cashel in Ireland by Pope Honorius III. He declines.

1223 Iberia: Following the death of his father, King Afonso II, Sancho II ascends to the Portuguese throne. His reign begins amidst tensions with the Catholic Church, stemming from disputes over ecclesiastical privileges and property. ​

1223 Novgorod: Mongols shatter a large mortal army of Russians and Cumans at the Battle of Kalka river, then mysteriously depart.

1223 Normandy: King Philip II, known as Philip Augustus, passes away in Mantes at the age of 57. His reign, spanning from 1180 to 1223, is marked by significant territorial expansion and consolidation of royal power. Philip's strategic marriages and military campaigns have transformed France into a formidable European power. He is succeeded by his son, Louis VIII.

1223 Rhine: Count Henry of Schwerin captures King Valdemar II of Denmark on the island of Lyø. This significantly weakens Danish influence in the Baltic region and shifts the balance of power among the northern European states.

1223 Stonehenge: Llywelyn ab Iorwerth, Prince of Gwynedd, crosses into Shropshire and captures the castles of Kinnerley and Whittington. This bold move prompts a military response from William Marshal, 2nd Earl of Pembroke, leading to a series of engagements that underscore the ongoing tensions between the Welsh principalities and the English crown. ​

Later in the year, attempts to broker peace between Llywelyn and William Marshal, mediated by Archbishop Stephen Langton, collapse. The failure of these talks leads to Llywelyn's excommunication and further military confrontations. IN october, Llywelyn ab Iorwerth, along with fellow Welsh princes Maelgwn ap Rhys and Rhys Gryg, formally submit to King Henry III of England.

1223 Transylvania: 20,000 families of Cumans settle in Hungary, under the agreement that they will become Christians and fight the Mongols if they return. 

1224: Francis of Assisi develops unusual wounds, lumps of flesh on his hands take the shape of nail heads on one side of the hand and sharp nail points on the other.

*These sound like magical stigmata, like those possessed by House Criamon members or like strange Twilight scars.*

1224 (Summer Solstice) Provencal: Flambeau tournament held at Castra Solis (domus magna).

1224 Rome: Emperor Friedrich II offers patronage to the covenant of Literatus. *This emperor is noted for his learning and patronage of the wise.*

1224 Rome: Filii of Literatus found a new covenant in Naples. The Naples covenant may have been founded with the Emperor's aid; the issue is not clear.

1225 Africa: The three century agreement (since 925, when Iberian sahirs joined the Order) banning covenants in Africa expires. At least some magi are eager to explore the new lands and the wonders therein.

1226 (February): Total lunar eclipse.

1226 Rome: Other covenants in Rome seek to undermine the alliance of Literatus and the Emperor.

1227: The Emperor’s court magician, the hedge wizard Michael Scotus is again offered an archbishopry, this time that of Canterbury, by Pope Gregorious IX. He declines this one as well.

1227 Sixth Crusade: Emperor Friedrich II embarks on crusade, seven years after promising it. His army is stricken with fever and delayed; Friedrich is, after 20 days delay, excommunicated for malingering.

*Rumor has it that magic may have been involved with the weird illness. Perhaps the Emperor’s friends at Literatus betrayed him?*

1227: Regional Tribunals are held a year early, to prepare for the Grand Tribunal of the next year. Presumably the Stonehenge Tribunal, late one year since 1202, will synchronize with the Order.

1227 Rhine: The Bjornaer Gathering of Twelve Years at Crintera.

1228 Normandy: Papal ban on teaching of Aristotle at the University of Paris.

1228 (Summer Solstice) Provencal: Flambeau tournament held at Castra Solis (domus magna), at summer solstice.

**1228 Seventeenth Grand Tribunal.**

House Tremere appeals the Theban Tribunal of 1207’s non-conviction of the disbanded League of Advisors for interference with mundanes.

*Should this appeal be successful, it could have important consequences for*

*the autonomy of Tribunals in general and the Theban polity in particular.*

1228: The Sixth Crusade finally departs under the Emperor’s command (while still excommunicated). It lands in Egypt with little opposition. The pope’s calls for Friedrich’s army to abandon the Emperor are in vain. 

1228: Pope Gregorius IX invades the Emperor’s lands in Italy.

1229: The Sixth Crusade secures Jerusalem, Bethlehem, and Nazareth, and a corridor of land, all in six months. Friedrich II is crowned King of Jerusalem while still excommunicated. The Emperor returns to Italy and quickly drives out his foes, but the Pope Gregorius IX excommunicates him again.

1229: End of the Albigensian Crusade.

1230: The pope and Emperor are reconciled; the Emperor is absolved. For appearance’s sake, Freidrich repays the pope’s "leniency" by issuing a strong edict against heretics, strengthening the newly formed Inquisition. However, Frederick makes it clear that Roman Magi, particularly those of Literatus, are to be spared the Inquisitor’s torch (at least in the early years of the purge).

*Jokes persist in the Imperial court, retelling how Friedrich discovered the pope in his chambers, in the company of a goat, three strong servants and a bucket of miller’s grease. *

1230–1234 Novgorod: A civil war between the Knights of the Sword and the Church, over the conduct of the Knights toward their ecclesiastical superiors. *This points to a disunity in the Church, possibly of use if a crusade is preached against the Order.*

C. 1231: Papal Bull Excommunicamus forms the Inquisition to root out heresy. They focus initially on the Albigensian movement, under the guidance of the Dominicans.

1232 (Summer Solstice) Provencal: Flambeau tournament held at Castra Solis (domus magna), at summer solstice.

1232 Transylvania: Tremere conference at Coeris.

1232 Normandy: A mortal, Hubert de Burgh, is accused of taking from the vaults of the king of France a magic gemstone that conferred invincibility in combat, and delivering it to an enemy of the king. 

1233 (September): Total lunar eclipse.

1235: The strained peace between the Empire and communes of northern Italy comes to an end. Hoping to gain greater independence from the Empire, northern cities aid Friedrich’s son, Henry, to rise against the Emperor. Friedrich‘s forces, populated even by Saracens loyal to him - evidence of Friedrich II’s still liberal mind - defeat the communes.

1235: Regional Tribunal Meetings.

1235 Rome: The twenty-fifth Verdi Competition.

1236 (Summer Solstice) Provencal: Flambeau tournament held at Castra Solis (domus magna), at summer solstice.

1237 (January): Total lunar eclipse.

1237 Rome: The magi of Literatus take an active role in destroying a group of diabolists in Milan, at the behest of Friedrich II.

1237 Novgorod: The Knights of the Sword subordinated to the Teutonic Knights. This is intended to resolve lingering problems of obedience by the Knights of the Sword. The Teutonic Knights descend hereafter into brutality and barbarism. *Indicating deep corruption among the crusaders by this point.*

1237: The Mongol Horde returns and conquers much of eastern Europe. In the midst of this, the Teutonic Knights wage war on the Russians, who are caught between two invading armies.

1238–1311: Life of Arnold of Villanova, physician and alchemist. 

1239: Emperor Friedrich II excommunicated for the second time. The pope calls him a heretic, rake, and the Antichrist.

1239 Rhine: The Bjornaer Gathering of Twelve Years at Crintera.

1240 (Summer Solstice) Provencal: Flambeau tournament held at Castra Solis (domus magna), at summer solstice.

1240 Rome: Magi of Literatus covenant helps Friedrich II capture hundreds of clergy. A special Tribunal disbands the covenant later in this year. \
*The covenant in Naples picks up where Literatus left off, before Friedrich II corrupted Literatus.*

1241 Novgorod: 

1241 Novgorod: The Mongols devastate the nobility of Poland, Moravia and the Knights Templar at the Battle of Legnica (Liegnitz) April 9th. They withdraw later in the year, as mysteriously as they had come, but keep control over Kievan Rus. \


1241 Transylvania: A second Mongolian army invades the Kingdom of Hungary, and crushes Hungarians, Croatians, Austrians and Templars at the Battle of Mohi on April 11th. 

*More than half of all settlements of the Kingdom of Hungary are destroyed. The combination of massacres perpetrated by the Mongols, the famines induced by their foraging, and the simultaneous devastation of the countryside by the fleeing Cumans result in an estimated loss of a fifth to a fourth of Hungary's population, up to half a million people killed.* \
*County of Leczyca raided by Mongols, effect on Leczyca covenant unclear.*

1242: Regional Tribunal Meetings.

1242 Transylvania: Tremere conference at Coeris.

1243 Rhine: Famine sweeps Germany, spurred by a plague of rats.

1243: Nearly two years after the death of his predecessor, Innocentius IV becomes pope. Innocentius is determined to eliminate the blight called Friedrich II.

1245: Emperor Friedrich II deposed by order of the pope. Civil war breaks out across Germany.

1247 (December): Total lunar eclipse.

1247 Rome: Some magi of the Roman Tribunal move against demons afflicting the court of Emperor Friedrich II.

*Whether the Emperor himself is influenced by demons is hotly disputed.*

1247: The Order hosts the Fourth Secular (every 120 years).

*Some discussion of canceling it, because of the unprecedented feast that follows in 1248.*

1248: Seventh Crusade, led by Louis IX of France begins.

1248: A new Cathedral is begun in Cologne to more greatly honor the Relics of the Three Magi. Their reliquary has become a great pilgrimage site, and the old cathedral is considered inadequate.

1248 (May 22): A total eclipse of the sun progresses across Europe, beginning in Hibernia and crossing to Levant in the space of four hours, ending over Jerusalem.

*Effect hotly debated and highly anticipated.*

1248: The Order celebrates the 2,000th anniversary of the founding of Rome. A feast of unprecedented opulence and size hosted by the magus who most impressed the other Gastronomers during the Jerbiton annual feasts at Valnastium between 1218 and 1228.

1249: Regional Tribunal Meetings.

1250: Seventh Crusade ends with the complete rout of the French, and Louis’s capture by the egyptians at the battle of Fariskur.

1250: Emperor Friedrich II finally dies, worn down by long wars or the sins of his soul. The battles of Friederich’s reign leave northern Italy in ruins, and under the rule of wicked tyrants. Furthermore, rivalries between cities and parties intensify, causing ceaseless fighting for the next half century. It’s as if the evil of Friedrich, 

at the end of his life, is so great that it lives in the minds and hearts of Italians for years to come. Indeed, Friedrich II is remembered as the Anti-Christ.

*His last days are spent in a Cistercian monastery, no doubt in an attempt to save his soul as Friedrich Barbarossa had done. However, it’s believed Friedrich II’s soul is carried by demons to the pit of Mount Etna.*

1251 The Shepherd's Crusade: A popular crusade of poor shepherds and peasants from the Low Countries and northern France who went out with the objective of aiding the captive Louis IX and rescuing the Holy Land from the infidels. After his release from the Egyptians, Louis sends his brothers to France to obtain relief and his mother Blanche of Castile, acting as regent, endeavour in vain to find reinforcements as neither the noblemen nor the clergy will help. At this juncture the citizens rise up, announcing that they would go to the king's rescue. At Easter time 1251, a mysterious person known as the Le Maître de Hongrie (Master of Hungary) began to preach a crusade to the shepherds in the north of France, always holding a map supposedly given to him by the Virgin Mary. He draws large crowds and allow them to take the cross without papal authorization.

1251 Rhine: The Bjornaer Gathering of Twelve Years at Crintera.

1252 Transylvania: Tremere conference at Coeris.

1253 Rome: The twenty-sixth Verdi Competition.

1254: The Great Interregnum. The Imperial Throne remains empty from the death of Conrad IV to the reign of Rudolph, nineteen years later.

1255 (July): Total lunar eclipse.

1256: Regional Tribunal Meetings.

1257: Germany and Italy afflicted by crop failure, famine, and disease. These disasters are thought to be Divine punishment for widespread sin. This in turn gives rise to the flagellant movement, whose members live in strict discipline, wear uniforms, and conduct public and private self-flagellation to atone for the sins that have offended God.

1258 Levant: The Mongol horde take Baghdad, roll the caliph up in a carpet and trample him to death - following the Mongol custom that forbids the shedding of royal blood onto the ground.

1258 (May): Total lunar eclipse (in southern Europe).

1259 Rome: Some magi of this Tribunal wage a campaign against demon-influenced rulers in northern Italy.

1260 Levant: The Mongols are turned back when their advance is checked in Syria by a Mamluk army from Egypt.

**1261 The Eighteenth Grand Tribunal:**

It has been observed that in the mundane world, the coming decades will see increased conflict between Church and kings. The Church press claim of universal authority stemming from its spiritual authority.

1262 (August): Total lunar eclipse (western Europe only).

	

1262 Transylvania: Tremere conference at Coeris.

1263: Regional Tribunal Meetings.

1263 Rhine: The Bjornaer Gathering of Twelve Years at Crintera.

1265 (December): Total lunar eclipse on Christmas day.

1266 Rome: A mortal, Charles of Anjou, was some years before crowned king of Sicily by the pope. In this year he arrives to press his claim, and among his advisors are the magi of Windgraven. 

*This leads to a period of Roman-Provencal Tribunal friction.*

1270: The Eighth Crusade, led by Louis IX of France. The army is afflicted by plague after landing in Carthage, and the king dies.

1270: Regional Tribunal Meetings.

1271 Rome: The twenty-seventh Verdi Competition.

1272 Transylvania: Tremere conference at Coeris.

1273: The Great Interregnum ends after nineteen years with the election of Rudolf I.

1275 Rhine: The Bjornaer Gathering of Twelve Years at Crintera.

1276 (November): Total lunar eclipse.

1277: Regional Tribunal Meetings.

1280 (March): Total lunar eclipse.

1282 Rome: The magi of the Provencal covenant Mistridge begin a campaign to oppose their old enemy, Windgraven, in the Roman Tribunal.

1282 Transylvania: Tremere conference at Coeris.

1284 Rhine: A strange piper leads away the rats of Hamelin using the magical music of his pipe. After the people of that town refuse his fee, he leads away their children. 

*Whether he was demon, faerie, or Hedge Wizard, we know not.*

1284: Regional Tribunal Meetings.

1287 (October): Total lunar eclipse.

1287 Rhine: The Bjornaer Gathering of Twelve Years at Crintera.

1289 Rome: The twenty-eighth Verdi Competition.

1291 (February): Total lunar eclipse.

1291: Regional Tribunal Meetings.

1292 Transylvania: Tremere conference at Coeris.

**1294 Nineteenth Grand Tribunal: The Roman and Provencal Tribunals clash over issues of cross-Tribunal interference and meddling in mortal affairs.**

The Church continues to press claim of universal authority stemming from its spiritual authority, but falls increasingly under the influence of the French crown under Philip IV (*Philippe le Bel*), who it has come to rely on for military aid. 

1294 Stonehenge: Famine in England, possibly due to excessive exporting of grain.

1298: Regional Tribunal Meetings.

1299 Rhine: The Bjornaer Gathering of Twelve Years at Crintera.

1300 Iberia: Last of the known diabolic Flaming Shadow magi killed.

1302 Transylvania: Tremere conference at Coeris.

1305: Regional Tribunal Meetings.

1307 Rome: The twenty-ninth Verdi Competition.

1307–1314: The Knights Templar are accused of heresy and idolatry, discredited, tried, and the leaders of the order executed. The surviving Templars are spread through other Crusading orders and the lands of the Templars seized by local mortal authorities.

*It seems that the kings to whom the Templars loaned money did not wish to pay it back.*

1309: Papal seat removed to Avignon.

1311 Rhine: The Bjornaer Gathering of Twelve Years at Crintera.

1312: Regional Tribunal Meetings.

1312 Transylvania: Tremere conference at Coeris.

1315: A three year famine strikes western Europe.

1319: Regional Tribunal Meetings.

1320: Pope John XXII issues Bull Super Illius Specula, condemning ritual magic as diabolism and heresy.

1322 Transylvania: Tremere conference at Coeris.

1323 Rhine: The Bjornaer Gathering of Twelve Years at Crintera.

1325 Rome: The Thirtieth Verdi Competition.

1326: Regional Tribunal Meetings.

**1327 Twentieth Grand Tribunal:**

1327 Rhine: A great fire burns much of Munich.

1332 Transylvania: Tremere conference at Coeris.

1333: Regional Tribunal Meetings.

1335 Rhine: The Bjornaer Gathering of Twelve Years at Crintera.

1337 Beginning of the Hundred Year’s War between England and France over English claims to the throne of France and the French holdings of the English king.

1340: Regional Tribunal Meetings.

1342 Transylvania: Tremere conference at Coeris.

1343 Rome: The thirty-first Verdi Competition.

1346 Normandy: Reports of a new mundane engine employed at the Battle of Crecy. The engine propels a solid stone or metal ball with a thunderclap. 

1347–1351: The Black Death ravages Europe, killing roughly one-third of the mundane population.

1347: Regional Tribunal Meetings.

1347 Rhine: The Bjornaer Gathering of Twelve Years at Crintera.

1352 Transylvania: Tremere conference at Coeris.

1354: Regional Tribunal Meetings.

1357 Iberia: Destruction of Estancia-es-Karida covenant by its own magi, fleeing the Inquisition. Most of the covenant escapes through its Hermes Portal, but one stays behind to collapse the building.

1359 Rhine: The Bjornaer Gathering of Twelve Years at Crintera.

**1360 Twenty-first Grand Tribunal:**

1361: Regional Tribunal Meetings.

1368: Regional Tribunal Meetings.

1375: Regional Tribunal Meetings.

1382: Regional Tribunal Meetings.

1389: Regional Tribunal Meetings.

**1393 Twenty-second Grand Tribunal: **

1396: Regional Tribunal Meetings.




# APPENDIX


## Thoth, Hermes and Mercurius

*"Quod est superius est sicut quod inferius, et quod inferius est sicut quod est superius."*

*(‘That which is above is like to that which is below, and that which is below is like to that which is above.’ *or simply* ‘As above, so below’)*

– Hermes Trismegistus (Hermes the Thrice-Great). The beginning of the "Tabula Smaragdina" (Emerald Tablet)

Hermes was born from the union of Zeus with the nymph Maia, on Mount Cyllene in Arcadia, and later took on the role as messenger of the Greek god Zeus. With winged helmet and feet he would fly on the winds and deliver information, threats, orders, or whatever else it was that Zeus required; and was the god of mental activity, gambling, and business. Hermes loved trickery and sudden turns of fate, often bringing victory to heroes and gods in need; as god of travelers and night, he also rules dreams, prophecy, and escorts the dead to the underworld.

During the Greco-Egyptian period, the Greeks saw in Thoth many similar attributes to their own god Hermes and the two were essentially married in name to represent the same ideas. During the Roman period, the god Mercurius, or Mercury was added, and with it, one of the more significant astrological associations for *Thoth-Hermes*, also known as *Thoth-Hermes-Mercurius*.


## Works Cited - version 1.1

[Note: this section unchanged by the 2.0 compiler. However, with v2.50, the entries from books not under Open License - that aren't covered by Open License material - has been removed.]

Most of the works cited are Ars Magica supplements, but a few "mundane" texts were consulted. Most of the abbreviations of the titles are likely to be fairly obvious to the long-time Ars Magica player, but for the less experienced a full list of supplements is provided.

Ars Magica Texts:

BaS: Blood and Sand

BCC: The Broken Covenant of Calebais

Covenants: Covenants

DatB: Dragon and the Bear

FA: The Fallen Angel

HoH: Houses of Hermes

HtM: Heirs to Merlin

Iberia: Tribunals of Hermes: Iberia

Kabbalah: Kabbalah: Mythic Judaism

LotN: Lion of the North

Maleficium: The Maleficium

MB: Medieval Bestiary

MBRE: Medieval Bestiary, Revised Edition

ME: Mythic Europe

Mistridge: Mistridge

MND: A Midsummer Night's Dream

MS: Mythic Seas

ON: Ordo Nobilis

OoH: Order of Hermes

PD: Pax Dei

Rome: Tribunals of Hermes: Rome

Saga Pack: Saga Pack

SoI: Sanctum of Ice

Stormrider: Stormrider

RotSR: Return of the Stormrider

Tempest: The Tempest

Triamore: Triamore: The Covenant at Lucien's Folly

UT: Ultima Thule

WG: The Wizard's Grimoire

WGRE: Wizard's Grimoire, Revised Edition

WT: A Winter's Tale

Mundane Texts

These are some of the texts used to prepare the non-Mythic Europe specific entries.

Legends of the World, Edited by Richard Cavendish

Magic in the Middle Ages, by Richard Kiekefer

The People's Chronology, Edited by James Trager

Other texts were consulted and titles not noted; this is particularly true of the "Era of" entries for Charlemagne, Beowulf, Arthur, etc. Several entries of "legendary historical" characters were extrapolated, and no authority is claimed or cited. If Lyonesse sank toward the end of Arthur's Reign, and Beowulf’s predecessor’s sent Saxons to England in Arthur's predecessor's period, then all three are roughly contemporary. Similarly, the fall of Troy is a historical event around 1200 BC, and is in the later Greek Heroic Age, so the Heroic Age occurred around 1200 BC. The exercise of confirmation is left to the reader.

Images

Arts and Houses symbols originally designed by Richard Thomas for Ars Magica.


## Works Cited - version 2.0x

A harsh but difficult choice was made to remove all citations in the timeline, as the document is overhauled and rewritten to be player-facing directly (and to improve player legibility, and avoid too easy "cheating"). All the previous work cited pre-Arm5e works, and there are many inconsistencies. Rewriting everything to add Arm5e citations as well would have been a big task in itself. 

The ambition is also for the document to be more self-contained, which is why as many entries as possible have been expanded to put them in the proper contest.

When needed, it should not be that difficult to find the suitable Arm5e supplement.

Note: 2.20 adds Finis Terrae.

See Versioning Information below for available details.


## Versioning Information & Changes

This Chronology was based on OM version v1.2 which only had few rewrites and minor changes relative to "The Chronology of the Order of Hermes" (Version 1.1, completely pre-ArM5e).

You can find the source here (can be useful if you want to find the citations for earlier (pre-ArM5e) editions, which have been removed in these later versions): 

[https://forum.atlas-games.com/t/hermetic-timeline/1003](https://forum.atlas-games.com/t/hermetic-timeline/1003)


#### OM Version 2.0x to 2.06 updates & changes



* Posted this to the Atlas forum (if you have a copy, and not using the gdocs, you can look here for latest version and discussion): [https://forum.atlas-games.com/t/ars-magica-chronology-an-in-progress-updated-really-big-fat-timeline-of-the-order/171381](https://forum.atlas-games.com/t/ars-magica-chronology-an-in-progress-updated-really-big-fat-timeline-of-the-order/171381)
* Tons of formatting, cleanup and language improvement
* Removal of Age of Aries dates (as in ArM5e) on all entries - much less clutter (it’s just a matter of 139 year difference, or actually 138 when year 0 does not exist in used calendars)
* Removed the word "Mythic" from all references such as "Mythic Europe", "Mythic Pounds" etc. No self-respecting magus or maga would refer to themselves as Mythic…
* Changed all entries of Dav’nalleus to Gaelic name Damhan-Allaidh as per ArM5e standard.
* Rewrote the foreword
* Added an about the Order blurb section

* Added and clarified the Four Major Crises of the Order.
* Uncounted clarifications and minor adjustments not breaking canon or changing dates.
* Adding depth and commentary to uncounted entries - while trying to avoid breaking canon as much as possible.
* Adding ambiguity and (as in later revisions) uncertainty to many entries. After all, this is a colored "historical record" with unknowns and errors. However, no outright known errors have been added.
* Contemporary Christian-centric view of creation of the World and its age. *Atlantean prehistory, what nonsense!*
* Schism War entries rewritten and attempted to bring more in-line to recent canon (a few too many sources makes it a somewhat difficult job, some select choices have been made)

However, Flambeau’s history has had a lot of (more or less controversial) changes over the years (mainly towards nerfing Flambeau), and especially with Arm5e canon. Note that this chronology retains a few items of the older version. There are a few more important historical facts from this book that should be added, but these don’t have established dates in this book unfortunately. Waiting a bit with those.

    * A note on sale of magical items becoming restricted in 1061 (for whole order) as written in this book does not make sense since there is no Grand Tribunal this year. There is a regional Tribunal but that does not make sense either to be so fast. We’ll stick with earlier canon that this is where Quaesitor Iernilus' investigation starts, and the decisions are in 1068 at the Roman Tribunal. The rest of the Order can be considered to slowly adapt afterwards.

#### OM version 2.20 The Finis Terrae update 2024-06-07



* Merged in chronology from the spanish 5e Iberian supplement "Finis Terrae". Huge thanks to Kuni Mizomura al-Tŷtalī and Antomonio who kindly provided 120+ entries over roughly 11 pages of similarly formatted Chronology. Several fixes and merges still needed to be made for continuity (and an entry or two had different time from previously, e.g. Concietta 923 or 930), but this made it a lot easier (feel free to provide more such)
    * You can find these as "Iberia" entries from 698 through 1218. Note: Not all Iberia entries are new. 
    * Some items were added to Grand- and Rhine Tribunals. 
    * These provide some new excellent entries in the history of the Order.
    * Note that Finis Terrae may not be seen as fully canon as it is by separate publishing and not available in English. However, IMHO all the entries fit in great with the rest and deserve the spot.


#### OM version 2.21 update 2024-08-06



* Numerous minor edits as found (as usual)
* Version bump due to re-review of Faith & Flame. Many added Provencal entries.


#### OM version 2.22 update 2024-09-04



* Belated rework and update on Pralix and The Spider War, more in line with the 5e and Thrice Told Tales in particular.
    * Edited some, but mostly new entries 798 to 810.
    * Moved several old entries out of timeline (saved in the Appendix entries)
    * Brude Deathless was removed/replaced in 5e canon with Damhadh-Duidas (although several things still seem different, and they made his tradition Infernal! Not personally a fan of that…). The name change is likely to have his kinship with Damhan-Allaidh be more apparent in the name. Good ole Brude heavily features in 3e Lion of the North - Loch Leglean Tribunal - which I delved personally into earlier (and therefore it was heavily featured in the earlier timeline). Brude is now removed from timeline (as the name doesn’t exist anywhere in 5e). Old entries are in the Appendix items. The 1099 Entry was simply replaced with Damhadh-Duidas.


#### OM version 2.23 update 2024-12-15



* Thanks to Ptitboul who prompted further update to The Spider War entries at the beginning of the 9th century. They’ve been cleaned up a bit further (including the Four Major Crisis Entry at the beginning - an earlier oversight). This includes the founding of Ex Miscellanea and Diedne vs Pralix fight. The earlier "Canon" date for founding of Ordo Ex Miscellanea does not make sense in relation with the Spider War. Date of founding has been moved from 816 to 811 to fit better with the rest of history. The 816 date was actually not part of any of the main 5e iterations (I could find no date set, or where I found that date eaerlier). Overall the Chronology should now be very much in line with what you’ll find in the Core Rules, Thrice Told Tales (has the 5e full appendix on the Spider War) and Houses of Hermes books. \
One identified exception: According to Thrice Told Tales; "Pralix never rejoined the Order". IMHO this really does not make sense as 1) Her entire Order did, and 2) She’s revered and noted as an additional Founder in so many other places. Her heart might not have been in it, and she stuck to Cad Gadu - but not rejoining, and not being at Grand Tribunal 817 does not make sense.
* Several entries with Quendalon and Guernicus, including 817 Grand Tribunal have been extended.
* 830 Rome entry on Magvillus was fixed after maastrictian1 kindly pointed it out.
